54551769.title: Why Spring Gets Confused - NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException Explained
54551769.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54551769.transcript: I hope you had a very good break. Since right now we are into the new section, I'm going to create a new project which is specific to these new sections so that all the code changes that we're going to discuss inside this section we can do in this new project. As of now, I have created a demo project under the section1 folder. Instead, this time what I'll do is, I'll try to create a new project. This time also, I'm going to give the name as demo followed by the folder I'm going to mention as section2. You can find all the code that we're going to discuss inside this section under the folder section2 inside the GitHub repo. Build system will be Maven, JDK will be 25 under the advanced settings, I'm going to have this group ID and artifact ID as soon as I click on this create, I'm going to get an option to open this new project in this window or a new window. I'll select this new window so that we are going to have two different tabs. So this is the new project under the section2, and this is the project under the section1. So what I'll do is, I'll try to copy that dependency related to spring framework into this pom.xml as well. So first I'll go to the other demo project and open this pom.xml. So here I have dependency with the name spring-context. Let me copy the same and paste in the new project. So the new project is present here under the section2. Here the pom.xml is already present. Let me paste the dependency details here. Once the dependencies are pasted, you need to click on this sync maven changes. With this now we have converted this maven project into a spring based project. So let me close this pom.xml. Next I'm going to define a package under the Java folder. The package will be com.eazybytes.ex1. So ex1 means example one package. Throughout this section, we're going to discuss various examples covering the spring core concepts. For each of the example, I'm going to maintain a separate package so that it is going to be super clear for you whenever you want to refer the code. Let me press enter followed by I'll try to copy the code from this project into the new project. Let me copy these beans config and demo main class. So here I'll try to paste all the content. Next I'm going to rename these file to example1. So let me type example1. Before that, I need to fix some compilation issues. So here I need to fix the import statements. So this Vehicle class we need to import from the right package first let me delete these invalid import statements. So now I'm going to import the Vehicle from the correct package. The same applies for project to config as well. Once the import statement issues is fixed, we need to rename the class name as well to example1 so that all the compilation issues will get resolved. So now I can close the demo project from the section1. Since we copied everything, we should be good and close the same. As a next step, I want to show you one of the most famous exception that you may receive while you are dealing with the spring beans. This exception is no unique bean definition exception. When multiple beans of same type are defined in the spring context, and if you try to attempt to retrieve a bean by type, spring cannot determine which instance or which bean to provide. For example, here I have three different methods returning the bean of type Vehicle. Off course, the name of the beans are different. From the first method, the name of the bean will be vehicle1 followed by vehicle2 and at last we are going to have vehicle3 bean. So in this kind of setup, if you ask the spring context to provide the bean of type vehicle, it is going to have confusion because there are three different beans of same data type available. In this kind of scenarios, we are going to get a no unique bean definition exception. Let's try to see the demo of this, followed by we can try to understand how to fix this exception. What I'll do is, under this ex1 package, we have ProjectConfig file. Let me open the same here we have import issue. Let me delete this and import this Vehicle again from the correct package. Next what I'll do is, I'm going to get rid of this string and integer related beans. Instead, I'll try to copy the same method two more times and rename the method names to vehicle1(), vehicle2() and vehicle3(). Under all these three methods, I'll try to provide different names for the vehicle. Under the first method, I'm going to mention the vehicle name as Audi followed by Honda under the second method and at last, I'm going to mention Ferrari. In total my spring context, it is going to have three different vehicle beans with the different names with this kind of setup if you ask the spring context to provide the bean of Vehicle data type, since there are three different beans, it is going to simply throw the NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException. I can show you the demo as well. Let me go to example1 here i'm going to get rid of all these unnecessary code that we discussed previously. Once the code is deleted what I'll do is, if I try to execute this code I'm going to get an exception. So here I'm asking the spring context to provide a bean of type Vehicle, but there are three different vehicle beans. That's why the spring framework, it is going to throw an exception. The exception name is NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException. Under the exception details, we have these information. No qualifying bean of type Vehicle, expected single matching bean but found three beans with the names vehicle1, vehicle2 and vehicle3. Whenever you're facing this exception, you need to clear the ambiguity that spring framework has. So how to do that? It's very easy when you are invoking the get() method, you can pass both bean name and vehicle data type or you can simply pass the bean name as well. So this time I'm going to invoke the getBean() method, which accepts two input parameters. The first one is name of the bean followed by data type of the bean. So under the name of the bean, I'll mention the bean name as vehicle1 followed by data type of the bean, which is Vehicle.class. So with this now, I should not be getting any exception instead I will get the output as Audi. So if I execute this program as you can see, we got an output with the vehicle name as Audi. I hope you understand on how to solve the NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException. As a developer, you need to make sure you are providing enough information to the spring framework or to the spring context so that it is not going to have any ambiguity while it is retrieving the bean from the context. So here my spring context has three different beans of same data type. So while invoking the getBean() method, if I pass the bean name along with the bean data type, it is not going to have any ambiguity. You can also invoke getBean() method by simply passing the bean name as well. Let me show you that demo as well. What I'll do is, I'll try to copy these two lines one more time. This time I'm going to mention the variable name as vehicle instead of mentioning the vehicle1. I'm going to mention the vehicle2 bean here, and I'm not going to provide any data type. Since there is only a single bean inside the spring context with the name vehicle2, i'm not going to get any exception since we are trying to get the bean by using name alone. We need to perform the typecasting manually by mentioning Vehicle here. So with this now, if I try to execute this program after performing the build, I'm going to get the output Audi and Honda as expected.

54558071.title: Naming Your Beans - Creating Custom Bean Names
54558071.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558071.transcript: We know by default, when we are trying to create a bean by using @Bean annotation, the name of the bean will be same as the method name. But what if we want to give a different name to our bean or if we want to provide multiple names to a given bean. Let's try to understand the same inside this lecture. When you want to provide your own custom name to your bean, there are multiple approaches the first one is this @Bean annotation, it is going to support a name parameter to this name parameter, you can provide your own custom bean name. So here I have provided the Audi vehicle as a bean name. The same will be maintained inside the Spring context. The other approach is instead of invoking the name parameter, we can also invoke value parameter. On top of the second method vehicle2, I'm mentioning the bean name as Honda vehicle the same will be maintained in the Spring context. And the third approach is, we don't have to mention any of these parameters directly to the @Bean annotation we can pass the bean name. So here I have mentioned the bean name as Ferrari vehicle. So with this now all your beans, they are going to be maintained with the names that you have provided in the Spring context. The same beans you can refer inside the code to get the bean from the Spring context. These three approaches that we have discussed so far, they only support providing a single name to the bean. But what if we want to provide multiple names to a single bean? We should be able to achieve that as well very easily. So there is a concept called Bean Aliasing. By using this concept, we can define aliases by passing multiple names to the @Bean annotation using a String array. First we need to mention these curly braces followed by all the bean names we can provide them as a strings that are separated by comma value with these now with these bean can be accessed by using any of these name. We also have an option to provide the description to the bean as well. Apart from the name, we can also provide the description by using these @Description annotation, whatever value that we provide to the @Description annotation so this information will be useful, especially during the debugging process. Let's see the demo of all these topics. What I'll do is, I'll try to create a new package under this demo project. Let me create the package with the name com.eazybytes.ex2. Ex2 means example 2. Into this example 2, I'm going to copy all the code that we have under the example 1. So once this code is copied, I'm going to rename this example 1 to example 2. After renaming this class, you need to make sure you are referring all the vehicles and project config class from the example 2 package. If you are using example 1 package, then you are going to refer to the old code that we discussed under the example 1. Even under this project config as well, you need to make sure you are referring the Vehicle class from the example 2 package. We can get rid of all these comments because we already have these details in another example code. Now this time what I'll do is, I'll try to provide various names first I'll try to use name parameter. By using this name parameter, I'm mentioning the bean name as audiVehicle. I'll try to do the same on top of the second method as well. But this time instead of name, I'm going to use value parameter. So the name of the bean, I'm going to mention as honda Vehicle followed by on top of the third method, I'm going to mention the bean name as ferrari Vehicle. But this time I'm mentioning the bean name directly without using any parameters. We can also use @Description annotation. So to this @Description annotation, I'm passing a value like this is a Vehicle class bean. Here I'm trying to provide some simple information, but in your real applications, you may have some complex description details. So those details, you can mention them happily by using the @Description annotation. Let me save these changes and go to the example 2. So here this time, if I try to get the beans by using vehicle 1 and vehicle 2, obviously I'm going to get the exception. So let me execute my program by using this example 2 class. So don't try to click on this debug, because if you see it is using example 1 class. To use this example 2 class, you can use this debug option and as we expect, we are getting no such bean definition exception. So this time the bean names are different. So what are those? audiVehicle, the same I'm trying to use here next I'll try to use ferrariVehicle. So when we execute this program, we are going to get the output as expected. So first we have Audi followed by Ferrari. We can also provide multiple names as well as we discussed previously. So what we can do is, to this Ferrari vehicle bean I can try to provide multiple beans. So I'll just mention Curlybrace and inside these Curlybraces, I can mention multiple bean names. So the second bean name I'm going to mention as my FavoriteVehicle as a bean name. So now I should be able to access this Ferrari vehicle with any of these names. So if I try to mention these bean names inside this code, I'm going to get the same output. Let me execute this program. As you can see, we got Ferrari as the output. I hope you're clear with what we have discussed inside this lecture. Feel free to leverage these approaches when you want to provide a custom name to your spring bean.

54558073.title: @Primary - Choosing the ‘Default’ Bean
54558073.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558073.transcript: In the previous lectures, we saw an exception with the name NoUniqueBeanDefinition exception. We know how to solve that exception as well. There is also an alternative approach as well to solve that exception. Let me show you the same inside this lecture. Inside our spring context, we have three different beans of same data type vehicle. If I try to fetch one of the bean by passing only the data type, I'm going to get this exception. Let me show you the demo here. I'm mentioning the variable name as vehicle and to this variable name, I'm assigning the output that I'm going to get when I'm invoking context.getBean() and to this getBean() method, I'm passing the data type as vehicle.class. I can copy this statement and paste it here. If I mention vehicle variable name here and start this program, I'm going to get the NoUniqueBeanDefinition exception. One of the option we know how to solve this by providing the bean name, we can solve this problem. But apart from these, we also have a better and recommended approach as well that approach is by using @Primary annotation. If you mention @Primary annotation on top of any of these methods, then you are telling to the spring framework anytime if you have an ambiguity to which vehicle bean to consider, please use this hondaVehicle bean because we are marking this bean as primary bean. If developer is not providing enough details and the spring framework has ambiguity, it is going to provide you the bean that has been marked with the @Primary annotation. Now after making these changes, if you try to execute the same program, we are going to get the output Honda here. Let me execute this program. As you can see here, we got the Honda output. Always prefer using this @Primary annotation to clear the ambiguity to the spring framework. I hope you are clear on how and when to use this annotation.

54558077.title: Modular Config Made Easy with @Import
54558077.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558077.transcript: So far, we are defining all our beans in a single configuration class with the name ProjectConfig. Here you may have a doubt, is it mandatory to define all the beans definition in a single class? Off course, it is not mandatory you can create any number of configuration files. Let me show you the demo around this as well. So under this config package, this time I'm going to create one more Java class with the name as AnotherProjectConfig. On top of this class also, I'm going to mention @Configurationannotation followed by i'll try to create a bean of type String. I'll try to define a method which is going to return String object. The method name I'm going to mention as helloWorld() and from this method, I'm trying to return a String object. In the similar lines, we can create any number of beans. Maybe I'll try to create a bean of type Integer. So let me define a method which is going to return Integer with the method name as LuckyNumber(). From this method, I'm going to return a number 16. If you see right now, we have two different configurations where we have provided the details of multiple beans. So this way you can create any number of configuration files, but you need to make sure to provide all these configuration details to the Spring context. So inside the example 2, we're trying to invoke the constructor of AnnotationConfigApplicationContext to the same so far we have provided a single configuration class, but this constructor it is going to accept any number of configuration class. So here if you see, there is a spread operator. So whenever you see this spread operator inside Java, it's a confirmation to you that so-and-so method or constructor, it is going to accept any number of parameters as an input. So what we can do is, along with the ProjectConfig.class, we can also provide AnotherProjectConfig.class as an input. With this now, we should be able to fetch the beans that we have defined in this new configuration class as well. So what I can do is, I can copy this code and paste it here. So this time, I'll try to fetch the bean of type String. Let me mention the variable name as helloWorld. The same, I'll try to pass as an input to this System.out.println(). So here instead of vehicle name, I'll just mention String value from Spring context is so-and-so value. So let me save this, do the build once the build is completed, if I execute this program I'm going to get the HelloWorld as expected. Instead of defining all your configuration class like this, there is also a better alternative. In real applications, we're going to have more than 20 configuration files or sometimes even 50 configuration files as well. So mentioning all those configuration details as an input to this constructor is a very cumbersome process. Under such scenarios, we can also use another recommended approach. The recommended approach is, by using @Import annotation. So what we can do is, on top of these any one of these configuration class, we can mention @Import annotation to this @Import annotation, under these curly braces, we can mention another configuration classes like AnotherProjectConfig.class. Since I have a single class, I'm trying to mention the same. In case if I have multiple classes, either I can mention all of them here, or I can also make sure that all these configuration classes they are following some inheritance hierarchy, just like how we have inside Java. So with this now, I don't have to mention another ProjectConfig.class here. If I remove these, still my code is going to work because we are passing only ProjectConfig.class here. When Spring Framework look at this configuration class, it also sees an import statement here from this import statement, it will go to this class if this configuration also has another import, it will go to that import class as well. So this way, the Spring Framework will make sure to load all the configuration classes along with the beans that you have configured inside them. So if I save all these changes and do the build and execute the program, this time also I'm going to get the same output. Whatever we discussed around the @Import annotation, I mentioned all those details and example in this slide for your reference. Always use this annotation when you have multiple configuration files inside your project.

54558083.title: Bean Creation the Easy Way - @Component
54558083.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558083.transcript: So far, we are creating the spring beans by using @Bean annotation. Inside this lecture, I'm going to show you another alternative approach. So this approach is most commonly used by the real developers as well. For our demo purpose, I'm going to create a new package with the name ex3, ex3 means example 3. Into this package, I'm going to copy all the code from ex2 package. Once the code is copied, first we need to rename this example2 with example3 so that we will not have any confusion. Next I'll go to the config package. Here I'm going to delete this AnotherProjectConfig and go to this ProjectConfig. So here I'm going to delete these @Import annotation followed by I'm going to delete all the code that we have written to create the beans by using @Bean annotation. Because we are going to use a different and best approach. Now I'll go to the Vehicle class. On top of this class, I'm going to mention an annotation with the name @Component. When we are keeping these annotations on top of a Java class behind the scenes, the spring framework it is going to create the bean of this class. So now the responsibility of creating the object of this class by using the new operator is going to be with the spring framework. After mentioning this annotation, we need to go to the ProjectConfig file and on top of these file, we need to mention @ComponentScan annotation. Since real applications are going to have thousands of Java classes, we can't expect the spring framework to scan each and every Java class and look for these annotations so that it can create a bean. That is going to create serious performance issues. So that's why as a Java developer, we need to give enough clues to the spring framework about the packages or the classes where we have defined these annotation @Component. By mentioning @ComponentScan annotation, I'm going to give those clues to the framework. Now I need to type base packages. So to these base packages, we need to provide the list of packages where we have defined the beans. So first, let me mention curly braces followed by I'm going to mention this package name com.eazybytes.ex3.beans. We need to mention the package name as a String. That's why I have surrounded this package name with a double quote. Now with these during the startup of my application, the spring framework is going to look for the configuration classes like ProjectConfig. On top of this class since we have mentioned the @ComponentScan annotation, it is going to visit all these packages and scan for the classes which are marked with @Component. And behind the scenes, the bean of this class will be created. So now I can go to these example3, and here I'm going to get rid of all these code that we have written in the previous lectures. Since we are going to have a single bean of type vehicle, I can use context.getBean() by passing vehicle data type and here I have mentioned that ProjectConfig class as an input to these AnnotationConfigApplicationContext. So now if I try to execute this program, you're going to have a surprise. So let me execute this example3. As you can see, inside the output we are seeing the name of the vehicle as null. Though the bean is available inside the context, but this bean object it is not carrying any data related to this vehicle. This is because nowhere we have initialized the name of the vehicle. Whereas when we are using the @Bean annotation, the developers are going to have enough flexibility to create the object on their own and populate any data into this object. So this is one of the major difference between @Bean annotation and @Component annotation. Whenever you're looking to have complete control on how your object is getting created along with the populated data, then this is the most recommended approach. Otherwise you can simply follow with the @Component annotation approach. The reason why in real applications, most of the developers that are going to use this @Component annotation is usually in an enterprise application we will try to create the spring beans for Java classes, which holds the business logic inside the Java methods for simple POJO classes or data carrier classes, which transfer data from one place to other place for such a simple POJO objects, we are never going to create them as beans because those objects they are developed for the purpose of data transferring and these data will keep changing from request to request. Whereas the Java classes where we have written all our business logic so those Java classes and their objects are going to remain for all kinds of requests that the application is processing. Because for everyone, the business logic is going to be the same. Just for our simple understanding purpose, while we are discussing the spring core concepts I have taken this simple vehicle class. But when we start building a real enterprise application, I'm going to show you what are the best practices that you need to consider to create a bean. For now, focus on learning the various annotations and syntaxes that you need to follow to create a bean successfully. And these @Component bean, it is also called a stereotype annotation. Inside spring, there are many other stereotype annotations, which we are going to discuss in the coming lectures. The reason why this annotation it is called a stereotype annotation is, in English stereotype means commonly accepted pattern or approach. Since mentioning the annotation on top of the class is a commonly accepted approach inside the spring framework, these annotations they are called as stereotype annotations. There are many other advantages with these stereotype annotations, which we are going to discuss in the coming lectures. In this vehicle class, I can also create one more method with the name sayHello(). So whenever someone invoke this sayHello(), this statement is going to be printed. The statement is printing hello from component vehicle bean. So what I can do is, I'll go to the example3after this statement is printed, I'm going to invoke the sayHello()method by using vehicle bean. So as soon as I press ., you'll be able to see I got an option to invoke the sayHello() method. If the bean or the object is not created, I'm going to get null pointer exception. Since the bean is getting created by the spring framework, we are going to get the output when we invoke this sayHello(). If I execute this program you'll be able to see, I got the expected output saying that printing hello from component Vehicle Bean. By looking at all this information, you might be inclined to use @Bean annotation because it is giving you enough flexibility to populate the data. But as I said in real applications, there'll be very rare scenarios where you may want to populate some data into the object to bean that is going to be created. Under such scenarios, you can happily use this approach. We should also be able to populate some data into the object bean when we are using the @Component annotation as well. I'm going to show you those approaches in the next lecture. For now, I'm assuming you are clear on how to create a spring beans by using @Component annotation. When you are using this annotation, please make sure you are mentioning the @ComponentScanAnnotation along with the package details on top of the Configuration class.

54558157.title: Bean Lifecycle Hooks - @PostConstruct & @PreDestroy
54558157.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558157.transcript: Let's try to understand on how to populate some data into the bean that is created by the spring framework using @Component annotation. Right now, the developer don't have any opportunity to populate some data. Off course, to this name we can assign some default value, but the same default value will be considered for all the beans or the objects that is created from these Vehicle class. So this is not the recommended approach. Instead what we have to do is, inside the same class where we are using this stereotype annotation, we need to create a method here I'm trying to create a method public void and the method name I'm trying to give as initialize(). So this method name can be anything inside this method we can write any logic to populate data into the Vehicle Bean object. But to make this work, we need to tell to the spring framework to invoke this method once the bean is created or once the object of the vehicle is created. Once the bean is ready, then only we can populate the data inside it. So to convey the same to the spring framework on top of this method, we need to mention an annotation PostConstruct, but this annotation is not from the spring framework library instead it is from the Jakarta Enterprise Edition library. As I said before, spring framework is inspired from the Jakarta Enterprise Edition or the Java Enterprise Edition. That's why few of the specifications which are good inside the Jakarta Enterprise Edition, we should be able to use them inside the Java framework without making any changes. So let's try to use this annotation. This annotation is at the @PostConstruct, but to use this annotation first we need to add a library from the Jakarta Enterprise Edition. To know the details of this library, I came to the maven repository website. Inside this website, you can search for Jakarta annotation. In the search results, we are going to have this result with the name Jakarta Annotations API. Please click on the same as of today, this is the latest version 3.0.0. I'm going to click on the same and copy these maven dependency details. So in case if you are using other build tool like Gradle or any other tool, you can click on the respective tab and copy the dependency details. Now I need to go to the pom.xml file that I have inside this project. Here just after the spring context dependency details, I'm going to add the new dependency details that we have copied. Whenever you add new dependencies into the pom.xml, this icon is going to appear to sync the maven changes. Please make sure to click on the same, which is going to take care of downloading the new libraries into your local repository. Now on top of this method, I'm going to mention @PostConstruct annotation. With this annotation, we're telling to the spring framework, please execute this method once the bean is created. Whenever this method is getting executed, I want to assign a value to the name variable. So this indicate the current bean object using the same I'm invoking the name field and I'm trying to set a value called Audi. So now if I save this and do the build this time, if I execute the example3 program again, I'm going to get the expected output. This time here instead of null, we have the Audi value. This way, whenever you want to have some control, once the bean is created, you can use this @PostConstruct annotation. Please remember you need to use this only when you are using the stereotype annotation like @Component. In case if you don't want to use these @PostConstruct annotation, which is from the Jakarta library, spring framework also provide another alternative to show you that alternative I'm going to comment this code followed by I'll go to the top of the class and here this class, it needs to implement an interface with the name initialize bean. When we are implementing this interface, we need to implement a method with the name after property set. Let me click on the same. Now inside this method, I can have the same code like this . name is equal to maybe this time instead of Audi, I'm going to define as Tesla. With these changes now, if I try to execute the example3 code, this time I'm going to get the output as Tesla. So this is the spring provided approach, but I feel this is a lengthy approach like you have to implement the initialize bean and you had to overwrite the method. Whereas using the @PostConstruct annotation is a pretty easy one. While using the @Bean annotation as well, we can try to define some initialization method as well. For example, if you go to the ProjectConfig under the example2 here, while we are using the Bean() method, we can invoke a parameter with the name initMethod to this initMethod we can pass the name of the method that spring framework can invoke once the Bean of this class is created. But usually we don't use this approach because anyway, we have enough flexibility inside this method to populate the initialized data. But you can use this approach in the scenarios where if this vehicle1() method is calling some other third party method and it is providing the return object of the vehicle and before you transfer that as a Bean, if you want to populate some data then you can define some initMethod by providing some method name like initialize(). So whatever method name that you have mentioned here, you have to define a method with the same name inside the Vehicle class. If you click on this initMethod inside the documentation also, they clearly highlighted this is an optional parameter, not commonly used given that the method may be called programmatically directly within the body of a Bean-annotated method. What they mean with this statement is, instead of mentioning the initMethod here, the developer can directly call that method by using this Vehicle object inside the body of the method. That's why this approach is less commonly used. Let me delete the same and go to this Vehicle class. So this is about @PostConstruct. That means once the bean is created or initialized so these methods like after property set or this initialize method will be invoked by the framework. And these methods will only be invoked only once in the bean lifecycle and when is that is going to happen? Once a bean is successfully created and the dependencies are injected into this bean. Once this operation is completed, then only these methods will be invoked by the framework. Just like how we have a chance to invoke our business logic once the bean is created, we also have a flexibility to invoke our business logic just before the bean is getting destroyed. Usually when the application is getting shut down or when the program is being terminated, the spring beans, they're going to be destroyed from the spring IOC container. So just before that destroying process, if you want to have some flexibility to execute some code, like you may want to execute some cleanup code, or you may want to close some of the connections or the resources hold by the bean. So all such operations you can also perform for the same you need to use an annotation with the name @PreDestroy. So just like how we have these initialize method, we can create any other method. On top of this method, I'm going to mention the annotation @PreDestroy. This gives a confirmation to us that our method is getting invoked just before the destroying of the bean inside the spring context or inside the spring IOC container. In case if you don't want to use the annotation from the Jakarta enterprise edition, you also have a flexibility from the spring framework. Let me comment this code. This time you have to implement a interface with the name Disposable Bean. So let me click on this implement methods. Now, if I go and execute the example3 program, I'm going to have the same output. @Bean annotation also have an option to execute some business logic before destroying the bean. So let me go to these ProjectConfig. So here, just like how we have initMethod, we also have destroy() method to this destroy() method we can pass the method name, which we have defined under the Vehicle class. This approach is also not commonly used because we can execute whatever logic that we want to execute inside the method body() itself, or we can invoke the same destroy() method from the method business logic. But I'm trying to share this information so that you are aware about all these three options. Whatever we discussed inside this lecture, I documented in these four slides for your reference. 54558161.title: @Bean vs @Component - What’s the Difference ?
54558161.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558161.transcript: So far, we discussed on how to create Spring Beans by using @Bean and @Component annotations. Inside this lecture, let's try to understand the difference between them so that you're going to have good clarity on when to use these annotations. When a developer is using @Bean annotation, one or more instances of the class can be added to the Spring Context. So previously we saw, we created multiple Beans of Vehicle class by writing multiple Java methods. But when a developer is using @Component annotation, only one instance of the class or one Bean of the class can be added to the Spring context. The reason is, we're going to mention this annotation on top of the Java class. whereas we're going to use the @Bean annotation on top of the Java method. Inside a Java class, we can write any number of Java methods. Due to this, always go with the @Bean annotation if you have a requirement to create multiple Beans of same data type. Otherwise, you can go with the stereotype annotation. By using @Bean annotation, we can create an object instance of any type of class, including the classes present inside the libraries like String, Integer, Double. Previously, we saw that we're able to create a Bean of String class, Integer class, Double class, and all these are the classes present inside the JDK library or Java library. Whereas, when you are using @Component annotation, we can only create an object instance or a Bean for the application classes only which are created by the Dev team. The reason is, we can't go into the JDK library or to the library classes and mention @Component annotation. We can only reuse the library classes, but we can't edit them. For that reason, by using @Component annotation, we can't create the Spring Beans of the framework or the library related classes. But for the classes which are created by the developer, we have complete freedom to mention this annotation on top of the class. To create a Bean by using @Bean annotation, the developer need to write more code like separate methods to create a bean instance. Whereas creating a bean instance with @Component annotation is very easy, the developer has to simply mention this annotation on top of the class. Moving on to the next difference, while creating a bean by using @Bean annotation, the developer is going to have full control in creating and configuring the bean. When I say full control, inside the method logic the developer can write any kind of logic to initialize the data inside the object or to perform any validations. So all such logic can be written inside the Java method where we have mentioned @Bean annotation. Whereas while we are using @Component annotation, developer will not have any control in creating and configuring the bean. To some extent, developers can use annotations like @PostConstruct, @PreDestroy annotations to have some control to initialize the data or to execute some logic before Destroying the Bean. But they are not going to give full flexibility like in the scenario of @Bean annotation. In the similar lines, while we are using  @Bean annotation, Spring framework is going to create the bean based upon the instructions or the values provided by the developer inside the Java method. Whereas when someone is trying to create a bean by using @Component, the Spring framework is going to take the charge of creating the bean and post that, developer will have access to the object. In this scenario, the developer can't use new operator and invoke the constructors of the class to create the object. The object will be created by the Spring framework behind the scenes. I hope you are clear with these differences. Now I'm assuming you have good knowledge around @Bean annotation and @Component annotation. Please feel free to leverage any of these options based upon your requirements. But in real applications in majority of the times, developers are going to use stereotype annotations like @Component. You are going to witness the same when we start developing an enterprise application by using Spring Boot and Spring Framework.

54558169.title: Meet Spring’s Stereotypes - @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller
54558169.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558169.transcript: Inside Spring Framework just like how we have @Component stereotype annotation, in the similar lines, we also have other annotations as well like @Service, @Repository, @Controller, @RestController. . By using these annotations as well, we should be able to create the beans from a Java class. But here you may have a question, which is when we already have @Component stereotype annotation, then what's the purpose of other stereotype annotations? To understand the explanation that I'm going to do about these annotations, first you need to understand what are the different layers that we are going to have in a typical Java backend development. If you consider this box as a Java backend application or any backend application that is developed based upon Python or any other language, the request is going to come into this backend application from the client application. This client application can be a browser application or a mobile application or an microservice or an other third party service, it can be anything. As soon as the request sent to this backend application, it is going to be handled by the controller layer or the presentation layer. This layer is responsible to receive the request from the client applications. Once it performs all the validations on the request, it is going to call the next layer, which is the service layer this layer is also responsible to return the response to the client application once all the business logic is executed. So this is the first layer. After this controller layer, we're going to have the service or business logic layer. Inside this layer only, we are going to write actual business rules, business validations or any data transformations. Once we executed all our business logic, in case if we want to save our data inside a database or if we want to read the data from a database, this service layer it is going to call the repository layer to fetch or store the data. This layer, it is also going to handle the database transactions. If there is an exception happens, it is going to take care of rollbacking of the data that is partially committed onto the database. The last layer that we have after the service layer is Repository or Data Access Layer. Inside this layer, we're going to write all the database interaction related logic. We're going to write a database CRUD related operations. CRUD means create, read, update, delete. All these operations related logic, we are going to write inside this layer itself. From this layer only, the communication with the database is going to happen by using various libraries and frameworks like Spring Data JPA, JDBC. From this repository layer only, we are going to execute the queries onto the database to fetch the data or to delete the data, update the data or insert the data. So this is how that typical backend application is going to be developed. These are the three mandatory layers for any enterprise backend application. You may have a question, why can't we write all the logic inside a single class ? Let me answer the same. Usually in an enterprise application, your business logic is going to have thousands of lines of code. If you try to write all that logic in a single Java class, maintainability of the code is going to be tough and the reusability of the code also is going to be less. Whereas if you try to separate all the logic into these three layers, like controller, service and repository layers, the maintainability is going to easy and the reusability is also possible. Let's imagine you have a method inside this repository layer, which is going to read the data from a given table. If you want to invoke the same method from other service class, you should be able to easily invoke that without duplicating the code. The same applies for service layer as well. If you have certain business validations, which needs to be performed across multiple scenarios, all those logic can be reused if you write the logic inside the service layer. Using these controller layer only, we are going to build REST APIs that we are going to expose to the client applications. Once we start building an enterprise backend application by using Spring Boot and Spring, we're going to use all these standards. For now since we are exploring the basics of the Spring Framework, I am trying to use a simple Maven application. I hope you got some clarity around the Layers in a Backend application. If you take this knowledge, now you should be able to understand the purpose of remaining stereotype annotations. You need to mention @Controller annotation on top of the Java classes, which are being maintained in the controller layer. If you are creating a controller class that is responsible to handle the incoming web request, then it is a recommendation to mention @Controller annotation on top of that Java class. Whereas if your Java class is going to present inside the service layer and it handles a business logic, then it is a recommendation to mention @Service annotation on top of that class. In the similar lines, on top of the Java class where you have written all the database related logic, you need to mention @Repository annotation. In case if you are looking to develop REST APIs, then you need to mention @RestController annotation, which is a simplified version of @Controller annotation that return JSON response from the REST APIs logic. Whereas when we are using @Controller annotation, the response will go in a HTML format. We're going to discuss more details about this @RestController annotation in the coming sections. You can assume all these annotations are the children of these @Component annotation. You can use this @Component annotation if you want to create a generic bean, which is not specific to any layer, if there is a class that is going to be used by all the layers and such scenarios you can happily use the @Component annotation. Otherwise you need to use these stereotype annotations based upon the layer where the java class is present. If you try to use the @Component annotation on top of the business logic layer, you're not going to get any compilation errors or the exception, but still using the @Service annotation is the recommendation because they are going to improve the readability of your code. When someone looks at these annotation, they can easily recognize that class as a service layer class. The same applies for @Repository and the @Controller annotations as well. Regardless of which stereotype annotation that you're trying to use, you need to always make sure to use the @ComponentScan annotation on top of the configuration class to enable the automatic scanning of these annotations inside a given package. We already saw an example around the usage of @Component and the @ComponentScan in the previous lectures. For now, I'm not going to show you any demos around these three annotations because once we start building a spring boot web application, we are going to use these annotations extensively at that specific point of time I'm going to remind this concept one more time. Let me show you what source code that we have inside these annotations. First I'm going to open the @Controller annotation, search for Controller and select these classes if you're not able to see a Controller interface inside the spring-context library, please make sure you have selected these all places. If you select only project files, it is going to look for this class inside your project source code. Since we are looking for a file inside the library, inside the framework code we need to make sure we are selecting these all places. Let me open these annotations. As you can see, this is an annotation that we can mention on top of a Java class. This annotation is internally using @Component annotation as well. That means all the functionality of the @Component annotation is going to be leveraged by this annotation, but we need to use this @Controller annotation on top of the controller layer. Here they also highlighted on the Java comments. So this annotation indicates that an annotated class is a Controller. For example, a web controller that handles the incoming web traffic. We can also look for @Service annotation, which is present in the spring context library. So this is also an annotation that is leveraging @Component annotation. This annotation also we can mention only on top of the Java class. So we need to use these annotation whenever we want to indicate a class as a service class. Next let's see what do we have inside the @Repository annotation. Inside this annotation also we have @Component annotation followed by on top of the Java comments, they clearly highlighted that the annotated class is going to be considered as a repository class. Let's try to see what do we have inside the @Bean annotation. On top of the @Bean annotation, the target type is mentioned as METHOD. That means we can use this annotation only on top of the methods, but not on top of the class. I can also show you a demo. For example, if I go to these Vehicle class and on top of these class, if I try to mention @Bean annotation, I'm going to get an compilation error saying that @Bean is not applicable to type. That means we can't use this annotation on top of the class. The same applies for stereotype annotation. We can't use this stereotype annotations on top of a Java method. For example if I mentioned this here, I'm going to get a compilation error. Always if you want to understand where we can use an annotation, you need to look for this target. So inside the target, if we have the type that means we can use on top of a class whereas if we have a method, that means we can use that annotation on top of a method. If both the type and method are mentioned, that means we can use that annotation on top of a class and method as well. I hope you're clear about these stereotype annotations. This is a very important topic because usually inside the interviews, people may ask you questions around this. What is the difference between these annotations? Please make sure you have no confusion around these annotations. Coming to the demos, we're going to see a good amount of demos in the coming sections when we start building an enterprise backend application.

54558171.title: Method-Based Bean Wiring - Theory
54558171.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558171.transcript: So far, we focused on creating the Spring Beans. As of now, in all the previous lectures, we only created the beans by using various annotations. From now onwards, we are going to understand how to establish the dependencies between multiple beans while using Spring Framework. Dependency injection is another basic concept inside the Spring Framework. If you are already aware about this dependency injection, wiring, and auto wiring, feel free to skip all the lectures around the wiring of the beans. Usually, in enterprise applications, as I said before, we are going to write the logic in multiple layers. Let's imagine, I have written three different classes like VehicleController, VehicleService, and VehicleRepository. If I created beans for all these classes, they are going to stay like this inside the Spring context. As you can see, there is no dependency between them. With this kind of setup, what is going to happen, when the controller layer, it want to invoke the logic inside the service layer, it cannot invoke because it has no dependency on the service bean or on the repository bean. The Spring context does not aware about the dependencies. So to establish these dependency hierarchy, we need to tell to the Spring context or the Spring Framework to establish that dependencies as well between the beans while these beans are getting created by the framework. Establishing these dependencies is also called as Bean wiring. It is a process of connecting these dependent beans within the Spring IOC container. Manually managing these dependencies can become complex. So Spring provides both automatic style of wiring and manual style of wiring. First I'm going to show you some manual style of wiring followed by, we're also going to explore the options around the Autowiring as well. So when you perform the wiring between these beans properly, this is how the Spring IOC container is going to maintain all these beans. When it is creating the vehicle control bean, it is going to make sure to create the dependent beans as well. So in this case, the dependent bean is VehicleService bean and this VehicleService bean, it again has dependency on the VehicleRepository bean. Spring IOC container will make sure to create all these beans in a proper order, followed by it is going to establish the dependencies between them as well. Let me show you some code snippets on how to perform these wiring manuals, followed by we can see that demo as well. First I'm going to show you some no wiring scenario. As part of this scenario, we're going to simply create two different beans and we're not going to establish the dependencies between them from the Spring IOC container perspective. As you can see here, I have a Person class and Vehicle class. The Vehicle class, it has a single field name called name. Coming to the Person class it has two different fields like name and vehicle. This vehicle field is of type of Vehicle. From this code, it is very clear that this Person object or class, it has a dependency on the Vehicle. Usually in real life also, a vehicle will be owned by a person and the person is going to dependent on the vehicle to travel from one place to other place. So that's why I have taken this simple scenario, but in real applications, we're going to have the dependencies in these style like controller bean it is going to dependent on the service bean. We're going to explore these examples and standards when we are building an enterprise backend application. For now, let's consider the simple person and vehicle example. Once I created these Java classes, I'm going to create the beans of these classes by using the @Bean annotation. So there is a vehicle() Java method inside this method I have created a new object of Vehicle and into this, I have set the name as Toyota. With this now the Vehicle bean is ready inside the Spring context. Now coming to the Person bean, I have created a Person object by using the new operator followed by I have set a name to the person with the value Lucy, but I have not written any code related to the vehicle. I'm not setting any Vehicle object into this field with that the Person bean, it is going to have the null value against to these Vehicle object. So here, what I'm doing, I'm simply creating two different beans independently. And the Spring framework, it does not know the dependency between the Person and the Vehicle. That's why it is going to treat these two beans as two different independent beans. If someone is trying to access these vehicle field by using the Person bean, they're going to get the null as an output. Now let's try to understand how to establish the wiring manually in this scenario, primarily we have two different wiring options. The first one is by using a method call. This time, what I'm going to do is, while I'm creating the Person bean, I'm going to invoke the setVehicle() method and to this method, I'm going to assign the output that we are going to get from the vehicle() method. So here I'm invoking the vehicle() method. So what is the output that we are going to get from the vehicle() method? We are going to get the Vehicle bean. The same I'm trying to set into these vehicle field of Person bean. With this now, the Spring framework or the Spring context, it knows that the Person bean is dependent on the Vehicle bean. And internally, it is going to perform the wiring between these two beans. Here how I'm trying to achieve the bean wiring by using the method call. So with this setup, if I try to execute this code, where I'm trying to get the Person bean and Vehicle bean from the context, followed by, if I try to execute the person.name, vehicle.name and person.vehicle, this time from this last statement, I'm going to get an output saying that so-and-so person has so-and-so vehicle with the name Toyota. We're not going to get the null value. If something is not clear, don't worry, we're going to see this demo in few minutes. Let me show you the next approach as well. This time we are going to perform the wiring of the beans manually by using method parameters. To the person method, I'm going to pass the vehicle as an input parameter, followed by the same input to method parameter, I'm going to set by invoking the setVehicle() method. This time I'm not invoking this method directly, instead I'm passing this vehicle as an dependency to this person() method and the same parameter I'm trying to assign to the setVehicle() method. When we have this kind of setup behind the scenes, what spring framework it is going to do is when it is trying to create the bean of Person, it is going to see that this person method, it has a dependency on the Vehicle object or Vehicle bean. So it is going to check, are there any configurations inside the spring context related to Vehicle bean? If you already have such configurations, first it will make sure to create the Vehicle bean. The same Vehicle bean will be injected as a dependency by the spring context behind the scenes. So by the time we are passing these veh variable as an input to the setVehicle(), we are setting the Vehicle bean itself as an input to this vehicle field of Person. And with that, the wiring between these two beans is going to happen and we are going to get the similar output that we saw in the previous slide. I hope you're clear on what is wiring and how to perform these wiring while we are using @Bean annotation. Let's see the demo of these examples in the next lecture.

54558183.title: Autowiring Demo — Field vs Setter Injection
54558183.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54558183.transcript: In the examples that we have discussed in the previous lecture, we as a developer manually performing the wiring by invoking the setter() method on top of this person bean and assigning this vehicle bean. Though this approach may be required under certain scenarios, but this is not the recommended approach. Instead, there is a better and simple approach as well let's try to understand what is that approach. The name of this new approach is AutoWiring. The reason why this approach is called as auto wiring is, the spring framework is going to take care of wiring the beans automatically and injecting that dependencies as well. We as developers, we just have to make certain configurations and without the wiring and dependency injection, everything is going to take care by the spring framework. When we are following this auto wiring, it is going to eliminate all the need of manual object creation by using the new keyword we don't have to invoke the setter() method like we have done in the previous lecture. The spring framework is going to find the right dependency and inject it automatically into a given bean. So this is going to make our applications loosely coupled and easier to maintain as well. With loosely coupled, we're going to get a lot of benefits as well when we are using this auto wiring. These benefits include reduced boilerplate code, improves code readability, supports loose coupling, and spring is going to manage all the dependencies efficiently without me writing any manual code. Inside spring there are three main ways to perform the auto wiring by using Field Injection, Setter Injection and a Constructor Injection. Whenever we want to perform the auto wiring, we have to mention these @Autowired annotation. If you are mentioning these annotation on top of the field, then it is going to become the Field Injection. Whereas if you are mentioning the same on top of the setter() method, it is going to become Setter Injection . and in the similar lines, if we are mentioning the @Autowired annotation on top of the constructor, it is going to become Constructor Injection. Let's try to see the demo of all these three approaches so that you are going to clear on how to perform these auto wiring by using these @Autowired annotation. To show you that demo of auto wiring this time, I'm going to create new couple of beans. The first one, I'm going to call it as car. Previously, we have the Vehicle bean right now I'm trying to create a new bean of type Car on top of this class I'm going to mention @Component annotation. So when we are mentioning the stereotype annotation, we know what is going to happen behind the scenes. This Car class, it is going to have a name field, getter and setter() along with that to String() method. We can copy this code from the Vehicle. So maybe what I can do is, I can copy this entire code and paste it here. We may need to make some changes here instead of Vehicle we need to mention Car here also I'm going to mention Car bean created, followed by here also I'm going to mention Car. With these changes, the Car bean is going to be created in the similar lines, I'm also going to create another Java class with the name Engine. Let me mention the class name as Engine. On top of this class also, I'm going to mention @Component annotation, followed by I'm going to paste a few lines of code into this class. You can get this code from the GitHub repo as well. If you see here, I have a name field and a constructor along with the getter() and setter() and toString() method as well. Apart from these methods, I also have a method which is going to be invoked once the bean is created, because I have mentioned these @PostConstruct. So for this engine, I'm giving a name as V8. In the similar lines, I'm also going to create @PostConstruct method inside the Car as well. So here I'm going to mention the car name as Kia. So with this now, two different beans are ready inside my application, but right now they don't have any dependency between them. So what I'll do is, just below this name field I'm going to create a field of type Engine with a variable name as engine. In real world also, a vehicle is going to dependent on the engine, the same analogy I'm trying to follow here. Now on top of this field, if I mention @Autowired annotation, that dependency injection or the wiring of these two beans Car and engine is going to taken care by the spring framework. Out of these three Autowiring approaches, Constructor Injection is the most recommended approach for production code. It is the best practice for you to always perform the Autowiring by using the Constructor Injection. So there are certain reasons why developers are not encouraged to use this Field Injection and Setter Injection. Let's try to understand them before we try to close this lecture. One of the primary drawback of Field and Setter Injection is, your unit testing is going to become tougher because you can't mark the dependencies very easily. Let me explain what do I mean here. Whereas when we are using the Constructor Injection, it is going to be very easy. I'll explain how Constructor Injection is going to work in the next lecture. Whenever you have a doubt on which auto wiring you should use, I would recommend you to go with the Constructor Injection because that is the standard recommended approach.
