54559355.title: Singleton Scope - One Bean to Rule Them All
54559355.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54559355.transcript: Inside Spring Framework, we have another important concept with the name Bean scopes. As part of the Spring Framework, a bean can have any of these 6 scopes. By default, if we are not defining any scope, the bean is going to have a single ton scope. So what is a Bean scope? By using the Bean scope, we can define how Spring should create and manages bean instances. We can control how many instances of a bean that Spring can create. In the similar lines, we can also control when these beans will be created by the Spring Framework. Inside this section, we are going to explore and see the demos of singleton and prototype Bean scope. Once we start building a web application by using the Spring Boot Framework, we are going to discuss about the remaining Bean Scope. I also highlighted here that these last 4 Bean Scopes, they are applicable only for the web application. As of now, we didn't develop any web application, it is a small Spring application. That's why let's try to focus on Singleton and Prototype inside this section. Singleton scope is a default scope, that means when you are trying to create a bean inside the Spring Framework, that bean is going to have the Singleton scope behind the scenes. This Singleton scope is inspired from the Singleton design pattern that we have, this pattern ensures only one instance of a class is available per application. In the similar lines, Spring Singleton scope also maintains one instance per bean definition within the Spring context. If multiple bean definitions of the same type are declared, then Spring will manage separate single instances for each unique bean definition rather than enforcing a single instance across the entire application. We know there is an option to define multiple beans of a same data type when we are using @Bean annotation. . Basically, we can define any number of java methods that return same Vehicle object or same class data type. On top of all these methods, if you mention @Bean annotation all of them they are going to return multiple beans of same data type. In that scenario, we're going to have multiple bean definitions. For example, if I have multiple methods like v1, v2, v3, which are going to return three different vehicle beans with the Singleton bean scope behind the scenes, the Spring Framework will make sure that each of these bean definitions, they are going to have a separate single instance bean. So this will be v1 bean, and there'll be v2 bean, and there'll be v3 bean. Whereas if you are using stereotype annotation to create a bean, we will left with an option to define only a single bean definition. We can't create multiple beans of same data type when you are using stereotype annotation. In this kind of scenario, since there is only a single bean definition, there will be a single bean instance maintained by the Spring Framework. Here I have given an example on how to use the Singleton scope when we are defining a bean by using stereotype annotation. So there is a class with the name MyService. Inside this class, we have a constructor with a statement as MyService instance created. On top of this class, I have mentioned the scope as Singleton by using this @Scope annotation. And this is completely optional because even if you don't mention the scope as Singleton behind the scenes, anyway it is going to construct the Singleton because it is the default bean scope. Now let's try to understand what is going to happen when we define a bean scope as Singleton. When the bean is Singleton, and if this bean is requested from the context multiple times, the same instance of the bean will be provided by the Spring Framework. So here if you see, I'm trying to invoke the GetBean() method on top of the context couple of times and both the times I'm passing MyService.class as a data type. And from these two lines, we're trying to catch the output by using two different object references like service1 and service2. If you check the hash code of both these objects, it is going to be the same because both these objects references, they're pointing to a same instance of the bean. Let's see the demo of this Singleton scope very quickly. I'm going to create a new sub package with the name ex7. Under this package, I will try to create another sub package with the name beans, inside these beans, I'll try to create a java class with the name MyService. On top of this class, I'm going to mention @Component annotation followed by, I'll try to define a default constructor and inside this constructor, I'll try to have System.out.println() with the statement, MyService Bean is created. We know by default, the scope of this bean is going to be Singleton, but if you want to specifically define again, the Singleton scope you can use @Scope annotation, and to this @Scope annotation, we can pass the singleton as the value. Instead of providing the spelling, we can also read the spelling from a constant present inside the BeanDefinition. So this BeanDefinition is an interface, inside this, we will have a constant with the name SCOPE_SINGLETON. If you open this constant, it is going to have the same value Singleton. If you feel that you're going to make some mistakes with this spelling, you can use this constant as well to mark a bean as Singleton. As I said, this is completely optional. Even if you're not mentioning this, the bean still it is going to have a Singleton scope. Let's go and try to create another sub package with the name Config and inside this, I'm going to create a file with the name ProjectScopeConfig. On top of this, I'm going to mention @Configuration annotation followed by @ComponentScan, by using this @ComponentScan annotation, I'm going to invoke the base packages and I'll mention the package details where I have defined my beans. So this is a package name, let me paste the same here now I'll try to create a main class with the name Example7. Inside this, I'll try to create a main() method. Let me load the Spring context by using AnnotationConfigApplicationContext and to this, I need to pass class details where I have defined my configurations. The class name is ProjectScopeConfig.class on the left hand side, I'm going to catch the output with the variable name as context. Now from this context, I'm going to get the bean of type MyService.class. The output I'm going to catch by using a variable name as myservice1. Let me duplicate this line and rename this variable name 2. So if you see, I'm asking the Spring context to give the bean two different times. And both these times, I'm providing the same data type MyService.class. So with the singleton scope as we discussed, regardless of how many times you're asking and regardless of how many different places you're trying to fetch the bean of MyService or regardless of in how many places you are trying to inject this MyService as a dependency, in all those places, the same instance of the MyService bean will be used across the application. We can confirm the same as well by checking the hash code of both these objects. So what I'll do is, I'll try to print MyService.hashcode() output, let me print the same here and if I try to execute this program, I'm going to get the same hash code. So if you see the memory location or the hash code of both these objects are same, that means we are pointing to the same object. Now let's try to understand why Spring team, they made the singleton as a default scope and under which scenarios we should be fine with these default scope. The main reason of making every bean as singleton by default is, to achieve the performance. If you're not making the scope as singleton, and if the framework tried to create a new bean every once someone asked, in enterprise application, it is going to have some performance problems, because every time if someone asks if the bean is getting created from scratch behind the scenes, it is going to take some milliseconds and for the enterprise applications where they have humongous amount of data or traffic, they're going to face some performance problems. This is one main reason. And the other reason on why the singleton scope is made as default is usually we will try to create the beans of the classes, which we want to share across the application. For example, if you take these MyService inside this I have defined a constructor and I can define a hundred different methods with various kinds of business logic. Regardless of where this bean is getting used inside my application, I want all of them to have the same business logic. There is no meaning in providing multiple instances or separate instances throughout the application, if my java class is a stateless class and it only carries the business logic. What do I mean by stateless? If your java class, if it is not holding any data that needs to be different for different different requests or for different users, such classes we can call them as stateless because they're not holding any state or data that is unique to different users or different requests that are being processed by your application. So these are the main reasons on why singleton scope is considered as a default scope. Always, when you are having a doubt on which scope to use I would recommend you to go with the default and don't try to change that behavior. In case if your java class holds some data that needs to be unique for different users or different requests, then you can try to change the scope like prototype. When we are discussing the prototype scope, it is going to make more sense to you.

54559361.title: Eager vs Lazy - When is Your Singleton Created ?
54559361.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54559361.transcript: When we are using singleton scope for a bean, by default the bean is going to be created during the startup of the application itself. I can also preview the same. Let me go to example 7, and I'll comment all this code. With this, I'm never going to ask the Spring context to provide me the MyServiceBean. But still, my singleton bean is going to be created during the startup let me save this, and if I execute this example 7, I'm going to have a statement in the console, MyServiceBean is created. So from where this statement is coming? It is coming from the constructor of the MyService class. So this gives a confirmation to us. By default, Spring Framework it is going to create the bean of Singleton eagerly. We can change this eager behavior to the lazy behavior. We just have to mention  @Lazy annotation and with this, now my singleton bean is not going to be created during the startup. Instead, it is going to be created when someone asks the Spring container to give the singleton bean. If I execute the example 7, this time we will not see the statement that we have from the constructor. . Because no one asks the Spring IOC container to provide the bean of MyService and with that, it never created this bean. So that's the advantage of lazy. Now if I go to the example 7 and uncomment all this code, we're going to see that the bean is going to be created. What I'll do is, I'll try to keep a breakpoint at line number 11 and execute this program so far inside the console, we don't have any statement. As soon as I'm done with the execution of line number 11, at which I'm asking the Spring context to give me the bean of MyService at this point of time, the bean of MyService is going to be created. And that's why I'm seeing this statement inside the console. This way, you can also control when a singleton bean is getting created. Do you want during the startup, which is the default behavior, or do you want to change it to lazy so that only if someone is asking, then only it gets created. By default, eager initialization is going to be followed. Let's try to understand more details about these eager and lazy by looking at their differences. As I said before, eager initialization is the default behavior inside Spring framework. But this default behavior can be changed by using the @Lazy annotation. We can use this annotation with the stereotype annotations or with @Bean annotation as well. When we are using with Eager, the singleton bean will be created during the startup of the application. Whereas the lazy singleton bean will be created when the app is trying to refer the bean for the first time. So the concept of Eager and Lazy, it is only applicable for the singleton bean scope but not for the prototype scope. When we discuss about prototype scope, you're going to get some clarity on why this easy and lazy are not applicable for it. When a singleton scope has Eager initialization, the server or the application will not start if the bean is not able to create due to any dependent exception. So this is how the behavior is going to be. Whereas with the lazy exception, the application will start successfully but at runtime, whenever that singleton bean is getting referred for the first time, at that point of time, if there is an exception happens due to the dependencies, then your application is going to throw an runtime exception in between while you are executing a business logic. As a developer, you should be fine with these default Eager behavior, but there will be certain scenarios where you can go for the Lazy initialization because going with the Lazy initialization has its own advantages. For example, let's imagine there are some thousands of singleton beans inside your application. If you make all these thousands of singleton beans as eager, which is the default behavior, then your spring context will occupy a lot of memory and with that, your startup of the application also is going to be increased because all these thousands of singleton beans, since they're going to be eagerly instantiated during the startup of the application, definitely you will see some time is getting consumed during the startup. Similarly, as if you mark few of the beans as lazy, then your startup time is going to be improved. But don't try to make all your beans as lazy. By marking all your singleton beans as lazy, though your startup time is going to become better, the runtime execution of your business logics will have performance problem. So this performance problem is negligible for smaller or medium applications. Similarly, if someone handles millions of requests of data each minute, but then they may see some performance problems. Let's imagine you marked all these thousands of singleton beans as Lazy. With these, what is going to happen when the end user is triggering some business logic by clicking a button or by navigating to the next page and such scenarios, your business logic is going to be executed and if you are referring all these lazy beans for the first time as part of that scenario, the spring context, it will take few milliseconds of time to create that singleton instance bean for the first time. Whereas with Eager, you're not going to have that problem because all the beans will be created during the startup itself. That's why as a developer, you need to balance between the eager and lazy singleton beans. Most of the times you can go with the eager one. There are certain scenarios where you can go with the lazy option and achieve some improvement if your application is processing humongous amount of data. So what are those scenarios? Let's try to understand the same. Eager can be followed for all singleton beans, which are required very commonly inside an application. Let's consider a e-commerce application. Inside an e-commerce application, searching for the products, placing an order, canceling an order all these are the most commonly executed use cases or business cases. The singleton beans that are handling these business use cases should be marked as eager. So that the end user, whoever trying to execute that scenario for the very first time is not going to get impacted due to the bean initialization. You can consider creating the lazy beans that are going to be used in a very remote scenario inside an application. If you take the same e-commerce application, there will be some remote scenarios or very rare scenarios like closing the account or adding an address or changing the mobile number. These are all the very rare cases and the end user is not going to perform these rare use cases on day to day basis. So for these cases, you can mark those beans as lazy so that that bean will be created only when necessary. If you try to eagerly create all the rare scenario beans as well, you are unnecessarily going to occupy the spring context memory. So that's why based upon your use case, you can switch between these Eager and Lazy at any point of time if you have a confusion, I would advise go with the default eager initialization. I hope you are clear around singleton scope. Please always make sure to use the singleton scopes for the classes that do not carry any data which is going to change from user to user. All your service classes, controller classes or repository classes which are going to strictly have the business logic, all of them you can mark them as singleton scopes.

54559367.title: Prototype Scope - Fresh Bean Every Time
54559367.section: Spring Beans Deep Dive — AutoWiring, Scopes and More
54559367.transcript: Since we already discussed and understand on how the singleton scope is going to work, now understanding Prototype scope is going to be very easy. When someone uses Prototype scope, it is going to follow the exact opposite behavior of singleton scope. With prototype scope, every time we request a reference of a bean, Spring will create a new object instance and provide the same. Compared to singleton scope, prototype scope is rarely used inside the applications and we need to use this scope only in the scenarios where we need a new instance each time such as object representing user input. Here I have taken an example with the class name as UserSession. So inside this UserSession, we're going to have a sessionId and this sessionId value is going to be created whenever someone invokes the constructor of user session or whenever someone is trying to create the object of this class. Inside the constructor, I'm trying to generate a random sessionId by using UUID helper class. This is going to generate a unique ID, the same I'm trying to assign to the sessionId. Let's imagine there is an e-commerce website and whenever an user try to access that website, we want to assign a sessionId, which is specific and unique to that user. If there are thousand different users accessing the application at the same time, all of them should get different, different sessionId's for their browsing. If everyone has the same sessionId then you know what is going to happen they're going to see each other's data. So in this kind of scenarios, we can make these bean as prototype. How to do that? It's the same. This is a @Scope annotation and to this, we need to pass the value as prototype. So with this now, if I ask the Spring context to give me the bean, every time someone is trying to access that bean, either by invoking the get bean or during the dependency injection or during the auto wiring, in all those scenarios, the Spring framework, it is going to give a new instance of the user session and that's why when you try to check their hash codes by using these double equal operator, they are going to have different hash codes. And with that, we are going to get the false as an output with this code. Let's see the demo of this prototype very quickly. Under the beans package, I'm going to create a new java class with the name UserSession. On top of this class, I'm going to mention @Component followed by I'm going to mark the @Scope of this bean as prototype scope. To this @Scope annotation, I can pass BeanDefinition.Scope_Prototype as a value. With this, now this bean is going to have prototype scope. Let me add a field with the name sessionId and I'm also going to add a constructor inside the constructor. I'm going to assign the sessionId by using UUID.randomUUID.toString(). So this UUID is a class available inside the java util package. Whenever we invoke this method, it is going to give us random unique ID. The same I'm trying to assign to this session ID by invoking these toString(). Next I'm going to create the getter() method for the session ID so that we can print onto the console. Using this getter() method, it is going to simply return the session ID that we have inside this class. Now let's go to the example 7 class and here this time what I'll do is, I'll try to ask the bean of UserSession. So let me replace this with UserSession in both the places followed by I'll update this variable name to user1 comma user2. So what I'll do is, I'll try to first print the hash code of both these objects followed by I'll try to print the session IDs as well by using user1.get sessionId. Similarly I'll try to display the sessionId from the user2 as well. After saving these changes, if I try to execute this program, you'll be able to see the hash code of the user1 and user2 are completely different. Because since the bean is having prototype scope, every time we ask for the bean, the spring context or the spring IOC container, it is going to give us a brand new object. That's why we're able to see the sessionIds are also completely different in both these user1 and user2. But the moment that you try to make these UserSession as singleton scope, you are going to see the actual problem. So this time the hash codes are same, the sessionIds are also same. That's why please be careful when you're marking a bean as singleton, which carries the data that is unique to different users or for different requests. Let me revert back to prototype scope so that we should not have any problems. This time I got the expected output. Before I try to close this lecture, let me try to revise the differences between singleton and prototype one more time. We know inside spring framework, the default scope is singleton, whereas to use the prototype scope, you need to explicitly define the @Scope annotation along with this constant. When we are using singleton scope, the same object instance will be returned every time we refer a bean inside the code, whereas with the prototype scope, a new object instance will be returned every time we refer a bean inside the application code. When a bean is having singleton scope, we can control when the bean is getting created, whether it should be during the startup or during when the first time being referred. Whereas with the prototype scope, we don't have to decide with the lazy or eager because it is going to give a brand new instance every time someone asks. That's why these prototype scope beans, they're not going to be initialized during the startup. For this scope, there is no concept of eager or lazy. You need to consider the singleton scope for the immutable objects. So what is the meaning of immutable object? Once the object is created, if you don't want to change anything inside that object and everyone should have the same copy for such scenarios, you can go with the singleton whereas you should use the prototype scope for the mutable object. Mutable objects means even after creating the object, we should be able to update the data inside that object. When we are using prototype scope, this should not create any problem because whatever data that is changed inside the mutable object, that is going to be specific to a user or a customer. Since each user is going to get their own copy of this prototype bean, even if they change some data inside the bean, it is going to work and reflect in their own session itself. We can avoid all that work by simply using starter projects provided by the Spring Boot framework. I hope we are seeing the power of Spring Boot. In the very similar lines, Spring Boot has many, many advantages. We're going to explore the magic of Spring Boot throughout this course.

54565481.transcript: In the previous lecture, we built a very small REST API by using these highlighted code. On top of that DemoController class, I used an annotation @RestController. The client application can be anything. In these kinds of scenarios, the client application, usually the browser or mobile application, they are going to simply display whatever response sent by the backend application. Let's try to discuss the same. We know by using Spring and Spring Boot framework, developers, they can build powerful web applications. This Spring MVC approach is a legacy approach it is like listening to the music by using cassette. By using this model, developers, they used to control the format of the data that they want to share with the UI. And by using this view, developers can control on how to display this data on the UI side. And by using this controller layer, developers, they used to write their backend logic. So when someone is building MVC based application, it is entirely responsible to fully prepare the view along with the data in response to your client request. And this application, they need to execute their business logic, they can prepare the data and this data, they can render into an HTML template. When you are exposing your backend logic by using REST APIs, you will have several advantages. These applications, they are going to send their response by using JSON. And this JSON can be consumed or processed by various kinds of client applications like a mobile application or another backend application or an UI application that is built on Angular or React. Regardless of what is the type of your client application, still, you should be able to use the same common backend logic. Whereas if you are building your backend logic by using Spring MVC, only browsers, they can act as a client application, a mobile application or another backend application or an UI application that is built by Angular or React all of them, they can't understand this data because you're trying to send the response in an HTML format. When we are building an application using REST API, the app is responsible to only process the request and response with only data ignoring the view. Since this is the modern and recommended approach throughout this course, we're also going to build our backend application by using REST APIs alone. In this next slide, I have a tabular representation highlighting the differences between @Controller and @RestController annotation. Coming to the next set of differences, when a developer wants to build an MVC based application, the developer has to use@Controller annotation and with that, the methods inside the controller, they are going to send their response using the HTML format. All the rendering of the view based upon the dynamic data is going to happen on the server side. This is one of the serious disadvantages of MVC application. If all the view rendering is happening on the server side for each and every action, the request has come to the server and it is going to take some time to process and respond. That's why if you look at the older applications, which are developed a decade back, whenever you perform an action, they're going to be refreshed and they're going to be super slow. So the coupling between the view and your backend logic is going to be tightly coupled. The client applications, usually they will be browser. The typical use case will be UI based monolithic applications where you have all backend logic, UI logic as well. Now under the REST API based applications approach, we need to use @RestController annotation. 54565491.transcript: Behind the scenes, Spring Boot follows certain principles. One of them is Convention over Configuration. This means Spring Boot, it is going to give you some sensible defaults so that developer don't have to manually configure everything. I can give you an example. Let's imagine you are trying to build an Spring Boot web application. In this kind of scenario, we know a typical web application requires a web server and the code has to be deployed at a given port number. Instead of asking the developer to configure the web server or the port number, what Spring Boot it will do is, it will go with the convention what majority of the developers in the industry are using. With that convention, it will go ahead and configure the project to use the Tomcat server. Once a server is configured, it is also going to assume to start and deploy the code at the port 8080. This way, for all the functionality that Spring Boot is going to provide, it will always go with that default assumptions or default configurations by following this Convention over Configuration. At any point of time, if the developer want to overwrite the default configurations, the developer can do that very easily by going into a file called application.properties. So this is an important file inside the Spring Boot applications. Inside this property file, we should be able to overwrite the default configurations by simply mentioning some properties along with their values. I also mentioned some supporting principles that SpringBoot follows. By using these Opinionated Defaults, Spring Boot is going to provide some pre-configured default. For example, if you are trying to build a web application by adding a starter project, it is going to configure your web application with the pre-configured libraries or beans. For example you may require web server, JSON mapper, logging support, DB support. If so and so starter project is on the class path, it is going to configure so many things for you so that your web application can start quickly and you as a developer can focus on the business logic. Spring Boot is also going to take care of Auto-Configuring the beans. This is another important principle that Spring Boot follows behind the scenes. So based upon the dependencies that we have added inside the pom.xml, it is going to create all the required beans during the startup. For example, if we add a dependency related to H2 dependency, which is an in-memory database, then behind the scenes, it is going to configure all the beans that are required to interact with a H2 database. We're going to discuss about H2 database as well in the coming sections. Similarly, if you are trying to interact with a database like MySQL or Oracle, you're going to add a starter project with the name spring-data-jpa. In this scenario, it is going to configure the bean of entity manager, which is responsible to interact with the actual database. Let me show you all of this using a demo so that it is going to be very clear for you. Whenever we are starting our web application, as I said before, it is using embedded Tomcat server and deploying our code at the port 8080 with the context path as forward slash. So what is a context path? It is a root path that you can use inside your application before mentioning the REST API path. As a developer, if I want to overwrite these default configurations, I should be able to do that for the same, I need to go to these resources folder and here there will be a file with the name application.properties. Let me save these changes do the build once the build is completed, I'm going to restart my application. This time once the application is restarted, you will be able to see the Tomcat is initialized at the port 8081 and the context path also, it considered the path that I have provided slash backend. Now, if I try to access my REST API with the 8080 port, I'm not going to get any response. That's why I need to use the 8081. If I press enter, I'm getting a 404 not found because I should also mention the context path. So what is a servlet context path that we have configured? It is backend. After the backend, we need to mention slash home. If I press enter, I'm going to get the response that I'm looking for. This way the developer, they can always override that default to properties. We're going to explore all these properties throughout this course. In case if you are wondering, how do I know these property keys and values? You can find all these properties details in the official documentation. Inside this official documentation page, towards the end there is an appendix link if you open that, there is a link with the name common application properties. Inside this page, you will be able to see thousands of properties that are supported by the spring boot framework. In case if you are dealing with the database, there'll be Data related properties, Docker related properties, Testing related properties, Transaction related properties, Actuator,cache. So there are a good amount of properties. Let me search for the property server.port. So this is the port that we have configured. So the default value is 8080 is going to be used as a server HTTP port. This way, you can always come to this official documentation and look for the properties that you want to override. Now, let me show you that demo of Auto Configuration as well. Previously, I was telling you that spring boot it is going to do a lot of Auto Configuration of the beans during the startup, but we're not able to see that in action. In order to see the Auto Configuration in action, we need to mention the property debug as true. By default, it is false. I'm trying to override the default value to true. The reason why it was set to false is usually developers, they don't want to see everything that is happening behind the scenes in the framework. 54565499.title: REST API Best Practices
54565499.section: Mastering Spring Boot REST API Development - From Zero to Expert
54565499.transcript: As a Java backend developer, you're going to create hundreds of REST APIs throughout your career and you're going to work on thousands of REST APIs, testing them, debugging them, fixing defects in them, modifying them. That's why at the starting of your backend journey, it is very important for you to understand what are the standards that you need to follow while building an REST API. Building REST API is very easy. But if you follow the best practices and standards while building these REST APIs, that is going to differentiate you from other normal developers. I want you to shine as a backend developer in whatever organization you work. That's why before we actually start implementing some REST APIs, I want to spend  few minutes explaining you the standards that you need to follow while building REST APIs. By the way, I'm very sorry. So far, I have used this REST API word multiple times, but I assume that you already know this REST API. In case if you are new to this REST API, let me quickly explain you about what is REST API. REST means Representational State Transfer. Using REST, we can make applications communicate by using HTTP protocol. In the world of web, we're going to have so many applications. There'll be UI applications, backend applications, mobile applications, microservices, all these applications when they want to communicate with each other, they're going to follow REST. When the communication is happening using REST API, the communication can happen by either using JSON or XML format. But as I said, JSON is the mostly used format in the REST API because it is going to make your REST APIs lightweight. Since REST APIs are going to use a HTTP protocol for the communication, the REST APIs they need to be exposed by using various HTTP methods. Inside HTTP protocol, we have various methods like GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, and many other methods as well. But most of the times, developers are going to build the REST APIs by using these HTTP methods. You need to select one of these HTTP method for your REST API based upon what logic your REST API is going to execute. If the REST API is responsible to only fetch the data and send to the client application, that means if it is only performing the reading operation and some scenarios, we have to use HTTP GET method for the communication. How to expose the REST API by using HTTP GET? It's very easy. We have to use the annotation @GetMapping. We already use this annotation inside our REST API. According to the HTTP POST method, this method we need to use whenever our REST API is going to create new data inside the database. If a client application is sending the data and that data is being inserted into the database for the first time, then it makes sense to use the HTTP POST method. To expose your REST API using this method, you need to use @PostMapping annotation. For all the annotations, the mapping is going to be same. Only the method name is going to change. For GET method, it will be@GetMapping, whereas for the POST method, it will be @PostMapping. In the similar lines, we have annotations like @PutMapping, @PatchMapping, and @DeleteMapping. The next HTTP methods PUT and PATCH, you have to use them when your REST API is updating the existing data. There is a difference between these two methods. If your REST API is doing a full update of the data, like replacing the old data with entirely new data, then you need to go with the PUT method, otherwise if the update is, partial you are only modifying only a selected few columns, under such scenarios, the PATCH method has to be used. And the last one is self-explanatory, whenever your REST API is deleting the data or removing the data from the database, you need to use the DELETE HTTP method. How to build these REST APIs inside Spring Boot? We already discussed about it. We need to use the @RESTController annotation. This annotation is a combination of @Controller and @ResponseBody annotations. By mentioning this annotation on top of a controller class, we are telling to the controller to handle the HTTP requests that are coming from the client applications. And all the methods that we are going to have inside the controller class, all of them they are going to automatically serialize the return value into a JSON format or XML format based upon our requirements. And this JSON data is going to be sent as part of the HTTP response under the body or the payload of the response. This is the sample snippet code on how to develop a REST API inside Spring Boot. Whenever we want to expose a REST API, we need to make sure to mention this annotation and on top of the method, we need to mention @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping based upon our requirements. While using these mapping annotations, we should also provide the API path. Using this path only, the client applications, they are going to invoke our business logic. In case if you want your REST API to support multiple API paths, you can use this path parameter and inside the curly braces, you can provide all your paths. So here I have mentioned the forward slash and the forward slash hello as the two different paths that are supported by this API. So anyone who are trying to send the request to any of these paths will be forwarded to this business logic and once this logic is executed, the response is going to be sent back to the client applications using a JSON format. If you want to configure only a single path, then there is no need of mentioning these path and curly braces. You can directly provide the single path that you're looking to configure. And while naming these REST API paths, you need to follow certain standards. You should always use nouns, not verbs while defining your REST API paths. For example, if you are building an REST API related to users like by using these REST APis someone can create a new user or fetch all users, load a specific user, update user details, delete a user. If you are building these kinds of REST APIs, then it always makes sense to have the path as users in a plural form. And before these users, it is always recommended to have a common prefix like api,api,api to other developers who are looking at your controller as soon as they look at this prefix path API, they're going to understand you're trying to expose some REST API paths, but not the Spring MVC paths. Please make sure you are using the noun of your resource. In this scenario, the resource is users. So in all the places I have used users, users, users only. It is not recommended to use verbs like getUser, createUser, removeUser. That is a bad practice. So here if you see, the path is same for most of these REST APIs, but the supporting HTTP method is different. For example, for the first one and the third one, the path is same. If someone invokes this path by using HTTP GET method, then they're going to get all the users inside the response. Whereas if they are invoking this third REST API by using POST, then it's an indication to the API to accept the user data and insert it as a new record inside the database. When someone wants to deal with a specific user, then this path is going to be used here after the users I'm mentioning some dynamic placeholder id. The client applications are going to send values like 1, 2, 3. So by using this value, I'm going to fetch the user from the database and return the same as a response because it is a GET method. Whereas for the PUT or POST, by using this user id value, we are going to update the user details based upon the data that client is trying to send, but the DELETE REST API also, if someone sends the user id, by using the same I'm going to delete the user from the database. Usually while the client applications are trying to invoke the GET API or DELETE API, they're not going to send any data in the request body. They only going to send the data using these params available inside the path. Whereas for the POST and PUT, since we are going to insert the enter new user or update the existing user, the user related data apart from the id or any other primary data, remaining all the data will be shared as part of the request body. When we see the demos of these REST API, it is going to make more sense to you. The next best practice is around the response status code that we need to return to the client applications once the processing of the request is completed. If a client application invokes a GET method REST API, we are going to fetch the data and send back the same to the client application. In this kind of scenario, the overall response status code we need to send as 200. Inside the industry, there are some standard HTTP status codes, but 200 means we are trying to convey to the client application that the request is processed successfully. And this 200 means OK as a description. For the POST, we need to send the response either 201 or 202. 201 means the data is created in the backend database, or we can also send 202 that means it is accepted and it will get processed at a later point of time. For the PUT and PATCH, once the update is successful, we can return 200 or 204 that means no content. For the DELETE also, we are going to follow the same, either 200 or 204. So whenever you are sending the response status code by using two series, then it is an indication to the client application that the request is processed successfully at the backend side. For the negative scenarios, we have 400 and 500 errors. 400 we need to send as a response when the bad request is received. If you are expecting the user id in a number format, but client application they are sending in the alphanumeric format, we need to do the validation and make sure to not accept that request. Without processing that request, we need to send the 400 bad request as a status response. 401 means if someone is trying to invoke our REST API without any security. 403 we need to send that someone is trying to access our REST API with valid credentials. They provided valid login details or valid authentication details, but they don't have a privilege to invoke that REST API. We are going to discuss about these 401 and 403 in detail while we are exploring about the Spring security. Coming to the 404, we need to send this when someone is trying to access a resource that does not exist. For example, if someone is sending the user id as 456, and there is no user inside my database with this user id, we need to send these 404. 405 means method not allowed. That means the client application they are trying to invoke a REST API with an invalid method. If I am exposing a REST API with a path api slash user that supports HTTP GET method, and if the client application they are trying to invoke this path using POST or any other methods other than GET, then this status code needs to be shared to the client applications. For any null pointer exceptions or runtime exceptions inside our business logic, we need to send these 500 internal server, whereas if the backend server is temporarily unavailable due to some overloading of the traffic or any other issue, we need to send these 503 service unavailable. The 400 series status code we need to use in the scenarios when the request is not invoked properly. Whereas 500 series response codes we need to send whenever we accepted the request as a valid request, but we failed to process that request and send the response. These are the most important status codes that we need to follow, but apart from these, there are many other status codes as well.

54565505.title: Anatomy of an HTTP Request and Response
54565505.section: Mastering Spring Boot REST API Development - From Zero to Expert
54565505.transcript: Inside this lecture, let's try to understand anatomy of an HTTP request and HTTP response that we are going to use inside REST APIs. Anytime if a client application, if they want to send some requests to the backend application, they are going to use HTTP protocol. We already know about this story. So this is a client application and this is a backend application. So while they are trying to send the request, the request is going to come in an HTTP request format and once the request is processed, the backend application it is going to send the response by using HTTP response. So the response is always going to travel from backend  client and the request, it is always going to travel from client to backend. So first request will come, followed by the response will be sent to the client applications. Let's try to understand what exactly is allowed as part of these HTTP requests. For example, here I have given an example that client applications, they can use to send the request to the backend REST API. This is the formula that you need to remember about the HTTP request. So whatever marked with the asterisk symbol, they are optional in nature. HTTP request is a combination of HTTP Method, the Path to the REST API, any Path Variables, any Request Params, Request Headers and Request Body. As I said before, whenever a client application want to consume a REST API, first they need to provide the HTTP method that is supported by the REST API. Here they are providing the GET as a HTTP method. This method indicates what is the type of action that the client want to perform. And we know what are the valid values that anyone can send as part of this HTTP method. After the HTTP method, the client application they have to provide the path to the REST API. Here the path is api slash customers. Using this path only, the backend application it is going to identify what is the resource that the client application they are trying to interact. From a backend application, we can expose hundreds of REST APIs. For example, I can expose a REST API related to users and another REST API using profiles and another REST API related to products. So based upon these API path, the backend application is going to identify which resource the client application want to interact with. So here the path is api slash customers that means the client applications, they want to interact with the customer related resources. After the path the client applications, they can also send the path variable. Path variable is a dynamic value that client applications can send as part of the REST API path. So this is completely optional. So here after the customers, they are providing a path variable with the name 101. This is going to be completely dynamic value based upon these path variable, the backend application, they can identify the customer. In this scenario, the customer ID will be 101. Similarly, we can define any number of variables. For example, I can have a path like api slash users slash 123 slash profile slash 456. So this way I can have any number of dynamic path variables and whatever non-dynamic values that we have inside the path, those represents the static path. So while building a REST API path, that is going to have a dynamic path variable. Inside the Spring Boot application, we're going to define it with by using these curlibrace and within the curlibraces, we're going to mention some placeholder like ID. So whatever dynamic value that client application is going to send, that will be mapped to this placeholder and by using the same, we can execute our business logic. When we see the demos, this is all going to make sense to you for now, try to understand the important components inside a HTTP request. After the path variables, we can also have @uery params. Again, these Query params are also completely optional. We can pass any number of Query params. So once the path is completed, we need to have question mark followed by all the query params can be passed by using key and value. Here the query param is active and its value is true. Usually these Query params are used by the REST APIs to filter the data or sort the data or modify the data. Here I have provided the example. First we are going to have a question mark followed by key and value and a key and value. The Query params, they need to be separated by using these ambassad symbol. This way we can define any number of query params that are supported by these ambassad symbol. So these four paths, they are going to be present in the API path. These four parts we can identify by looking at the API path that the client application is trying to invoke. So this is the complete API path that they are trying to invoke. This is the HTTP method. This is the API path. This is the path variable. This is a query param. So the path and the query params, they should be separated by using question mark followed by we can define any number of query params that are supported by ambassad symbol. So this HTTP 1.1 indicates what is the version of the HTTP protocol that someone is trying to use for the communication. This is not part of the HTTP request. I just mentioned it for your reference on how an example is going to look like from the client perspective. Next the client applications, they can also send some metadata related information as part of the Request Headers. Inside these headers, they can send some authentication related information like username, passwords or content type, language details or location details. All this kind of metadata they are going to send as part of the request headers. If you see, this is an example of request header that the client application is trying to send. Every header, it is going to have a key here the key is authorization followed by colon. So the key and value, they are going to separate it by colon. After the colon, we are going to have the value for that header. Next under the request body, the client applications, they can send the data that they want to send to the backend server. Usually the body of the request, they are going to be leveraged for the POST and PUT operations. For example, if a client application is requesting my backend REST API to store the user information, the user may have a lot of details like first name, last name, mobile number, email, address details, gender details. Since such humongous data is not possible to send as part of the path variables or Request params, usually this kind of large data client applications they have to send as part of the Request Body. So this Body in other words, we can also call it as payload. Here I have given an example. Some client applications, they have sent the user data by using JSON as a syntax. This is how the JSON object is going to look like. To keep it simple, I just mentioned a single field in the example. So this is the key and this is the value. Just like how we have HTTP requests, we also have HTTP response that will travel from backend application to the client applications or the UI applications. So client applications are the applications who are trying to consume our REST APIs. Understanding the anatomy of HTTP response is very simple. It is going to have status code, which is a mandatory field followed by Headers and Body. Both Headers and Body are optional. As part of the status code, the backend is going to send the status like 200 or 400. These status codes we already discussed in the previous lecture. By looking at this status code, the client application, they can understand whether the request to processing is successful or not. Next, as part of the response Headers, the backend application they can send some metadata or any extra information that they want to share to the client applications. For example, content type is one famous response header. Under this content type header, the value is application slash JSON. By sending this value inside the response header, the backend application it is telling to the client application that I'm sending the response data by using the JSON format. Next under the response body, the actual data will be returned by the backend server. And this data usually it will be in JSON format, but if required we can also send it using XML format as well. For example, if a client application asked for the user details with an ID 123, we're going to send all the user details as part of this ResponseBody. I hope you're clear with the anatomy of the HTTP request and HTTP response. From the next lecture, I'm going to build some simple REST APIs that I'm going to use to show you the demos around the anatomy of HTTP request and HTTP response. Once we are clear with the basics of REST APIs, then we can start building actual REST APIs that are required by our UI application.

So let me save this,do the build once the build is completed, I'm going to restart my application. From the browser, I'll try to invoke this complete path. As soon as I press enter, I'm going to get the response based upon the business logic that we have written. For example, if I replace this 456 with 458, the same I can see inside the response as well. That means we are able to read the path variables by using these annotations. So this is the beauty of Spring Boot. As a developer, we don't have to worry much. We just have to use some annotations and certain configurations and with that, the heavy lifting tasks like extracting these data from the HTTP request that is sent by the client application is going to be taken care of by the Spring Boot framework. With these code changes, now if someone is trying to invoke the API path without passing these dynamic values, then they are going to get 404 error. For example here, if I try to invoke the API path without passing these 460, I'm getting a 404 error. That means whenever client want to invoke this REST API path, they should definitely make sure to provide both this userId and postId. In case if you want to make a particular pathVariable as optional, you should be able to achieve that by simply mentioning required as false. With this, now the PathVariable postId, it is an optional PathVariable. But there is no supporting API path that we have configured because inside the API path, we are always expecting the postId and post as a mandatory, whereas here we have marked this as optional. To make this work, either you can create one more RESTAPI with something like this, where you can update the REST API path as api/ dummy /user/ userId and you can update the method name also. And here you can maintain the required is equal to false, whereas in the top method, you can maintain the PathVariable as mandatory. That's one option. Instead of that, we can also follow a better alternative. As we discussed previously, a REST API method can support multiple API paths. So here what I can do is, I can mention curly braces and inside this curly braces, I'm going to define one more API path. This time I'll mention the API path up to this userId only. So whenever a client application, if they are passing the PathVariable postId, then this API path is going to match and this method will be invoked. But since the postId PathVariable is not sent by the client application, the postId value will be null. Inside our business logic, we should handle these null postId value as well. What I'll do is, inside my method logic I'll first create a response String variable followed by I'll try to have a if condition where I'm going to check if the postId is null, I will update this response String variable to have the value like Fetched user with id userId. Now from the browser, if we try to invoke the complete path by providing userId and postId, we are going to get this output. In case if I'm invoking only the user and userId path, then I'm going to get this output. This way, we should be able to play with the Path Variables based upon our requirements. Let's continue this discussion of PathVariable inside the next lecture as well.

54573549.title: Installing Postman & Testing APIs
54573549.section: Mastering Spring Boot REST API Development - From Zero to Expert
54573549.transcript: We are going to build many other REST APIs throughout this course. As of now, to test a backend REST API, we are trying to invoke the same by using the browser. But using the browser, we should be able to invoke only the GET REST APIs directly. This way I'm trying to group all the REST API invocations that I'm trying to make as part of this Spring course. And with that, it is going to ask you to save this collection JSON file into your local system. I will export this collection and upload to the GitHub repo for your reference. Once you downloaded the collection JSON file from the GitHub repo, you need to click on this import option and into this window, you need to drop the collection file that you have downloaded and with that, you're going to get the same Spring collection into your Postman tool as well. Under this collection, I have created a dummy folder. Whenever you want to create a folder under a collection, it's very easy. You just have to click on these three dots and select the option of add folder. Since we are right now testing some dummy REST APIs, I have named this folder as Dummy. Next, under this dummy folder, I created a new REST API invocation with the name Path Variable. Let me show you how I have done that. I clicked on this add request and it is going to show me a New Request. I can give the name to the request along with that, I can enter the URL and select the HTTP method. So that's how you can create a new request. Let me close this and delete this one. Now I'll go to this PathVariable. So the request name is Path Variable. Since we are trying to test some Path Variable related scenarios, I have given this name. To test a REST API, I need to make sure to select the supporting HTTP methods. So far we built the REST APIs that supports only the GET method. That's why I need to make sure GET. And coming to the path, I need to mention the complete path. For example, localhost 8080 /api/dummy/users/123/address/456. If I click on the send button, I'm going to get the response, whatever our Java method returns. If you see the response is coming in a JSON format, since it is a simple field, it is trying to display as a normal string, but behind the scenes, the framework, it is going to send the enter response as a JSON. In the coming lectures, we are going to build some complex REST APIs, where we are going to return an entire Java object as a response. Under such scenarios, you should be able to see the entire JSON object being displayed under this response. But coming to the @RequestMapping annotation, we should be able to use it on top of the Java class as well. Now, let's try to understand under which scenarios we need to mention this annotation on top of the Java class. Whenever you are building some REST APIs inside a Java class, many of the times, you will end up having a common prefix path for all your Java methods. For example, inside this UserController, we have various Java methods. For all these Java methods, there is a common prefix, api/dummy/users. So this prefix path is common for all these methods. In these kind of scenarios, instead of repeating the same prefix path on top of all the methods, what I can do is, I can simply mention here against to this annotation. So let me type /api/dummy/users. With this, now I can get rid of all these prefix path. Let me do the same here as well. I need to update for other methods. Here also, I'm going to delete this prefix path. Let me save these changes, do the build, once the build is completed, I'm going to restart my application. From the postman, I'm trying to invoke one of the REST API that we have inside our Java methods. If I click on the send button, I'm still getting the same response. That means whatever prefix path that we are mentioning by using this @RequestMapping annotation, the same will be added before the API paths that we have mentioned on top of the Java methods. This is one of the great advantages of @RequestMapping. I strongly recommend you using this annotation at least on top of the Java class. Whereas on top of the Java methods, we have alternatives like shortcut annotations, like @GetMapping, @PostMapping. Let me explain you on how to use this annotation on top of the Java method as well. For the same, I'll go to the DemoController here as of now, we are using GetMapping annotation. What I'll do is, I'll simply mention @RequestMapping annotation. And to this annotation, I'm going to mention the path as forward /home. The primary difference of using @RequestMapping compared to the @GetMapping is when you are using this @RequestMapping annotation, your REST API method, it is not going to be restricted to a specific HTTP method like GET method. When we are mentioning this annotation @RequestMapping, we're telling to the spring framework or spring boot framework that we are fine to accept any kind of HTTP method while invoking this REST API. I'm going to show you the demo of the same by using postman. So from the postman, I'll try to add a new request. And I'll just mention the path of this request as HomeRequest, followed by I'm going to mention the path as /home. Now, let me go to this home request and if I try to click on the send button, I'm getting the hello world. Let me do the same with the post with the post. Also, I'm going to get the same response. Let me do with the delete with the delete also, I'm getting the same response. So whenever you are into the scenario where you don't want to restrict your REST API method to a given HTTP method, then you can go with the syntax that we have here. But following these syntax is generally not recommended because it is not a good practice to expose the same Java method for all kinds of HTTP methods. Instead, by using these @RequestMapping annotation, we should be able to define the HTTP method as well. For the same next to these @RequestMapping, we can either invoke the path parameter or value parameter to the path and value parameter. We can pass the list of paths that this REST API is going to support. Since I have a single path, I'm mentioning the same inside the double quotes. But if you are looking to provide multiple paths, you need to mention the curly braces. And inside the curly braces, you can mention multiple paths by separating them using comma. After the path, I can invoke the method parameter to this method parameter. I can pass a single method like RequestMethod.GET with this configuration. Now my REST API, it is going to support only the get HTTP method. Instead of defining this lengthy syntax, you can follow the shortcut annotation like GetMapping. The same applies for other type of methods like POST, DELETE, PATCH, PUT, and so on. But the beauty of this annotation is it is going to allow you to define multiple HTTP methods. I need to mention curly braces. And inside these curly braces, I can mention multiple HTTP methods. Now I have mentioned post method as well. With this, now my REST API, it is going to support both GET and POST method, but not the other methods. Let me save this restart my application. First, I'm going to invoke with the DELETE method. I should get 405 error. Yes, we are getting 405 error let me use GET with the GET, I'm getting a successful response. With the post also, I'm going to get the successful response. Apart from these method param and path param, this annotation supports many other params as well. If you open this annotation source code and look for the list of methods by pressing command F2L or control F2L shortcut, you will be able to see that this annotation supports all these parameters. So by using path or value, we should be able to pass multiple REST API paths that our Java method should support. Similarly, by using method, we can pass multiple request to methods. You'll be able to see the return type of this method is array of request method. That means we can define multiple request methods. Similarly, there are other important annotations like headers(). By using these headers(), we should be able to accept the HTTP request headers. When we are discussing about the headers concept, I'm going to explain about this the same applies for params also by using the params, we can accept the query params or the request to params that are coming from the client application. We have consumes as well. By using these consumes, we can restrict what type of content that our REST API can consume. For example, here, I'm going to mention consumes and to this consumes, if I mention the value as application/json, then my REST API is only going to accept json as an input format. If someone is trying to send the request by using the XML, it's going to throw an error. In the similar style, I can also mention producers parameter as well. To this also, I can mention application/json. By using these producers, we are telling to the client applications that what is the response content type ? What is the format that this REST API is going to use to produce the response? Since we have mentioned this value for the client applications, the framework is going to convey that this REST API method is sending the response by using the json format. If you open any of these consumes or produce documentation, you will be able to see what are the valid values that we can send. We can send a single value or multiple value based upon our requirements. As of now, we have mentioned application/json, which is supported by these asterisk mark as well. But apart from these json or any other applications format, we can also send the response by using text plane as well. So all these valid values you can get from the media type constant. If you look for media type inside these, we have values like APPLICATION_ATOM_XML, which is going to leverage this syntax. Similarly, if you keep scrolling, this is the value for GraphQL and this is the value for json. Instead of hard coding this value, you can also leverage MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE. For example, we can go to DemoController and here I can mention MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE . In the similar lines, we also have another important parameter with the name version. We are going to discuss about this version when we are discussing about the API versioning standards in the coming lectures. I hope you are clear on when to use this @RequestMapping. If your REST API supports a single HTTP method, then always go with the shortcut annotations like GetMapping or PostMapping. Whereas if you want to support multiple HTTP methods, you can happily use this @RequestMapping. The other scenario where you can use the @RequestMapping is whenever you want to define a parent path for all your REST APIs inside a Java class. Under such scenarios, you can mention this annotation on top of the Java class along with the parent path that you want to define. Inside the code, please make sure to comment these consumes and produces related logic and remove this comma and close the annotation. 54573563.title: Reading Payloads with @RequestBody
54573563.section: Mastering Spring Boot REST API Development - From Zero to Expert
54573563.transcript: So far, we are building GET Rest APIs and by using these GET Rest APIs, we can read the existing data. It's like read-only operation. The client applications, they are going to ask what kind of data that they are looking for by sending the required information as part of the path variables or the query parents. But when coming to the POST RestAPIs or PUT or PATCH RestAPIs, the client applications, they may send an entire object inside the @RequestBody. For example, if I'm exposing a Rest API, which is going to allow the client applications to create a brand new user inside the database. In this kind of scenario, the client applications, they are going to send all the user related information inside the @RequestBody, like the name of the user, email of the user, mobile number, gender, his address details. Based upon our business requirement, we can accept any kind of data. To read that data that is coming as part of the request body, we need to use the @RequestBody annotation. Just like how we have @RequestHeader annotation, in the similar lines, we have @RequestBody annotation. This time, let me create a @PostMapping annotation, and I'm not going to mention any path against to this annotation because whatever parent path that we have, if the client application, if they're mentioning these, and if they're invoking the POST Rest API, then the request will be forwarded to this method. Let me create the method as public String createUser(). And to this method, I need to mention the annotation @RequestBody. Now the challenge comes, previously, we are using the data type of the method input parameter as a String or Long. In all the places we are using primitive data types. By using these primitive data types, the client applications, they can only share a single data. So in this createUser() scenario, we are expecting a lot of data inside the @RequestBody. For example, here, I'm going to create a new request with the name @RequestBody. And this request, it is going to follow HTTP POST method. Coming to the path, it will be api/users. Now by using this body tab, I need to select this raw option. And in this dropdown, I need to make sure I'm selecting the JSON because we are going to use the JSON as a format to communicate the data between the client applications and the backend server. To start a JSON object, we need to mention curly braces. And inside this JSON object, we can create any number of fields related to the user. For example, I can mention the name and the name will be Madan Reddy. Similarly, I'm going to mention email. And to this email, the value will be tutor@ezbytes.com. Next I'm going to mention gender.
