54573581.title: Why API Versioning Matters
54573581.section: Mastering Spring Boot REST API Development - From Zero to Expert
54573581.transcript: The Apple company every year, they will try to release new versions of their products like iPhone and Mac. Do you know the reason? Since Apple system want to regularly update their products, they will make sure to adopt all the latest trends and release the new products on a regular basis. Otherwise they can't survive in the business and they may fall behind the competition. In the similar lines, when we are building REST APIs, over time, developers might need to make some changes inside these APIs. These changes include adding new fields, changing the request and response format, modifying the business logic, deprecating any old features. When the developers are making these critical major enhancements in their REST APIs, there is a good chance that issues may arise on the client side who are consuming the REST APIs. Because the developer is trying to change the behavior of the REST API all of a sudden. To avoid breaking existing clients who are consuming the REST APIs, we need to use a standard called API versioning. There are multiple approaches to version our REST APIs, but inside this course, I'm going to explain you four different popular options. Before we try to discuss the details around these options, first, let me explain you by taking a real scenario on why API Versioning is mandatory? Let's imagine there is a food delivery company with the name FoodRush, and inside this company, developers developed many REST APIs that powers mobile applications, website, restaurant partners, delivery partners, and third-party integrations. The mobile apps are being used by 1 million users. Similarly, website is being used by 500k daily visitors. There are 5000 different restaurant partners, 10,000 delivery drivers, and 50 business partners. All these client applications, they are consuming the REST APIs that are exposed by the FoodRush. Let's imagine these developers, they are not following any API versioning. Initially, when they started this FoodRush company, they exposed one of the REST API, which is going to return the restaurant details. So this is the complete API path. Based upon the restaurant_id inside the API path, the REST API, it is going to return the restaurant details by using this response format. This is a JSON object. Inside the JSON object, we have a restaurant key with the restaurant name value, followed by there is an items object. This items is an array object. We can have any number of item objects. Just to save some space, I have provided only one item details. So this is the item ID, item name, item price, and a boolean value indicating whether this item is available right now in this restaurant. So this is the original response. But after a few months or after a few years, since this FoodRush is becoming very popular, the developers on a disaster day, they decided to improve their API by making the response more detailed. This time, if you see inside the response, they made the restaurant as an object. Previously, it used to be a String. Right now, it is an object. So inside this restaurant object, we have various fields like name, ID, and rating. And the items field is renamed to menu items. This is the new name and this is again an array. Inside this array, we can send any number of menu item object details. If you try to understand the item object as well, there are new changes introduced. The pricing is no more a normal String. It is an again an object where they are trying to mention the amount and the currency coming to the availability this is also became an object inside the availability, they have booleans like in-stock and what is the preparation time to prepare an item. Developers they tested their business logic and everything is working and they push this code into the production. And with that, do you know what is going to happen? All the client applications who are consuming these REST API, they are going to get some runtime exceptions, null pointer exceptions, whether it can be inside a website or inside a mobile application or whether inside a restaurant partner application, all of them, they will not work because there are some good amount of critical changes on how the response is being sent. Off course, in real enterprise applications, the developers, they're not going to push these changes all of a sudden, they are going to send an email to all their client applications to update their code. Even if that communication happens as well, still there will be some challenges. Let's imagine these Food Rush developers, they are pushing these new REST API changes on the date, December 20 and the same date at the same time, all the other client applications, they should also make their changes and push into the production. Do you think it is going to happen? Off course not. Few of the client applications, they may say they don't have bandwidth and with that they may not push the changes on December 20. That's why to avoid all these kinds of challenges, we need to follow the API versioning strategy. Now this time, let's try to understand how the same problem will get resolved by using the API versioning. When the food rush company started their business, they are going to build a REST API with this path. Inside the path, if you see, there is a path variable with the value V1. That means any client application who are trying to invoke this API, they are going to receive the original response format. Any new enhancements that the developers, they are going to introduce, they are going to achieve by introducing new REST APIs with a new version number inside the path variable. With this now, the food rush backend employers, they are giving options to the client applications saying that whenever you are ready, please update your code to use the V2 versions so that you will receive a detailed response format based upon the version 2 new feature changes. With this kind of setup, let's try to understand what is really is going to happen in real enterprise applications. During the week 1, the version 2 API is going to be launched. During this time, the version 1 still works. There won't be any disruption and the new features will be available under the V2. Email will be sent to all the client developers about the V2 and requesting them to update their code as soon as possible. During the month 1, let's imagine the mobile application team, they have updated their code they published a new app version that uses V2 REST API. In case if any of the Android application user or iOS application user, if they have not updated their app, still the mobile app is going to work with the older V1. Eventually, when the user updates at their own pace, then it is going to start consuming V2. The same applies for Partner Migration. During the month 2, the partners may migrate to V2. This way we're not forcing any deadlines to any client applications. Let's imagine, after giving enough time to all client applications, during the 6th month, a notice can be sent to all the client application developers saying that this V1 right now it is deprecated and it will be removed in another 6 months. With this deprecation notice, the client applications, they'll make sure to update to latest V2, and with that, the most amount of traffic will come to V2. After the 6 months deprecation notice period, the V1 REST API will be sunset and it is going to be turned off with confidence. And by the time all clients might have successfully migrated, there won't be any business disruption as well. I hope you're clear on why API versioning is very important. In the next lecture, let's try to understand what are the various options that we have to implement this API version inside any backend application.

54573589.title: API Versioning Strategies
54573589.section: Mastering Spring Boot REST API Development - From Zero to Expert
54573589.transcript: Inside the industry, developers they use these four famous approaches to implement API versioning. The first approach is, by using the Path Versioning or URI Versioning. Inside the REST API path itself, we are going to have version numbers like v1 and v2 and so on. Instead of this v1 and v2, you can also have other values like 1, 2. It's up to your business logic what value you want to maintain to represent the various versions of REST APIs. In the similar lines, we also have RequestParameterVersioning. If you don't like maintaining a path variable representing the API versioning, you can leverage the Query Params or RequestParams as well. Here, the client applications, they have an option to send a QueryParamVersion and with a value like 1,2. . Again, these Query Param name and value can be anything based upon your business requirements. In case if the developers, they don't want to pollute their API paths by using these versioning numbers, they can go with the last two approaches. The third approach is by using the request headers. Since client applications can send any kind of metadata, we can ask the client applications to send a specific header, for example, I can ask them to send a header with this name and with this value. Again, this header and this value can be anything and you can decide based upon your business requirements. And the last approach is by using the MediaType. So this approach, in other words, we can also call it as Content Negotiation. Both the client applications and the backend applications, they can perform the negotiations on how they want to exchange the data. So that communication they can perform by using the Media Type. Inside the industry, there is a standard header that the client applications, they can use. For example, here, if you see while they are invoking this REST API inside the request headers they can send these standard request header, this header cannot be changed always it should be accept only. But the value can be anything. Here I'm following one of the industry standard. Based upon this value, the backend server, it can easily identify that so and so client application, they are looking for a JSON response by using the V1. V1 means version 1. Instead of V1, we can have any other values like version 1 or 1 alone it's up to our business logic. Whenever the client application is sending this kind of value, it's an indication to the server saying that Return Version 1 of this API response formatted as JSON. There is a good history around this value. Let's try to understand the same. Whatever value that we are trying to send along with the accept header, we call it as MIME. So what is MIME? It is a standard way of telling the computer what type of data is being sent so that it can be handled correctly. In simple words, MIME describes the format of the content or format of the communication between the client and server. We have used all these words like application, vnd, myapp, v1, +json inside these values. Let's try to break down each one of them and try to understand the purpose of them. The first application part represents top level MIME type. This is the general category of representing data, by mentioning this application value, we are trying to convey that two different applications are trying to communicate with each other. And vnd means vendor specific. Different organizations, they are going to have their own specifications on how they want to send the response and the format that's why every vendor, they can have their own vendor specific format. That is what this vnd conveys. So this application, vnd they are always static. But coming to the myapp, it represents an application name or company name. Instead of myapp, you can mention your company name. And after the myapp, you can mention the V1. So if you see, there is a syntax that we are following here. After the application, we are mentioning forward slash followed by vnd.app name.v1 and at last, we are going to mention +json. So with  V1, we are providing what is the API version that we want to use. Similarly, V2, V3 values can be sent by the client applications. And the last part +json indicates what is the format of the data that is being used for the communication between the client and the backend server. Since we are mentioning JSON, the JSON is going to be used for the communication. You may have questions like why vnd? What is this vnd? As I said before, vnd stands for vendor specific. It means the media type is specific to a given organization, not a global standard. For example, inside the industry, very bigger organizations like GitHub, Stripe, Twitter, they all use this standard to implement API versioning. They'll follow these same syntax, but at the place of app name, they're going to have their company name like GitHub. So this is a custom JSON format defined by GitHub, and this value will get used only by the GitHub REST APIs. In the similar lines, developers can define their own company values. Usually this media type versioning is majorly used inside the company. The reason is, they're not going to pollute the URLs with V1, V2 by using path variables or by using the query params. As always, the API versioning is going to be done using the headers rather than paths. The REST API URL, it is always going to remain stable and it is never going to change even though more number of versions are getting released on the backend. If you don't like using these media type versioning, you can also use the request header approach as well. In that approach also, we have all these advantages. So now, before I try to get started with the creation of backend REST API, I want to share a standard that you need to follow while creating the packages inside your Spring Boot backend application. Of course, you can create the packages with any name, but in enterprise applications, they're going to follow certain standards. If you learn those standards at this stage, then it is going to make your life easy. Though Spring Boot does not enforce a package structure, but following a clean architecture practices makes our project scalable, readable, and easy to maintain. The first basic rule that you need to take care while creating a Spring Boot backend application is you need to keep your main class at the root package so that Spring Boot can scan all components automatically. For example, if this is your main package, under this package only, we need to have the Spring Boot main class and under this root package, you can create any number of sub packages. While we are trying to create sub packages, there are two popular approaches. The first one is by layer I'll explain to you what do I mean by layer. This is a traditional architecture that developers usually follow. The other approach is by feature or by domain. This is the latest recommendation for large enterprise applications or for microservices. Let's try to understand more details about these approaches. When I say by layer, we are going to organize our code or our packages based upon their technical roles. Long back, we discussed what are the different layers that we are going to have in a typical backend application. We're going to have a controller layer, service layer, and repository layer. Inside the controller layer, we're going to write all the client-facing logic like REST APIs. Whereas inside the service layer, we're going to write all the actual business logic. And under the repository layer, we're going to write all the logic related to database. Similarly, we can create other packages like entity, exception, config. We're going to talk about this entity package when we start discussing about the Spring DataJPA basically, inside this entity package, we're going to place all the POJO classes that represent our database tables. Similarly, all database related classes or interfaces will go under this repository. This approach is a traditional approach. And from last two, three decades, developers, they are following this approach. The advantages of this approach is it is easy to understand, good for small to medium projects, and it is a common and beginner friendly approach. But coming to the disadvantages, with this approach, it is hard to scale for the large domains or for the large enterprise applications. What is a domain? So each of the business department or business entity, we can consider it as a domain. Since enterprise applications, they're going to have a lot of domains in their business. It is going to be hard to scale this package structure because the business logic is spread across multiple layers. So for a small to medium projects, you can happily use this approach. But for the enterprise applications, we need to create the packages by feature or by domain. At the top, we are going to have the root package. Under this, we're going to create sub packages like user, job. If you take our job portal scenario, we're going to have domains or features like user related, job related, company related. One of the major advantages, your project, it is going to have high modularity. That means all the logic related to the user, it is going to stay inside these package only. Similarly, all the job related logic will be present under these package. So this way, you are separating the domain logic across multiple modules or across multiple packages. So with this, it is going to be easy to maintain and scale, easier team collaboration, improves domain driven design. You can have multiple teams working independently across multiple packages. For example, the team that focus on job related logic, they always have to work on these and they don't have to worry about the user package. I hope you're clear with these advantages. Now coming to the disadvantage, this approach requires slightly more initial planning. So you have to think about what are the domains available inside your organization. Accordingly, you need to create them. And once the domain packages are created, again, you need to create the technical sub packages. Regardless of which approach you are trying to use, always you can create additional supporting packages. For example, there'll be a config package where you are going to define all the configurations related to your application in the similar lines common, where we are going to place all reusable helper classes or interfaces. Similarly, security, exception, util. So this way you can create any number of supporting packages while creating the packages please make sure you are following these naming conventions. The entire package name should follow lowercase and avoid plural for domain names and make sure you are following the consistent naming across your entire application. I hope you are clear with these project structure approaches. I'm going to follow this approach throughout this course because it is the standard or the recommended approach for the bigger enterprise applications.

The beauty of DevTools is, when it is trying to deploy our latest code changes, it is not going to restart the application from scratch. That's why we are able to see this efficiency in the startup time when a developer is starting the application manually for the first time. Obviously, all the libraries and their beans, auto-configurations, everything has to be loaded. That's why it has taken 2.3 seconds. But once the server is started successfully and the developer made some changes, this time Spring DevTools, what it is going to do is, it is going to only reload the beans or auto-configurations related to the code changes that we have done. It is not going to touch any of the framework jars or framework related beans because they are already bootstrapped. So behind the scenes, DevTools, it is going to do this kind of optimizations. As a developer, you will never realize that the application is restarted behind the scenes. You can happily keep testing your code changes without restarting the application. So now if I try to invoke this API, I'm getting the Company's List. Let's test the same DevTools scenario one more time. I'm cleaning the console after cleaning the console, I'm going to the Company Controller and here I'm updating this to Updated Companies List and I'll open the console and triggering the build. As soon as the build is triggered, you will see this time also the application is restarted within 0.8 seconds. Now if I click on the send button, I'm going to get the Updated Companies List as an output. I hope you are clear about DevTools. I strongly recommend you adding this dependency into your Spring Boot projects. In real enterprise applications as well, almost all the backend developers, they are going to add this dependency because this dependency improves their productivity.

54616689.title: Introduction to DTO pattern
54616689.section: Spring Data JPA - Databases interaction Without Pain
54616689.transcript: From our back-end REST API, we are trying to send the response to the client application by using the entity class itself. Here, we are trying to send the List of Company records as a response to the client applications. The same we can also confirm by looking at the response under the postman. We receive List of Company objects as a response. Each of these fields inside the JSON object, they represent a database column inside the company's table. But sending these entity objects directly to your client applications is not a good practice. Instead, we should follow DTO pattern. Let me explain what is this pattern and why we should use it. DTO means Data Transfer Object. The DTO pattern is a design pattern that allows us to transfer data between different parts of our application and what are these DTOs? These are the simple objects that contains only data and they will not have any business logic. This makes them ideal for transferring data between different layers of our application such as presentation layer and the data access layer. There are certain advantages or benefits that we are going to get when we transfer the data by using DTO objects. Let's consider a scenario there is a Customer entity class representing customers table inside my database. And this entity class, it is going to have various fields like name, email, mobileNumber, password, and SSN. All these java fields, they represent database columns under the customers database table. If I try to share the customers data by using this Customer entity objects to other layers like controller layer or presentation layer or to the client applications, there will be a problem. We are going to share some details unnecessarily to other layers. For example, this password and SSN, I don't want to share them to other layers or to the client applications because these fields, they are going to hold sensitive information. In this kind of scenario, what we have to do is we have to create a DTO class. Here, I have created a DTO class with the name CustomerDto. Inside this DTO class, we can define the java fields from the entity class, which we are comfortable sharing with the other layers. Here, I have mentioned the fields like name, email, and mobileNumber, but not password or SSN. This is how we are going to restrict ourselves from sharing unnecessary data to other layers or to the client applications. Once the customer data is loaded from the database and the object of this CustomerEntity is created inside the repository layer, what do we have to do ? We have to execute some mapper logic. By using this mapper logic, we are going to transfer the required fields from entity object to DTO object. For example, I can transfer name, email, and mobileNumber. And during the copying of this data, if required, I can write some business logic to transform that data as well. Let's imagine there is a gender column inside the database and inside the database, we are going to store gender value like M and F. But while you are sharing this information to the client applications, you may want to send the gender information as male and female. So this conversion from M to male and F to female, we can write under this mapper logic so that by the time the gender data is copied from DB entity to DTO class, we're going to have these transformed data. So this is one other advantage. In the similar lines, we have many other advantages. Let me show you one significant advantage. Let's imagine you have two different database tables like Customer and Accounts. Inside the customer table, we are going to have all the customer related personal information. Whereas under the accounts, we're going to have the bank account details of the customer like accountNumber, accountType, and branchAddress. This way we're going to have a good amount of tables in real applications as well. Let's imagine there is a scenario where one of the client application, it can be an UI application or it can be an another backend API. They want the information both from these Customer and Accounts table. If you're not following these DTO pattern, you can send either customer or accounts data as an output from your REST API. You can't send two different objects as an output. And with that, what is going to happen? The client applications, they have to make two different REST API calls, one to fetch the customer information and the other one to fetch the accounts information. And this is going to introduce unnecessary traffic into your web application. To handle these kinds of scenarios also, we can use DTO pattern. I'm going to create a DTO class with the name like CustomerDetailsDto. Inside these DTO class, I can define all the Java fields from both these entities. So I want to send name, email, mobileNumber. Similarly, I want to send these three fields as well. Any field which I don't want to send from these entities, I can ignore it. And I'm not going to maintain the corresponding Java field inside the DTO. So once I have the DTO class, I need to write the mapper logic. This mapper logic will be executed once the customer and accounts details are loaded from the database. Inside this mapper logic, I'm going to write a logic to read the required fields from the customer and populate into these three respective fields. In the similar lines, I'm also going to read the required information from the accounts object and copy them into the DTO class. And once the DTO class is ready, we are going to send the response by using the object of this class. This way, the client applications, they're going to receive the combined information with a single REST API call. I hope this is clear. I also highlighted some of the benefits of using the DTO pattern. The first one is around reducing the network traffic. Just now we discussed this scenario. When you are using the DTO pattern, you can batch up multiple pieces of data into a single object, which can reduce the number of network requests that need to be made by the client applications. The next advantage is around the serialization. Within these DTO class or within this mapper logic, we can control on how our objects will be transferred over the network. What is serialization? Serialization is nothing but sending the data inside the Java objects to the client applications by using formats like XML, JSON, and so on. Let's imagine today you are trying to send the information to the client applications by using the JSON format. In the future, if you want to use some other format instead of JSON, then the changes will be only inside the mapper logic and inside these DTO class. There won't be any other changes inside other layers like controller layer or inside the repository layer. This way, you can always control on how the data needs to be transformed to the client applications by using these DTO pattern. And the last advantage is around the decoupling of the layers. When we are using the DTOs, the different layers that we are going to have inside our application, like presentation layer, data access layer, they're going to be decoupled from each other. Even if there is a change is happening inside these entity classes, it is not going to affect the presentation layer or the controller layer, because for the other layers, we're going to transfer the data by using the DTO class, but not using the entity objects. I hope you're clear on what is this DTO pattern. In the next lecture, we can try to implement this pattern inside our backend application as well. Inside the industry, few developers, they are going to use other names as well, instead of DTO. Few projects are going to call these DTO objects as VO objects. VO means value objects. And similarly, few people, they call it as TO. TO means transfer objects. So regardless of what name that developers and projects are using, they are going to solve the problems that we have discussed so far inside this lecture.

54616715.title: CORS - Browser’s Overprotective Parent
54616715.section: Spring Data JPA - Databases interaction Without Pain
54616715.transcript: Inside this lecture, let's try to understand what is CORS. CORS means Cross Origin Resource Sharing. What is the meaning of origin? Origin is nothing but your domain URL or application URL. When two different applications which are having two different origins. If they are trying to share the resources with each other, then cars come into picture. That's why this name has been given. Cross origin means two different origins. They are trying to share the resources. For example, here I have given a scenario when a CORS issue may arise. A front-end application might be running at this port. Just like our application, the ReactUI application, it is running at this given hostname and port number. And a back-end API might be running at this given port number. So if you see, these two are different origins because the port numbers are different. In these kind of scenarios, the browser, it is going to stop the traffic between both of them due to this CORS policy. Why browser is doing? We know inside the web, the browsers are going to be used to a great extent by every human on this earth. Due to this, it is the responsibility of the browsers to stop any bad actors or hackers who are trying to hack the sessions of the end-users. So that's why whenever browser sees that a communication is trying to establish between two different applications, it is going to stop that communication by default. For example, think like I'm trying to open netflix.com and this is the origin name or domain name for the Netflix application. But behind the scenes, if this Netflix application, if it is trying to communicate with some other application, which is having some domain name like example.com, then this is an indication to the browser that two different applications are trying to communicate with each other. And I should stop that communication by default because this example.com might belong to a hacker who are trying to trick the end-user to steal the session from the netflix.com. We saw this behavior in action as well in the previous lecture, and we saw this kind of message. Most of the people, they think a CORS is a security issue or security attack, but this is not a security attack. Instead, it is that default protection provided by the browsers to stop sharing the data or communication between two different origins. How the browser is going to identify if the origins are different, it is going to consider all these parameters. We know each of the domain URL. It is going to have various parameters like what is the HTTP protocol, whether it is HTTP or HTTPS, what is that domain name? So this domain name is nothing but the host name and similarly a port number. So usually a combination of all these will be the origin. If any of these parameter is different, then browser is going to assume two different origins they are trying to communicate with each other. So here I have an example, a UI application, which is hosted at a domain, domain1.com it is trying to communicate with the backend API, which is deployed at this domain. Here, the protocol is same, but these domain1 and domain2, they are two different domains. And with that, it is a clear indication to the browser that two different origins are trying to communicate with each other. And it is going to stop the communication. In our local scenario here, if you see the HTTP protocol is same, the host name is same, but the port numbers are different. That's why we saw in the previous lecture that the browser is trying to stop the communication between these two applications. So this is the history about cors. But there might be some very valid scenarios where two different applications might want to communicate with each other. For example, a Netflix website may want to communicate with a PayPal website during the payment by the end user. The same applies for any organization as well. I might deploy my UI application at a domain like example.com. Whereas all my backend REST APIs, they might have deployed at some other domain name like example1.com. And I want to allow the traffic between these two origins. Here, I have a very valid scenario. In these kinds of scenarios, we should be able to communicate to the browser from the backend saying that if the request is coming from so-and-so UI application, please allow the traffic. How to communicate that to the browser? Let's try to understand the same. There are multiple options available inside the Spring Boot to fix the CORS policy. The first one is you can use the @CrossOrigin annotation on top of your RestController class or on top of the REST API method itself. Here on top of the REST API method, I have mentioned the @CrossOrigin annotation and using this annotation, I invoked a property origins and to these origins, I have mentioned the origin details of the UI application. With this, what is going to happen is whenever the browser sees the two different origins, they are trying to communicate with each other. The browser, it is going to first send a preflight request to the backend server. So preflight request is a request that is going to be triggered by the browser before the actual request. So what is the actual request that we are trying to send? The UI is trying to invoke the REST API, which is hosted at this path. But before invoking these REST API, the browser, since it detects cross-origins, they are trying to communicate with each other. It is going to send a preflight request to the backend. As a response to this preflight request, the backend should send some confirmation to the browser to allow the traffic from so-and-so domain name. When we are using this annotation, the Spring Boot is going to take care of everything in the backend. It is going to prepare a response to the preflight request that is coming from the browser. And as part of this preflight response, it is going to send all the information that is required to establish the communication between our UI application and the backend application. But the drawback with this approach is if you are mentioning this annotation on top of a REST API method, then these configurations, it is only going to work for this REST API method. In future, if you are trying to add one more REST API method for that, again, you have to define this annotation. If you want to make these configurations applicable for all the REST API methods inside your controller, you can keep the same annotation on top of your controller class. And this is how we are going to enable the cors communication between two different origins. So it is the backend that should always decide from whom that it wants to accept the traffic. Because inside the backend only, we are going to store all the sensitive data. The UI application can only send the request to the backend APIs, but it is the browser and the backend application they are going to decide whether to allow the traffic between the UI and the backend APIs. This option is very simple to use, but there is a drawback with this option. In real applications, we are going to have a good amount of controller classes. It will be a very cumbersome process to mention this annotation on top of all those classes. And in future, if you want to update these origin value to something else, then you need to visit all the controller classes and the REST API methods to update these values. Instead of defining these configurations on top of the controller class or on top of the REST API methods, we should be able to configure the CORS configurations globally for our entire backend application. I'll show you the other options that we have for global CORS configurations. But before that, let me highlight you here instead of mentioning these value, if you mention asterisk, then your backend REST API, it is going to accept the traffic from all kinds of UI applications. Please don't try to use this asterisk symbol for production applications. It is like attracting all the hackers inside the world into your backend application. In case if you want to mention multiple origins, you should be able to do that as well. After these origins, you just have to mention curly braces and within these curly braces, you can define multiple origins and all these origins, you can separate them by using comma. Let me show you the second option. This option is by using a Filter. You have to create a config file inside your Spring Boot application and this file name can be anything. On top of this file, you need to mention @Configuration. Inside this config file, you need to create a bean of type CorsFilter. So here I have created a Java method on top of this method, I have mentioned @Bean annotation. By the time this method executes, it is going to return the object of CorsFilter. The same will be converted as a bean by the Spring framework. But before you try to return the object of CorsFilter, you need to convey all your CORS related requirements by using the object of CorsConfiguration. So once you create the object of this CorsConfiguration, you can invoke various methods like setAllowedOrigins(). Using this method, you are going to define what are the list of origins from where you want to accept the traffic. So here I have defined only one origin detail. But in case if you want to accept from multiple origins, you can define all of them by using comma as a separator or you can also mention asterisk, but mentioning asterisk is not recommended. Just like AllowedOrigins, we can also invoke the setAllowed() methods. Using these allowed methods, we can define for which kind of HTTP methods we want to accept the traffic. Whether we want to accept the traffic only for the GET methods or POST methods. In case if you want to accept for all kind of HTTP methods, you would need to mention asterisk symbol. But before mentioning asterisk symbol, you need to make sure you are invoking these Collections.singletonList(). Using this config, we should be able to define what request headers we want to accept from the UI application. So here I have mentioned I want to accept only a request header with the name content-type. All the remaining headers that my UI application is trying to send, those will be filtered by the browser. So the backend will never receive any other headers other than this header. And similarly, we can also invoke one more method with the name setExposedHeaders(). Using the exposed headers, we should be able to define what headers that we want to expose from backend to UI application. We also have a method with the name setToAllowCredentials(). To this, we can set a value as true. With this configuration, we are telling to the browser to send all the credentials as well to the backend application. When I say credentials, all the cookies that are created by the UI application or any authentication related tokens, all of them will be passed on to the backend application. This way there are multiple methods available inside these configurations. By using all these methods, we should be able to control what kind of traffic that we want to accept. Once this Configuration object is created, the same we need to pass as an input to the method of registerCorsConfiguration(). So where this method is available under these UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource. So first I have created the object of this class. Using this object, we need to invoke registerCourseConfiguration(). And here I have mentioned the URL pattern as forward slash followed by two asterisk symbols. With this syntax, I'm telling to the framework, please apply these course configuration for all the URLs or the REST API paths available inside my application. And at last, we need to pass the source as an input to the constructor of CorsFilter. Once we return the object of this CorsFilter, the spring boot framework is going to take care of applying these CorsConfigurations globally for your enter backend application. Next, we have an option to configure the CorsConfigurations by using spring security. Spring security is a project inside the spring ecosystem using which we can secure our spring boot applications. We are going to discuss about spring security in the coming sections. Using the same, we are going to enforce authentication and authorization. During the same discussion, I'm going to explain about this option on how to configure cors related configurations by using spring security. The last option that we have is by using web MVC configurer. We already implemented this interface inside our project as part of implementing the API versioning strategy. Inside the same class, this time, you need to override a method with the name addCourseMapping(). And this method, it is going to provide a corsRegistry() as an input. Using this registry, we need to invoke addMapping(). And to this mapping, we can provide the URL pattern or the API path patterns for which these cors configuration has to be applied. Here, I have mentioned the mapping as forward slash API slash two asterisk symbols and with this, I'm telling for all the rest APIs, which are having a prefix as API, for all of them, apply these cors configuration. After the addMapping(), we can invoke allowedOrigins(), allowedMethods(), allowedHeaders(), exposedHeaders(), allowedCredentials() methods to provide our cors related configurations. So here I have mentioned a single domain name. Similarly, you can define any number of domain names. The same applies for HTTP methods, allowedHeaders() and exposedHeaders(). We can also invoke a maxAge() method. To this method, we can pass a numeric value. Here I'm passing 3600. This 3600 means the framework, it is going to consider it as 3600 seconds. That means 60 minutes. So for one hour, the browser, it is going to cache these configurations and it is not going to make any preflight requests. If a client application is invoking the same backend REST API. After the one hour, the browser, it is going to make a preflight request to fetch these configurations again. By using these max age, we are going to have some performance improvements. Instead of browser asking for each and every request, it is a good option to cache these cors configurations on the browser side for a limited period of time, like for one hour or for one day, based upon the business requirements. These are the options that we have. We're going to implement one of them inside the next lecture. When we make these cors configurations inside our Spring Boot application, by using any of these options, what is going to happen behind the scenes is the Spring Boot framework. So by looking at these response headers, the browser will decide whether it has to allow the traffic or not. At last, I highlighted when not to use the cors configurations. If the communication is happening by using a same domain name, then there is no cors is required because they both belongs to the same origin. So there is a lifecycle management property. To this property, there are three different values that we can pass. The default one is start and stop. With the default value what is going to happen is, whenever you are starting your application, the containers they are going to be started. And when you stopping the application, the containers will be stopped. But in case, if you don't want the spring boot application to not start or stop the containers, you can set the value none, or if you are looking only to start the containers, but not stop the containers during the stopping of the application, then you can go with the start only. Inside the docker ecosystem, just like how we have docker run command, we also have some docker compose command like docker compose up, docker compose down and docker compose start and docker compose stop. There is a good difference between these four docker compose commands. When you are using docker compose up behind the scenes, based upon the docker compose file that you have provided, if required, the framework is going to prepare the docker images, based upon the docker images, it is going to create the docker containers from scratch. Whereas with the docker compose start command, only the existing stopped containers will be stopped. So that's the difference between up and start coming to the down and stop. When we are using the down command, all the containers, they're going to be deleted when we are running the down command. Whereas with the stop command, the containers, they are not going to be deleted, instead, they will simply stop. So by default for this property, up is the default value. 54665003.title: How Hibernate Creates Your Tables - Understanding ddl-auto
54665003.section: Building the Contact Us Feature - Entities, APIs, and JPA Magic
54665003.transcript: The Spring Data JPA project, it supports a property with the name spring.jpa.hybernate.ddl-auto. By mentioning this property inside the application.properties file, we should be able to control on how the database schema is being managed by Hibernate. We know Spring Data JPA framework, it just follows JPA specifications or standards. But behind the scenes, this framework or this project, it is going to use one of the implementation framework of the JPA specifications. And by default, Spring Data JPA uses Hibernate, which is an ORM framework as the implementation framework behind the scenes. By using this property and assigning a value to this property, we are going to tell to Hibernate on how it has to auto-manage the database schema like tables, columns, constraints, every time our application is starting. Depending on the value that we are providing to this property, the Hibernate can create or update or validate or delete our database schema. So this property, it is going to work in various modes like builder mode, cleaner mode, upgrade mode, inspector mode, and hands-off mode. These are my own definitions. Based upon the value that we are trying to provide to this property, the Hibernate, it is going to enter into any of these mode. As part of the builder mode, the Hibernate is going to create the required tables and database schema. Cleaner mode means it is going to perform the delete operation. Next as part of upgrade mode, the Hibernate, it is going to perform the update of the database tables and schema. Next as part of the inspector mode, the Hibernate framework, it is going to perform the validations during the startup of our application. It is going to validate that our database schema and the entity classes are matching with each other. Otherwise it is going to throw an error. At last we have hands-off mode. As part of this hands-off mode, the Hibernate is not going to do anything. That means we can completely disable the management of the database schema by the Hibernate. In that case, the developers, they are going to take care of database schema and entities manually. So let's try to understand what are the various values that we can provide to this property. The first value that we can assign is create. When we are mentioning these create value during the startup of our application, every time the Hibernate, it is going to drop the existing tables and create the brand new ones. That means all your data, tables, everything, they are going to be created. It's like breaking the old house and building it fresh every time. You can use this value in the scenarios where you are doing early development, testing a new schema. But please note that when you are using this value, all your existing data is going to be lost whenever you restart your application, because the tables are going to be recreated. The next value that these property supports is create-drop. This is very similar to the above one, but when we are using these, apart from creating the tables on startup, the Hibernate, it is also going to drop them when we are shutting down the application. It's like setting up a tent for the day and removing it when our session ends. You need to use these value, especially around the use cases of unit testing and in-memory databases. The third valid value that we can provide is create-only. So when we are mentioning this value, the tables will be created only once. They will never be dropped. It is like build it once and walk away, never tear anything down. There is a clear difference between this create and create-only. When you are using this create, the database tables, they are going to be recreated every time you restart. Whereas with this value, only the first time the database tables will be created. And after that, if you try to restart your application multiple times, then the tables are not going to be recreated because they already created during the very first startup. You can use this value under the use cases of initial migrations. Next we have drop-value. When we are mentioning this value, the Hibernate, it is only going to drop all the tables. It is never going to recreate them. It will be the responsibility of the developer to create the database tables manually. But the dropping of the tables will be taken care by the Hibernate. It is like leveling up the entire building, but not constructing anything new. So the use cases for this will be very rare, mostly used as part of the cleanup by using tools or scripts. Next we have none. When we are using this none, the Hibernate is not going to do anything. The database admin or the developer will be the boss. They have to create the schema they have to manage it and drop it based upon their requirements. And this is the value that is recommended for production environment. If you are building a production ready enterprise application, we typically don't want to give the responsibility of managing the database schema to the Hibernate framework. Typically inside the enterprise applications, what is going to happen is there'll be a separate DBA team who always manages the database schema. Even the developers also, they will not have privilege to create the tables or columns or updating the schema as well. Any change that a developer requires, they have to formally submit a request to the database team after doing enough brainstorming and after receiving enough approvals, the database team, they are going to execute the SQL scripts manually. So once the tables are created, the developers can create the entity classes and repository interfaces. That's what we have followed in the previous section, but I'm trying to discuss this scenario as well so that you can leverage this option, especially during the unit testing scenario or when you are building a low priority application or some demo applications. After the none, we have truncate. When we are using these truncate value, it is going to delete all the rows, but keeps the table structure. That means every time you restart your application, you're going to get empty tables. You need to use this value under the use cases where you want to reset the test data without dropping the tables. Next we have update value. Whenever we are using this value, what the Hibernate framework it is going to do is, it is going to verify what configurations you have defined in your entity class. Accordingly, it will try to match that database schema as well. If there are some mismatches or if you're not following some standards, the framework is going to take care of updating the schema behind the scenes. For example, inside the entity class, you have created a field representing the age column, but this age column is missing inside the corresponding database table. So in this scenario, what is going to happen is during the startup of my application, since the framework, it is going to identify a mismatch between the entity and the database table. It is going to update the existing table to add the new columns. And even if there is a scenario where the entire table is missing, in such scenarios, a brand new table also will be created by the framework. You need to use this value only during the development stages. Like I said, during the production stages, please make sure you are using the none value. This update value is typically not safe for production because it can break schema or cause silent issues behind the scenes. At last, we have validate value. This validate value is like a read-only mode of update. With the update, what is going to happen it is going to identify the issues and automatically update that database schema to fix the mismatches. Whereas with validate, the Hibernate framework, it is going to throw the errors during the startup along with the validation error details so that the developer can decide to update the entity class or the database schema. So when we are using this validate, no changes will be applied onto the database schema. Only validations report will be published during the startup. If there are some validation errors identified, the startup of the application also will fail. This is like someone visiting our house to identify the issues, but they can't touch anything or repair any issue. Whereas the above one, it is like fixing all the small things in our house as needed. First, they are going to do inspection followed by applying the fixes as well. We can use this validate for staging environment and production environment as well. Inside the next slide, I highlighted what are the recommended usages based upon the environment. Inside the local development environment, you can use either update, create, create to drop. During the testing environment, you can use create to drop or validate, whereas inside the production, please make sure you are using either none or validate. By default, when you are using embedded database like H2 database, this property, it is going to have a default value of create-drop. That's why when we are using the H2 database with the internal memory, the tables are created during the startup and they are deleted during the shutdown. Whereas in the scenarios where you are using a normal production database like MySQL, then this property, it is going to default to none value because none is recommended for production applications. That's why to be on safe side, the framework team, they defaulted to none for typical production databases like MySQL, Oracle or Postgres. I hope you are clear on the purpose of these property. As part of the next lecture, let's try to see the magic of this property.

54665107.transcript: We, as humans, we always look for opportunities to improve ourselves. The first one with the name Company followed by Contact. If you closely look at the code that we have written in both these entity classes, there are four audit related columns or fields that we repeated inside both these entity classes. What are these fields? These are createdAt, createdBy, updatedAt, and updatedBy. We know the purpose of these fields. By using these fields, we are going to perform the auditing of the data, like who created the data or who updated the data, because in real enterprise applications, the production data will be a valuable or secure data. Anyone who are updating to these data, we need to track them. Otherwise there won't be any accountability and it will result into the business damage. But the problem with these approach that we are trying to follow so far is, we are repeating these four fields related code in both the entity classes. By the end of this course, we are going to create 20 more entity classes. And in real enterprise applications, you will have hundreds of entity classes representing all your database tables. Do you think it is a good practice to repeat all these four fields in all the entity classes? Of course not. There has to be a better approach. By taking the help of Spring Data JPA framework, we are going to move these four fields to a parent class. On top of that, we're also going to give the responsibility of populating these audit related columns to the framework itself. As of now, we are manually populating the data into the createdAt and createdBy columns. For example, if you go to the ContactServiceImpl, while I'm inserting a new record into the contacts table, we are populating the createdAt and createdBy manually. But this is again, is a not recommended approach because the framework should be able to know about what is the current time apart from that date and time. We can also give certain clues to the framework about who is the current logged in user and the same current logged in username will be leveraged by the framework to populate the data into the createdBy column. Off course, as of now, we have not implemented security inside our application, but in the coming sections, we are going to implement security by using the Spring Security Framework. And at that point of time, we are going to update our application code to consider the logged in username to populate under these createdBy and updatedBy. So far, we have scenario only to populate createdAt and createdBy, but in the coming sections, we are also going to have some scenarios where we are going to update the existing data under such scenarios, we need to populate the data under the updatedAt and updatedBy columns. Enough of talking Let's see the demo what I'll do is under the entity package, I'll try to create a Java class with the name BaseEntity. This name can be anything. Inside this class, I'm going to mention those four fields. Let me go to Company and here I have four fields. I'm going to cut the code from here and paste under the base entity followed by now I'll go to Contact and here I'm going to delete the code related to createdAt and createdBy. However, these mappings are interpreted in the context of each entity class, which inherits the mapped superclass. I know this is super complex to understand. Let me explain you in simple language. Whenever we are using this annotation, we're telling to the framework, please don't read these BaseEntity as an entity class. On top of that, we're also telling whatever class which is extending these BaseEntity for that child entity class, please consider these Java fields. For example, if I go to the Company entity class and here, if I extend the BaseEntity class by typing extends BaseEntity, now, whatever fields that I have defined under the BaseEntity, they will be mapped to the Company's entity. And in fact, in real enterprise applications, this is a standard that developers will follow the database administrators. Now let's try to implement the third step. In order to implement the third step, I'm going to create a new Java package with the name audit. Since I'm looking to define all the auditing related logic in a separate package, I have created this sub package. On top of this class, we need to mention @Component annotation because we need to create a bean of this class. Now, I'm going to give a name to the bean. The name will be AuditAwareIMPL itself. I will keep the first letter as small case letter. The third step is completed. We can move on to the last step. But before that, here I want to clarify a question that you may have. You may ask me, Madan, we are providing only the current auditor details, but not the current date and time details. The answer is very simple. The Spring Data JPA is capable of identifying the current date and time. We are done with all our four steps. Let me do the build. Once the build is completed, I'll restart my application just to make sure to reflect all my code changes properly. From now onwards, we don't have to provide the audit related data from our business logic. I can go to the ContactServiceImpl and here I will comment both these two lines of code. This time, if I invoke the same REST API, the created date and created by will be taken care by the framework. Let me click on this send button as you can see, I got an error seems the code that we have written, it's not working the createdAt is still is going as null value. Let me check what mistake I have done. Under the BaseEntity class, seems I forgot to mention some annotations. On top of the Java fields, we need to mention such annotations. Because by looking at the simple column related configurations, my Spring Data JPA cannot easily understand which field has to be considered for updating the createdAt time and similarly for createdBy, updatedAt and updatedBy. To give that clue to the framework, we have to mention @CreatedDate followed by @CreatedTimestamp. By mentioning both these annotations, we are telling to the framework, please consider the Java field to populate the current date and timestamp while someone is trying to create a new record inside the database. At last, on top of this updated by field, I need to mention an annotation @LastModifiedBy. But there is a small problem with this configuration. By default, the Spring Data JPA framework, it will try to populate all these four fields during both insert and update operation. 54665121.title: Document Your APIs Like a Pro with SpringDoc OpenAPI & Swagger
54665121.section: Essential Backend Skills - Exceptions, Validation, Auditing & Bean Scopes
54665121.transcript: Inside real enterprise applications, developers, they will end up developing hundreds of REST APIs. And these REST APIs can be invoked by various kinds of people, like third-party API developers or UI app developers or mobile application developers or QA team members who are trying to test these REST APIs. Obviously, all these developers and QA team members, they will not have any clue about your REST API. They may have too many questions like what is the request that I'm supposed to send? What is the response that I'm going to get? What kind of validations that a REST API has? What are the various response status codes that a REST API can send? This way, they may have hundreds of questions around the REST APIs. To solve the doubts or queries of all these people, you may follow a very basic approach. For example, you may organize a meeting and to this meeting, you can invite all these developers and testers where you're going to explain about the REST APIs. The other approach is you can try to document about the REST APIs and the documents can be shared to the other developers or QA team members. While these basic approaches, they are going to help you to avoid questions from the other people, but they are not going to help you in 100% of the scenarios. Many times new people will be onboarded onto the team and a such scenario, you can't schedule a new meeting for all the new members that are joining. Similarly, sometimes you may update your existing REST APIs with the new enhancements. Whenever you're making enhancements to your REST APIs, you can't go and update these documentation every time this is going to be a cumbersome process and some way to make these approaches to work without any issues, you will end up doing a lot of coordination with other team members. Most of the times you will end up answering the questions from the other team members. And with that, you're going to have a feeling like I'm exhausted and I can't take any more questions. At the end of the day, the job of the developer is to focus on the business logic, but not to entertain all this kind of drama. That's why I'm going to teach you a better alternative approach on how to document our REST APIs by adding a simple starter project into the Spring Boot, so that anytime anyone has a question around your REST API, you can simply redirect them to a URL where they can open and understand more details about the REST API. Enough of talking let me show you a demo. There is a website with the name springdoc.org If you scroll down on this website, they are recommending to add a dependency to the Spring Boot application. This is the dependency when we add this dependency behind the scenes, what it is going to do is, it is going to analyze all the REST APIs that you have defined in your backend application. Based upon this analysis, it is going to generate OpenAPI specification. There is a standard called OpenAPI. By using this standard only, all the REST APIs has to be explained in a standard or a consistent approach. If you go to the website with the name Swagger.io, this Swagger team, they have developed a lot many tools around the OpenAPI specification. I'll come to this website at a later point of time. But right now, let's try to understand on how to generate the OpenAPI specifications for our REST API. As a very first step, we need to add this dependency. Inside the pom.xml, I'm going to add the dependency with the name springdoc-openapi-starter-application. Let me save this and sync the maven changes. After adding this dependency, I just have to do the build and restart my application. Inside the console logs, you can search for Swagger and you will be able to see there is a statement saying that swagger-ui.html endpoint is enabled by default. To disable it in production, we need to set a value false to this property. So let's try to access this HTML page. I will type localhost 8080 forward slash swagger-ui.html. 54665127.title: Introduction to Request, Session, Application & Websocket Bean scopes
54665127.section: Essential Backend Skills - Exceptions, Validation, Auditing & Bean Scopes
54665127.transcript: Long back, while we are discussing the core concepts of the Spring Framework, we discussed about Singleton Bean Scope and Prototype Bean Scope. Apart from these two scopes, we also have four other scopes, which are applicable for web applications. So far, we made a good progress inside this course. And as of now, we also know on how to build a web application by using the Spring Boot Framework. This is the right time to discuss about the remaining scopes as well. So whatever scopes that we are going to discuss for the next few lectures, they are applicable only for the web applications. In case if you are developing a non-web application or a CLI application, these scopes they are not applicable, whereas the Singleton and Prototype scopes, they are applicable for all kinds of applications. Even inside a web-based application, we should be able to define the beans with the Singleton and Prototype scopes. As part of this lecture, I'm going to give a quick introduction about all these four scopes followed by from the next lecture, I will show you a demo as well. We know on how the web applications are going to work, the client applications are going to send the request by using the HTTP protocol. So whatever request that we are getting by using the HTTP protocol from the client applications, in other words, we can also call it as HTTP request. When we want to create a bean by using the @Request Scope, we need to use this annotation and whenever we are using this annotation, the bean will be created for each and every HTTP request that is being processed by an web application. Just like how a prototype scope bean is going to be created whenever someone is trying to refer that bean, in the similar styles, whenever someone is sending requests to the web applications by using the HTTP request, in all such scenarios, a new bean will be created. Whatever bean that get created, it is only valid for the duration of a HTTP request. We need to use these scope when we want to store some request specific data, like Storing temporary user inputs like form data. So this is the syntax that we need to follow to create a bean of request bean scope. If multiple users sends multiple requests, each of them, they're going to get a separate request bean or separate instance of the class. As soon as the request is complete, the bean is going to be destroyed. Next we have session bean scope. Just like how we have HTTP request, we also have a concept called HTTP session. When a user started interacting with a web application from a browser, a new HTTP session will be created and the same session will be maintained until the browser is closed or the session is expired. When we create a bean with the session scope, a new bean instance will be created for each user HTTP session. We need to use these bean scope when we want to store user specific data across multiple requests in a single session. For example, if I want to store some logged in user details or shopping cart information, which needs to be survived across multiple HTTP requests, then this scope is a good consideration. This is the syntax that we need to follow to create a bean with this scope. As I said before, whenever the end user opened a browser and trying to access a web application, at that specific point of time, a session scope to bean is going to be created. The same bean instance will be used across multiple requests from the same user. As soon as the session expires, the bean is going to be destroyed. Next we have Application Bean Scope. When we are defining a bean with this scope, a single bean instance is going to be shared across the entire application. If the application is being used by millions of end users, they may have millions of sessions and millions of HTTP requests sending to the backend for all the sessions and for all the requests and for the entire application. If you want to have a single bean, then you need to define the bean with the application scope. This scope needs to be used when we want to store global data shared across all users and all requests. For example, I can store some global statistics data, like number of visitors that are visiting my website. This is the syntax that we need to follow when we are using this scope, only one instance of the bean is going to be created across entire application. The bean is going to be destroyed only when the application stops. Next we have Web Socket Bean Scope. You will see this scope is being used very rarely by the developers because this scope is limited to only web socket scenario. So what is a web socket? Web socket is a continuous connection between the client and the server. Unlike HTTP, which works using request response, web socket stays open and allows real-time two-way communication. Whenever you are chatting with WhatsApp or Facebook Messenger or any other chat applications, in those scenarios, the web socket protocol is going to be used. Because using HTTP there is going to be a costly process. If you want to create a HTTP request for each and every message that someone is trying to send, it is not going to be a scalable solution. That's why under the scenarios of live chat, stock price updates, notifications, multiple player games, in all these scenarios, web socket protocol is used extensively. Whenever you are building business logic for these kinds of scenarios, you can mark the bean with this scope by using this annotation. With this, what is going to happen? A bean will be created for each web socket connection and destroyed whenever the connection closes. To take an example of chatting application itself, each web socket client who initiated the chat, they're going to get their own bean or object. These beans, they don't interfere with other chats that are happening. From the next lecture, I'm going to show you the demo of request session and application scope. I'm not going to show you the demo of this web socket scope because building web socket applications is out of the scope of this course. But anytime in the future, if you end up developing web socket based applications, please make sure to use this bean to make your life easy.

If I click on this send button, you will be able to see I'm getting the 6 as an output. Inside the console also, you will see this statement only once. I hope you are clear with the ApplicationScope. And here you may have a very important question ? which is Madan, this application scope looks very similar to the singleton scope. Then when we create a bean with the singleton scope, the same bean will be shared with the entire application. Then what's the difference between these application scope and session scope? Let me try to answer the same. The main difference between the application scope and singleton scope is, who create and maintains a bean. . We know Application Scope we can only use when we are developing the web application. When we mark a bean with this annotation, the bean instance will be created and managed by the Servlet context. We know all our web applications, they're going to process the request and response by using the servlets behind the scenes. That's why we deploy our web applications into the servlet containers like Tomcat. Inside that servlet container, there is a concept called servlet context. So this servlet context will create and maintain the ApplicationScopeBean. Typically, you're going to have one instance of ApplicationScope bean for your entire Spring web application.
