So in these kinds of scenarios, each web application, they're going to have their own separate servlet context under the servlet container. Let's imagine under a servlet container or under a Tomcat server, there are two applications deployed like application1 and application2. So both these applications are going to have their own servlet context. In this kind of scenario, if both these application1 and application2 has the same code, the servlet context will create a separate ApplicationScopedBean for both these servlet context. But usually inside a web application, we are going to deploy only a single web application into a single servlet container. That's why most of the times we are going to have only one instance of Applicationscope bean inside the servlet container. Whereas coming to the singleton scope beans, they are going to be created and maintained by the Spring container. When we are discussing about the basics of Spring framework, we created the object of Spring container by using a class called AnnotationConfig Application context. So whenever we are creating a bean of this class, a Spring container will be created behind the scenes. If you are trying to create multiple spring containers inside a spring application, then multiple instances of the singleton bin will be created. But 99% of the times developers, they're going to have only a single Spring container inside a Spring application. That's why most of the times we will see one singleton instance bean available throughout the application. . I hope you understand the primary difference between these scope beans. In the scenario of application scope, the soullet context will create and manage the bean, whereas in the scenario of singleton, the spring container will create and manage the bean. So this application scope, it is going to work only inside of web applications like Spring MVC applications or Spring Boot based web application, whereas singleton scope, it is going to work in all type of applications. It can be a Web CLI or Batch applications as well. To configure these scope, we need to use this annotation on top of the Java class, whereas to use the singleton scope, we don't have to mention any annotation because that is the default scope inside the spring ecosystem. The bean that is created with the application scope, it is going to be shared across the entire application for all users and HTTP requests and HTTP sessions, whereas the bean of singleton will be shared across the entire Spring container. The application scope bean will be destroyed when the application stops, whereas the singleton bean, it is going to be destroyed as soon as the Spring container shutdown. The use cases to use the application scope is, to store global application state like visitor count, application wide configurations and so on, whereas for the singleton, the use cases will be, if you want to have a bean which can be accessed throughout your application, you can mark such classes as singleton. For example, if you want to create a service class or repository class or component class, which can be accessed throughout your application, for all these scenarios, we can go with this singleton default scope, and with that, the same bean instance will be leveraged across our entire business logic. For the 99% of the scenarios, you should be fine with this singleton scope. Don't create this application scope unless you have a scenario to store the global data and share across the request and sessions.

54666259.title: What You’ll Learn - JPA Relationships Roadmap
54666259.section: Mastering JPA Relationships - From Basics to Real-World Mapping
54666259.transcript: Inside our Job Portal application, as of now we are displaying only the company's details. But by the end of this section, I also want to display the job details as well inside this portal. Let me show you a demo on what I'm trying to achieve inside this section. Inside the job portal application, apart from company's details, we're also going to display the job details as well. So there is a job section, and here, by default I'm going to display only the 6 jobs, but in total, I'm going to load around 1000 jobs. So here there are 6 jobs, but we should be able to see 994 remaining jobs as well. If I click on this load more jobs, it is going to show me a few more jobs, and I can keep click on this to see the more number of jobs. If the end user click on any of these jobs, for example, on this job, it is going to take the user to the job detail page and here if you see this job is attached to a company called Tesla. So there are some details of location, full time or contract. What is the pay for this job, job description, what are the requirements, job overview, and a small description about the company which posted this job. So these are the benefits and perks. We're also going to have buttons like login to apply or login to save. We're going to focus on security in the coming sections. For now, what I'm trying to say here is, each of the job should be tagged to a company. Let me go to the homepage, and here if you see, if I click on any of the company like Google here, there'll be details around the Google company, like when it is founded, what is the size of the company, what are the total number of employees working in it, what are the number of open positions. So here there is also a link to view all jobs. As soon as someone click on this button, this should display all the jobs that are posted by the Google recruitment team. So there are 39 jobs posted. This is what we are going to achieve by the end of this section. Off course, we're not going to make any UI changes we're only going to make the required backend changes because this is a backend focused course. So to achieve all these functionality, as of now, we have a table with the name companies, but we don't have any table that is capable of storing job details. We are going to create the jobs table in the coming lectures. But here you need to be aware about one important advanced topic. Since that data between companies and jobs, they are related to each other, we can't create an independent jobs table. So whatever jobs table that we are going to create, it should have a relationship with the company's table or it should have a foreign key reference to the company's table. Let me share more details around this. Let's imagine there is a company with the name Amazon and its details we are going to store inside the company's table. So there'll be columns like name of the company, when the company founded, what are the locations where the company is working ? Apart from all those columns, we are also going to have a ID column in the company's table. Let's imagine for this Amazon company, the ID value is 1. If you want to store the job details that are associated to this Amazon, this is how we need to maintain the jobs table. So the jobs table, it is going to have its own primary key ID column each job that recruiters are going to post, it is going to have an unique number like 101, 102, 103 and 104 and so on. Against each job, the recruitment team, they are going to provide various details like what is the title of the job? What is the location of the job? What is the type of the job? What is the pay structure? We are going to have many other columns that are going to hold the job related information. But apart from these main columns, we should also have a column with the name company_id and this company_id column should have a foreign key link with the company's table. So here, when I'm storing the value like 1 in all these job related rows, this is an indication that all these jobs are related to the company with the ID 1. So what is that company? It is Amazon. So if you see here, these are not simple independent tables. They are related to each other. In this kind of advanced scenarios, usually if we are not using Spring Data JPA framework, how developers, they are going to populate the information is, they are going to write the Join Queries. For example, if there is a scenario where I want to fetch all the jobs that are posted by Amazon, then I need to write an SQL query that joins both these tables so that I can fetch the expected results. But writing and maintaining all these complex join queries is going to be a cumbersome process for the developers. That's why inside the Spring Data JPA framework, there is a concept called relationships. By using these relationships, we are going to communicate to the Spring Data JPA if two tables are related to each other, based upon the configurations that we provide, the Spring Data JPA, it is going to take care of SQL generation so that we as a developer, we never have to worry about writing the complex query to fetch the data from both the tables. 54666281.title: FETCH vs CASCADE - The Two JPA Concepts You Must Never Confuse
54666281.section: Mastering JPA Relationships - From Basics to Real-World Mapping
54666281.transcript: I know you are very eager to see the demo of our code changes, but before that, let's spend a few minutes understanding in detail about fetch configurations and cascade configurations because these are very important and applicable for all type of relationships inside Spring Data JPA. We discussed on a high level about the purpose of fetch type configurations. By using these fetch type configurations, we are going to tell to the Spring Data JPA what needs to happen when related entities are loaded from the database. For example, should the related data be loaded immediately when the parent is being loaded or should it be loaded later only when needed. We can configure either fetch type as lazy or eager. With the eager configurations, the Spring Data JPA framework, it is going to load the related entities as well right away along with the parent entity. In our scenario, when it is loading the company related information, the JPA framework, it is also going to load all the jobs related information as well. If you want to avoid that, you need to mention the fetch type as lazy. I have a real life analogy as well. You can think like eager as ordering the main dish along with the side dish at the same time, even if you're not hungry for the side. In this scenario, what is going to happen ? Even if you're not hungry for the side dish, it's still, it is going to come because you have ordered the main dish and side dish together. Whereas with the lazy, it's like ordering only the main dish first so that at later point of time, if you feel hungry, you are going to order for the side dish. These fetch type configurations we can provide for all these annotations. So all these annotations, they support a parameter with the name fetch to which we can either configure eager or lazy. In case if you're not configuring these fetch type configurations, behind the scenes, the Spring Data JPA framework, it is going to consider some default fetch type based upon the relationship. So for the @OneToOne and @ManyToOne, the default fetch type is eager, whereas for @OneToMany and @ManyToMany, the default fetch type will be lazy. There is a logical reason why Spring team, they have gone with eager for these two relationships whereas lazy for these two configurations, if you see for @OneToOne and @ManyToOne scenario, the child entities are the child objects there will be only one record for the parent record. Since there will be always only one child entity, there won't be any performance problem in loading that single record into the memory. That's why the framework team, they configured eager in this scenario. But let's imagine in the scenario of @OneToMany or @ManyToMany, the child records will be many. For example, for a single company, we may have thousand job records. Loading all those thousand job records eagerly will attract some performance problem. That's why here by default, the framework is going to consider lazy in case if the developer have a scenario where the developer is always going to look for the child records, then the developer always have an option to override these to the eager. I hope these default fetch type makes sense to you. You can remember these default fetch type very easily. If you are seeing one towards the end, you can assume eager because loading one record won't create any problem. Whereas if you're seeing many towards the end, the default fetch type will be lazy. Next we have cascade configurations as well. So what is cascade? By using cascade configurations, we are telling to the Spring Data JPA framework, if I do something to the parent entity, automatically do it for the child entity as well. This is like a chain reaction. Changes made to one entity get automatically applied to the related entities. For example, if I am deleting a company record, all the associated child records will be deleted automatically by the Spring Data JPA. So why we need to configure these cascade configurations? To reduce the boilerplate code, to avoid manually saving or deleting the related entities, to keep the entities always in sync automatically. In the code configurations that we have done previously, we configure the cascade configurations with the constant as all. Apart from ALL, we also have other cascade types like PERSIST, MERGE, REMOVE, REFRESH, DETACH. So let's try to understand what is the meaning of each of these cascade type. If we are mentioning the cascade type as PERSIST, then the Spring Data JPA framework is going to save the child entity when the parent is being saved. For any other operations like delete or update, the Spring Data JPA framework is not going to make any modifications onto the child record. The MERGE is like update when you are trying to update the parent entity, automatically the child entity records also will be updated. Remove is for delete. When you are deleting a parent record, all the associated child records will be deleted by the framework. Next, we have REFRESH inside the Hibernate framework, at regular intervals of time, the framework, it is going to refresh the parent object. As part of this refresh operation, the framework is going to fetch the latest data from the database for that record. So when the parent is being refreshed, we are telling to the framework to refresh the child records as well. Next we have DETACH with detach, whenever the parent is being detached from the persistent context, the child entities will also be detached. So what is persistent context ? The Hibernate framework, it is going to store all the entities data, like companies data or jobs data inside a small memory called persistent context. Any changes that we are applying by using the Company object or the Jobs object, those changes will be tracked by the persistent context so that when we are executing methods like save() or remove(), those changes will be applied inside the database. In case if we don't want the framework to track our objects for some scenario, then we have an option of detaching those objects from the persistent context. Under such scenarios, we're telling whenever the parent is being detached, please also detach the child records as well. In case if you want to apply all this cascade type, you also have an option of ALL. If you want to mention only two cascade types, you can do that as well by mentioning persist and remove separately. So here I have an example. So first I have created an Company object. We know this Company object represents the company's table, and this is a parent entity object. Let's imagine after these Company object, I updated some company related fields, followed by I also created a couple of job objects like job1 and job2. These objects represents the child entities. After creating these objects, let's imagine I updated some data into these Job objects. Later on, I added both the job1 and job2 into the jobs List available in the company object by using this code. So these are all Java code. Now towards the end of the logic, I'm invoking the save() method available inside the CompanyRepository and I'm passing only the Company entity object, which is a parent entity object. If we configure the cascade configurations, since we are trying to insert the company record, the corresponding child objects also will be saved into the database. If you don't have any cascade configurations, then it is your responsibility to invoke the save() method by using the JobRepository interface. So you have to invoke something like JobRepository.save() and to this save, you can either pass job1 or job2 or both of them. Since we want to avoid all that boilerplate code, it is always recommended to go with the cascade configurations, but please be cautious while you are selecting the cascade type. Based upon your business logic and based upon the criticality of your data, you need to select one of the cascade type in case if you're not comfortable giving the responsibility to the Spring Data JPA framework, you're always welcome to ignore these cascade configurations and manually execute your operations on the child record or onto the related data.

54666283.title: @OnDelete Explained - What Really Happens When the Parent is Deleted
54666283.section: Mastering JPA Relationships - From Basics to Real-World Mapping
54666283.transcript: As part of this lecture, let's try to understand the purpose of @OnDelete configurations that we have done inside the job entity class. By using these @OnDelete annotations, we are telling to the database directly to automatically delete the related records when a parent record is deleted. While we are creating a jobs table by using the insert statement, next to the foreign key configurations, we also mention these ON DELETE CASCADE. Let me show you the same. This is the SQL statement that we have executed. After the foreign key configurations related to the company id column, towards the end, I have mentioned these ON DELETE CASCADE. . Since we have mentioned these configurations, the JPABuddy, while it is generating the job entity class, it also generated the On Delete configurations. Basically, by using these On Delete configurations, we are directly telling to the database to take care of deleting the child records as well when a parent record is being deleted. The Hibernate framework or the JPA framework, they won't get involved in this scenario. It is always going to be the responsibility of the database server. Even if someone is trying to delete the company record directly inside the database, in that scenario as well, the database is going to take care of deleting the corresponding child records or job records. While using these @OnDelete annotations, we can also provide various constants to these action parameters. So far inside our logic, we have mentioned a constant with the name cascade, but apart from these cascade, we also have other options as well. Let's try to understand what are those. The other valid values are NO_ACTION with these no action we're telling to the database. If a parent is being deleted, please don't delete any child record. So this will be that default behavior, but here you may have a question, but what if the foreign key column inside the child table is a not null column? So in that scenario, obviously the database is going to throw an foreign key constraint violation exception, maybe at later point of time in the transaction. But if your child table has a foreign key column as nullable column, then this NO_ACTION is going to make sense because the child records are going to be retained and there won't be any exception as well. So such a child records with the null foreign key column, we call them as orphan records. With cascade, we are telling to the database to automatically delete the child rows as well when the parent role is being deleted, we are using these constant as of now inside our code. We also have RESTRICT, RESTRICT means immediately block the deletion of the parent if any child exists, this is strict version of NO_ACTION with restrict, we can't delete the parent record unless we delete the child records by ourself. So this is like a safety net. If someone is trying to delete the parent record, they have to make sure that there are no child records for a given parent. Next we have SET_NULL with the set null whenever the parent being is deleted, but the child records under the foreign key column, the NULL value will be set. But to use this option, the foreign key column should be a nullable column. Instead of null, you can also set some default value as well by using these SET_DEFAULT constant. I hope these constants are clear to you. I know you may have a confusion between @OnDelete annotation and CascadeType.REMOVE. So we also have Cascade type configuration, which is going to do the pretty similar job, but there is a very major difference between both these two approaches. Let's try to understand what are those. With OnDelete, the delete operation is always going to happen at the database level. It is the database responsibility, whereas with the CascadeType.REMOVE, it will be the responsibility of the Hibernate framework, it is the Java code that is going to take care of removing the child records. In the scenario of OnDelete, the database will delete the child records, whereas with the CascadeType.REMOVE, Hibernate is going to delete the child records. Performance wise, this option is faster because the database directly handling it, whereas Hibernate option is slower because there is an extra layer between database and your code. This is the example which we already explored. We need to use this approach when we want the database to manage the deletions and this one when we want the application level control. The main difference you have to remember is @OnDelete is a database rule, whereas Cascade type is a Hibernate instruction. Basically these @OnDelete configurations, we need to mention inside the entity class, which is owning the relationship. Since the jobs table has a company ID foreign key column, this is the right place to mention the @OnDelete. I hope everything is clear, but before I try to close this lecture, I want to clarify an important doubt that usually developers will have. So if you closely observe here, we have made one delete configuration, which is taking care of deleting both the parent and child records. And if you go to the company also, here also we have some delete related configurations. Let inside the company also under the CascadeType.All we have remove as well, which is going to take care of removing the child entities and here we also have orphanRemoval as true, which is also going to take care of deleting some orphan records. So if you closely understand, we are trying to handle that delete related scenarios by using three different approaches. This is the first one, and this is the second one, and on delete is the third approach. You may think that we are performing a lot of duplicate effort here around the delete operation, but that's not the case. Let me explain you each of these approaches they have their own unique scenarios. Let's imagine someone is deleting the parent record, which is a company record by using the Java code. Like maybe they might have executed like companyRepository.remove() method and to this remove() method, they might have passed company object, which is a parent object. In this scenario, what is going to happen with the CascadeType.All configurations since the parent is being deleted, the child records also will be deleted automatically. When the child records are also deleted automatically by the JPA framework or by the Hibernate framework, then there is no involvement of orphanRemoval configurations because we don't have any orphan records. So let's consider an another scenario. In this scenario, someone is trying to remove a child from the parent by invoking the parent.get jobs. So what is the parent here? It is a company object. So from the company object, they invoke the get jobs() method and by using these get jobs, they again invoke the remove and to this remove() method, they passed and job object. So if you see here, we are not deleting the parent record here. We are only deleting the child record. So in this kind of scenarios, this cascade will never come into picture because the cascade will come into picture only when the parent record is getting affected. But with this code, we are only deleting the child record. There is also another possibility, someone might set null by invoking the job.setCompany. So here also what we are doing, we are removing the relationship between the job and company. In both these scenarios, what is going to happen? The job record or the job object, it is going to become an orphan. Unless we have this orphanRemoval as true, this kind of orphan records will stay inside your database. That's why to handle these kinds of scenarios, this orphanRemoval true configuration is very much required. Now let's go to the third approach, which is @On Delete. With the help of these @OnDelete configurations, we are making sure that there won't be any orphan records in the database. Even if someone is trying to delete the company record manually, directly inside the database, this configuration, it is also going to be useful in the scenarios. Let's imagine one of the new developer, he forgot making these cascade configurations and orphanRemoval configurations inside the company entity class. One day, the same developer deleted the company object, which is the parent object directly by invoking the remove() method by using the companyRepository. Since there are no cascade configurations and orphanRemoval configurations, we will end up having a orphan record in the database. Such orphan records will be taken care of by this third configuration. I hope this is all making sense to you. So none of these configurations are duplicate to each other. They are handling different flavors of deleting a record. I know I'm taking a lot of time explaining all these theory concepts, but all this is very much required for a backend developer. All these concepts that we discussed, these are applicable for all kinds of relationships, not only for one to many or many to one, they are applicable for other relationships as well. In the next lecture, let's see that demo of our code changes.

54686811.title: What Happens Under the Hood - Spring Security Internals
54686811.section: Spring Security Essentials - Behavior, Control & Inner Mechanics
54686811.transcript: We need to make good amount of changes on the backend application to adopt the Spring Security Framework. But before we try to jump on to those changes, first let's try to understand what is the Spring Security internal flow, so that this knowledge is going to help us in adopting and implementing advanced features of Spring Security Framework. This is the internal flow of Spring Security Framework there are good amount of individual components which are playing different roles inside the authentication flow. When an end user is trying to access an REST API or a web path, the Spring Security Framework first it will decide whether a given path is a secured path or a public path. If it is a public path, none of this flow is going to come into picture, the end user request will directly send to the controller layer. So after this Spring Security layer, we are going to have the controller layer towards the end. In the case of public APIs or public paths, the request will be directly forwarded to the controller layer. Whereas if the end user is trying to access a secured API path, the Spring Security Framework identifies the same and it will check whether the end user authentication is already completed or it is yet to complete. All these housekeeping activities will be done by the Spring Security filters. Inside the Spring Security Framework, there are good amount of filters like around 15 to 20 filters are there performing various different activities. One of the primary responsibilities of the Spring Security filters is, to understand whether a given request API path is a secured path or a public path. If it is a secured path, they need to identify whether the given end user is already authenticated in the previous sessions or not. If the end user is not previously authenticated and he's trying to access the secured API path, these filters are going to take care of redirecting the end user to the login page. We saw a default login page as well in the previous lectures. Once the end user submitted the credentials, one of the filters present inside this Spring Security filter, it is going to extract the username and password from the HTTP request and convert that into an authentication object. This authentication represents an end user credentials along with the username and password. This authentication is also going to have information to identify whether the authentication is successful or not. Once this authentication object is populated by the Spring Security filters, the request will be forwarded to the authentication manager. So whatever I have highlighted here like Authentication, Authentication Manager, Authentication Provider, Password Encoder, UserDetailsManager, UserDetailsService, all these are interfaces. Behind the scenes, Spring Security Framework, they also provided the implementation classes for these interfaces as well. The implementation class of this AuthenticationManager, it is going to take care of triggering the authentication. So the whole responsibility of performing the authentication is with this AuthenticationManager. To this Authentication Manager, the Spring Security Framework, it is also going to pass the authentication implementation class object. So the Authentication Manager has all the details to initiate the authentication like username and password. This Authentication Manager, it is only going to take care of initiating the authentication, but it is not going to perform the actual authentication. The actual authentication will be taken care of by the Authentication providers. In a Spring Boot application that is using Spring Security Framework, it can have any number of Authentication Providers. That's why I have mentioned multiple boxes here. The reason why Spring Security supports multiple Authentication Providers is, usually in a web application, they're going to support multiple styles of authentication. The end user can trigger the authentication by simply using the OTP. There is also an option of submitting username and password. So there are multiple flavors of authentication,to support all these multiple flavors of authentication, we can define multiple Authentication Providers. The job of this Authentication Manager is to pass the authentication object details to all the Authentication Providers that are registered inside a web application. It is going to iterate all the applicable Authentication Providers, and it will ask the Authentication Providers to perform the authentication. If any of the Authentication Provider confirms that the authentication is successful, the same information will be sent back to the Spring Security filters and Spring Security filters, they are going to store the authentication details into the Security Context so that in future, if the same end user is trying to access different security API from the same browser, the Spring Security filters, they are not going to redirect the end user to the login page. Instead, they're going to leverage the authentication details already stored inside the Security Context, and accordingly, they're going to forward the request to the controller layer. This way Spring Security filters, they're going to avoid triggering the authentication logic for each and every request. We already saw a demo in the previous lectures. By default, Spring Security framework, it is going to create a cookie with the name JSESSIONID. When an end user is trying to make a request for the very first time, he will be displayed a login page. The end user will enter the credentials, the credentials are valid, and if the authentication is successful, those details will be stored inside the security context along with the JSESSIONID. The same JSESSIONID will also be sent inside the response to the browser or to the client applications. The client applications, they're going to store this information inside cookie. In future, when the same end user is trying to access a secured REST API, the browser, it is going to automatically attach the JSESSIONID cookie to the request. If this cookie is present inside the request, the Spring Security filters, they're going to validate if the cookie value that they stored inside the security context is same. If yes, they're going to ignore all this authentication logic and forward the user request directly to the controller layer. So the actual logic of authentication is going to be present inside the Authentication Providers. These Authentication Providers they're going to take help from two different components to perform the authentication. The first component is UserDetailsManager or UserDetailsService. At the end of the day, if the end user is trying to log in, we need to validate whether the login details are matching with what we already stored inside the database. To perform that comparison of the credentials, first, we need to load the existing credentials from the storage system like database. That responsibility of loading the user details from the database or from a storage system will be taken care by these UserDetailsManager or UserDetailsServiceComponent. To this component, we just have to pass the username that we received. Based upon the username, this component will load the user details from the database and the same will be forwarded to these AuthenticationProvider. Inside the Authentication Provider, the password comparisons will happen,we're going to have two different passwords, the password that we received from the end user and the other password that we received from the storage systems like database. Both of them will be compared. If they are same, the authentication will be considered as success otherwise, it will be considered as failure. There is also another component with the name Password Encoder inside the Spring security framework. Passwords are sensitive fields we should never store them inside database or in any other storage systems using plain text. If my password is Madan, the same value should not be stored as it is in plain text format in the storage systems. If someone is storing the passwords using plain text, it is a serious problem because anyone who has access to the database or to the storage system, they should be able to query that table and identify the passwords of all end users. To avoid such problems, we have this Password Encoder interface. There are good amount of implementation classes for this interface. The responsibility of these password encoders is to hash our passwords before storing them into the database. Hashing is a concept where if you provide plain text as an input to these hashing algorithms, they're going to convert that password into a format like encrypted format, which no one can understand. We're going to see the demo of these Password Encoders in the coming lectures. The responsibility of this Password Encoder is, to store the passwords in a properly secured manner. While we are registering a new user, we need to take the help of PasswordEncoder to store the password in a proper encrypted format. If the same user is trying to attempt the login operation, these Authentication Providers, they are going to take help from the Password Encoders to identify if the user entered password and the already stored passwords are same or not. Hashing is a very beautiful concept. I discussed about this hashing in detail inside my Spring Security course. Using hashing, we can convert a plain text value into a hash value. By using this hash value, the reverse mechanism is not possible, which means from this hash value, no one can understand what is the original plain text value. Even if someone tried to manage to hack your database by looking all the hash values that we stored inside the database, they cannot guess or they can't know the plain text passwords. But with that, you may have a question? Which is then how the passwords comparison is going to work during the login operation ? It's a great question. We're going to discuss that in the coming lectures. I hope you're clear with the Spring Security internal flow. I know I discussed in a hurry. Since this is not a Spring Security focused course, I'm not trying to discuss everything in detail. If you are interested to know everything about the Spring Security framework, please enroll into my Spring Security focused course. To help you to remember this internal flow easily, I prepared one more internal flow by taking SDLC lifecycle as an analogy. In real applications, the projects, they are going to be developed by using various software development lifecycles like Waterfall, apart from Waterfall, people also follow some agile style to develop the softwares. I have taken the same SDLC lifestyle and prepared this analogy Spring Security internal flow. Let's try to understand this internal flow so that we can remember the actual Spring Security internal flow more clearly, just like how an end user can submit his credentials to initiate the authentication flow. Very similarly, inside the SDLC lifecycle, the requirements to develop a software application will be provided by the client or the business people. They are going to provide the requirements. These requirements will be received by the business analyst or the product owners that are working inside a project. So these business analysts and product owners, they are playing the role of Spring Security filters. For example, these business analysts or product owners, they are going to check whether the given requirement is already handled inside the application. If yes, they are going to let the business people know that it is already handled and taken care. In the similar lines, if the requirements are too impossible to implement, such scenarios also these people, they are going to handle. Whereas if the given requirements are proper and valid, these people what they are going to do is, they are going to convert the requirements that they receive into an user story. Inside real projects, people they are going to use products like Jira, Confluence. Using these products, we can document all the requirements that we receive. Since the requirements that we receive from the clients or business people, they are going to be in a raw format. These business analysts or product owners, they are going to convert the requirements into a format called User Story. So this user story is nothing but our Authentication object that we discussed previously. If you go to the previous slide, user story represents Authentication, and these Spring Security filters are represented by business analysts and product owners. Once a User Story is created, the same will be forwarded to the Delivery Manager. Delivery Manager is a person who takes the responsibility of delivering the requirements, but this person will never implement the requirements with his own hands. Instead, he's going to rely on the team leads. So that same Delivery Manager, which is similar to the Authentication Manager, is going to forward the User Story to the team leads. To this Delivery Manager there can be multiple team leads reporting. One team lead can be focusing on the UI, the other team lead might be focusing on the backend. Similarly, there are other team leads focusing on database. So based upon the type of User Story, the Delivery Manager is going to forward the User Story to the corresponding team leads. Once the team lead accepts the User Story, the team lead is not going to implement the requirements, everything on his own. He's going to take help from developers and QAs. That's why what this team lead will do is, he will rely on the developer and the testing people to implement the requirements and to perform the unit testing. The same team lead is also going to rely on a security guy to scan for any vulnerabilities inside the code and to make sure there won't be any cyber threat attacks on the web application. Who are these team leads? They are nothing but Authentication Providers, and these User Details Manager and User Details Service, they are nothing but developers and QAs, and this security guy is nothing but Password Encoder. Once all these requirements are implemented, the same will be communicated back to the Delivery Manager. The Delivery Manager will communicate back that to the business analyst and product owners. The product owners, they're going to update the status of the user story and store them inside the products like Jira or Confluence. In future, if there is some question around this User Story, they're going to refer the Jira to know what is the current status. Just like how we have Jira here, we have security context to store the authentication details. Please try to digest all the information that I have shared with you. If you have any questions, don't worry. As we are making progress inside this course, it is going to be clear for you. Towards the end, I have another slide where I have mentioned all the points that we discussed around the components that are involved inside the Spring Security internal flow. I hope you're clear. With this knowledge, let's try to enhance our backend application by using Spring Security Framework.

54694931.title: Encoding Vs Encryption Vs Hashing
54694931.section: Spring Security Authentication - From Passwords to Tokens
54694931.transcript: Inside this lecture, we're going to focus on understanding on how to handle the passwords in real applications. As a beginner into the software development, any junior developer will assume that the passwords will be stored as it is inside the storage systems like database. For example, if an end user set the password as 12345 during the registration process, you might be thinking that the same value will be stored inside the database so that during the login operation, we can compare the already stored password with the user entered password and if they are same, we can allow the login operation. This can be your assumption. That is what I also predicted inside this slide as well. This is the flow that is going to happen when you save the passwords using plain text inside the database. But this approach is not recommended for production applications or for enterprise applications. The reason is very simple. If you start storing the passwords in a plain text inside a storage system like database, it is going to bring some integrity and confidentiality issues, anyone who has access to your production database, they should be able to query the User table. Inside this user table, they are going to have both username and password. Since the password is stored using plain text, with that they can mimic an end user and perform any actions that they want. That's why this approach is not recommended for production applications. Instead, we need to look for an option which is going to help us to store the password in a format which can't be understood by anyone, even though if they get access to the production database. Inside the industry, when we want to transfer the data from one form to other form, we have three different options with the name Encoding, Encryption, and Hashing. Using all these approaches, we should be able to avoid storing the plain text password inside the database. But not all of these options are going to be suitable for us. We need to discuss one by one and get rid of the options which are not suitable for converting password values. The first option is Encoding. Encoding is defined as a process of converting data from one form to another, and it has nothing to do with the cryptography. When I say cryptography is a special subject that deals with the secret data, secret communication, all such concepts will come under the cryptography. With the help of encoding, we should be able to convert our plain text password like 1 2 3 4 5 into some encoded value. So by looking at this encoded value, no one can understand what is my original password. But since this encoding does not involve any secret, the encoded value can be completely reversible into a plain text value. If someone have my password encoded value, they should be easily reverse the encoding process with a concept called decoding. By giving my encoded value as an input to this decoding process, they can easily derive my plain text password. We have various encoding algorithms inside the industry like ASCII, BASE64, and UNICODE. Regardless of what algorithm you're trying to use to perform the encoding, all of them have a drawback, which is anyone can easily decode your original text value. That's why this option is not suitable to convert our passwords into some other format. With that, we can move on to the Encryption. Encryption is a process of transforming data in such a way that guarantees confidentiality. To achieve this confidentiality, encryption requires the use of a secret, which in cryptographic terms we call it as a key. For an encryption algorithm, we can feed two inputs like 12345 which is the end-user password, followed by some secret we can inject as another input. By using both these values, the encryption algorithms are going to convert the plain text value that you have feed into some other encrypted data. If anyone has this encrypted data, they cannot derive the original text value by using decryption process unless they know the secret key that we have used during the encryption process. As long as someone is able to securely store the secret that they have used during the encryption process, no one should be able to derive the original text value. But in real applications, we can never guarantee that no one can see the secret key. Server admins, operations team members, or platform team members, since they are going to have access to the production servers, they can easily understand what is the secret that you are using inside the code for the encryption. Once they have the secret and encryption algorithm details, they can easily perform the decrypt operation and derive the plain text value. Since there is a risk of losing the secret key, this option also is not suitable to convert the passwords. With that, we left only with the Hashing process. Inside Hashing, we are going to feed our plain text password values to a hashing algorithm. These hashing algorithms are going to convert your plain text password value to a hash value. Plain data that once hashed, it is non-reversible. For example, if I feed a plain text password like 12345 to my hash function, and this hash function is going to derive some hash value for my plain text password value, even if someone gets hold of this hash value, they cannot really reverse the value to the plain text value. Because hashing is always a one-way process and hashing algorithms, they don't support reversing the hash value into a plain text value. This hashing seems to be a good option to convert our passwords before storing them into the database. I know you may have too many questions at this point of time around Hashing. Don't worry, we're going to discuss about hashing in detail in the next lecture. Before I try to close this lecture, let me show you a visual representation on how encoding, encryption and hashing is going to work. As I said, for encoding, if you feed the plain text value, the plain text will be converted into an encoded text. But if someone tried to decode the encoded text, they can easily get the plain text value. In the similar lines, during the encryption, we can feed the plain text value along with the secret key, and with that, we are going to get encrypted text. Anyone who wants to decrypt the encrypted text to a plain text value, they need to provide the secret key that is involved during the encryption process. Otherwise, they're not going to get the plain text value. Whereas inside the hashing, when a plain text value is feed to a hash function, the input value will be converted into a hash value or in other words, we can also call it as digest value. Once a hash value is calculated for a plain text value, we cannot reverse the hash to a plain text value. There is no reverse mechanism inside the hashing. Let's continue discussing more details about hashing in the next lecture as well.

54694939.title: How Passwords Really Stay Safe & introduction to Password Encoders

And with that, hacker has a clue saying that that so-and-so users, they are using the password 1 2 3 4 5. Similarly, inside the Brute Force attack, the hacker is going to perform the trail and error. If someone is using simple passwords like 1 2 3 4 5 or password, by using all these most common possible passwords, the hackers, they're going to perform the trail and error. At some point of time, they're going to identify all the weak passwords used by the end user. Because one more beauty of hash values is, these hash functions, they're not going to be super quick to give the hash value. They're going to demand some CPU power. All this process may seem to be very complex to you, but believe me my friend, since we are using Spring Security, this framework, it is going to provide some industry recommended password encoders that are capable of generating random salt value and leverage the password hashing algorithms like bcrypt. As a developer, you never have to worry of generating a salt value or forming the hash function. You just have to invoke the methods provided by these password encoders. I'll share more details of password encoder in few seconds. Before that, let's try to understand how the passwords are going to be validated with the hashed passwords. During the registration process, we know what is going to happen. The end user is going to enter the password like 1 2 3 4 5 to this password. We are going to add some salt value. The combination of plain text password and salt value will be taken as an input and the output hash value we are going to store inside the database. Now, during the login process, the user is going to enter the same password to this password, the password encoders, they are going to add the salt value which we stored inside the database. After adding the salt value, they are going to calculate the hash value again. If the newly calculated hash value and the stored hash value is same, then it is going to convey the login success, otherwise the login is going to fail. Inside the next slide, I have mentioned details around the password encoder. Password encoder is an interface inside the Spring security framework. It has two different abstract methods, which are very important. One is encode. This encode method we need to use during the registration process. When you pass a plain text password as an input to this encode method, it is going to generate a salt value. By using the same salt value, it is going to generate a hash value and the hash value will be the output from this method. The same hash value we are going to store inside the database. There is another method with the name matches. To this matches method, we need to provide two input parameters. The very first one is the raw password, which is entered by the end user and the second parameter is whatever hashed password that we stored inside the database. From this hashed password or encoded password, this matches method, it is going to derive the salt value and the same salt value will be added to the raw password and it's going to calculate the hash value again. If this newly calculated hash value is same as the second parameter value, then it is going to return the boolean as true, otherwise it is going to return false. It is also going to expose a default method with the name upgrade encoding. By default, it is going to return false. If needed, this method can be overrided to return true. When this method is returning the true as an output, the hashing process, it is going to be performed multiple times to make the hash value super tough to guess. Spring security framework also provides various implementation classes for this password encoder interface. The classes are NoOpPasswordEncoder and StandardPasswordEncoder, pbkdf2PasswordEncoder, BcryptPasswordEncoder, SCryptPasswordEncoder, and Argon2PasswordEncoder. The first two password encoders, they are not recommended for production applications. When you are using NoOpPasswordEncoder, your passwords are going to be stored using plain text value. In the similar lines, when someone is using StandardPasswordEncoder, it is going to use some legacy hashing function, which is not recommended based upon the recent advancements happened on the CPUs and GPUs inside the industry. When an application is using this password encoder, they are going to make the hacker's life easy because whatever algorithm that this password encoder uses, it is not super effective when a hacker is using latest CPUs and GPUs to calculate the hash value. Whereas the other password encoders, all of them are recommended password encoders. Whatever name you see like Bcrypt, Scrypt, Argon2 and pbkdf2, all these are the hashing algorithm names. As of today, Spring Security team is recommending to use the BcryptPasswordEncoder. The same we are also going to use inside our application as well. Regardless of which password encoder you're trying to use, the process of registration and login, it is always going to be the same. The way these algorithms hash the value is different. Other than that, all of them are secure and can be happily used inside the production applications. Inside this course as I said, we are going to use the BcryptPasswordEncoder.

54694955.title: JWT Tokens Explained - Modern Token-Based Authentication
54694955.section: Spring Security Authentication - From Passwords to Tokens
54694955.transcript: Before we try to explore more details about the tokens, let me show you a small demo on what is happening around the token generation with the default behavior of Spring Security Framework. To show you this demo, I need to enable the formLogin(). Let me replace this disable logic with this withDefaults() method. I'm going to save this to the build, once the build is completed, I'm going to invoke my company's related API. As of now, this is a secured api. As soon as I press enter, we can expect the login page. Here, I'm going to enter the credentials like madan and the associated password. As soon as I press enter, we know what is going to happen we're going to get the expected response. Now, if you go and open the developer console, under the application, you have a section with the name cookies. Under these cookies, you can click on this localhost:8080. As you can see here, there is a cookie generated by the framework with the name JSESSIONID. Inside this cookie, it is storing some token value this confirms that by default, Spring Security is also leveraging the token mechanism so that it doesn't have to ask the end user to enter their credentials for each and every request. As of now, I have already logged in that's why if I try to refresh this page one more time, you'll be able to see it is not asking me the credentials. This is because my browser is attaching these cookie details whenever I'm making a request to the domain with the name localhost:8080. If I try to tamper this cookie or delete this cookie, then immediately the backend, it is going to recognize and show me the login page. So here I can right click and select this edit value I'm going to enter some random value. This time, if I try to refresh the page, as you can see, the framework showed me the login page. So this is the beauty of tokens. Inside the industry, whenever someone wants to generate a token, there are two flavors of tokens. Let's try to understand what are those. The first category of token format is Opaque tokens and the second category is JSON Web Tokens in short form, we call them as JWT. Inside industry, people pronounce this JWT as JOT. When someone says JOT tokens, they are referring to these JSON web tokens. There is a very clear difference between these two tokens. Opaque tokens are typically random Strings with no inherent meaning. Whatever token right now Spring Security framework is generating with the help of JSESSIONID cookie, that token value is of Opaque token. It does not have any inherent meaning. It is a randomly generated string value, which is associated for a user session. These type of tokens are used to reference the authentication information stored on the server side. When someone is using Opaque tokens, the client applications, they need to make sure they are sending the same token to the backend server. On the backend application, the framework is going to check whether the token is really generated by itself. If not, it is going to throw an error. Coming to the JSON web tokens or JOT tokens, these tokens are self-contained that consists of three parts. Usually a Jot token will have a header, payload and a signature part. The beauty of JWT tokens is, we can share certain information about the end-user inside this token itself. For example, I can share the email details of the user or the username of the user, mobile number or the roles of the user. So all this kind of user-related information I can embed inside the Jot token itself, so that when we share the same to the client application or to the UI application, they can try to read the information present inside the Jot token and accordingly they can understand what are the end-user details. One more advantage of Jot tokens is, when we are generating a Jot token, we can set an expiration time. I can generate a Jot token with one hour expiration or one day expiration. It's up to me what kind of expiration time that I want to set. Since the expiration time is going to be embedded inside the Jot token, the client applications also they can understand when this token is going to expire. If the token is expired, they can redirect the end-user to the login page or they can make a request to the backend server behind the scenes asking for a new Jot token. Since these Jot tokens have many advantages inside the industry, you will see these tokens are being used extensively compared to the Opaque tokens. Inside our application as well, we are going to generate the Jot tokens by using a library. I'll discuss all those details in the coming lectures. For now, let's try to understand what is a Jot token and how it is going to look like. As I said before, JWT means JSON Web Token. It is a token implementation which will be in the JSON format and designed to use for the web request. Jot is the most common and favorite token type that many systems use these days due to its special features and advantages. Jot tokens, they can be used both in the scenarios of authorization and authentication along with the information exchange, which means you can share certain user-related data in the token itself, which will reduce the burden of maintaining such details in the session or on the client or server side. A Jot token will have three parts separated by a period. As you can see, this is a sample Jot token. Whatever first part we have that is highlighted in the yellow color, this indicates header followed by there is a period after the period, we have another content in a green color. The second content inside the JWT token represents the payload of the token. After the payload, we have again a period and the last content that we have, which is highlighted in gray or white color, it represents the signature of the token. This signature part is completely optional, but most of the times you will see people using signature as well inside the JWT tokens. There is a very good important reason on why we should maintain the signature as well inside the JWT tokens, which I'm going to discuss in few seconds. Let's try to understand more details about the purpose of this signature. The last part of the token is the digital signature. This part can be optional if the party that you are trying to share the JWT token is internal and that someone who you can trust, but not open inside the web. The whole purpose of generating the JWT tokens is to share the same with the client applications. If these client applications are present within your internal company network, then you don't have to generate this digital signature because the chances of tampering the JWT token within the organization is very less. But if you're trying to share the same JWT token with a web application, which is available inside the web, under such scenarios, you need to make sure you are definitely digitally signing your JWT token with the help of the last part of the token. Let me share more details about the header and payload part so that we can discuss about the importance of the signature as well. Usually inside the header part, we are going to send the metadata information of the JWT token. The metadata information like what kind of algorithm we have used to generate the signature, all such details will be present inside these header part. But inside the JWT token, we are never going to send the value in a plain text format. Instead, we are going to convert the plain text value into a base64 encoded value. And this is how the base64 encoded value is going to look like. Now coming to the payload part of the token, inside the payload, we can send all user related information like username, email, mobile, address, what are the roles of the end user. So all such details we are going to send to the UI application. Once the login operation is completed, we can write a logic on what kind of information we can send to the UI application. Let's imagine inside the payload of the JWT token, I'm trying to send the role of the user as USER. With this user role, the end user, whoever is logged in, he can only invoke the REST APIs at the secured pages that are accessed to the role user. We are yet to discuss about the role-based access. We're going to do the same in the upcoming sections. But for now, I'm assuming you have a clarity on how the role-based access mechanism is going to work. When we implement role-based access mechanism, the user can only access the REST API only if he has a certain role. Apart from proving his identity by entering the username and password, the user should also have enough privileges or enough roles to invoke the REST API. So this kind of roles information we can also send inside the JWT token. Using the same roles information, the client applications, they can try to display various pages that the end user has access. And if the end user is trying to perform any actions on the UI application, the same JWT token is going to be sent to the backend server. And based upon the JWT token that the backend application is going to receive, it is going to check whether the user has so-and-so role or not and accordingly, it is going to allow the end user to access the REST API. But here there is a risk, what if one of the end user who is very smart is tampering the content inside the JWT token? For example, if we change this role USER to ADMIN, then he's going to get elevated privileges on the backend application. So this kind of tampering is possible when we are trying to share a JWT token to a client application. People can change their mobile numbers, usernames, email, roles, whatever we are sending into the JWT token payload, all such details can be changed. And the newly changed JWT token value can be sent again back to the backend server. Unless if the backend server is capable of identifying whether the token is tampered or not, this JWT token approach is going to be useless. To give those capabilities to the backend server to validate whether the token is tampered or not, we have these digital signature part. Let's try to understand more details about the signature, how it is going to work in the next lecture.

54694963.title: How JWT tokens validated using signature
54694963.section: Spring Security Authentication - From Passwords to Tokens
54694963.transcript: Understanding on how the digital signatures are going to work inside JWT token is somewhat complex. Let's try to put some good efforts in understanding the same. Whenever a back-end decided to generate a signature for a JWT token that it is going to issue, it is going to follow a formula. The formula is, first, it is going to decide which hashing algorithm it is going to use. Let's assume the back-end application is using SHA-256 algorithm. Once the hash algorithm is decided, whatever header content and payload content that we have inside the JWT token, they are going to be encoded with the help of base64 urlencoding. As you can see here, first, they are trying to convert the header into an encoded value. Similarly, the payload also, they are trying to convert the encoded value. In between them, there are going to be a period. This entire string of header encoded value, period, and payload encoded value will be the first input parameter to the hashing algorithm. Along with this first input parameter, the hashing algorithm, it is also going to receive a secret value which is known only to the back-end server or the back-end application. By using both these input parameters, the hashing algorithm, it is going to return a hash value or a digest value. We already discussed about what is hashing. Based upon the hash value, no one can reverse the plain text value. That means no one can see the secret value that is used by the back-end server to generate that digital signature. I hope you are clear with this formula. Now let's try to understand what is going to happen during the login operation. During the login operation, the back-end application, it is going to generate the header content and the body or the payload content of the JWT token. Inside the header, as I said previously, it is going to have some metadata information like what hashing algorithm it has used and what is the type of the token. So the type of the token here, it is mentioned as JWT token. This plain text header value will be encoded with the help of base64. And this is how it is going to look like. The same we are going to share to the UI application as well. In between this header and payload, there will be a period the same applies for payload and signature as well. Now coming to the body payload, inside the body payload, the back-end application can populate all the user-related information based upon their business requirements like username, email, address, mobile number, when is the token is issued, what is the expiration time of the token. All such details will be present inside the payload of the token. For simplicity, I have mentioned some simple values here. With the help of the name, I'm trying to send the end user name. Sub means subject. To this subject, we're trying to send some user ID value and iat means issued at. What is the time at which that token is issued. Similarly, we can set some expiration time, all those details. The simple reasons I didn't mention all the possible values inside this payload. Once the payload is generated, the same will be encoded and this is how it is going to look like. So these encoded values like header and body payload, we are going to share to the UI application as well. Since this follows encoding process, the UI applications are the client applications they can simply run the decoding process and they can decode these values into plain text and they can easily understand what is the username, what is the mobile number and what is the expiration time, all such details they can easily understand. Now coming to the signature part, the signature part is going to be generated based upon the formula that we have discussed here. The header encoded value and payload encoded value with a period along with the secret will be used to generate a hash value. The same hash value will be present inside the signature part of the token. So if you see here, we're sharing everything to the client application as part of the Jwt token. We are sharing the header encoded value, payload encoded value along with the signature. This is what is going to happen during the login operation. Now let's try to understand how the validation on the backend is going to happen to identify whether the tampering of the token is done or not. First, let's imagine the happy flow with the end user or the client application they didn't tamper any of the content. They're trying to send whatever Jwt token that they have received for the next REST API invocations. In this scenario, what backend application it is going to do is it will take the encoded header and encoded payload inside the Jwt token, and it is going to run the same formula along with the secret that is known to only itself. Since the encoded header and encoded payload and secret, everything is same, the backend application, it is going to get the same hash value. If the hash values are matching, then the backend application is going to allow the end user to invoke the secure REST API. If the hash values are not matching, that means someone tampered the token. Under such scenarios, the not equal scenario will come into picture and the REST API invocation will be stopped. Let's assume one of the end user or the client application, they tampered the content inside the payload. Maybe instead of John Doe, they changed the name to Madan. After changing the name to Madan, they encoded the whole payload value and they're trying to send the same encoded value as part of the body payload. Let's try to understand how the backend application can detect this tampering scenario. Since the backend is going to use the same formula to calculate the new hash value. This time, since the payload content change, the hash value is going to be different compared to what it has inside the signature. Since the hash values are not matching, it will end up into the not equal scenario. Here you may have a question, which is what if the client application or the end user, they are changing the signature itself. They can change the signature, but the signature can't be a valid signature from the backend perspective because they don't know the secret. Since they don't know the secret, they can't generate a valid signature and they can't send the same inside the Jwt token. So changing the signature is not even an option to the client applications or to the end user because to generate a new signature, they need to know the secret. With this formula, they can generate the encoded value of header and payload. Since they can't guess the secret, they can never generate a valid signature. Do you see how powerful the Jwt tokens are? We are giving everything to the client applications like header, payload, and signature, but still the client applications, they can't tamper even one letter of the token. As soon as they tamper the token, the backend application is going to detect the concept of hashing that we discussed previously. I hope you're clear. If something is not clear, don't worry, we're going to see all these concepts inside the demos as well. The backend applications, they need to make sure that the secret is properly stored and it is not known to everyone. The secret also should not be a simple value like one, two, three, four, five, or some randomly guessed secrets. So usually the secrets that are going to be used by the backend application to generate the Jwt tokens, they are going to be super complex, like they're going to be some random string values with all type of characters. With this knowledge of Jwt token, let's try to generate a token from our backend application once the authentication of the end user is successful.
