54702737.title: Magic of Derived Queries - How Method Names Become Database Queries
54702737.section: Database Authentication & CSRF Protection with Spring Security
54702737.transcript: If you check the methods present inside the JpaRepository, you will be able to find some methods provided by the framework for various kind of CRUD operations. Save() is a method which we can use to create or update an existing record, and similarly, we have delete related methods, which are going to help us to delete all records or to delete a specific record or delete a record by using the primary key ID value. In the similar lines, we also have find related methods, which are going to help us to fetch all records from a table or to fetch a given record by using a primary key ID value. But what if we have a scenario where we want to fetch or update or delete a record based upon our own custom where condition. For example, inside my database table, I may have many columns. And if my requirement is to perform CRUD related operations by using any of these column names, then whatever methods that are provided by the JpaRepository or by the JPA framework, they are not going to be helpful for me. For example, inside the roles table, we have a name column. Instead of fetching the record by using the primary key ID value by using the findById I want to fetch the record by using the name column. For these kinds of requirements, obviously the default provided methods, they are not going to be helpful because the JPA framework, it can't guess our database columns and provide all the possible methods. It is always the developer responsibility to handle these kinds of custom requirements. With this, you may think Madan, that means do we need to write our own SQL query? Do we need to execute them? Do we need to write a lot of database related logic? This can be a worry, but we don't have to do that. We just have to define an abstract method by following a JPA standard. Let's try to understand what is this concept. There is a concept with the name Derived Query Methods inside the Spring Data JPA. When we define a method by using these Derived Query Methods, the JPA, it is going to auto generated queries based upon the method name. We don't have to write SQL queries manually to execute any of the CRUD operations based upon our customer requirements. We just have to define the abstract methods by using this concept where we have to define them inside the repository interfaces. As of now, the repository interfaces like JobRepository or JobPortalUserRepository, they are empty. They are simply extending the JpaRepository, but inside them, we have never written any logic. So from now onwards, whenever we have a custom requirement to perform the DB operations, we just have to define an abstract method and the name of that abstract method we need to build by using the Derived Query Methods concept. Let's take an example. I want to fetch the list of companies by using a status, inside the status column we may have various values like Active, Draft and Close. So the valid values for the status can be anything, but by using a given status value, I want to fetch list of company records. So for these kinds of requirements, we can't use findById method or findAll() methods that are provided by the Spring Data JPA. Instead, we should define an abstract method inside the CompanyRepository interface. The name of the method should start with the findBy whenever we are looking to read the queries or to select the records from the database, it has to start with the prefix as findBy and the B inside the by has to be capital followed by you can mention the field name that is mapped to the status DB column. If you have considered the Java field name as Status, the same you need to mention here with the S as capital. By looking at this abstract method name, the Spring Data JPA will execute a select query based upon the Status value that we are providing as an input to this method. Anyone who want to fetch the company records based upon the Status, they can simply invoke this method by using the CompanyRepository bean. And when invoking this method, they have to provide the Status value that they want to consider as part of the WHERE condition value. The return type from this method, we can define either optional company or optional of list of company based upon our requirements. Similarly, a derived query method inside the Spring Data JPA consists of two main components separated by the By keyword. So the first one is introducer class. So this class, it is going to specify what is the type of operation that we want to perform on the database, whether we want to perform select operation, update operation or delete operation. So that clue we are going to give to the Spring Data JPA by using these introducer class. Common introducers include find, read, query, count, get. These introducer class, they are going to help us in fetching the records. We can also include distinct to ensure unique results in the query. Next after the introducer class, we are going to have the By keyword and after this By keyword, we are going to have the criteria class. This part begins after the By keyword and defines conditions on entity properties or entity Java fields. Conditions can also be combined by using And,Or keyword. Just a few seconds back, we discussed about this method name findByStatus. So with this, the JPA, it will try to fetch a record by using the Status field. Instead of findByStatus, you can also use readByStatus, queryByStatus. So they're going to have the similar effect. In case if you are using the countByStatus, you are going to get the output as count of the records with a given status. Let me share more examples so that you will be comfortable with these derived query methods concept. In this slide, I have mentioned what are the common words that we use inside that Derived Query Methods. findBy we already discussed whenever we are using findBy, it is going to find the records by a field. So if you consider customers table and inside the customers table, if there is a department column, if I want to fetch all the customers for a department value, I just have to use findByDepartment and to this method, we need to pass what is that department value that needs to be considered inside the SQL query. So for this method behind the scenes, this is the SQL query that is going to be generated by the Spring Data JPA. By using countBy, we can count the records by a field. So here I have mentioned countByDepartment and for this, this is how the SQL statement that is going to be generated. Next by using the existBy, we can check if the record exists for example, here I have mentioned the name as existByName, but this kind of Derived Query Methods, this is the SQL query that is going to be generated. Basically you're going to get a Boolean value true or false as an output from this method. Next for that delete operation, you can use deleteBy or removeBy. For example, if I want to delete a record based upon a name field, then I can define the name of the method as deleteByName and with that, this is how the SQL query is going to be generated. Similarly, I can use removeBy age with this method the records will be deleted from the customers table based upon the given age. Majority of the times we are going to handle the select queries only by using the derived query methods. And very few times we are going to execute that delete queries as well by using these derived query methods. But coming to the update operation, we never have to define any of these abstract methods. We can simply invoke the save() method to the save() method if you pass the updated entity object, the framework is going to figure out what is the associated record based upon the primary key ID value inside the entity object. Once the record is identified, the framework is going to check if there is any mismatch between the entity object and the record object. If yes, then it is going to execute the update operation for the changed values. Inside this very first table, we have considered the scenarios where we are trying to execute the queries based upon a single column. But what if you have a requirement to execute a query by using multiple fields? As I said before, we should be able to do that very easily. We just have to separate the Java field names by using AND, OR logical operators. For example, here I have mentioned the name of the method as findBy name and age. Since we have mentioned two Java fields here, both of them has to be provided as an input to the method. This select query is going to be executed where inside the WHERE condition, it is going to consider both the name and age. In case if you are looking to consider only one of the column value, you just have to use the OR operator and this is how the SQL query is going to be generated. Under the next example, I have used findDistinctByNameAndAge. With this, what I'm telling to the framework, please fetch all the distinct customers with a given name and age value. Let's explore more examples. Sometimes we may have a requirement to fetch the records based upon the greater than, less than or any other criteria. Basically, we're not going to use the exact equal operator and a search scenarios, we can use any of these examples. For example, with the findByAgeGreater than we will fetch the records which are having a age value greater than given value for less than the less than operator is going to be used. So first you need to mention findBy what is a column name followed by LessThan or GreaterThan. We can also use between for the scenarios where we want to fetch the records for age between two values. For the between related methods, we need to pass two inputs. What is the start age and what is the end age? For the String columns or the string Java fields, we can use like operator as well. For example, inside the SQL, we can use a like operator where I can mention if the customer name like MADAN, then it is going to look for the customer names which are having this value in between. For such scenarios, I just have to define this method findByName like to this method. We need to mention what is the pattern of the String that you want to search. We also have NotLike, which is going to do the exact opposite operation of the Like. Next we also have StartingWith, EndingWith, Containing when you're using the StartingWith or EndingWith and Containing. This is how the SQL statements that are going to be generated coming for the sorting related operations, after mentioning the method name like findByDepartment, we can mention what are our sorting requirements. So for sorting inside the SQL statement, we use OrderBy. So that's why after the department, we need to mention OrderBy followed by what is the field name. The field name is Age. After the field name, I can mention ASC, ASC means ascending order. I want to fetch the customer records based upon a given department value OrderByAge in a ascending order. In case if I'm looking to fetch the records based upon the descending order, I just have to mention findByDepartment, OrderBy what is the field name that I want to construct this time I'm considering the name field and after the field, I have to mention Desc, Desc means descending order. I have more examples. Let me share them as well. If you're looking to fetch the limited number of results, you can use find first or find the top three. The method name I have mentioned it as findFirstByOrderByAge ascending order. So here I don't have any WHERE condition requirement. That's why after By, I have not mentioned any Java field name directly I have mentioned OrderByAge ascending. In case if I have a requirement to fetch the very first record based upon a given field name, after the By, you can mention the field name as well, followed by you can mention your ordering requirements. By using findTop3, I'm trying to fetch top three records. This is how the SQL statement is going to be generated behind the scenes. So similarly, I can fetch top 10 records, 7 records or 2 records. I just have to replace these 3 with required number. Coming to the null value examples, if you are looking for the records, which are having department name as null, you just have to first mention findBy what is the field name field name is department IsNull() with this, the JPA framework, it is not going to expect any value as an input to the method because it knows inside the WHERE condition it just have to look for the Null record. Just like IsNull() we also have IsNotNull() where it is going to look for the records, which does not have null values under the department. If you are working with that date columns, you can use something like findByStartDate after. If you consider there is a column with the name start date inside the database table for that, the associated field will be started. So first I'm trying to fetch the records based upon the start date followed by, I have mentioned the after inside the method name to this method name I need to mention a date value as an input. So since I have mentioned after here, inside the WHERE condition, the greater than symbol is going to be used by the framework. Just like after we can also use before in the case of before the less than symbol is going to be used. We can also check multiple values inside the WHERE condition. Usually inside the SQL query, we use IN keyword to check the multiple values at a time. For example, if I am looking to fetch all the customers, which are assigned to the department HR and IT for these kinds of requirements, I can simply use the method name as findByDepartmentIn. To this method, we can provide the list of values that we want to consider under the WHERE condition. For example, inside your database table, if there is a column which is only going to store true and false value under such scenarios, you can use these method names findByActiveTrue(). So there is a column with the name active inside the database. That's why I need to mention active here, followed by if I mentioned true, then it is going to look for the records which are having active as true. If I use false inside the where condition, false will be considered. In case if I'm looking to fetch the records by ignoring the case value, I just have to use these ignore case. Here the method name I have mentioned it as findByFirstName, followed by ignore case. If I simply use findByFirstName, then the Spring Data JPA will try to fetch the record which is matching with the exact name that I'm providing. But since we are using ignore case here, so this is the query that it is going to execute. Basically it will ignore the case value and fetch the corresponding records. This way I can give more and more number of examples as well, but since it is not possible to discuss each and every scenario, what I would recommend is, always whenever you have a custom requirement to fetch the records or delete the records or to perform any other operation, please check whether if you can achieve that by using that derived query methods, you can take help from ChatGpt or any other AI tool as well. You just have to give the input to that and ask it if it is possible to achieve that requirement by using the derived query method. It is perfectly possible to build very complex derived query method names as well. Here I try to mix multiple conditions together. So findByDepartmentAndAge. Basically I'm trying to fetch the records based upon the department and age value followed by GreaterThan while fetching for a given age I want to consider GreaterThan symbol for the age column I want to consider the GreaterThan instead of equal symbol and towards the end I'm mentioning OrderByName ascending. So once the record is fetched based upon the given department value and AgeGreaterThan value, I also want to sort the records based upon the name in an ascending order. This way we can define any number of complex requirements by using the derived query method. For this method, this is the SQL query that is going to be executed behind the scenes. In case if your requirement is too complex or if you don't want to define lengthy derived query method names, we also have an alternative approach inside the Spring DataJPA that approaches by using the @Query annotation. By using this @Query annotation, we can give our own custom queries directly to the Spring DataJPA so that whenever someone is executing a given method inside the repository, whatever query that we have provided to this annotation will be executed by the framework. For example, on top of this method name, I can simply mention @Query annotation and to this @Query annotation, I can provide this entire query as an input. When a @Query annotation is present on top of a method inside the repository interface, then the Spring DataJPA, it is not going to construct the method name. It is going to simply execute the query that you have provided on top of the method. We're going to discuss about @Query annotation in detail in the coming sections. For now, I'm assuming you are clear about Derived Query Methods. By using this concept, let's try to enhance our registration REST API.

54702741.title: Enforcing Strong Passwords with CompromisedPasswordChecker
54702741.section: Database Authentication & CSRF Protection with Spring Security
54702741.transcript: Inside enterprise applications, when an end user is trying to register a new account, we usually try to enforce some conditions on the password that the end user is choosing. Because we don't want our end users to choose weak passwords. Due to weak passwords, there is a very good chance that a given account may get hacked easily by the hackers because weak passwords can be easily guessed by the hackers. It is our responsibility as a backend developer as well, to stop the end users from choosing a weak password. Off course, on the UI side, inside the registration page, there will be enough validations added by the UI developers like the password should be of at least 8 characters and it should have a combination of small letter, capital letter, one special character and one digit. Even with these kind of enforcements, but still end users, they end up choosing some weak passwords. For example, they may choose a password like password@1. This is a strong password according to the UI validations. But do you think it is a strong password? Off course not. This can be easily guessed by the hackers. That's why I strongly recommend you adding some backend validation as well to enforce the end user from choosing a meaningful strong password. We as a spring boot developers, we don't have to do a lot of work around this. We just have to take help from the spring security framework. Inside the spring security, there is an interface with the name CompromisedPasswordChecker. Inside this interface, we have an abstract method with the name check. To this check() method, if you pass the password, it is going to give you a compromised password decision. Inside these, we are going to have a boolean value to identify whether a given password is already compromised inside the dark web. To this interface, spring security team, they also provided an implementation with the name HaveIBeenPwnedRestApiPasswordChecker. This class, basically what it is going to do is, it is going to rely on one of the best rest API available inside the web. So this is the rest API,so this rest API is developed by one of the organization. Their job is to continuously perform the analysis inside the web and identify any breaches happened along with the compromised passwords. If you click on this, Who'sBeenPwned, they'll show you all the breaches that happened inside the web, along with that date when it is happened, what is the name of the Breach? How many accounts have been compromised? So this gives a confirmation to us that this is a very good resource to rely on, to check whether a given password is already compromised in one of the breaches happened inside the web history. We as a job developer, we just have to create the bean of this class and use it during our registration flow. Let me go to the JobPortalSecurityConfig. Inside this, I need to create a bean. 54702753.title: Solution to CSRF attack - Theory
54702753.section: Database Authentication & CSRF Protection with Spring Security
54702753.transcript: The solution to the CSRF attack is very simple, but it is a tricky one you need to put some effort to understand this. I am there for you to help you and explain. I just need your attention here. To defeat a CSRF attack, the backend application or the web application needs a way to determine if the HTTP request is legitimately generated via the application's user interface. As of now, the CSRF attack is possible because the Netflix backend server, it is not able to differentiate whether the request came from the Netflix UI code or from the other website UI code. When I say UI code, it is a JavaScript code. For non-web applications, CSRF attack is not possible. For example, if you are building a microservice where a given service is being invoked by another backend microservice, then there is no possibility of CSRF attack because there is no browser involved, there is no cookie involved, and most importantly, there is no hacker involved. CSRF attack is only possible for the web applications which are having the web pages that built by using the JavaScript. The best way to defeat the CSRF attack is, by using a CSRF token. A CSRF token is a secure random token that can be used to prevent the CSRF attacks. This token needs to be unique per user session and should be of large random value to make it difficult to guess. Let's take the same Netflix flow one more time. And this time we're going to introduce CSRF token into the flow. Consider the same Friday scenario, you logged in into your Netflix website. This time, the Netflix backend server, it is going to provide a unique CSRF token along with any other cookies that it generates during the login operation. So this CSRF token also can be shared as a cookie, inside your browser tab right now, you're going to have two cookies. The first one is related to the login cookie, which is going to be used by the UI application to send for all the further requests that it is going to make. The other one is a CSRF token, which is saved inside the cookie. So both these cookies, they tagged to the domain Netflix.com. After one hour, you open the evil.com website and you saw this malicious link. You got tempted and you click on that link. This time also, the hacker is going to execute his malicious script by using the JavaScript, is going to invoke the action on the Netflix. And since the request is going to the Netflix.com domain, the browser, it is going to attach both the cookies, the cookie related to the login and the cookie related to the CSRF token. Even though both the cookies are sent as part of the request, still the Netflix backend server, it is going to reject the request with the 403or401 error. I know you have a million dollar question here. How come backend is able to recognize this is a fake request? Because the browser sent automatically both the cookies. Let me explain the same. Whenever CSRF solution is being implemented by the backend server, we know the backend server initially, it is going to generate the CSRF token and attach it as a cookie. From the next request onwards, the backend server, it is going to expect the CSRF token in two different places. The first one is as part of the cookie, and the second one is under the Request Header. In the hacker scenario, the hacker should be able to send the CSRF token as part of the cookie. And in fact, he's not sending the CSRF token value manually inside the cookie. It is the browser behavior that is sending the CSRF token automatically inside the cookie. But to send the same CSRF token value inside the request header, the client application code or the JavaScript code that is sending the request, it has to read the CSRF token value under this cookie. Once it reads the cookie value, then only it can send the same value inside the request header. So the JavaScript code that is deployed under the Netflix.com website, it should be able to read the cookie because the cookie belongs to the same domain, Netflix.com. But the JavaScript code that is deployed under the evil.com website, this JavaScript code, it cannot read this cookie value or the CSRF token value inside the cookie manually. Because this cookie belongs to the another domain. The browser is not going to allow to read the cookies by a JavaScript code, which belongs to another domain. If you take this script logic prepared by the hacker, though this script is sending the action to the Netflix.com domain. But this script is being executed from a domain evil.com because this web page is deployed under the evil.com domain. Whatever JavaScript code that hacker is going to define here, it is not capable of reading the cookie manually. And with that, it can't send the cookie value inside the Request Header. But the CSRF token value as part of the cookie, it is going to reach to the Netflix backend server, because the browser is going to attach the cookie automatically while sending the request to the Netflix.com. Since the hacker website is able to send the CSRF token value only as part of the cookie, but not as part of the Request Header, the backend application can easily detect these and stop the CSRF attack. I hope you are understanding this. If not, don't worry in the next lecture, I'm going to implement the solution and show you a demo as well. Inside the industry, this is the most common solution that is being used. Off course, there are other approaches as well, but since this is a common one, I'm discussing the same. The default behavior of the Spring security is, it is going to stop all Non-safe HTTP method requests. . What is the meaning of Non-safe HTTP methods? Any HTTP method that is capable of changing or creating or deleting the data on the backend. Using GET() method, we are only going to read the data. So this is a safe operation. The hacker should be able to only read the data, but not update the data. That's why for safer methods, CSRF protection is not going to be enforced by the Spring security. Whereas for the Non-safer methods, the CSRF protection is going to come into picture. That's why we are getting 401 error when we are invoking the register REST API, even though it is a public page. In case if you are building a low severity application or an internal application which can't be accessed from the public web, under such scenario, you don't have to worry about the CSRF solution or CSRF attack. You can simply ask the Spring security to disable the CSRF protection. How to do that? It's very easy. You have to invoke the csrf() method. To this method, you need to pass a lambda expression. So this lambda expression, by using the lambda input variable, you need to invoke that disable() method. But I don't recommend using this for any production application, for any critical application or real enterprise application, I always recommend you implementing a proper CSRF solution. I hope you have some clarity on what is the solution that we need to use for CSRF attack. In the next lecture, let's try to implement the same solution by using the Spring security framework.

54707557.title: Why Logging Matters - The Backbone of Debugging & Monitoring
54707557.section: Logging in Spring Boot – From Basics to Production-Ready Setup
54707557.transcript: We are making a very good progress in building Job portal backend application at regular intervals of time. . We're also testing our backend application by using this UI app as well. In the same process, we're also understanding how the end-to-end flow works in an typical enterprise application. We need to develop many more REST APIs that supports various functionalities of various roles that we have inside the job portal application. But we already have a good knowledge on how to build the REST APIs. That's why what I'll do is, before we try to build many other REST APIs, I will try to introduce many other important concepts related to the backend application, so that you also won't get bored with the same kind of REST API information. So far, we already explored few important concepts like auditing, global exception handling, backend validation, spring security. Now as part of this section, we are going to explore on how to perform logging by using the spring and spring boot framework. What is Logging? Let's try to understand the same. Logging is the process of recording important events that are happening inside an application. This logging process, it is going to help the developers in debugging their code, monitoring and troubleshooting any issues that they are identifying. Logs provide various information like errors, warnings, execution flows, and performance insights. So these are the key benefits of logging. But here you may have a question, why should I use an advanced concept like logging when I'm able to print my statement onto the console by using the System.out.println() from the code java library? It's a very good question? Let's try to understand the same. If you're trying to implement logging by using the system.out, it has some serious drawbacks. The first one is, it does not support log levels. For example, you can't differentiate various log messages like errors, warnings, information, debug. Always the System.out.println(), it is going to print each and every log message that we are providing with no log severity details. Next, by using the system.out.println(), we can only log the details onto the console, but not into any files. But in real enterprise production applications, we won't run our application using IDs like IntelliJ. That's why in such scenarios, console logging is not going to help us. Instead, we should do the logging into the files so that whenever an issue identified inside the production, the developers they can open these log files and try to understand what happened at a given time. System.out.println(), it also does not support any formatting of the logs. Always every minute details of your log message needs to be provided as an input to the system.out.println(). That's why using the system.out.println() is not a production recommended approach. In fact, inside real enterprise applications, no team lead or no code reviewer, they're not going to accept a code if some junior developer is using system.out.println() in his code. I hope you are convinced on why we should do the logging. Now let's try to understand how Spring Boot is going to help us in implementing logging inside a backend application. Behind the scenes, Spring Boot, it is going to use SLF4J. SLF4J means Simple Logging Packet for Java. By using this SLF4J, the Spring Boot is going to perform the logging. As a developer, we don't have to add any dependencies. It is going to work out of the box. We just have to develop the log statements inside our business logic. And with that, the log statement will start printing onto the console and onto the files based upon our configurations. So what is this SLF4J? This is a common logging interface that Spring Boot uses to write the logs without caring about which logging framework is being used behind the scenes. Inside Java ecosystem and Spring Boot ecosystem, we can implement logging by using various frameworks like Logback, Log4j2, and JavaUtilLogging. These are the frameworks or the libraries that are responsible to perform the actual logging. But since we have multiple options here, various developers or various projects, they will end up choosing any of these logging libraries. And all these libraries, they are going to have various syntaxes or various methods to implement the logging. Since it is going to create a problem in the future, when someone wants to migrate from one logging to other logging library, they may end up doing a lot of code changes. That's why what Spring Boot does behind the scenes is, it is going to use SLF4J, which is a generic logging API. So this is like an interface. It does not provide any implementation. By using the generic logging API, we should be able to implement logging mechanism. And by default, this SLF4J is going to use Logback as the default logging library. If you want, you can switch to any other libraries as well. The beauty of SLF4J is, regardless of which logging library you are trying to use behind the scenes, the way you write the logging related logic is not going to change. That's why switching to other logging libraries is not going to be a painful process. You just have to add the required dependencies on the pom.xml. So inside the SLF4J, F means Facade. So what is the meaning of Facade inside English? Facade means it's a wrapper or a front door of a building. When we are using this library, it provides one common logging API and internally it forwards the logs to the actual logging framework based upon the which dependency that we have inside our project. By default, SpringBoot is going to use Logback. This is the default log format that you see inside any SpringBoot application. When you start your application inside the console, you're going to see certain logs, and if you have not provided any logging related properties around the customization of the log format, this is the default one that the SpringBoot is going to use. First, it is going to print the date and time, followed by log level. Under the log level, we have various log severities that are supported by the framework. We are going to discuss about them in a few minutes. After the log level, it also displays what is a process ID that is processing your business logic followed by the separator. Next it is going to mention the application name. So this application name you can define by using a property called spring.application.name inside your application.properties. In case if you are also providing application group by using this property, that information is also going to be displayed after the application name. Next it is going to display what is a Thread name that is processing the current business logic. Here the Thread name is main. Next it is going to display any tracing related information by using the correlation ID. It is not shown inside the above console message because tracing and correlation ID are the advanced concepts which I discussed under my microservice course. For a normal monolithic backend application, you don't need to worry about the correlation ID. Next we are going to have the logger name. Under this, it is going to display the actual source class name often abbreviated. So this is my package along with the class name. Next after this class name, it is going to print the actual log message. So this is that default format, but we can customize this by using a property called logging.pattern.console. We are already using this property inside our application.properties file by using this pattern only right now we are printing the logs onto the console by using various colors, which is going to make our local development fun. Before we try to explore about this logging, we need to understand the logging levels that are supported by the Spring Boot framework. Whenever you are trying to log a statement by using the, any of the logging library, these libraries they are going to ask what is the log level or the severity of your logging message. We can use any of these log level. For example, I need to use the error whenever I want to highlight about any of the serious problems that I have inside my application. For example, whenever a runtime exception is happening or a transaction is being failed, these type of issues I need to highlight by using the error log level. By using these warning, I can highlight potential issues inside my logs. For example, if someone is using deprecated API or if a given transaction is resulting into high memory usage, anything that may result into an error, I can highlight it using wARN. WARN means warning. Next, if you try to reduce the severity level, we have other categories like INFO. By using this INFO category, we can print any general application information like Startup messages, business events that are occurring inside our application. After info, we have debug by using this debug, developers they can try to debug their code. For example, they can try to print out what are the variable values, what are the steps that are being executed inside a business logic at runtime. Since developers, they can't debug the code inside the production environment, what they usually do is, whenever they are facing a problem in a given method, they're going to put these debug statements so that they can see what is happening at runtime, what value is coming to a variable, which if conditions are being executed. So everything they can track by using these debug level. Next, we have very lowest severity level trace. By using this trace, we can try to log most detailed information, like which methods are being invoked, which if condition is being invoked. So this is the most detailed log level. Usually developers, they don't use this trace because too much of logging also is going to impact the performance of the backend application. I hope you are clear with this log level. I will share more details around this log level when we are seeing the demo as well. Now let's imagine the developer implemented logging by using these log levels. At some places, the developer might have used either warning or error, whereas inside the actual business logic, the developer might have used INFo, DEBUG, TRACE based upon their requirements. The beauty of these log level and the spring boot logging is, when we implemented logging inside a backend application, we can control what kind of logs should be printed onto the console or onto the file. How to control that? It's very easy. We just have to use a property by using that we need to configure a log level. For example, if I configure the log level as error, then inside my entire backend application, only the log message that are having the log severity with the error are going to be printed onto the console. Whereas if I configure the log level for my application to warning, then both the warning and error related messages, they're going to be logged. Anytime, if you are configuring a log level, all the higher log levels are also going to be constructed by the framework along with the given configure level. For example, for the trace, these are all the higher log severities with this configure log level inside an application, all the logs with the log levels like TRACE,DEBUG,INFO, and WARN and ERROR will be printed onto the console. By default spring boot, it has configured the log level as INFO. That means if you are not customized this log level inside your spring boot application, by default spring boot, it is going to print all the logs with the log level info, warning and error. And this is a decent default choice that I recommend for everyone as well, because if you configure TRACE or DEBUG, you will end up having too many logs and that will impact the performance of your application as well. You need to configure these log levels only when you are trying to debug an issue or identify a bug. So this is a quick introduction about the logging in spring boot. I know you may have too many questions. Don't worry when we implement these inside our backend application, most of your questions will be answered.

54707641.title: AOP Fundamentals - Aspect, Advice & Pointcut Explained Simply
54707641.section: Aspect-Oriented Programming in Spring - Concepts, Advices & Demos
54707641.transcript: What is Spring AOP? AOP is a programming paradigm that aims to increase modularity by allowing the separation of cross-cutting concerns. This AOP concept is not specific to Java ecosystem or Spring ecosystem. The same AOP programming paradigm you will also see in other languages as well. So what are cross-cutting concerns? These are the features required in different parts of an application, but they are not the primary business logic. For example, Logging, any Security validations, any Auditing that we want to perform, any Transaction Management, Caching and so on. None of them are actual business logic. Those are supporting components that are required to run an enterprise application. This kind of non-business logic, we call them as Cross-Cutting Concerns. So this AOP programming paradigm, what it recommends is, it recommends moving all these cross-cutting concerns into a separate location so that these non-business logic can be maintained in a single place. That's what the definition also conveys. It aims to increase the modularity. Modularity means you separate your logics based upon their use case. You're not going to club everything inside a Java method. Without AOP, we saw what is going to happen. The entire code related to logging, any security or transaction management, it is going to get repeatedly mixed with our core business methods. And with that, the primary business methods, they will become cluttered and harder to read or maintain. Apart from maintaining issues, in future, whenever we want to upgrade any of these cross-cutting concerns logic, we will end up revisiting all the Java methods inside an application if someone is not using AOP. Whereas with AOP, we can define all the cross-cutting concerns in a single reusable component or module called Aspect. That's why this name has been given inside the AOP, A means Aspect, Aspect Oriented Programming. Once all the cross-cutting concerns logic defined inside an Aspect, the Spring Framework or the Spring Boot Framework, it is going to view this logic into actual business methods at the specified points based upon our instructions. So this process is called as Weaving. We're going to discuss more details about this weaving in few minutes. Inside this slide, I have given a sample snippet on how a Java method is going to look like without AOP and with the Spring AOP. So if you take an Java method with the name transferMoney(), inside this method, the actual business logic may be of two lines. But apart from this main business logic, we may have to perform a lot of security checks, logging, transaction management. With all these cross-cutting related logic, the Java method will become big and maintenance is also going to be tough. Whereas with Spring AOP, we will just focus on the actual business logic. All the cross-cutting concerns will be moved to another component with the name Aspect. Before we try to implement this Spring AOP inside our backend application, we need to understand few concepts of AOP. Whenever we are trying to implement AOP programming inside a backend application, we need to focus on 3 W's. These 3 W's, they're going to guide you in moving your cross-cutting logic into an Aspect. The first W is What? So first you need to ask what code needs to be executed as part of an Aspect. So here, when I say code, it should be a cross-cutting logic, but not the actual business logic. The actual business logic should always stay inside the Java method. Any cross-cutting logic, which is common for majority of your Java methods, such kind of code you need to identify. So whatever code that you decided to implement by using AOP, that will become Aspect. Using this Aspect, we define what code or logic we want the Spring to execute whenever we call a specific Java method or any other group of methods. Once you thought about WHAT, you should also think about WHEN. So you have some logic to be executed, but you should also decide when that logic to be executed, whether you want that logic to be executed before the actual method invocation or after the method invocation. That when part inside the AOP terminology, we call it as Advice, WHEN method needs to be executed? By using this Advice, we are going to give instructions to the Spring framework, WHEN it has to need to execute the logic inside a given Aspect. For example, is it before or after the actual method call this is called an Advice. So here method refers to the Aspect method, whereas here the method refers to the actual method that you have inside your application, which has the business logic. So don't get confused between these two different methods. After WHAT and WHEN, you should also think about WHICH. So WHICH method needs to be intercepted? Inside your application, you may have thousands of Java methods, which are handling various business scenarios. You as a developer has to decide for which Java methods you want these Aspect to be executed. Whether you want to execute the Aspect logic for all the Java methods or for a given set of Java methods, that complete control of execution you will have as a developer, you just have to communicate your requirements to the Spring framework by following some syntax, which we are going to discuss in the coming lectures. So this which actual business logic methods you want to be intercepted by these Aspect logic. So when the interception is happening, you should also decide when that interception should happen, whether it should be before or after. So this which we call it as point cut. So Aspect defines what logic we want to execute. Advice defines when our logic has to be executed. And Point cut defines which Java methods needs to be intercepted to execute the Aspect logic. These are the three main jargons inside AOP. Apart from these three, we also have a couple of more jargons. There is a jargon with the name join point, which defines the event that triggers the execution of an Aspect. Inside Spring framework, this event is always a method call. It's very obvious, right, you want a given Aspect logic to be executed only when a given actual business logic method is getting invoked. So that triggering point in AOP language, we call it as Join Point. Inside Spring scenario, this Join Point is always a method call. Next we have Target object. This is the bean that declares the method or the point cut, which is intercepted by an Aspect. So target object is nothing but the bean or the class where your Java methods are defined. I know you may have some confusion with these jargons. Let me explain these jargons by taking an example. So this is that typical scenario of AOP implementation. Let's imagine there is a backend developer. The developer decided to implement AOP inside the project. The developer wants some logic to be executed before each execution of method saveContactMessage present inside the bean ContactController. So here some logic, which is a cross-cutting logic is called as Aspect. And the decision of executing this logic just before the execution of actual Java method is called as Advice. The execution is join point because this is the triggering point for entire AOP functionality. Whatever method that holds the actual business logic, which is the saveContactMessage is called as point cut. By using this point cut, we are going to answer which, which of the methods and this is what and this is when, and this is the triggering point and this is the target bean. Why this ContactController is called as target bean means inside this ContactController only we have the target method, which is saveContactMessage. Before invoking this target method, the magic of AOP is going to happen based upon the instructions that we provide. Here you may have a question? How is all this magic is possible? So there is a method saveContactMessage inside the ContactController. If someone is calling this method, do they have to invoke these method directly or do they have to invoke any other AOP logic? Let's try to answer the same. The entire AOP programming paradigm, it works based upon the magic of proxies. When we implement AOP inside our application using the spring framework, it is going to intercept each method call and apply the logic that we have defined inside the aspect. This process of inserting the aspect logic into the target object is called weaving. For the outsiders, they don't have to make any code changes on the client-side invocation. They can invoke the Java method just like a normal method invocation. But behind the scenes, Spring AOP, it is going to use the proxy design pattern. So there will be an original bean where we define our business logic. Let's imagine this bean name is some class name like ContactController. Now, when we implement AOP, a proxy object is also going to be developed by the Spring AOP. So with that, what is going to happen behind the scenes is, whenever someone is trying to invoke a method inside this bean, the spring does not going to give a direct reference to the original ContactController bean, instead it wraps the target bean with a proxy object. With that, whenever someone calling a method inside the controller, they will end up actually calling the method inside this proxy. So this proxy object, it is going to intercept the call, it is going to execute the aspect logic based upon the advice that we have provided. Once the aspect logic is executed, the same proxy object delegates the call to the actual method of the target object, which is the original bean. So this is how it is going to look like without Spring AOP implemented, when someone is invoking this method inside a bean with the name like VehicleController or any other class, the method invocation will happen directly, whereas with AOP, in between there'll be a proxy object stays between the client and this method. Once the aspect logic is executed, then this proxy object is going to forward the request to the actual business logic. I hope you are clear with this quick introduction around the ASPECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING. By using this ASPECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING, we can configure various advices. When I say advice, we can tell to the AOP when it has to execute the aspect logic, whether it has to execute before or after or so on. Let's try to understand more details around these Advices in the next lecture.

54707645.title: Mastering @Around Advice - Full Control Over Method Execution
54707645.section: Aspect-Oriented Programming in Spring - Concepts, Advices & Demos
54707645.transcript: While implementing AOP inside a Spring Boot backend application, we can use any of these Advice Types. There is an Advice with the name Before. We can configure this advice by using this @Before annotation. If you are using this annotation on top of the aspect logic that we are defining, then it is an indication to the Spring Framework, please invoke the aspect logic before the target method starts. Just opposite to before, we also have After. When we are using this advice type, it is an indication to the framework to invoke the aspect logic only once the target method finishes its execution. In this scenario, with the after advice, the aspect logic will be executed regardless of target method is successfully executed or not. In case if you want to execute your aspect logic only if the target method logic is executed successfully, then you need to use AfterReturning Advice with these advice type the aspect logic is going to be executed only after the target method returns successfully. No exception should happen, whereas with the AfterThrowing advice type, the aspect logic will be executed only if the target method throws an exception. So these are the Before and After. 54731845.title: The Other Side of REST – Understanding API Consumers
54731845.section: Calling External REST APIs – RestClient & HTTP Service Client
54731845.transcript: With the concepts that we have discussed so far, you will be able to develop REST APIs, which can be invoked by other developers or other client applications. But apart from producing or developing the REST API, sometimes in real enterprise applications, you will end up in a scenario where you have to consume or invoke other applications REST APIs. Let's imagine there is a third party application, which you want to integrate into your backend application. So the communication between your application and the third party application, it is going to happen over HTTP protocol using REST API. This time you are sitting on the client side or on the consumer side. So whoever is invoking the REST APIs, we call them as consumers and whoever is exposing the REST APIs to the other client applications, we call them as producers. So far inside this course, we are only on the side of producing the REST APIs, but for the very first time, we are also going to explore on how to play a role of consuming the REST APIs as well, because there is a clear difference on the approaches on how we produce the REST APIs and on how we consume the REST APIs. Since I don't have any third party REST API to invoke inside my job portal, what I'll do is, I will take some fake REST API that is available inside the internet. One such famous option is JSON Placeholder. Once you navigate to this website, jsonplaceholder.typecode.com, here there are enough details provided about the fake REST APIs that are being exposed by this website. So here they have given a URL for one of the REST API. Let me copy the same and open in a new tab. If I press enter, this is going to give me a fake response, something like this. Inside the response, I have an userId, Id, what is the title of the post and the body of the post. If I remove this one, and if I invoke Posts API, it is going to give me all the list of posts that are being maintained by this fake REST API. So very similarly, we can use many other REST API paths. For example, there is a fake REST API path to create a resource. As you can see, by sending our request to this path, by using HTTP post method, we should be able to create or save a post against a given user. Off course, this is all the fake REST APIs, whatever you are providing, they are not going to be stored anywhere, but you're going to get that feel of invoking a REST API. Once I request this process, these fake REST APIs, they will also provide you the fake response. Similarly, there is a process to update a existing resource by using the PUT method. We have PATCH REST API as well, where we can use the PATCH method and towards the end, we have a DELETE resource REST API as well, using which we can delete a post by using the DELETE HTTP method. When you are using DELETE as well, no resource is going to be deleted on the server side. They're just going to give you some fake response. As you can see, POST is one of the REST API path. On top of that, we can perform various operations. Very similarly, this website, it also supports other paths as well. For example, there is an albums related path, users related path and todos related path. Let me take one of these base URL. This time, instead of POST, I can mention todos, which is going to give me all the list of todos. Under each of the todo object, we have a user ID to whom that todo belongs to, what is the title of the todo, and there is a boolean confirming whether that todo task is completed or not. So what I'm going to do is, throughout this section, I will try to invoke these POST and TODOS related REST APIs. In the same process, I will explain you how to make the code changes when you are acting as a consumer or when you are trying to invoke other third-party REST APIs. To get started with the consuming of the REST APIs, you need to look for the dependency with the name HTTP client. And when you add this starter project inside your pom.xml, this starter project, it is going to give you a couple of options to invoke the REST APIs. The first one is, ReatClient and the other one is RestTemplate. These HTTP client starter projects you need to add when you are building traditional web applications, which are going to process the request synchronously by using servlet tech stack. Inside Spring ecosystem, we also have reactive tech stack as well. By using these reactive, we should be able to build the reactive web applications as well. Throughout this course, we are only building a traditional web application where we are trying to process the request synchronously. But anytime if you are into a scenario where you have to build some asynchronous applications by using reactive tech stack, then you need to consider adding these reactive HTTP client starter project. . So this starter project, it is going to provide you an API with the name webClient. By using this WebClient, you can invoke other party REST APIs asynchronously. When I say asynchronously, you're not going to wait for the response to come. Instead, you will continue with the other logic that you have inside your business code. Reactive stack is out of the scope of this course. That's why I'm not going to focus on the WebClient. If you're ever into a situation where you have to build some asynchronous REST APIs, please do consider enrolling into the course where the tutor is going to talk about Spring reactive tech stack. For our web application, we need to select these HTTP client starter project, followed by we can click on the explore button. This is the dependency that we need to add into our pom.xml. Here just after my web MVC dependency, I'm trying to add the new dependency. The starter project name is spring-boot-starter-rest-client. Let me sync the maven changes, which is going to take care of downloading all the libraries under the starter project. As I said before, this starter project, it is going to give you a couple of options to invoke the REST APIs. The first one is, by using the RestTemplate, but I don't recommend you using this RestTemplate, in fact, I don't even also recommend to learn about this RestTemplate, because this RestTemplate is deprecated right now inside the Spring ecosystem. And in the future versions, the Spring team, they are planning to delete everything around the RestTemplate API. Instead, the Spring team is recommending to use the RestClient. Here there is also a note, as of 6.1, RestClient offers a more modern API for synchronous HTTP access. For asynchronous and streaming scenarios, we need to consider WebClient, which is going to come as part of the reactive starter project. Both the RestTemplate and RestClient share the same infrastructure. So many improvements made therein are shared as well. However, RestClient is a focus for new higher level features. The Spring team itself, they are telling don't learn and don't use the RestTemplate because they are planning to delete this. That's why inside this course, we're going to explore RestClient. So RestClient is a interface by using this interface and its associated API classes, we should be able to invoke other third-party REST APIs, by using HTTP synchronous pattern. From the next lecture, let's start making some code changes to invoke the REST APIs by using this RestClient.

54743801.title: Deploying Spring Boot to AWS Using Elastic Beanstalk

Whenever you want to deploy an application into the cloud, the most basic way of doing this is, you have to take a cloud server. Cloud server is very similar to your local system, but this cloud server is going to attach to the internet and can be accessed through a IP address. Inside AWS, we can get these servers by using a product called EC2. EC2 means Elastic Compute. If you click on this link, here we have an option to launch an virtual server by using this launch instance. Once you have a cloud server, you're going to copy your code into the server and install all the required libraries like Maven, JDK. Once all the required dependencies are installed, you need to run some commands, which is going to start the deployment of your application. Once the application is started, you should also configure the required security groups to control the access of your application. Such kind of decisions you need to take and implement them by using security groups. You should also take care of creating a load balancer. The purpose of the load balancer is, in case if you are deploying your application over multiple servers to solve the humongous traffic that you're getting, in this kind of multiple instances scenario, there will be a load balancer sitting at the front of all your instances. Whatever traffic that is coming from the outside world will be processed by the load balancer. From the load balancer, the request will be forwarded to the one of your application instance. Inside this page, you need to click on this create environment option. I'm fine with this web server environment option. Under the application name, I'm going to give it as job portal. 54745511.title: Meet RestClient – The Modern Way to Call REST APIs
54745511.section: Calling External REST APIs – RestClient & HTTP Service Client
54745511.transcript: Let's imagine a scenario where from my job portal backend application, I need to invoke these third-party REST API to fetch all the todos. Apart from fetching all todos, I also want to perform other REST API operations as well, like posting a new todoo, updating an existing todo, deleting a todo and fetching a given todo. To perform all these operations, I'm going to build some logic inside our backend project. But before that, let me create the required Dtos for our communication. So there is a dto package here, I'm going to create a new Java class with the name TodoDto. So this class is going to be a record class. Inside this record class, I need to mention the field names. So these field names has to match exactly with the field names that I'm getting inside the response. So inside the response, under each todo object, we're going to get these fields like userId, id, title and completed. . Let me mention those four fields inside my record class. So these are the fields. Now I'll go to the main package and try to create a new sub package with the name client. Now since my backend application is trying to act as a client application to invoke other third-party REST APIs, I'm trying to give this name to this package. All the logic around the REST APIs invocation are consuming the REST APIs, all of that I'm going to define inside this client package. Let me create a new sub package with the name service. Inside this service package, I'm going to create a new Java class with the name RestClientTodoService. On top of this class, I'm going to mention @Service annotation followed by @RequiredArgsConstructor. Into this service class, I need to inject the RestClient as a dependency. That's why I'm trying to create a field of type RestClient with the field name as RestClient itself. But here, if you see, I'm getting an error because there is no bean of type RestClient found inside our backend project. At the end of the day, this RestClient is just an interface. It is our responsibility to create a bean of type RestClient so that that bean will be injected into our service class. While we are trying to create a bean of RestClient, we can provide all the basic details around the third-party REST API, like what is the base URL, what are the default headers that we want to send. What I'll do is, I'll try to write some logic inside the constructor of this class. The constructor is public RegisterRestClientTodoService To this constructor, I need to pass an object of RestClient.Builder because this builder object of RestClient, it is going to help me to build a bean of RestClient. Since I'm going to use constructor injection, I can get rid of this @RequiredArgsConstructor because I want to write my own logic inside this constructor. Let me type this.RestClient is equal to builder.build()Method. So this build() method present inside this RestClient.Builder, it is going to give me a bean of type RestClient. Apart from this simple logic, we can also provide some other details while we are trying to create a RestClient bean. So by using this builder() method, if you try to see the list of methods available, there is a method with the name baseUrl(). To this baseUrl, I will pass the base path of my third-party REST API, so that this baseUrl(), I don't have to repeat inside my business logic whenever I'm trying to invoke a third-party REST API. So in this scenario, what is the base path? Ignoring this todo's path, remaining all the prefix path is the base path. I'm mentioning the same here. Apart from providing the baseUrl(), we can also provide various other details like defaultHeader, defaultCookie. . For now, I'll try to provide a defaultHeader. The header name will be accept and the header value will be application slash json. I'm telling to the third-party vendor that I'm going to accept only the json as a response. This way we can define many other details like we can define a request interceptor which is going to intercept each and every request, and inside this interceptor, we can write some logic as well. So there is a method which is related to the API version inserter, feel free to explore these methods and use them inside your real enterprise applications based upon your business requirements. For now, I'm fine with this baseUrl and defaultHeader. With this code, we know a RestClient bin is going to be created. Wherever we're trying to use this RestClient bean, by default, the request will be forwarded to this baseUrl path and a header will be added to the request with the name Accept and with this value. . Now using this RestClient bean, I want to perform various REST API operations like GET,POST,PUT,DELETE. Here inside this class, I'm going to paste few methods. You can get these methods code from the GitHub repo. Let's try to understand the new code that I have pasted. First, I have created a constant with the name TODOS_API. Under this constant, I have mentioned the REST API path that is specific to the todos. So after this base path, this todos is going to be appended. So since there is a forward slash here, I can get rid of these forward slash. Now let's try to understand what do I have inside each of these methods. So there is a findAll() method, which is going to fetch all the todos from the third-party REST API. Since find operation is a read operation, we need to invoke the get() method by using the RestClient. . When we are invoking this get() method behind the scenes, the request will be forwarded by using HTTP GET() method, followed by I need to invoke the URI, and under this URI, I'm mentioning the URI path that I want to append to the base path. So this is the path which I'm mentioning under this constant, that's why I have mentioned the constant name here. This way you can add many other details. Like if you want, you can add some headers, you can also add some cookies. Once you populated all the requested details, you need to invoke the retrieve() method. So this is the method which is responsible to actually make a REST API call to the REST API path that you have configured. Once the API invocation is completed, we want to understand what is the status that we have received inside the response. For the same, we need to invoke this onStatus() method on top of the retrieve(). Here to this onStatus() method, I have passed a lambda expression, which is going to implement predicate functional interface. So here, first I'm checking whatever HTTP status code that I have received, is it related to the error? If yes, I want to throw this IllegalStateException saying that failed to fetch todos. Otherwise, this body method is going to be invoked by the framework. Since I know inside the response body, I'm going to receive the list of todos, I have mentioned the return type of this method as list of todo. Based upon this return type, the framework is going to understand how it has to read the response and convert the response from JSON to Java objects. I can also mention this list of todo as a input to these parameterized type reference. So here under these diamond brackets, I'm mentioning the same list of TodoDto, but here I'm getting a warning saying that explicit type argument can be replaced with the empty diamond operator, because we already given enough clues to the framework on how the response body has to be converted by mentioning the details under the method signature. That's why let's get rid of this list of TodoDto here. I hope you're clear on what this findAll() method is doing. In the similar lines, I have defined one more method with the name findById(). So this method, it is going to accept a ID as an input value. And based upon this todo ID value, it will try to fetch a single record of todo, that's why I have mentioned the return type of this method as TodoDto. This time also you have to invoke the same get() method because still we are trying to read a single record. And under these URI, we can mention the todos as a URI path. On top of that, we also want to send a path variable. So that's why first I'm defining a placeholder and under this placeholder, I want these ID value to be considered. 54745527.title: Introducing HTTP Service Client – Declarative REST Calls
54745527.section: Calling External REST APIs – RestClient & HTTP Service Client
54745527.transcript: Apart from using RestClient to consume the REST services inside Spring and Spring Boot, there is also a better and modern approach is available. The drawback with the RestClient is, the developer has to write each and every logic like invoking the get() method, uri() method, retrive() method, body() method. So whenever developer decides to invoke a REST API, he or she need to take care of writing all the logic around the consuming of the REST service. But with the modern approach that we are going to discuss inside this lecture, we just have to define how our HTTP request should look like. And with that, the framework is going to take care of generating all the invocation logic at the runtime based upon the inputs that we have provided. This is very similar to Spring Data JPA repositories. If you go to any of the Repository interfaces that we have, nowhere we have written any logic to execute the SQL statements. It is the Spring Data JPA framework that is taking care of executing all the logic behind the scenes. . In the very similar lines, we also have an option around the REST APIs invocation. This option name is Http service client. Let's try to build an example so that it is going to be very clear for you. So this time inside the service layer, I'm going to create a new interface. This time we just have to define abstract methods. That's why I'm creating a interface with the name TodoService. On top of this interface, I'm going to mention @HttpExchange. So this is the annotation that we need to use. To this annotation, I'm going to pass the url details of my REST API. Let me invoke url property and to this I'm passing the path as jsonplaceholder.typecode.com slash todos. Now I have to define various abstract methods representing on how my REST API has to be invoked and on how my REST API request is going to look like. Let me paste few lines of code so that we can discuss all of them one by one. All these are abstract methods. The first method name is findAll(). The method names can be anything. The return type of the method should match with the return type of the data that the REST API is going to return. We know findAll() is going to return list of TodoDto, the same I have mentioned here. On top of this method, I have mentioned the annotation @GetExchange. So this is very similar to @GetMapping. When we are trying to produce REST APIs, we need to use @GetMapping annotation. But since this time, since we are trying to consume the other REST APIs, we need to mention this @GetExchange annotation. To this annotation, we can pass the path as well. Instead of mentioning todos here, I can mention to this annotation as well. But I'm going to maintain the todos on top of the interface itself. With this, now my Spring framework, it knows what HTTP method it has to use when invoking this REST API. And since I'm not passing anything as an input, it is going to consume the REST API without passing any request data. Whatever response that my REST API is providing, the framework will try to understand the JSON structure and convert that into a list of TodoDTO. Coming to the next method, which is findById(), we know when we want to fetch a todo based upon a given ID value, we need to pass the ID. So that ID, I want to pass as a path variable. That's why after the @GetExchange, I have mentioned the path variable, and whoever is going to invoke this method, they are going to pass the ID value as an input. So whatever ID value that they are going to provide, that will be binded to this ID path variable. That's why I need to make sure here to mention this @PathVariable annotation. If you are not mentioning this annotation, then this input parameter is going to be treated as a normal method input parameter. So the binding of this input parameter will not happen to this @PathVariable. The same applies for request params as well. In case if you are looking to send some request params or query params while invoking the REST API, please make sure to accept those details as a method input parameters and you need to use @RequestParam annotation, just like how we are using @PathVariable annotation here. Next, I have created a create abstract method and this method is going to accept the object of TodoDto. Since I want to send this entire object under the request body, I need to use these @RequestBody annotation. . If you want to perform some validations, off course you can mention @Valid annotation as well. Since this REST API has to be invoked by using HTTP POST method, I need to make sure to mention the PostExchange method. The response that we are going to receive will be converted into this TodoDto object. Next I'm using PutExchange method because I'm trying to invoke and update REST API. And to this REST API, I need to pass PathVariable along with the UpdatedDto object. That's why I'm trying to accept both of them as an input parameters for this method. Next towards the end, I have created a abstract method, which is going to help me to delete a todo record on the server side. Since it is a delete operation, I need to use @DeleteExchange annotation. To this annotation, I'm passing the PathVariable as an input. So whoever is invoking this abstract method, they need to make sure to pass the ID as a method input value. . This way you can define any number of REST API related abstract methods and all these methods they need to match with your REST API request format and REST API response format along with the path as well. In case if you are looking to send some request Headers, you just have to use @RequestHeader annotation. . To this @RequestHeader annotation, we can provide a value, something like demo followed by I can accept the header value by using a variable. So whoever is trying to invoke this method, they need to pass the path variable along with the @RequestHeader. The framework will try to send all these header values as well inside the request. So as a developer, we don't have to do anything. We just have to define the contract on how our REST API request is going to look like. So once this interface is created, as a very next step, you need to create a configuration class. So let me create a new package with the name config. Inside this package, I'm going to create a new Java class with the name HttpServiceClientConfig. On top of this class, I'm going to mention @Configuration annotation followed by I need to mention and very important annotation with the name @ImportHttpServices. To this annotation, we can pass the package details where our HTTP service interfaces are defined. Otherwise, we can also pass the list of interface names as well by using the types here. To these types, I can pass the interface that we have created. The interface name is, TodoService.class. Based upon these configurations behind the scenes, the framework is going to create a bean of type TodoService and into this TodoService bean, a bean of type RestClient is going to be added as a dependency. Behind the scenes, this HttpServiceClient approach as well, it is going to use the RestClient, it's just that the developer don't have to write all the logic. The developer can simply define the abstract methods and the underlying framework, it is going to take care of generating the actual logic by using the RestClient. Now I can go to the Controller class, which is TodoController. This time, what I'll do is, I will try to inject the TodoService as a dependency. Using this TodoService, I can invoke all the abstract methods that we have defined. So what I can do is, I can comment the very first line and I will try to invoke TodoService.findAll(). Let me do the same for other methods as well. So in the place of restClientTodoService, I'm simply using TodoService. Let me do the same for post  REST API as well. I'm going to comment the very first line for your reference here instead of restClientTodoService. I'm going to use TodoService. For the @PutMapping as well, I'm going to do the very similar change, and towards the end, we can update this delete REST API as well. So let me comment the very first line and here I will invoke delete method by using the TodoService. . So let me save these changes do the build, once the build is completed, this time we should see the very similar behavior. First I'm trying to invoke the getAllTodos request, which is going to provide me all the list of todos, inside the response. Let me invoke the GetToDo as well. This is also working fine. Let's try to invoke CreateToDoDto. This time, if I click on the send button, I'm getting 403 forbidden, seems my CSRF token is expired. Let me get the CSRF token first. So this is the token. I'm going to use it under the RequestHeaders. If I click on the send button, I received 201 status. Similarly, you can try to test put and delete as well, they are also going to work without any issues. My recommendation is always to go with the HttpServiceClient approach. As you can see, the job of you is going to be very easy because the underlying framework is taking care of writing or building the actual REST API invocation logic. You can go with the RestClient approach only in the scenarios where you want to have full control on how you are invoking your REST API. Most of the scenarios should be achievable by using HttpServiceClient approach as well. But in case if there is any peculiar scenario or some rare scenario where you are missing some control on how the REST APIs are getting invoked, then you can happily adopt the RestClient approach.
