54745535.title: Organizing REST Calls with HTTP Service Groups
54745535.section: Calling External REST APIs – RestClient & HTTP Service Client
54745535.transcript: There is a advanced concept of grouping, when we are building multiple interfaces by using HTTP Service Client approach. To demo you the same, what I'll do is, I'll try to repeat this @ImportHttpServices annotation one more time. This time, I will try to separate these two interfaces into separate annotations. Once we define multiple @ImportHttpServices, we can give a group name for each of these configurations. I just have to invoke group annotation here. I'm going to give a name as todos for this group name, very similarly, I'm going to invoke group here as well. This time I will mention the group name as Post. In our scenario, I have only one interface around the todos logic, and similarly for Post, I have one interface. But in real enterprise applications, you can imagine that you're trying to invoke multiple third-party REST APIs. And these multiple third-party REST APIs may belong to multiple third parties. Under such scenarios, grouping your interfaces by using these approaches, they are going to be helpful for you. So once you define the group, you can further customize on how the REST API invocation is happening. To further customize on how the RestClient is being used behind the scenes by the framework, what we have to do is, we have to create a bean of type RestClientHttpServiceGroupConfigurer. So let me mention the method name as groupConfigurer() on top of this method, I'm going to mention @Bean annotation. From this method, I'm going to return a lambda expression. This lambda expression, it is going to accept a input variable of type RestClientHttpServiceGroupConfigurer. . So the variable name can be anything. So under this lambda expression, by using these groups, I will invoke the filterByName() method. To this method, I can pass one of the group name like todos followed by I need to invoke the forEachClient() method. Because under a group, we may have multiple clients. As of now, we have a single client. So whatever configurations that we're going to provide for these forEachClient(), they are going to be applicable for all the clients that we have assigned to the group todos. To this forEachClient(), again we have to pass a lambda expression. This time, the lambda expression, it is going to have two input parameters. The first one is group details followed by restClientBuilder. So to this, the parameter name, I'm going to mention it as REST ClientBuilder. If you see, this group variable is of type HTTP Service Group representing todos group. And this parameter restClientBuilder is of type RestClient.Builder using which a bean of type RestClient we can create. So let me use this restClientBuilder, and here I'm going to invoke the baseUrl() method to this baseUrl(), I can pass what is a baseUrl() that needs to be used when any client method is invoked that is assigned to this todos group. So if you go to this Todos service, we have mentioned this baseUrl() already on top of this annotation. But since anyway, we are trying to create the bean of RestClient by ourselves, mentioning this basePath here is completely optional. I'm going to get rid of this by commenting. Let me copy this path and mention it under this baseUrl() method. Next I can invoke the methods like defaultHeaders() or defaultHeader(), requestInterceptor(), anything that we are capable of invoking while creating the bean ourself manually, just like how I invoke to defaultHeader() here. All these kind of capabilities now will be available under the HttpServiceClient scenario as well. By using the defaultHeader(), I can pass a defaultHeader(), which is going to be applicable for all the requests that are going to be invoked by using the interfaces assigned to this group. In case if we are looking to pass some security details, as we discussed previously, which is how to invoke defaultHeaders() method. And to this method, we need to pass the lambda expression here, by using these httpHeaders, I can invoke methods like SetBasicAuth(). It is also possible to invoke the requestInterceptor(). To this method also, we can pass a lambda expression, something like this. This lambda expression, it is going to have request body and execution as method input parameters. So this request represents the whole request that you are trying to send to the third party. In case if you want to intercept that request and add some headers, or if you want to make any modifications, all those changes you can define inside the body of this lambda expression. And once you made required changes to your request and body, by using this execution.Execute() method, you need to forward the request so that the request will be forwarded to the third party REST APIs. Once you are satisfied with all the methods that you want to invoke while defining the bean of restClientBuilder, while you are trying to create the bean of RestClient towards the end, you just have to invoke the build() method. So whatever lambda expression that we are providing for these, forEachClient() method. So this is going to be executed for each of the interface that is assigned to the todos. 54752553.title: Why Externalizing Configuration Matters in Real Projects
54752553.section: Mastering Configuration & Profiles in Spring Boot
54752553.transcript: We started leveraging properties to control the behavior of our application. Now you're going to give clear instructions to the operations team to update so-and-so property to get so-and-so behavior. For example, if the operations team are looking to update the jobs ttl cache time from 10 minutes to 5 minutes, they just have to update these property with the value 5. With this kind of instructions, everyone is going to happy inside your team, both on the development side and on the operations team side. But after using this approach for a few months or maybe a couple of months, the operations team, they are going to come back and they may complain saying that I'm happy with whatever properties approach that you have used to control the behavior of the application. But there is a problem. Every time I want to provide a new property value, I need to go to the application.properties, identify the property, update the value, rebuild the application and deploy into a server. So this is a very lengthy process. And moreover, there are some scenarios where I want to maintain different value for different environments. For example, inside the dev environment, the jobs cache ttl time, I want to use it as 1 hour. Inside the QA environment, I want to use it for 30 minutes. Whereas inside the production environment, I want to set it for 5 minutes. In this kind of scenarios, the operations team, they are going to put a lot of efforts. Every time they are deploying the backend application into an environment, they need to update the value inside this properties file, rebuild the application followed by deployment. Instead of following this lengthy process, they are going to ask you, is there any better alternative? That's where you're going to realize that this is a very valid scenario and I need to solve this problem. Let's try to understand how to solve this problem. Spring boot allows us to externalize the configurations or properties so that the same code works in different environments by leveraging different property values. So what is externalizing? Externalizing is a concept by using which you're going to feed the property value from outside of your source code. That's why this name externalizing has been given to this concept. By default, when we are not following this externalizing properties concept, the spring boot framework is going to look for the properties and their values inside the application.properties file or application.yml file. In case if you are using @PropertySource annotation, it is going to look into the custom property file that you have configured. But we can change this default behavior. We can also inject the properties and their values by using various external options. So these are all the options that we can use. The very first basic option is defining the property and its value inside the application.properties file or application.yml file. Apart from directly defining inside your source code, you can also define the properties and their values by using operating system environment variables or Java system properties or JNDI attributes or ServletContext init params or ServletConfig init parameters or command line arguments. In case if the same property is defined at multiple locations, then the spring boot, it is going to follow an order of precedence. As a spring boot navigates down through these approaches, the priority is going to increase. That means the properties and their values defined inside these application.properties, they are going to have lowest precedence compared to environment variables option. In case if you have defined a property by using both these environment variables and the command line arguments, then the values that we have defined by using the command line arguments is going to be leveraged by the spring boot framework. Because command line arguments has highest precedence compared to environment variables. Please remember this list carefully. As you navigate towards the down, the precedence is going to increase. Developers, usually most of the times they're going to end up defining properties either inside application.properties file or by using environment variables or Java system properties or command line arguments. Other three properties, they are going to be used rarely. That's why we're not going to focus on these three properties inside this course. Instead, we'll try to explore all the remaining four approaches I'll also show you some demos as well. We already saw a good amount of examples by using application.properties approach. Let's imagine there is a property defined with the key as P1 and for this, a value V1 is assigned. If the same P1 is also being injected by using the environment variables, but this time with the value as V2, since the environment variables has the highest precedence, this V2 value will be constructed by the spring boot framework against the P1 property. This way, we're going to control the behavior of our spring boot application without requiring to update the source code. The operations team members, they don't have to update the property directly inside these file. Instead, they can inject the new property value by using any of these external options. This way, the same package or the same jar of the application can be deployed across all environments. And even with the same package, the behavior is going to be different because the properties and their values are being feeded by using these external options. For our demos regarding the externalizing properties, I'm going to construct this property with the name spring.jpa.show-sql. By using this property, we're telling to the spring data JPA framework, whether it has to log the SQL statements that it is executing on top of a database. By default, we're using the value as true. We can override this behavior by using an environment variable called SHOW_SQL. But since as of now, we don't have any environment variable with this name, always true is being constructed for this property. And with that, we're always seeing the SQL statements inside our console and log files. But we can't live with these default true value in higher environments like production or testing environment. Because logging each and every SQL statement onto the log file or onto the console, it is going to attract some performance problems. And moreover, inside the production or inside the QA environment, the volume of the request, they're going to be high. With a higher volume of request processing, if you start printing all the SQL statements, your log files will end up with 90% of the SQL statements only. And that's not a very good way of handling the logs in higher environments. That's why what I'm going to do is, I will try to show you various approaches on how to override these property value from true to false by using various externalizing approaches that we have discussed so far inside this lecture. The same kind of behavior is applicable for any of the properties that you are using inside an application. It can be related to your framework property, or it can be our own property as well. Here we have defined our own properties as well. By using this externalizing concept, we should be able to override any of the property value.

54752559.title: Environment Variables, JVM Args & CLI Args Explained
54752559.section: Mastering Configuration & Profiles in Spring Boot
54752559.transcript: Environment variables is a popular approach for external configurations because they work across all operating systems. Java library also they provide a flexibility to the developers to read the environment variables by using system.getenv() method. But when we are using spring boot, we don't have to rely on this code. We can directly inject the environment bean into the business code. By using that environment, we can invoke the getProperty() method and to that method we can pass a property key name. We all know that story. But when you are trying to inject a property along with its value by using environment variable option, then you need to follow a syntax. The syntax is inside your property key, all the letters, they need to be changed to uppercase and replace the dots(.) and hyphens or dashes(-) with the underscores(_). For example, we know spring boot expects a property with the name spring.jpa.show-sql to control the logging of the SQL statements. If you are looking to inject a value against to this property by using environment variable option, then this is how you need to create the environment variable. So all the letters has to be converted into uppercase and the dots has to be replaced with the underscore and any hyphen also should be replaced with underscore. Here you may have a question, why can't I inject directly with this format? We can't use this format because all the operating systems, they expect the environment variables in this format. When we are providing a property key by using these format behind the scenes, the spring boot it is going to understand that this fellow is trying to inject the property value by using environment variable approach. And with that behind the scenes, it is going to convert that environment variable into a spring boot property by converting into a format, which is spring boot can understand. So this automatic conversion is called relaxed binding inside the spring boot. These are the syntaxes that operations team members, they can use to inject a property and its value by using environment variable option. Usually the operations team members, whenever they want to deploy an application or whenever they want to start a backend application, they're going to run a command java-jar and to this command, they're going to provide the location where the backend jar is present. If they simply run this command, then the backend application, it is going to start by using the properties that we have defined inside the application.properties file. Since the operations team member, they're looking for an option to change the property values from outside of the code before running these commands they have to first mention env colon, and what is the environment variable name followed by the value that they want to inject. And towards the end, they need to mention semicolon in case if they want to mention multiple environment variables, they can again repeat these key name and value followed by semicolon, and towards the end, they need to use this command. So this is the syntax inside windows servers. In case the server is using Linux based operating system, then operations team members, they can use these command and syntax. The next popular approach to inject the property values is by using JVM system properties. Whenever we want to pass JVM properties, we need to use a syntax, which is first we need to mention hyphen D followed by what is a property key name along with the value. This time also the operations team members while using Java hyphen jar command, just after Java, they have to use these hyphen D followed by what is the property key name along with its value. This time the operations team member, they don't have to follow the environment variable syntax. They can directly mention the property key name, whatever the Spring Boot is expecting. There is no need of converting to uppercase letter and replacing the dot with underscore and so on. The next common approach is, by using command line arguments. While running these Java command to deploy the application, the operations team members, they can try to inject the property along with its value by using this syntax. First they need to mention two hyphens followed by what is a property key name followed by is equal to and the value of the property, for command line arguments as well we can directly mention the property key name. There is no need of converting into uppercase letters, by using any of these externalizing configurations approach, we should be able to inject a property and its value. When you are injecting a property, there are two possibilities. The first one is there might be no property inside your application.properties file. For example, by using the CLI arguments, if you are trying to inject a property with the key as P1 and value as V1, if there is no already property defined inside the application.properties with the same key, then spring boot framework will try to construct these property and value inside your backend application. The other possibility is, there might be a property with the same key as P1 with a value as V. In this kind of scenario, the spring boot framework will construct these V1 value as these externalizing approaches, they have highest precedence compared to the value that you have defined inside the application.properties file. In case if the same property is being defined with the different values by using all these approaches, then whatever value that you have defined by using command line arguments, it is going to be constructed by the application because command line arguments has highest precedence compared to environment variables and JVM system properties. This is the order of the precedence which we already discussed in the previous lecture. Let's see the demo of all these three approaches in the next lecture.

54752593.title: Controlling Bean Creation Using Profiles & Conditions
54752593.section: Mastering Configuration & Profiles in Spring Boot
54752593.transcript: As of now, inside our Spring Boot backend application, we have created many, many Spring beans by using stereotype annotations like @Component, @Service, @Controller. We also created few Beans by using @Bean annotation as well. But the problem with the Beans that we have created so far is, they are always going to be created. But sometimes we may have a requirement to conditionally create the Beans so that it can open new powerful options to the developers in executing their business logic. This is especially useful when we want to switch configurations, enable features for certain environments, avoiding unnecessary beans inside production. That's why inside this lecture, we are going to focus on understanding on how to conditionally create a Bean. The first and most basic approach is, by using an annotation with the name @ConditionalOnProperty. To this annotation, we can provide the property key and the value by using this name and having value. With this kind of configuration, what is going to happen is whatever bean that you are trying to create inside your method by using this @Bean annotation, this bean will only be created when a given property is present with the configured value. This feature is going to be super useful in the scenarios where you want to create the beans only when the given feature is enabled. We can also control the creation of the bean by using @Profile annotation. Just above the @Bean annotation, we can use @Profile annotation and to this annotation, we can provide the profile name like dev or ! of dev. So based upon the condition that we have given, the bean creation will be controlled by the Spring Boot framework. With this configuration, this data source bean will only be created when the dev profile is activated. We can also create a bean conditionally only if the bean does not already exist. For this, we have to use the annotation @ConditionalOnMissingBean. When we are using this annotation, the bean of this MyService will be created only if there is no bean already created for this class. The next approach is by using @ConditionalOnClass annotation. By using this annotation, we are giving instructions to the framework to create a bean only if a specific class exists in the class path. For example, here I'm telling to create the bean of this MyLibraryBean only if the class of some library is present inside the class path. When I say class paths, I'm referring to the dependent libraries that we use in a typical backend application. There are multiple benefits with the conditional Beans. Developers, they are going to have clean and flexible configurations. We can activate a feature only when needed, reduce memory and startup time, avoid duplicate beans. So whatever approaches that I have discussed so far, I have used the examples by using @Bean annotation, but the same approaches can be used with  stereotype annotations as well, like @Component, @Service. With those annotations as well, we can use these concepts that we have discussed so far. Let me show you a very interesting example where we can use this Conditional Bean Creation concept. Usually in a typical enterprise application, the QA team members, they always going to reach out to the developers saying that to give flexibility to them to log in into the application inside QA environment without any valid password. Because QA team members, they're going to test various scenarios with various username and passwords. Off course usernames, which are emails they can get from that database, but coming to the passwords, they can't remember or they can't make a note of all the plain passwords that they have used while creating an account. If they try to look for the plain password inside the database, since we are using hashing, they can't find any plain passwords inside the database tables. With that, they are forced to make a note of all the passwords that they have used during the account creation. Sometimes even QA team members and developers are going to get a defect saying that this bug is happening for this user inside the production. To simulate that defect inside QA or dev environment, usually what big enterprise applications they're going to do is, they will try to clone the case of the user or the data of the user to the lower environment. And during the clone process, all the data of that user will be copied to the lower environment so that the same scenario can be tested. But to test that scenario, they require the password of the user. Off course, they can't find the password of the user because a hash value is being maintained inside the database. So in this kind of scenarios also, they look for an option where they can log in into any account without a valid password. This is especially a mandatory feature for QA and development environments. So let's try to implement this feature by using conditional bean creation concept. Inside our application, first, you need to think about where we are performing the password checks inside our application. If you recall our spring security discussions, we have implemented authentication provider interface. As you can see, this is the bean that implements AuthenticationProvider. Inside this authenticate() method, once the user details are loaded based upon the email, we are checking whether the passwords are matching with the password provided by the end user with the password hash that we have inside the database system. If the passwords are matches, then only we are allowing the user to access the remaining pages by creating the object of UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken. Otherwise, we're simply throwing a bad credential exception. For example, here for the employer Sanjana, if I try to enter some invalid password with only five or six characters, I'm getting an error saying that invalid username or password. But this kind of strict behavior is fine for the production application, whereas for the lower environment like QA or Dev, we should give a flexibility to log in with any kind of password. That's why what I'll do is, on top of this class, where we are using a stereotype annotation @Component, I'm going to use @Profile annotation to this @Profile annotation, I will pass the prod profile name, which is prod with this, what is going to happen? The bean of this class, which I'm trying to create by using this @Component annotation will only be created when the prod profile is active. Even if you try to active multiple profiles in those multiple profiles, prod has to be there. With this, we are left with the question. What about the non-production environments for other environments I want to create another bean of same type where I can skip the password checks. What I can do is, I can simply copy these class again into the security package. This time, I'll mention the class name as JobPortalNonProdUsernamePasswordAuthenticationProvider. On top of this class, I'm going to mention ! of prod. That means if the current active profile is not a prod profile, then please create the bean of this class. Inside this bean logic, I will try to get rid of this password check. As long as a user record is present for a given email, I'm fine with that. And that's why I'm directly creating the object of these UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken without checking the passwords. Some QA people, they will get more greedy and they may ask you to allow the login even with the invalid username as well. That's where you need to apply some logical thinking and stop them. Just ask them, if you are allowing any user to log in with any username, how are you going to load the corresponding user profile data? For example, we have implemented various APIs that can be invoked by employer, job seeker or admins. 54752823.title: Understanding @ManyToMany Relationships in JPA
54752823.section: Building User Profiles & Job Actions with Spring Data JPA
54752823.transcript: Once the profile is updated by the end user, the user can go ahead and start applying for the job or save a job as well. Inside the home page, the end user can see all the listed jobs here, he can either open any of these jobs, which will take him to the job detail page. Inside this page, the user is going to have these two options. As a next enhancement, let's try to implement the required backend changes that supports saving or unsaving a job. The end user can save a job from the job detail page or if the end user is present on the jobs page here under the jobs list, we are giving an option to save or unsave the job. To implement the required backend changes, first we need to think about what are the database changes that we need to do to implement this feature. We already have users table and jobs table where we are storing users related information and jobs related information. So if you try to understand the relationship between these two tables, like users and jobs, it will be @ManyToMany relationship. Why? Beacuse a user can save multiple jobs and similarly a single job might have saved by multiple users. Since there is a chance of having multiple relationship from both sides, in this scenario, we need to make sure to use the @ManyToMany JPA relationship out of all the four relationships that we have inside JPA, @ManyToMany is a bit complex one. That's why inside this lecture, I'm going to spend a few minutes explaining you about what is @ManyToMany, how to implement that by using Spring Data JPA, all those details I'm going to explain in detail inside this lecture. As I said before, we need to use @ManyToMany relationship when both sides of the relationships have multiple references to each other. We already saw an example inside our application, a user can mark multiple jobs as favorites and a job can be favorited by multiple users. This is how the  @ManyToMany relationship is going to look like. A user can favorite or can save multiple jobs, vice versa also possible, a job can be marked as favorites by multiple users. I also gave a very common layman scenario as well. Imagine a music streaming app, a single song can be part of many different playlists and a single playlist obviously can contain many different songs. So this both ways relationship can be implemented by using  @ManyToMany relationship. While implementing  @ManyToMany relationship, we need a third table. We already have users table and jobs table, but to store the  @ManyToMany relationship details around these two tables, we need an intermediate table. So this intermediate table, in other words, we can call it as a third table or joint table. Here you may have a question? Why do we need these joint table or middle table? Can't we reuse both these tables to establish the @ManyToMany  relationship? That's a great question. Let me try to answer the same. Let's imagine this table is not present inside your database schema. If a user has multiple favorite jobs, all those multiple job details, where are you going to store? Let's imagine you want to store inside the jobs table itself. Let's imagine a user with the ID 12 saved a job with ID 1 and ID 2. So against these job ID 1 and 2 records, you should also store the user ID detail as well like 12 and 12 here. In future, if some other user like 13 want to save the same job like 1 and 2, then again, you need to make duplicate entries inside the jobs table with the new user ID as 13 and 13. The problem with this approach is, inside all the duplicate rows, the details around the jobs will be same. The only detail that is changing between these duplicate records is the user ID information. The same also happens if you try to implement the saving mechanism in a vice versa manner. For example, if you are trying to store the favorite job ID details inside the users table itself, then you will end up duplicating the same user record again and again, just to store the favorite job ID details. In real enterprise applications, typically tables like users and jobs, they're going to have a good amount of columns like 50 columns, 60 columns, and even 100 columns as well. Just to store the job ID or user ID values, you will end up duplicating the entire record, which has a very similar data under all columns, except one column, which is either user ID or job ID. And that is going to bring a lot of drawbacks into your database. The main problem is you're going to unnecessarily duplicate the number of rows, which will increase the size of your database storage. So that's why to avoid these main problem, when you are trying to implement  @ManyToMany relationship, having a middle table or joint table is mandatory or recommended by the JPA team. As a developer, we have to create a joint table like saved_jobs. Inside this joint table, we should only have two columns. One is representing the user_id, the other one representing job_id. So both these columns, they are going to have foreign key link with the primary key columns of the jobs and users table. When a user trying to save multiple jobs, the user_id is going to be repeated. And for these user_id rows, the corresponding job IDs also will be maintained inside this joint table. So the beauty of the joint table is we end up only maintaining the data strictly related to the relationship. So when a client application as a backend application about to give me the saved jobs by the user ID 12, then the job of the Spring Data JPA is very easy. You just have to come to this table and look for the records with the user ID 12, and against to this 12, the job ID one and two are there. Based upon these job IDs, the actual jobs data will be fetched from the jobs table, and these jobs data will be provided to the client application. The vice versa scenario is also possible. Let's imagine a client application is asking the backend server, give me the list of users who saved a given job. In this scenario, the JPA is going to look for the records with the job ID as one against to these one job ID records, two user IDs are present like 12 and 13. These user details will be fetched from the database and both these two user details will be provided to the client application. I hope you are clear on why this joint table is required while implementing  @ManyToMany relationship. Now let's try to answer a question that you may have, which is, why can't we use @OneToMany relationship? . @OneToMany means a job is marked to favorite to only one user. And that is not going to make sense from our application perspective. That's why in these kinds of scenarios, @OneToMany or  @ManyToOne  is not going to make sense. You should strictly use @ManyToMany relationship, which allows sharing the jobs across multiple users. So this is how the database design is going to look like. There'll be a users table, jobs table and saved_jobs table, which is going to act as a joint table. This joint table connect with both of the main tables by using the foreign keys. When implementing  @ManyToMany relationship, please make sure to use a set, not a List while you're trying to create a field inside an entity class. The reason is very obvious. Using a set, we can avoid duplicate entries. For example, we know a single user can save multiple jobs. So to avoid the scenarios of loading the same job again and again into the entity field, if we define it as a set, the underlying collection data type is going to take care of avoiding duplicate entries. . Now coming to the bridge table, for this bridge table or join table, we don't have to create any Java entity class. JPA handles insertion of data into these join table based upon the @ManyToMany configurations that we define inside the users and jobs entity classes. When defining these @ManyToMany relationship, don't forget the inverse side mapping as well. Always use mappedBy on one side to ensure we have a bidirectional relationship that stays in sync on both entities. Let me show you some code snippets on how to perform these @ManyToMany relationship. To implement these relationship, we need to use two main annotations. The first one is @ManyToMany, and the other one is @JoinTable. So this @JoinTable is specific to @ManyToMany, we can't use this annotation with any of the other JPA relationship. Inside our backend application, the users table is being represented by the job portal user entity class. So this entity class is going to act as a Owning side. Why user is a Owning side? Because it controls insert or delete in saved jobs. So at the end of the day, inside our backend application, the saving of the jobs is going to be initiated by the end user. And since the end user is being represented by using the job portal user, we have to make sure that this is the Owning side in @ManyToMany relationship. So inside the entity class, which is acting as a Owning side, we need to make these configurations. First I need to create a field of type set of job. So the other side entity class is job. So here I'm using set instead of list to avoid the duplicate entries. Please make sure you are initializing the set by using these new HashSet so that for the JPA framework, it is going to be super easy to add or remove the entries from the saved jobs object. On top of this field, first we need to mention @ManyToMany annotation followed by, since this is a owning side, we should use a @JoinTable annotation to this annotation, first we need to provide the name of the join table. So here I have mentioned the table name as saved_ jobs. Apart from the table name, we should also provide the join table column details as well. First by using the joinColumns attribute or property, we need to tell what is the column inside the join table that is going to have a foreign key reference with the job portal user. So the column name is user_id, the same I'm trying to provide, but while we are providing this column name, we need to use again an annotation with the name @JoinColumn. So for this annotation output, we need to assign to the joinColumns. As a nextstep, we should also provide inverseJoinColumns as well. By using these inverseJoin columns, we need to convey what is the column name, which is going to have the foreign key relationship with the jobs table. So that column name is job_id. So here also we need to use the same annotation, the output from this annotation, we need to assign to the inverseJoinColumns. So under the joinColumns, we need to mention the column name, which is maintaining the foreign key relationship with the Owning entity, whereas by using the inverseJoinColumns, we need to mention the column name, which is maintaining the foreign key relationship with the other entity table. So these are the configurations that we need to do on the owning entity side. Coming to the inverse side, which is the job entity class inside this class also, we need to create a field of type of set of job portal user. On top of this field, we need to use @ManyToMany annotation. This time here, we need to use mappedBy attribute to this mappedBy, we need to provide the same field name that we have defined inside the Owning entity, the field name we have defined is saved jobs. The same I'm trying to mention here. After making all these configurations in order to perform various CRUD operations, we need to use this kind of code. Once the user record is fetched from the database for a given user ID, by using that user object, you just have to invoke that getSavedJobs() method, which is going to give you the list of savedJobs object, and inside that set, we need to add a brand new job details. That's why we need to invoke the add() method on top of the set object. Once adding the job, we just have to invoke the save() method, and to this save() method, we need to pass the user entity object. So this line is going to take care of making an entry into the join table. So behind the scenes, when this line is being executed, the Hibernate, it is going to execute this SQL statement. So the user ID information is going to be present inside the user object and job ID details will be present inside these job object. In case if the user is trying to unsave a job, then in that scenario, we just have to invoke the remove() method on top of the saved jobs set object. To this remove() method, we need to pass what is a job that we want to remove from the join table. So when this save is getting executed, the Hibernate, it is going to execute this SQL statement to delete an entry from the join table based upon the given user_id and job_id. So getting saved jobs for a given user is going to be very easy. We just have to fetch the user record based upon a given email. By using these user object, when we invoke that getsavedjobs method, the JPA and the Hibernate, they are going to provide as set of jobs that are associated for a given user. So here the default fetch type will be lazy, which is good. So that's why when this first line is executed, the saved jobs will not be fetched. Only when the developer is asking by invoking these getsaved jobs at this point of time only, the framework is going to look for the saved job records for a given user. As a important bestpractice, please make sure you are avoiding CascadeType.All when you are using @ManyToMany annotation. Because if you are deleting a user that could delete all the associated jobs as well, which is very dangerous in a typical enterprise application. At the end, I want to highlight that we can use these @ManyToMany relationship only when the join table has no extra columns other than two columns representing the pure relationship between the two entities. In case if there is a scenario where inside the join table, if you want to store some extra columns data, then using many to many is not recommended. We're going to discuss that scenario as well in the coming sections. For now, let's focus on the functionality of implementing saved jobs inside our job portal application.

54752851.title: When NOT to Use @ManyToMany in JPA
54752851.section: Building User Profiles & Job Actions with Spring Data JPA
54752851.transcript: Apart from saving a job, the end user can also apply for a job. When a user applied to a job, his or her application details will reach to the employer for their review. So that the employer can work on the next action. If you go to any of the job, like here, if I open the job, here there is an option to apply. As soon as I click on this, a pop-up is going to open to enter the cover letter. So the end user has an option to provide a brief cover letter using which he can introduce himself and explain why he is a great fit for this position. But this cover letter is an optional without cover letter also the end user can still submit the application. . To support the submission of the application, we need to develop required REST APIs on the backend side. So first we should build three different REST APIs. The first one that supports submitting an application. The second one that allow the end user to withdraw the application in case if we don't want to continue further, you can simply withdraw the application. The last API is going to help the end user to see what are the list of jobs that he has applied under this page. Before building REST APIs, let me first create a supporting database table. So this time I'm trying to create a database table with the name job_applications. Under this table, I'm going to have all these columns like id, which is going to act as a primary key followed by user_id and job_id, which are going to have foreign key reference with the users table and jobs table. Next we have applied_at column. Under this column, we're going to store the date and timestamp values based upon the time an user is applying for a job. Next we have status column. Under this column, by default, we're going to store pending as a status. So when an end user is applying for a job, the initial status is going to be pending. This status is going to be keep on updating based upon the actions that are being performed by the employer. When an employer reviewed this application and mark for the next phase, the status is going to update it to REVIEWED,followedby SHORTLISTED, INTERVIEWED, OFFERED,REJECTED,WITHDRAWN. So withdrawn is a status which is going to be updated when the end user himself is withdrawing the application. . So this status is visible both for the end user and to the employer. After the status, we have the cover letter. So this cover letter column is an optional column. Since under this, we are going to store a good amount of text data, I'm trying to use text data type here. Next we have notes column as well. And these notes column will be used by the employer for the internal notes. After the notes, we have audit related columns and foreign key related configurations. I'm also trying to create a unique key. The combination of user_id and job_id is going to act as a unique key inside this table. Let me create this table. Once that table is created, next we need to understand what kind of JPA mapping that we can use for this scenario. Here also you may be thinking to use @ManyToMany because a user can apply for multiple jobs and vice versa is also possible. A job might get applied by multiple users. This is a classical scenario of @ManyToMany, but here we can't go with the @ManyToMany because this join table, which is job_applications under these, we are storing a lot of extra information like applied_at status, ID, cover_letter, notes, and audit related columns. So in these kinds of scenarios, as I said before, it is not possible to use @ManyToMany. Let's try to understand why it is not possible. Whenever we are using @ManyToMany configurations internally, the JPA is going to think that the join table exists only to connect to user and job table. So JPA assumes the table has only two columns, user_id, job_id. There is no business meaning, no life cycle of its own for this table. Moreover, we're not going to create any entity class for the join table, but we can't use these kinds of configurations when we have a join table with the extra attributes. As of now, under our join table, we have many other extra columns. When these kinds of extra attributes are present inside your table and these kinds of scenarios, you need to ask yourself a question, where did these columns like status will go? Which entity is going to own it when these columns should get updated? Since there is no place in @ManyToMany to map status notes and other columns, it is not recommended to use @ManyToMany configurations. So here I have given a tabular explanation on which scenarios we need to go for @ManyToMany. So if the join table has a entity class, then we can't use @ManyToMany. We need to go with the other relationships like @OneToMany and @ManyToOne. If there are extra columns, we can't go with them @ManyToMany. If there are some business logic related columns, then it is not possible to handle those columns with the @ManyToMany, auditing related columns are also not possible to get maintained by using @ManyToMany life cycle control is also not possible when we are using @ManyToMany. If you closely look at these explanation, the whole problem on why these are all not possible with the @ManyToMany is, when we are using @ManyToMany, the join table is not going to have any entity class. If there is no entity class, then there is no concept of having extra columns, business logic, auditing, and any other life cycle controls. With this, now you may have a question? If we can't use @ManyToMany, then how to handle this scenario? Let me explain the same. First you need to create an entity class representing your join table, which is job_applications. Inside this entity are going to have all other extra attribute columns. But if you look at the configurations that we need to do on top of the user and job field, these are the configurations. Basically we have to use @ManyToOne JPA relationship because many job application records belongs to a single user. And in the similar lines, many of the job application records, they belongs to a single job. Since the join table is going to have the foreign key columns, we should also make sure to use @JoinColumn annotation. And to this annotation, we need to mention what is the exact column name. We already know about the purpose of these @OnDelete annotation. Long back we discussed about this. Now coming to the configurations that we have to do inside the respective entity classes like Job and JobPortalUser in those entity classes. We need to define the bidirectional mapping configurations, here, since we are using @ManyToOne, then definitely on the other side, the mapping should be @OneToMany. And to this annotation, we need to use mapped by along with the field name that we have defined inside the job application entity class. The same kind of configuration we have to do inside the JobPortalUser entity class as well. We need to make sure to create these fields by using set, whereas inside the job application entity, since they are going to be mapped to a single record here, we have to create the fields as a normal fields. We don't have to use any collection data types. After making all these configurations, saving, deleting, reading, updating the records is going to be very easy. For example, if a user is applying for a job inside the service layer, you have to write this kind of logic. First, you have to create a brand new object of job application into this object, you need to assign user object, job object, followed by you can populate other columns like status. Towards the end, you need to make sure you are invoking the save method by using the jobApplicationRepository interface. In case if the end user is trying to withdraw the application, we just have to define a derived query method inside the Repository interface with the name like deleteByUserIdAndJobId. And that is going to take care of deleting the record inside the join table. Coming to the fetching of the applied jobs for a given user, first, you need to fetch the user record based upon the email. By using this user object, we just have to invoke the getJobApplications, which is going to give us a set object on top of that object, we need to invoke the stream() followed by map(), and towards the end, I'm trying to transfer that data from job application to into a Dto class. And finally, I'm going to return all the collected Dto objects as an output from my service layer. I hope you're clear. If something is not clear, don't worry. When we are seeing the demo of this code, it is going to be more clear for you. Before I try to close this lecture, let me complete that JPA related configurations. First, what I'll do is, I'll try to create a brand new entity for these join table with the name job_applications. This is the class name. I'm going to have BaseEntity as a parent. If you scroll down for the user_id and the job_id, the JPA buddy, it is already recommending to use @ManyToOne. We are fine with these default recommendations. 54753139.title: Spring Caching Essentials - @Cacheable, @CachePut & @CacheEvict
54753139.section: Making APIs Faster with Spring Cache – Concepts to Practice
54753139.transcript: Welcome to new section. Inside the previous section, we built required back-end REST APIs for the admin user, we have tested all these pages, right now, inside this section, we are going to focus on the employer related functionality. You know, inside our application, we have these three different roles, there is a user of type employer. If you try to log in with her credentials, you will be able to see the menu is going to change for the employer role. Basically, the employer can see all the job postings by her company. and if needed, the employer can post a new job requirement as well. By the end of this section, we're going to build all this functionality, but before we try to focus on these regular back-end REST APIs development, first let's try to learn a new concept inside this section. The new concept that we're going to focus is Caching. As a back-end developer, you should also be comfortable on how to implement caching inside the back-end business logic. What is caching? Caching means storing frequently used data inside the memory so that the next time the application can reuse the data instead of recalculating or refetching it from the database. By adopting the caching, we can improve the performance of an application to a great extent. Off course, we can't use caching for each and every scenario. You need to be very cautious on implementing this caching for a given scenario. There are certain requirements that you need to be aware whenever you want to implement caching. I'm going to explain that in a few minutes. First, let me answer a question that you may have, which is how come caching is going to improve the performance of the application? For that, I'm going to take a scenario, let's imagine there is a back-end database and inside this database, we have various kinds of data. And usually this database will be deployed in a separate server location. And your back-end application is going to be deployed in some other server. These two servers, one is which is having your application code and the other one, which is having database installed,they both can be present at two different countries or two different continents or within the same city. So regardless of where the servers are deployed, in a typical scenario, when a client application is making a request to the back-end REST API, this back-end REST API is going to rely on the database to fetch the required data. So to fetch that data, the back-end REST API, it needs to make a call to the database server, and this call has to travel over the network. So that's why whenever you want to perform database operation or any complex operation, usually they're going to take a few milliseconds of time. So it can be 50 millisecond or a hundred millisecond. As the amount of traffic that your application is handling is going to increase, the dependency on the database server is also going to increase. And with that, there is a very good chance the performance of your application may get reduced, that's why we need to rely on the caching wherever applicable. In the scenario of caching, what is going to happen is, only during the first request that data will be fetched from the database followed by the data will be stored inside the memory. When I say memory, this memory is RAM memory. So there are famous products around cache like Redis. What all these products they will do is, they will store that data inside the RAM memory so that when the next request comes, the data will be fetched from the cache instead of the database. Reading the data from the memory is going to take only microseconds. Whereas with the database scenario, it is going to take milliseconds of time. So there will be thousands of times of improvement because when we are trying to fetch the data from the database, the request has to travel through the network and the database server has to read the data from the disk. At the end of the day, all these databases, they are going to store the data inside the disk space or inside the file system. So reading the data from the disk space is more costlier compared to reading the data from the RAM memory location. I hope you are convinced with all this discussion on why caching is faster compared to traditional database calls. Inside Spring Boot, implementing this caching is very easy. We just have to learn about few annotations and with that, everything, the framework is going to take care. The caller or the client application, they will never know whether the data came from the database or from the cache. By this time, it might be very clear for you on why we need caching. If not, let me repeat all these advantages. So caching, it is going to improve the performance by avoiding repeated database or API calls. With the caching, we can provide faster response times for the client applications. So caching is also going to reduce the load on backend because it is going to get rid of unnecessary database queries, which will bring very less stress on the microservices or on to the database servers. Caching is also going to improve the user experience because on the UI side, the pages are going to load faster since the backend SDPs are going to respond quicker. Enabling caching in Spring Boot is very simple. We just have to add this starter project inside the pom.xml followed by you need to mention this @EnableCaching annotation on top of any configuration class. These are the three common caching annotations that are used by developers on their day-to-day work. The first one is @Cacheable. By using this annotation, we're telling to the framework, please read that data from the cache if it is present,otherwise you can fetch from the database. For the first time, the framework is going to save that data into the cache memory so that all future calls or future requests can be provided with the cached data. Next, we have @CachePut annotation. We're going to use this annotation to tell to the framework that whenever a given method or a given logic is being executed, please update my cache records. In the similar lines, we have @CacheEvict as well. By using this annotation, we're going to tell to the framework, remove entry from the cache or reset the cache whenever a method that is annotated with this annotation is being executed. @Cacheable annotation, it is going to support POST and GET operation. When I say POST, it is going to help us in saving the data into the cache and reading the data from the cache. Where as @CachePut is like PUT operation or PATCH operation using which we are going to update the cache. @CacheEvict is like DELETE operation. It is going to help us in removing that data from the cache or resetting the entire cache. We're going to see the demos around this annotation in the next lecture. At that time, it is going to be very clear for you. As I said before, we should not try to use caching at each and every place. There will be very limited scenarios inside your backend application where you can implement this caching. Let me provide you some examples to explain about these caching related scenarios. Let's take a scenario, there is a method which is going to have complex logic. Let's imagine the logic inside this method is going to take 100 milliseconds of time to complete the execution. Here, I have taken the method name as computePiDecimal(). And to this method, we're providing precision value. So we know by default, the PiDecimal value is 3.14. So this is with the 2 precision value. But let's imagine you have a scenario where you want to calculate pi value for the 10 precision. In this scenario, the method logic, definitely it is going to take more time because it has to execute a complex operation. So every time this method is getting executed with the 10 precision, it is going to take 100 milliseconds. But executing the same method with the same input every time, it is going to bring some performance related issue, that's why on top of this method, I can give the @Cacheable annotation and provide the name to the cache. So here I have provided the name as pi decimals. Inside the memories, all these data, they're going to be stored by using map structure. There'll be a key and there'll be a value. So usually whatever method input parameter that you're providing, that will be stored as a key and whatever cache data that you are storing by using this annotation, that will be stored on the value. So let's imagine during the very first request that this method is being handled, the precision is provided as 10. So there'll be a cache entry will be made with the 10 and whatever this method is returning as an output that will be stored under the value and in future, if someone is calling with the precision as 20, then there'll be a separate key value will be maintained and the output will be maintained against to that key. So this entire maps data, they are going to be stored with the name as piDecimals. Next time, if someone is executing the same method with the precision as 10 or 20, the framework is going to check if there is any key matching with the 10 or 20. If yes, then it is going to simply return the value present inside the map without executing this business logic. And that is going to save a hundred milliseconds of time on your backend side. Let's take another example where we're trying to fetch static database results. Let's imagine there are some records inside a database table and whatever data that we're trying to fetch in this scenario, it is going to remain always or most of the time. That means the data inside the database table, it is not going to change very frequently. For example, in our application, we have a roles table. Inside this roles table, we are having three records like ROLE_JOB_SEEKER,ROLE_EMPLOYER,ROLE_ADMIN. We know these data is not going to be changed. It is always going to be static, even if it is changing, maybe once in a week or once in a month, a new record will arrive into these roles. Under these kinds of scenarios also we can leverage caching. So whenever someone is invoking our derived query method inside the repository, like find role by name to fetch the role record based upon a given role name, we know a database query has to fire to fetch the role entity object. But if we use the @Cacheable annotation on top of this method, then that database records, they're going to be stored inside the cache so that whenever someone is trying to query the same database table, instead of firing that database query, the framework is going to return the role entity object from the cache data. Here I have given the name to my cache as roles. Let's imagine for the first time someone is calling this method with the role name as ROLE_JOB_SEEKER. So this will act as a key and whatever role entity record that this method returns, that will be stored under the value. So in future, if someone calls the same method, the framework is going to return the entity record present inside the cache instead of making the database call. So these are the scenarios that you need to consider to implement the caching. Inside the next slide, I have given some examples on how to use these annotations. We already saw the example of @Cacheable. Now let's imagine there might be a scenario where the data inside the database record might get updated. For example, if we are exposing a save method inside our RoleRepository interface, which is going to update an existing role record, then it will make the cache data as invalid data because that is outdated data. So that's why on top of this method, we can use @CachePut annotation, and to this annotation, we need to provide value as roles, which is our cache name coming to the key, the key value should be role.name. So inside these role object, we are going to have name field, which we used as a key when we are creating this cache data very first time. So if someone is trying to update the role record object for a role like role job seeker, then this annotation, it is going to take care of updating that specific cache data for this key with the latest data. In the similar line, just like how updating is possible, deleting of the data from the database is also possible. For example, here there is a method called deleteRoleByName(). So there is a very good chance that someone can invoke this method and delete a role record from the database entirely. In this scenario, we have to use a @CacheEvict annotation. To this annotation, we need to pass the cache name, which is roles. And the key is whatever this method receives as an input. So whenever the record from that database is getting deleted, the same record against to this key value will be deleted from the cache data as well. That means both key and value entry will be deleted from these roles cache. In case, if someone is trying to delete the entire data inside the database table by using methods like deleteAll(), in this scenario also, we have to use the @CacheEvict, but this time we have to use a property called all entries with the value true. By using this property, we are telling to the framework, I'm deleting the entire data inside the database table,please make sure you are also resetting my entire cache with the name roles. So these are the tips that you need to follow for effective caching. Always use cacheable on read-only or static lookup methods. Coming to the cache boot, you need to use this annotation. Whenever updating records is happening by using this annotation, you will make sure to sync the cache data with the DB data. @CacheEvict we need to use when deleting the data. Don't try to use this @Cacheable annotation on top of the write or update operations.

54753149.section: Making APIs Faster with Spring Cache – Concepts to Practice
54753149.transcript: To get started with the caching inside our Spring Boot backend application, we need to add a new starter project as a dependency. You need to select these and click on explore. Inside the pom.xml file, you need to look for this dependency with the artifact id as spring-boot-starter-cache. Let me copy this dependency and paste inside my pom.xml. Since we are into the new section, I have copied the previous section code into this new section folder. You can find all the code that we are going to discuss inside this section under this section folder. Let me add the new dependency followed by I am going to click on this sync maven changes. First, let me show you the caching by using the default cache mechanism inside the spring boot application. . By default, the spring boot application, it will try to store this cache data into the memory of the application. But for the advanced use cases, we need to rely on the products like Redis, Caffeine. So there are a good amount of cache related products, regardless of which underlying product that you are trying to use, the way you use the @Caching annotations is always going to be the same. First inside this lecture, let's see the demo by using that default behavior. As of now, I have not added any caching product related dependency into our backend application. With these, the spring boot application will try to store the cache data inside the memory of the application. So whenever you restart your application, the cache data will get wiped off. Let's try to identify a few of the scenarios where we can use the caching. First, I'll go to the controller with the name, AuthController inside this AuthController, we know whenever someone is trying to register for the very first time by invoking this register related API, first what we are going to do is inside this business logic, we are going to give that new user a default role of job seeker. To assign this role to the registered user, we are invoking this method, findRoleByName() which is present inside the RoleRepository interface. But we know inside the roles table, the data is not going to change frequently. We are going to always have these three records only inside these database table. So that's why what I'll do is, on top of these method, I'm going to mention @Cacheable annotation with the name as roles. So please make sure you are importing the @Cacheable annotation from the org.springframework.cache.annotation package. If this changes, what is going to happen is, whenever this method is being invoked for a given role, at that point of time, a database query is going to be invoked, whereas for any other request, which is invoking the same method with the same name value, the data will be fetched from the cache data. This is one scenario where I want to use caching. Now let's go to the another controller with the name CompanyController. Inside this method, we have a rest mapping, which is going to be invoked by the admin user to fetch all the company details from the database. So company's data also is a kind of static data. They are not going to change very frequently. We're not going to onboard a new company very frequently, whereas the jobs data is very dynamic because the whole purpose of job portal is to create the new jobs, update them, delete them based upon the employer requirements. That's why caching these company's data for admin user will be a good idea. Let me navigate to the method present inside the service class here, on top of this service method, I'm going to mention @Cacheable with the name as companies. So for this method, since we are not providing any input parameters, so behind the scenes, what framework will do is, it will try to store the company's related cache data with the key as empty value. So the key will be like empty value against to that empty value, this entire list of company DTO will be stored as a value. Always the method input parameters that are going to be considered by the framework to create the keys for the cache data. I can show you all that code as well. If you go to the framework class with the name ConcurrentMapCacheManager. So by default, when we are not using any of the cache related products inside our application, I said that the cache data will be stored inside the memory of the application. So this is that class which is responsible to store the cache data inside a ConcurrentMap. Inside this class, you will see all the methods which are responsible for the caching related logic. So here there is a method with the name getCache(). So to this getCache() method, if you pass the cache name, it is going to provide you the cache. The cache data is represented by this cache interface. If you go to this cache here, you're going to have all the methods related to fetching the data and putting the cache data into this cache object. Coming to the cache key strategy, there is a class with the name SimpleKeyGenerator inside the framework. So this class is going to be used by the framework to generate the key for your cache data. So there is a generate() method, which is going to accept the target object method name and the parameters. So based upon the parameters, the key is going to be generated. here, they are again internally invoking this generateKey() method. To this generateKey() method, the params are going to be passed as an input. If the method input params length is zero, this generateKey() method is going to return an empty key. In case if the params length is one, here it is for checking if it is not an array, it is going to simply return the param name as an return value. Otherwise, all the param details will be passed to this simple key constructor. So this constructor will be invoked if the number of parameters are greater than one or if the single parameter is of type array. If you open this SimpleKey constructor, here there is again logic to calculate the hash value based upon the number of input parameters. So this is all the internal logic that framework is going to leverage to calculate the key value for the cache data that you are trying to store. So this is all internals. In case if you want to understand in depth, you can try to debug all this code after implementing the caching related logic. So to this method, since there is no method input parameter, this company's data will be stored against to an empty key. Under the RoleRepository, since here we are passing a single input parameter to the method, this input parameter value will be considered as a key when this role entity object is being stored into the cache data. After mentioning this @Cacheable annotation at the required methods, we should also mention another main annotation to enable the caching inside our Spring Boot backend application. I'll go to our main class, which is JobPortalApplication. On top of these class, we need to mention an annotation, @EnableCaching. So this is very important, otherwise the caching is not going to work inside Spring Boot application. After making all these changes, we need to restart our backend application because we have added a brand new dependency into our application. So now to show you that demo of caching inside JobPortal UI application, I'll try to login as an admin. You can try to test this scenario from Postman as well. So under the admin menu, we have a option with the name company management. So when I click on this link, we know behind the scenes, the companies will be loaded from the database and displayed to the admin. Since we are doing this very first time after restarting our application, there won't be any company's cache data. Inside the console log, this time we are able to see the select statement because this time, since the cache is invalidated as part of the company creation, the company's data is refreshed again from the database. Let me click and inside the console logs, this time I'm able to see there is an SQL statement is present because during the delete operation, the cache data is invalidated and with that, the data will be fetched again from the database. .
