This gives a confidence to us, whatever changes that we have done around the cache evict inside the company repository interface, they are working fine. The @CachePut annotation also, it is going to work in a very similar manner. Just that in this scenario, we have not used @CachePut because we didn't save the cache data by providing key. That's why we have to invalidate the entire cache by using @CacheEvict annotation. Before closing this lecture, let me share more details on how Spring decides the cache key. Under the case one, I have taken an example where a method has a single input parameter. With this kind of method, if you're trying to use @Cacheable annotation, as I highlighted, whatever method input data that you're trying to provide, that will be considered as a key. For example, if someone is invoking this method by using ID as 1, then the key will be 1. And for this key, whatever method result will be stored. So this can be some user1, and similarly, if someone is providing key as 2, then for that key 2, a different user two will be stored inside the cache. In future, if someone is calling the same method with one of these keys, the data will be fetched from the cache, otherwise a new cache entry will be made based upon the new key value. Coming to the scenario where method has multiple parameters, it can be two or three or four or any number of parameters. So in this scenario, the cache key will be combination of all parameters. For example here, based upon the email and the role that I'm providing behind the scenes, the framework is going to calculate the hash value and that hash value will be treated as a cache key value. Now let's see a scenario where a method has no parameters. So we already discussed about this. If a method is not accepting any input parameter, then behind the scenes, Spring is going to use SimpleKey.Empty as a key. So there'll be only one cache entry and the method executes only once subsequent calls always use the same cache. As I said before, behind the scenes, the Spring framework, it is going to use SimpleKeyGenerator to calculate the cache key. The thumb rule is very simple. 0 params means empty key will be used. 1 param means the param value itself. In the scenario of multiple params, all the param values will be used to calculate the hash value and that hash value will become the cache key value. Now there might be some scenarios where you may want to provide your own custom cache key. Under these scenarios, you need to use the key attribute against to the @Cacheable annotation. For example, here, I'm providing to construct this ID value as a key, which is the default behavior. Even if I don't provide this, anyway, the framework is going to construct this as a cache key. But if you consider a scenario like where an user object is being provided as an input to this method. Under these kinds of scenarios, it is always advisable to define your own custom cache key. So here by using the key property, I'm providing a key value by mentioning this hash user.email. So this is the Spring Expression language. When you are mentioning hash user, we are telling to the framework to refer to these user attribute and insert this user object. We are going to have a email field, that email field we want to consider as a cache key. So these are the other common examples that you can use when you are using key attribute. So why custom key matters? Defining the custom key is a must when your method is accepting objects as an input parameter. So in this scenario, if you're not using key attribute to the @Cacheable annotation, then these user objects may be treated as differently because every user object, they're going to have different hash codes, even though the content inside the user object is same, but still two different user objects, they're going to be considered as different because their hash code is different. That's why in this kind of scenarios, the cache is not going to work as expected. The recommendation approach is, always to use the key attribute to define your own custom key, and by using this key attributes, you can provide any property value of the user object to consider as a cache key. So these are the common mistakes that developers usually do when implementing caching, please try to avoid them. Similarly, don't try to use the entire object as a cache key, and finally, don't forget that same method with different params is equal to different keys.

54753165.title: Preventing Stale Data with TTL-Based Caching
54753165.section: Making APIs Faster with Spring Cache – Concepts to Practice
54753165.transcript: As of now, inside the RoleRepository interface, on top of this method, we are using this @Cacheable annotation. For the roles cache data, nowhere we have used @Cache put or @CacheEvict annotation because there are no scenarios inside our backend application where the roles data may get updated or may get created. So with that, we are going to have a disadvantage, which is these roles cache data, it is never going to be refreshed. Let's imagine if there is a scenario where the application server is not restarted for a month or for two months. And with that, these roles cache data, it is going to stay inside the memory of the server for more than a month or two months. This is usually a not acceptable approach in an enterprise application because in real applications, there'll be some hundreds of scenarios where these roles data may get externally by using database scripts or by some other applications. That's why as a best practice, whenever we are trying to create a cache, we should also configure an expiration time or an refresh time. For example, if I configure 10 minutes as an expiration time, after 10 minutes, the cache will get automatically invalidated by the framework so that after the 10 minutes, the very first request that comes to the backend application will fetch from the database and the cache will be refreshed with the latest data. To implement all these kinds of advanced use cases, we can't rely on the default in-memory cache that is provided by the ConcurrentMapCacheManager. Instead, we need to rely on a good caching related product. If you go to the SpringBoot official documentation here, you can search for Cache and there is a dedicated section with the name Caching. So this is present under the SpringBoot reference IO caching. Inside this page, if you try to read the documentation, the SpringBoot caching mechanism, it supports all these cache providers. So these are all the famous cache providers inside the industry like Redis, Couchbase, Infispan, Hazelcast, Caffeine and so on. Inside real enterprise application, the developer has to develop the caching mechanism by using any of these providers. Most of the enterprise applications, they rely on Redis or Hazelcast, Couchbase, Infispan or Caffeine. Inside our course, I'm going to use this Caffeine option. The reason why I'm using this CaffeineCache provider is, it does not require any separate installations. Whereas if you are going with other providers like Redis, it expects you to set up a Redis inside your local system. Just like how we set up MySQL database, in the similar style, we need to install and set up Redis as well. Since I'm trying to avoid all those operational stuff, I'm trying to use this Caffeine option. Let's try to understand how to use this Caffeine as a cache provider inside our SpringBoot application. As I said before, we should always try to implement caching with some expiration time or refresh time. So this concept is called as TTL,so TTL means Time To Live. In other words, this time to live is nothing but expiration time to our caching. So to control the expiration, we need a real cache provider like Caffeine or Redis, because the default in-memory cache does not support TTL. To use Caffeine cache provider, which is a lightweight and in-memory fast provider, we have to add this dependency inside our pom.xml. Caffeine is a high-performance in-memory caching library, which is developed by the makers of Guava library. This is designed as a modern replacement for concurrent HashMap or EhCache provider. Caffeine is ideal for caching read-heavy, frequently accessed data scenarios. Once we add this dependency, as a next step, we need to define a Bean of type CacheManager. When we are creating this Bean with the help of a Java method, we need to write this kind of logic. First, by using the constructor of the Caffeine cache class, I need to provide a cache name followed by what is the expiration time. So here I'm providing 10 minutes as an expiration time. So this expiration time, I can set by invoking expireAfterWrite() method, which is available under this Caffeine.newBuilder(). So with these configurations, I'm telling to the framework, once the cache is returned, please set the expiration as 10 minutes. And by using maximum size, I'm telling to the framework, under these companies cache, you can only store up to 500 different key and values data. And towards the end, we need to invoke the build() method. So this is going to give me an object of type CaffeineCache for the same I have provided this name. In the similar styles, I need to define all the caches that I want to create inside my application. Under the second configurations, I'm trying to create another cache with the name jobs using these jobs cache, I'm going to store all the jobs related data, which is going to be automatically refreshed after 10 minutes with the maximum size as 5000 records. And towards the end, I'm trying to create the roles related cache as well, for the roles, I have set that expiration time as one day. Since inside my application, roles are going to be updated very rarely, that's why I have increased the expiration time to one day, so this expiration time can be completely modified based upon your business requirements. You can also define a cache for a single minute or 30 seconds 20 seconds based upon your business requirements. Once all the required cache objects are created, these objects we need to provide as an input to the set caches method. This set caches method can be invoked on top of the SimpleCacheManager object. So first I have created SimpleCacheManager object by using this new operator followed by I'm trying to invoke the set caches method to this set caches method, I'm passing all my cache related objects as an array. And towards the end, we need to return this manager, which is of type cache manager. So all these code we need to write inside a configuration file, so once we have written these configurations, this is how the framework is going to create your caches behind the scenes. So these are the cache names with their TTL duration and with their maximum entries. It is also possible to define these cache configurations by using properties as well. For example, by using spring.cache.type, I can mention to the framework, what is the cache provider that right now I'm trying to use. So here I have set the caffeine followed by I can create any number of caches by using the spring.cache.cache names. Here I have provided the cache names like jobs, comma roles. If I want to create many more caches, all of them, I need to mention by using comma as a separator. For all these caches, we can provide specifications by using spring.cache.caffeine.spec. To this, I'm providing maximum size as 5000, expire after access as 600 seconds. So all these specifications has to be separated by using comma as a separator. But there is a disadvantage with these properties, whatever specifications that you provide, they are going to be applicable for all your caches. Whereas with this approach, you're going to have an opportunity to define different configurations for different caches. As part of the next lecture, let's try to update our caching mechanism by using CaffeineCacheProvider.

54753667.title: @Transactional Demystified – How Spring Really Manages Transactions
54753667.section: Mastering Transactions in Spring Data JPA – From Basics to Real Pro
54753667.transcript: When an admin user logged in into our JobPortal application, under this menu, there is an option with the name contact messages, which will take him to this page. Inside this page, the admin has an option to close an existing new message. We already saw the demo of this in the previous section. But the reason that I'm trying to show you one more time is, whatever code that we have written to handle this updating of the message to close is not up to the best standard. For example, as soon as the admin click on this close button, this REST API inside the ContactController is going to be invoked. From this controller, we are invoking this method inside the ContactServiceImpl. So here what we are doing, based upon the ID value of the record, first we are trying to fetch the record, followed by we are updating the status by invoking this contact.setStatus() and towards the end, we are providing this updated contact entity object to the save method. And this save method is going to take care of updating the record with the latest status value. But there is a problem with this approach. The problem is, first we are trying to execute the SELECT query followed by here at this point of time, an UPDATE query is going to be executed to just update a column value we are executing unnecessarily two SQL queries. The ideal way or the best approach is, to update the status column by using single UPDATE SQL statement. That's why as a next step, we are going to focus on resolving this issue inside this method. But before that, we need to understand few of the new concepts inside the Spring Data JPA framework. As of now, whenever we are interacting with the database from our Java code, we didn't consider one of the important aspect, which is transactions. Let's forget about our job portal for a few seconds. Imagine we are trying to transfer money from a given saving accounts to a checking account in a bank. And we're trying to achieve these by using a Java code. So whenever we are performing this action, we don't want half the process to happen, like deducting the amount from the saving account, but forgetting to add to checking account, which is going to create some confusion to the end user. So that's where transactions come into picture. They are like all or nothing promise. If we are doing this transaction, when we are performing the transferring of the money, it should be either both these operations are successful or there should not be any changes to both these accounts. So inside database also, a transaction ensures a group of operations like read and writes, either all succeed or all fail together, which is going to keep our data consistent and reliable. This follows ACID principles. ACID principles are the famous database concepts that each and every backend developer should be aware. So what is this ACID? ACID is a shortcut of Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. Let's discuss about them one by one. Atomicity means all or nothing, either you should be executing all the operations or you should not be executing any of the operation. Coming to the consistency, it says rules has to be followed when we are updating the data inside the database. For example, if you take an age column under this age column, whenever we are trying to update some data, it should not have any negative value. Like I should not update the age value to -5 or  -10, which is not valid. Similarly, databases they are going to follow many rules by using unique columns, foreign key columns. All these rules comes under these consistency, because with the help of these rules, the data is going to be maintained in a well consistent manner inside the database. Next we have Isolation. This Isolation recommends that the transaction should not mess with each other. For example inside a application, if two transactions are running parallelly, these two transactions should not interfere with each other. They should be executed in an isolated manner. So whatever partial updates are done by these transaction two should not be reflected to these transaction one. Next we have durability. Durability says, once you commit the transaction into the database, whatever data that got updated, it should stay forever unless someone is trying to delete or change the data. Inside our backend application, we are using Spring Data JPA, which is helping us to handle that database interactions via Java persistence API. The same framework, it also provides an annotation with the name @Transactional. This is going to be our magic wand for managing that database transactions with minimal configurations. No need to write any boilerplate code. For example, this is a sample code snippet that developers they used to write before Spring and Spring Data JPA. So if you closely try to understand that code that has been written here, first the developer has to fetch a connection based upon the data source object. By using this connection object, the developer has to invoke the setAutoCommit() method to this false value has to be provided, with this what we are telling to the database is, don't commit any of the data that I'm providing unless I'm invoking the commit manually. After disabling the auto commit, we can execute any number of SQL statements. Once we are done executing all the SQL statements towards the end, we need to invoke the commit() method. Unless the transaction is committed by using this commit() method, none of the database changes that we have executed by using the SQL statements will not be committed to the database. Let's imagine there is an exception occurred when we are executing the SQL statements. There might be some three SQL statements like 1,2,3. So this is going to insert some record, and this is going to update some column, and this is going to delete some record. So let's imagine there is an exception occurred while deleting the record. So at this point of time, what should happen? Since the third operation failed, the first two operations also should be rolled back. That's why inside the catch block, by using the same connection object, the developer has to invoke the rollback() method so that all the previously executed SQL statements will be rolled back and those changes will not be applied into the database tables. By looking at this code, it is very clear to us that developers, they used to write a lot of boilerplate code, but if we use @Transactional annotation, it is going to make our lives super easy. By the end of this section, you're going to become a master on how to use these @Transactional annotation. In order to use these @Transactional annotation first, we need to understand how to enable this annotation. . So there are multiple approaches based upon the type of the backend application that you're trying to develop. If you are developing a backend application by using only spring, but not the springboot, then you need to make sure to use this annotation @Enable transaction management inside a configuration class. . Whereas with springboot, you don't have to use these annotation as well. You can directly start using the @Transactional annotation, because inside the springboot whenever we want to use a spring data JPA, we are going to add that specific starter dependency inside our pom.xml. And whenever that dependency is present inside our classpath, the springboot auto-configuration, it is going to auto-enable the TransactionManagement by using this annotation behind the scenes. Everything is going to be ready for the developers to use this annotation. Now coming to the question of where to use this annotation? We can put this annotation on top of a class, method or interface based upon our business requirements. If we are mentioning this annotation on top of a class, it is going to be applicable for all the public methods in that class. So typically we're going to mention this annotation on top of the service layer classes. We also have a option of mentioning this annotation on top of a method as well. When we're using this option, whatever class level transaction settings that we are providing by mentioning this annotation on top of the class, those will be overrided. You need to mention the annotation on top of the method only if you are looking for a fine control for a given method. We can also mention on top of the interfaces. Typically these interfaces refer to the repository interfaces that we have inside of our backend application. We know inside repository interfaces, we are going to define many of the abstract methods by using derived query methods and @Query annotation for all of them if we want to feed some transactional related configurations, you can simply use this annotation on top of that repository interface. My recommendation is always to use this annotation inside service layer methods or on top of the service layer classes. Don't try to mention this annotation directly on top of the repository interfaces or repository methods unless you are overriding some defaults provided by the Spring Data JPA framework. Here you may have a question? which is, Madan so far we have written a lot of database related logic by using the methods from the Spring Data JPA and the derived query methods. And nowhere we have used this annotation and our backend application is also working without any issues. Then why should I worry about these @Transactional annotation? This can be your question? Let's try to understand the same. Here comes the tricky part. When we are using the Spring Data JPA provided methods like save, findAll and so on, the transactional related functionality will be provided by the framework itself out of the box. For example, when you are invoking method like findAll, which is going to read the data behind the scenes, the framework is going to use this @Transactional annotation with the configuration as read only as true. When someone is using these configurations, it is an indication to the framework that right now read operation is going to be executed and mentioning these read only as true during the read operation is going to bring some performance improvements. We're going to talk about these configuration in the coming lectures. This is the story around the read methods like findAll. Now coming to the scenario where we might have invoked the save method to update or insert a new record and such scenarios, the framework it is going to use plain @Transactional annotation. I hope you're clear so far, in simple words, we can say whenever a developer is invoking the methods exposed by CrudRepository or JPA repository from the framework, then the developer don't have to worry about mentioning these @Transactional annotations, because behind the scenes, the framework is going to provide these transactional features out of the box. But what about the scenario where a developer has written a derived query method or he's trying to execute a custom query by using @Query annotation. In this scenario also, so far we are executing a lot of business logic by using @Query annotation and derived query methods, but nowhere we have not used to @Transactional annotation, but still our code is working. Our code is working because so far we executed only the read only operations by using the derived query methods or by using @Query annotation. If a read operation is going on, then mentioning that @Transactional annotation is completely optional, because you're not going to do any damage to the data even if a runtime exception happening. Mentioning of the @Transactional annotation is mandatory when you're trying to update or delete the record by using your derived query methods or by using @Query annotation. On top of that @Transactional annotation, you should also take care of mentioning @Modifying annotation as well to convey to the framework that you are trying to modify some data by using your own custom methods. We are going to see a lot of demos around these custom queries on how to use these @Transactional annotation and @Modifying annotation in the coming lectures. Developers they also have an option of overriding the framework provided transactional properties. I said before, whenever a developer is invoking the save method or findAll method from the CrudRepository, they don't have to provide that @Transactional annotation. But what if there is a scenario, the developer want to overridee the default transactional behavior under such scenarios, what the developer has to do is, inside the repository interface, the developer can override the same method like save on top of that method the developer has to mention @Transactional annotation along with the custom configurations. For example, to this @Transactional annotation, we can provide a timeout configuration. Let's imagine the framework is timing out the query after three seconds. If a developer want to update this to five seconds or 10 seconds, what he has to do, the developer has to simply define the same abstract method inside the repository interface on top of that method. He can use the @Transactional annotation along with this timeout and to this timeout property, he can provide value like five seconds, which is going to overwrite that default behavior provided by the Spring Data JPA framework. We're going to see a lot of demos, don't worry if something is not clear. Overall, I would say always try to use these @Transactional annotation inside your service layer. We're going to discuss about the reasons on why we should use these inside the service layer compared to the repository layer in the coming lectures while we are seeing the demos. In case if your service method is only reading the data, but not updating the data under such scenarios, mentioning the readOnly as true is strongly recommended for performance reasons. Inside the next lecture, let's try to understand more details around this readOnly configuration.

54753673.title: Hands-On - Using @Transactional in a Production-Style Backend
54753673.section: Mastering Transactions in Spring Data JPA – From Basics to Real Pro
54753673.transcript: Let's try opening any of the repositories that we have. For example, there is a repository with the name CompanyRepository. And all our repository interfaces, we know they extend JPARepository interface. Let me open this and what I'll do is, I look for the implementation classes for this interface. If I click on this icon, we should be able to find one class with the name SimpleJpaRepository. All the magic that the Spring Data JPA framework is doing is going to present inside this class. For example, if I invoke findAll() method which is going to return list of entity object, this is the code that it is going to execute behind the scenes. But the important point that I want you to aware here is, on top of this class, if you see, we have annotation @Transactional with the configuration as readOnly as true. That means by default, when a method inside this class is executed, a new transaction is going to be created by the framework with the readOnly configuration as true. And that's why if you invoke any of the find methods like findAll() or findById() method, they don't have any @Transactional annotation again on top of this method, because they are going to inherit whatever transaction configurations that we have defined on top of the class. But if you take a method, which is going to update the record or delete the record like save method, the framework team, they again mentioned that @Transactional annotation. Since the same annotation is mentioned again here, whatever readOnly configurations that are defined on top of the class, they will be overrided and whatever operation that we are going to execute inside this method is going to be considered as a non-read operation or not a read operation. The same kind of behavior you can see for delete() method as well. All these delete() methods, we know they are going to help us to delete the record from the database. Here also, they have overrided the readOnly configuration by mentioning the @Transactional annotation again. . I hope this gives a clarity about all the discussion that we have done in the previous lecture. I said that whenever we are using any of the methods provided by the Spring Data JPA framework, like findAll(), save methods, we don't have to worry about transaction configurations because behind the scenes, the Spring Data JPA framework, it is going to take care of the same for us. But what about the custom methods or derived query methods that we are trying to execute? For example, inside this companyRepository, by using all these methods, we are trying to fetch list of company records along with their associated jobs. In these scenarios, even though if you are not mentioning @Transactional annotation, still your code is going to work because you are only performing the read operation here. Nowhere you are updating or deleting the data by using your custom methods, but it is strongly recommend to always use a @Transactional annotation even for the read operation methods as well. So what I'll do is, I'll go to the CompanyServiceImpl and here on top of this class, I'm going to mention @Transactional annotation. So please make sure to import this annotation from the org.springframework.transaction.annotation To this, I need to provide the readOnly configuration as true. So with this, what is going to happen? By default, all the public methods that I have inside this class, they are going to derive these transactional configurations. For private methods, the transaction configurations are not going to be considered. I'll explain the reason in the coming lectures. Once you define this annotation, you need to revisit all your methods which are going to perform the database operation. For example, this getAllCompanies, we know it is going to invoke these methods inside the repository, which is going to simply perform a read operation. We're not doing anywhere update operation. That's why I'm fine that these methods are inheriting these configurations. Due to that reason, I'm not going to mention the @Transactional annotation again on top of this method. With these configurations, what the framework is going to do is, as soon as someone invokes the service method from the controller layer, a transaction is going to be started. And that transaction, it is going to have the readOnly configuration. In case if there is an exception occurred inside the repository layer or inside these service method logic, then that transaction is going to be rollbacked by the framework automatically. And for readOnly operations, typically there is no need of rollbacking the transaction because these methods, they didn't modify any of the data. Let's see if there are any public methods which are performing database related operations. There are no such methods. So I'm good with this configuration. With these configurations, I don't have to define the @Transactional annotation again on top of these repository interface, because with the configurations that we have defined inside the service layer, a transaction is already started. And we know these methods are going to be invoked from the service methods. But what if there is a scenario where we are invoking one of the methods provided by the Spring Data JPA For example, here I can invoke the findAll() method from the JpaRepository. 54753683.title: Optimizing Database Reads with @Transactional(readOnly = true)
54753683.section: Mastering Transactions in Spring Data JPA – From Basics to Real Pro
54753683.transcript: I said before, when we are using these readOnly as true, along with the @Transactional annotation, it is going to boost the performance of a backend application. Let's try to understand in detail how the performance is going to be improved. Whenever we are setting these readOnly as true, we're telling to the frameworks like Spring Data JPA and Hibernate saying that, Hey, I'm just reading and I'm not performing any updates. With that, the framework is going to skip a bunch of background works. Let's try to understand what are these background activities. Hibernate will skip Dirty checking entirely when we're using readOnly as true. Normally, whenever we are trying to read that data from the database, the Hibernate, it is going to take a snapshot of the every loaded entity to compare later for changes. Whereas with the readOnly true, the entities, they are loaded in readOnly mode. No snapshots stored, no dirty checks during the flush operation or during the commit operation. By using these flush and commit operations, the Hibernate framework, it is going to push the updated changes into the actual database table. When we're using the readOnly true, since Hibernate is not going to create any snapshots, it will result into faster CPU usage, especially if we are loading hundreds or thousands of entities. No iterating over these entities as well to compare the current state with the snapshot state. Let me explain on why Hibernate will try to create a snapshot of the entity object whenever we are loading the data from the database. So if you go to the method that we have created, which is closeContactMessage() here, first we're trying to invoke the findById() method. Since we have mentioned this @Transactional annotation without readOnly configuration, what Hibernate it is going to do, it is going to store the current state of this contact object at line number 84 into a snapshot. And that contact object, which is stored in a snapshot, will have the status value as new. Later, by using the same contact object, I executed the setStatus() method, which is going to update the status from open to closed. And when I'm invoking the save method, the Hibernate first, it will identify whether it has to perform the insert or update operation. Since the contact object that we are providing is having a primary key ID value, the framework will decide to perform the update operation. And during the update operation, the framework has to understand which are the fields that are updated by the business logic. To identify those differences, the framework will try to compare with the snapshot object that is created at the line number 84 with the current contact object that we provided at the line number 89. Unless this snapshot mechanism is present inside the Hibernate framework, it cannot really do the magic. Here taking of the snapshot is mandatory. But what about the scenarios where we are simply performing the read operation? For example, inside this method, we are only going to perform the reading of the data. That's why on top of this method, we have not mentioned @Transactional annotation. Instead, it is going to inherit the readOnly configurations that we have provided on top of the class. With the readOnly configuration, the Hibernate is not going to create any snapshots and that is going to provide some performance improvements. The readOnly configuration, it is going to reduce memory usage, because whatever snapshots that the Hibernate framework used to create for thousands of entity objects, all of them, they are going to eat a surprising amount of heap space, especially when you are executing bigger queries. With the readOnly mode, we are telling to the framework, discard all the loaded snapshots completely, which will result into lower memory footprint per transaction. And this is a great advantage for high concurrency backend applications. With readOnly 2, the framework, it is also going to optimize the flushing operation. Whenever we are setting this readOnly as 2, the Hibernate will set this flush mode to manual there won't be any automatic flushing, because there is nothing to flush for the Hibernate framework. If we are only performing the read operation, since there are no updates are happening to the data, by disabling this automatic flushing, the Hibernate framework is going to bring some performance improvements. The next advantage is around that Database Level Hints. Whenever we are using readOnly configurations, the Spring Data JPA framework, it is going to pass on those readOnly configurations to the underlying JDBC library as well. So databases like PostgreSQL, MySQL, or Oracle, they all support these readOnly configurations. By accepting these configurations, these actual database servers, they can do some performance improvements as well. For example, they are not going to lock any of the rows. The reason is very simple. There is no need of locking of the row because there are no updates are happening. Only reading of the data is happening. The database servers, they can avoid unnecessary transaction overhead, and sometimes they can also reject accidental writes as well when we are passing these readOnly configurations. But please note, not all databases optimizes equally based upon these readOnly configurations. Here I mentioned a scenario as well. Imagine you are trying to fetch 10,000 entities or 10,000 records from the database. Without readOnly, Hibernate will store 10,000 snapshots, which will result into heavy memory and slow flush checks. Whereas with the readOnly, there won't be any snapshots, no checks, quicker executions, and less garbage collector pressure. Always please make sure to mention these readOnly configuration at the service class level, which will be default for all the methods, then override with the plain @Transactional annotation for the right methods. The same best practice we also adopted inside our backend application. So these readOnly configurations, they are going to shine, especially in the Read-heavy scenarios or when loading large object graphs. For smaller queries, the gain is very small, which you can't observe inside your application. But mentioning these readOnly configurations, they are going to future-proof your code, so that in future, if a new developer is trying to build a new method inside your service layer that is going to read thousands of records, under such scenarios, these default configurations will be inherited by the method. Even if the developer forgets about mentioning those readOnly as true, still your application is going to be benefited because you have mentioned this annotation on top of the service layer class. I hope you are clear with all these explanations. So far, we discussed about read operation only. From the next lecture, let's focus on the update operations as well. There might be some scenarios where we may want to update the data based upon our own derived query methods or by using @Query annotation. For such scenarios, we need to follow a different strategy.

54753699.title: Rollback Rules in Spring - Checked vs Runtime Exceptions
54753699.section: Mastering Transactions in Spring Data JPA – From Basics to Real Pro
54753699.transcript: The whole purpose of using @Transactional annotation is, to roll back the transaction when an exception is arising inside our Java code. For example, inside this closeContactMessage method, I may have multiple DB update operations like under this very first operation, I may update some status followed by I may have another transaction which is going to insert some record in another table and there might be another DB transaction to delete a record. Usually, the business requirement will be either all these three operations should be successful or nothing should happen. That is the whole reason on why we are using the @Transactional annotation. If there is a scenario where the first two operations are completed but there is an exception occurred at the third operation, then the framework, it is going to roll back the changes that we have done under this step one and step two. Unless all these three steps are completed successfully, the framework is not going to actually commit the data into the database. As long as your service methods are annotated with this @Transactional annotation, you should be confident around the rollback functionality. You don't have to manually rollback the transactions, but by default, the rollbacking of the transactions will happen only for the runtime exceptions. Using this @Transactional annotation, we should be able to invoke some rollback related properties. So let me open these rollback for and let's read the documentation on top of this method. Here they clearly highlighted by default, a transaction will be rolled back on runtime exception and error, but not on checked exceptions like business exceptions. The reason why this behavior is built inside the framework is, usually inside the real applications checked exceptions or business exceptions should not result any rollback of the data. Unless there is a runtime exception happen like null pointer exception, the transaction should not be rolled back. The same is adopted inside the framework as well. But in case if you want to roll back for all kinds of exceptions, including business exceptions as well, then you need to mention rollback first with the value Exception.class. We know inside the Java, every Exception, including runtime and checked exception, they are going to be jailed elements of this Exception class. But please be very cautious when you are using this Exception.class against to this rollback for, because even in the scenario of any business exception happening inside your code, still the transaction is going to be rollback. Let me show you some demos around this. First, what I'll do is, I will remove these rollback for configurations. And just after invoking these repository method, I'm going to throw an runtime exception. One such runtime exception is null pointer exception. So let me create a new object of null pointer exception and to the constructor, I'll pass a message saying that it is a bad day. And towards the end, I need to comment this return statement. Let me save these changes, do the build. Since null pointer exception is a child of runtime exception, the transaction should get rollback. . So whatever updates that we have done on top of a contact message record based upon this ID value, that status value should be rolled back to the previous new status. Before testing my scenario from the UI, I'm going to keep a breakpoint here. Now I will click on this close button, which is going to update the status of the record. The request stopped at my breakpoint. Let's see what is the ID value. The ID value is 7. Let's remember that I'm releasing the breakpoint. Now I'll go to the database and here under the contacts table, I need to look for the ID with the value 7. For this record, if you see, the status value is still NEW. That means whatever updates that we have done to that data by using this logic, it is rollback because there is a runtime exception occurred. This time, let me throw an checked exception, maybe instead of NullPointerException, I'm going to throw an IO exception. So this IO exception is a checked exception. If you open this exception, you'll be able to see this is extending exception class. So since we are throwing a checked exception, the method has to handle this. So what I'll do is, I will add exception to the method signature and select this option of changing all the methods in the hierarchy. So let me save these changes, do the build once a build is completed, during the build operation, I got a compilation error message inside the controller layer. Here, I need to select this add exception to the method signature. Let me do the build and trigger the update operation. This time I'm clicking on this close and confirming the operation. This time also the ID value is 7. Let me release this breakpoint. Now I'll go to the contacts table and refresh this data. So this time, if you see, the status is closed. That means the rollback didn't happen because this time a checked exception occurred inside our code. So this should give a confirmation to you that the rollback is only going to happen for runtime exceptions by default. But you can change that behavior by using rollback for and providing Exception.class so that for all kinds of Exceptions, the rollback is going to happen. In case if you want to provide a few limited set of exception details, you should be able to provide all those details by using calibrase and inside this calibrase, I can mention details like IOException.class followed by SQLException.class. So all the list of exceptions you can mention in case if you want to go with some rejects pattern as well, you should be able to do that instead of invoking the rollback for, you have to invoke rollback for class name. So to this, if you mention something like Exception, then for all the Exception classes, which are having this Exception in their name, those exceptions will be considered for rollback in the transaction. Just like rollback configurations, we also have noRollback configuration as well. For example, by using these noRollbackFor, if I provide SQLException.class, then the rollback operation is not going to trigger when these Exceptions happen. You can play with these configurations based upon your requirements, but most of the times you should be fine with the default behavior provided by the framework. That's why throughout this course, I'm going to simply use @Transactional annotation and will not explicitly configure any rollback related configurations, but I'm trying to provide this information just for your reference so that you can use this rollback configurations in your real applications based upon your business requirements. Let me remove this breakpoint. I'm also going to delete this code and uncomment this code. Let me delete these throws IOException. I need to go to the interface as well. 54753705.title: Understanding Transaction Propagation Without Confusion
54753705.section: Mastering Transactions in Spring Data JPA – From Basics to Real Pro
54753705.transcript: When using this @Transactional annotation, we can also configure on how the transaction can be propagated. . So there is a propagation property to this we can pass various valid values by using the propagation enum. If I open this, inside this enum, we have the constants like MANDATORY,NESTED,NEVER,NOT_SUPPORTED, REQIED,REQIRES_NEW and SUPPORTS. . As a backend developer, you should definitely have the knowledge around the transaction propagation and what are the options that you have and which one to use based upon a given scenario. That's why inside this lecture, let's try to understand about what is this transaction propagation along with other details. By using the Transaction Propagation, we are conveying what should happen if a transactional method is called when another transaction already exists. Based upon the transaction propagation value that we are trying to configure, accordingly, the behavior is going to change inside the framework. The very first valid value that we can provide to the propagation is required. This is the famous one. And by default, the framework, it is going to be used this one behind the scenes. When we are using the required, it is an indication to the framework, if a transaction already exists, join into it, otherwise you can start a new one. For example, inside the service1 class, there might be a service method with the name M1. And there might be another service class like service2. And inside these, we may have another method with the name M2. Both of these methods, we have the @Transactional annotation. So when the method one is called from the controller layer, a new transaction is going to be created. Now let's imagine from this method one, the M2 method present inside the another service class is getting invoked. Since we already mentioned this @Transactional annotation on top of this M2 as well, this time, the framework is going to look for the propagation configurations that we have done. With the required, what is going to happen is, since there is already a transaction available by the time this M2 method is invoked, the framework will simply reuse the existing transaction and join into it. . Otherwise, if there is no transaction available, it is going to start a new transaction. We can configure this propagation by using the propagation property and to this, we can provide the enum value called REQUIRED. Even if you are mentioning the simple @Transactional annotation behind the scenes, the required is going to be considered as that default value. For example, here, if you see there is a method with the name placeOrder(). And from this method, we are calling other methods like saveOrder(), saveOrderItems(). So using the saveOrder() method, we are going to save the order details, whereas by using the saveOrderItems(), we're going to save the order item details inside the database table. So when the saveOrder() method is getting executed, a transaction anyway will be created. And when the second method is invoked, the existing transaction will be reused. When we are using required, we are telling there should be one transaction and that transaction will be committed when all the methods are successfully executed. If there is a failure in any of these methods, the framework will rollback all the changes that we have done as part of the single transaction. Why this propagation is default? Because this is the behavior that developers want 90% of the time. After required, we have supports. When we are using supports, we are telling to the framework, if a transaction exists, use it. If not, run without a transaction. So whatever method that we are executing with the supports propagation, it is going to work with or without a transaction. It is going to be used commonly for readOnly operations. You may have a question here. How is it possible to execute a database operation without a transaction? As I highlighted here, usually we need a transaction only for the update operations. The whole purpose of transaction is to rollback the changes in the case of exception. Whereas with the readOnly operation, we're not committing any code changes. That's why even without the transaction, the framework should be able to execute your select query. For such readOnly operations, you can use the propagation as supports. Next we have Mandatory propagation. We can configure these by invoking the mandatory constraint. With this Mandatory propagation, we are telling to the framework, a transaction must already exist if not, throw an exception. You can use this kind of propagation in the scenarios where you want to execute some operations as part of another parent operation. For example, the debit operation must be part of the checkout operation. Calling Debit alone should be illegal. In this kind of scenarios, you're going to simply mention mandatory Propagation for debit operation. So that if someone is trying to invoke this Debit operation directly, it is going to throw an exception. Whereas if the same Debit operation is getting invoked after the checkout operation, then the framework is going to process the logic because a transaction is already exist. Next we have REQUIRES_NEW. Using this propagation, we're telling to the framework, pass the current transaction and start a new transaction always. Developers typically, they may want to use these request new propagation in the scenarios where if main flow fails, still they want to execute the later operation. For example, take a scenario where we have a main operation followed by, we're going to make an entry into some audit related table. In this scenario, even if the main flow operation fails, the operation related to audit log should be committed. The same applies for other kinds of scenarios where we may want to maintain login attempts, failure history, and sending notification. So all these operations, typically we want to execute them regardless of main flow. Under such scenarios, you can use these REQUIRES_NEW propagation. Next we have NOT_SUPPORTED. With this propagation, we're telling to the framework, run without any transaction. Even if there is one exist already, suspend it. So there is a clear difference between these not supported and these optional transaction. These optional transaction is going to use that transaction if it is already available, if there is no transaction, then only it is going to execute without a transaction. But in the scenario of not supported, the logic is always going to be executed without any transaction. If there is already an active transaction available, it is going to suspend it and execute the logic without a transaction. This option you need to use in the scenarios where you don't want any rollback support. Typically in the readOnly operations where you are looking for better performance and no locking. But please be very careful. No transaction means no safety net. You should not try to update any data. The next propagation that we have is NEVER. With this propagation, we're telling to the framework, if a transaction exists, throw exception. Don't execute the business logic. Usually we're going to use these NEVER propagation in the scenarios where we are making externalApiCall(). Let's imagine there is a method M1 and inside this method, we are making an externalApiCall(). On top of this method, if you have a @Transactional annotation, what is going to happen? The transaction is going to keep waiting unless you're going to get the response from the externalApiCall(). . This will result into long logs. That's why as a safety measurement in the scenarios where you don't want to run your logic as part of a DB transaction, you can mark them as propagation as NEVER. So if a developer is invoking these externalApiCall() as part of a database transaction, the framework is going to throw an exception and we'll stop the developer right there. At last we have NESTED propagation. By using these NESTED propagation, we're telling to the framework, create a sub transaction inside the main transaction. So here, if you see, there is a method with the name processItems() on top of this method we have mentioned the propagation as NESTED, while processing the very first method like process item one, a sub transaction will be created, which is dedicated to this method. Similarly, another sub transaction will be created for the second method. In the scenarios where the second method is having some runtime exceptions, then only the changes that are applied as part of the second method will be rolled back and the rollback that is happening inside the process item two, it is not going to have any impact on the data that is applied as part of this process item one, because that is completely independent another sub transaction. So this is like having checkpoints in a video game. If you fail and restart the game, the game is going to start from the checkpoint, but not from the beginning. In this slide, I have given a summary of all the transaction propagations. As I said, REQUIRED propagation, it is always going to join the existing transaction. In case if there is no transaction, it is going to create a NEW transaction. This REQUIRED propagation is useful for Default business logics. SUPPORTS will join the existing transaction, but it is not going to create any new transaction. It is used for readOnly scenarios. And coming to the MANFATORY, the existing transaction must exist. It is never going to create a new transaction. You need to use this MANDATORY in the scenarios where you want to apply some strict rules. Request_NEW will suspend the existing transaction and create a new transaction. You need to use the REQUIRES_NEW in the scenarios where you want to perform some auditing or logging of some data into the database. NOT_SUPPORTED will suspend the existing transaction and it is also not going to create any new transaction this means your entire logic is going to be executed without any transaction. And this propagation is useful in the scenarios of reports. When we are trying to generate the reports, we usually read a lot of data from the databases. While executing those reading operations, we don't want to have any transactions. That's where we can use NOT_SUPPORTED. With NEVER, if there is an existing transaction available, an error will be thrown and it is never going to create a new transaction. This propagation you need to use as a safety mechanism when you are making a call to the external API's. NESTED is going to create the sub transactions as a save points and creating a new condition is always going to be conditional. If you have multiple methods invocation, the new sub transactions will be created based upon multiple method invocations. So with the NESTED, we should be able to do the partial rollbacks. Since required is the most recommended transaction propagation throughout this course, I'm going to use the required only, but if you have any business use cases, which requires different transaction propagation, feel free to use any of the propagations that we have discussed.

54753709.title: Choosing the Right Isolation Level in Real Applications
54753709.section: Mastering Transactions in Spring Data JPA – From Basics to Real Pro
54753709.transcript: If you open the source code of this @Transactional annotation, there are many properties that we can leverage while using this annotation. We already discussed about rollback and propagation, readOnly. The next important property that I want to discuss is timeout(). Using this timeout() property, we should be able to define what is the maximum amount of time that you want to wait when a DB operation is getting executed. For example, I can simply invoke timeout property here and pass 10 seconds. Usually 10 seconds is a too long, but if there are some bigger queries inside your application, you may need more time to execute and get the results. For those special scenarios, you can use this timeout property and provide your own timeout value. In case, if you want to wait for short time, you can mention one, two or three seconds based upon your business requirements. But most of the times we should be fine with the default timeout behavior provided by the underlying transaction manager. After that timeout, the next important property that I want to talk about is isolation(). To this isolation property, we can provide various isolation valid values. By default, this default constant will be leveraged by the framework. So there is a enum with the name isolation inside this enum, we have constants like DEFAULT, READ_COMMITTED,READ_UNCOMMITTED,REPEATABLE_READ,SERIALIZABLE. . Let's try to understand more details about these constants and when to use them. In a typical real enterprise application, hundreds of users will be changing the data inside the database at the same time. If they interfere with each other, data may get messy. That is where isolation levels come into picture. By using these isolation levels, we are going to define walls between the user transactions, but there are some trade-offs for these isolation levels. If you're going with the Low isolation, you may get faster performance, but higher risk of weird data. Whereas with the High Isolation, you're going to get perfect data accuracy, but there'll be slower performance because to provide you the perfect data accuracy, the database is going to enforce some locking mechanisms, which will result into the slower performance as other transactions will be waiting for the database to give access to a given data. To understand these isolation levels in detail first you need to understand three different jargons around the isolation. The first one is Dirty Read. If the system or the framework is allowing you to read another transaction data without committing that transaction, then dirty reading is going to come into picture. For example, if you're reading data that another person changed, but has not saved or committed the transaction yet, if they undo their change, then whatever data that you read will become fake. That's why this concept is called Dirty Read, but there are certain advantages with dirty read. I'll come to that. Next we have Non-Repeatable Read. Let's imagine you read a row and the same rows is updated by someone else. After a few seconds, if you read the same row again, this time you're going to get different data, because someone else updated the row that you are interested. So it is like book changing its text while you are still reading it. The names non-repeatable read is very suitable this scenario because whenever you are repeating the read operation, you may get different data if someone else is updating the same data. If you want to have the same data, you should not repeat the reading operation. That's why this name Non-Repeatable Read. Next we have Phantom Read. Let's imagine of a scenario, you're trying to read 10 different record items from a database table, after you read the records, someone else inserted a new record. And with that, if you try to read and count again, the number of record items, suddenly you are getting 11 rows or 11 items. What happened here? If phantom appeared, what is the meaning of phantom? Phantom means ghost. Suddenly it goes to record appear that's why this name has been given to this concept. I hope you are clear with these three jargons or concept by using these three concepts only I'm going to explain the isolation levels. The very first level of isolation level supported by the Spring Data JPA is, READ_COMMITTED. . If you are defining this isolation level by using the isolation property against to the @Transactional annotation, what you're telling to the framework is, allow a transaction to see the changes the moment they are updated inside another transaction. Even if the other transaction is not committed its data, the vulnerability of this isolation is, we will see Dirty Reads. What are Dirty Reads? We already discussed here, as part of the dirty reads we should be able to read other transaction data even before it's committed to the database. In case if there is some rollback is happening, or if the other transaction undo its change, then whatever data that we read is going to become fake. The same problem is going to happen with this isolation. But you may have a question then under which scenarios we need to use this isolation? Usually this isolation will be used very rarely, but if speed or performance is matter, and if you're looking for high performance and accuracy does not matter under such scenarios, you can use these isolation level. For example, usually inside social media applications like Facebook, they may want to display the likes counter on a post where being off by one is not a disaster. So their accuracy does not matter, but they want to have speed. After these READ_UNCOMMITTED, we have READ_COMMITTED. So this is like next level of isolation. When we are using these READ_COMMITTED, we are telling to the framework, please allow a transaction to read that data only if it is committed by the other transaction. If there is uncommitted data, such data will not be appeared to other transactions. The transactions will only see the data that has been officially committed or saved into the actual database table. So the benefit of this approach is, there won't be any Dirty Reads, but the catch is with this approach, we might still see Non-Repeatable Reads. What is Non-Repeatable Reads? We read a row and someone else updated it and committed it. And if you read it again, and this time we are going to get different data. So with the read committed, we are going to have the non-repeatable reads, but we're not going to have any Dirty Reads. This READ_COMMITTED is a default transaction isolation provided by the databases like Postgres SQL and SQL server. In case, if you want to avoid both these Dirty Read and Non-Repeatable Reads, then you need to go for the isolation with the property with the constant REPEATABLE_READ. With this isolation, what is going to happen is, whenever a transaction read a row, the database freezes that row data for that specific transaction. If some other transaction updated it, our transaction still keeps seeing the version it started with. With that, the benefits are there won't be any Dirty Reads and No Non-Repeatable Reads as well. The catch here is we might still see some Phantom Reads. For example, if new rows are appearing, we can't avoid that. This isolation level is recommended for the scenarios where you want the data to stay consistent while you are executing your transaction, especially in the scenarios where you are generating the financial reports and executing some calculations. So during these calculations, you want the numbers to stay consistent for such scenarios this isolation level is going to be super app. MySQL database is going to follow this isolation level by default. That means whenever you are reading a record or a set of records as part of a transaction, even if some other transactions modify some data in those records under your own transaction, you will see the same initial version data that you started with. So that's a beauty of MySQL database. Yeah, you may have a question? How does the database server is going to maintain my own version, which is specific to my transaction? To achieve all this functionality behind the scenes, the database, they are going to use a concept to call Snapshot. When you are starting a transaction with a isolation level as REPEATABLR_READ behind the scenes the database, it is going to take a mental picture of the data as it exists at that exact millisecond. So for the rest of your transaction, you're going to look at the same picture, whereas the reality is other users, they can still change the live data, they can update rows and delete them. With this, if you query the same rows 10 times in the same transaction, you will see the same exact values every single time, even if another user changed and saved that row in the meantime, as part of the other transaction. How this Snapshot works behind the scenes, they are going to use versioning. The database does not actually freeze the original row, instead, it creates versions. Imagine there is a row representing a bank balance. The row ID is 1 and the balance is 100. There is a transaction A started with the Repeatable Read isolation and it reads the balance as $100. Now another transaction B starts and updates the balance to 200 and commits the data as well. The database now technically has two versions of this row in its storage. Older version will have $100 until the transaction A is completed. Whereas the current version is going to have $200 and this current version will be provided for every other transaction. So if the transaction A reads again, the database checks the transactions A's start time and says, Oh, you belong to the $100 era and with that, the transaction A sees $100 again. So if you closely understand the difference between multiple isolation levels, like READ_COMMITTED and REPEATABLE_READ, under the read committed, the database gives you the latest saved version at the moment you ask. If transaction B saves a change midway through your work, your second read will show the new value. Whereas with the repeatable read, the database gives you the version that was saved at the moment your transaction began. The updated values are always ignored. While these repeatable read freezes the existing rows that you have looked, but it does not freeze the entire table or it is not going to stop in adding another new records. So this results into a weakness called phantoms. If you want to avoid even phantom reads as well, then you need to use the isolation level SERIALIZABLE. So with this isolation level, we are telling to the framework, when a transaction is reading a given set of data, please make sure that no one is updating that data and also no one is introducing a new record under that data. So with that, the benefit is we are always going to have perfect data with zero nightmares. The catch here is it is going to be very slow because the other transactions, they have to wait for each other until the lock is released by the database server on a given data set. So when the lock is going to be released, once your own transaction is done processing, you need to use this SERIALIZABLE isolation under the scenarios where you are executing extremely critical tasks where a hundred percent accuracy is required, like a complex banking transfer involving multiple accounts. To achieve these kinds of isolation, the database often places range logs on the data. For example, if your query is looking for the users between the age 20 and 30, the database is going to log the entire range so that no any other transaction can add a new user until your transaction is done processing. As I said before, this isolation level is useful in the scenarios where you are executing some extremely critical tasks. For example, you're trying to check the balance across five different accounts and ensure the sum is exactly $1 million. If yes, you want to distribute a 1% bonus to all five accounts and verify the sum is now exactly $1,010,000. In this kind of scenario, if another transaction adds a new account or move money between these accounts while you are calculating, your math will be wrong. That's why using transaction with the isolation serializable ensures that for those few milliseconds, the database will stop so that your math calculation stays perfect. So these are the four levels that we have. As a developer, there'll be very few scenarios where we want to decide on which isolation level to be used, but most of the times we should be fine with that DEFAULT isolation level. There is a constant with the name DEFAULT under the Isolation enum. This is the one which is used behind the scenes by default. So when we are using these default isolation, we're telling to the framework, please go ahead and use the whatever isolation level that the database server is providing. . For example, when you are using MySQL, behind the scenes, this MySQL database server, it is going to use REPEATABLE_READ. For Postgres and Oracle database, the default isolation levels are READ_COMMITTED. So we're telling to the spring framework, please don't do any magic here and respect that database settings. So this is the summary table of all the isolation levels with the READ_UNCOMMITTED, we're going to have the fastest behavior, but it is going to bring all the disadvantages like Dirty Reads, Non-Repeatable and Phantoms. With the READ_COMMITTED, we can avoid a Dirty Reads, but we'll still have Non-Repeatable and Phantoms. That's why this read committed is somewhat fast compared to other last four isolation levels. So for REPEATABLE_READ, only Phantom reads will be allowed. So here the speed will be moderate and towards the end we have serializable where we can avoid all the drawbacks like Dirty Reads, Non-Repeatable and Phantom Reads here the speed will be slowest. Most of the times we should be fine with this READ_COMMITTED or REPEATABLE_READ that's why these are the default isolations provided by these famous database servers. Throughout this course, inside our backend application as well, I'm not going to configure any isolation related configurations. I will go with the default isolation level. Since I'm using MySQL database server as a backend database, the REPEATABLE_READ isolation level will be constructed by the Spring Data JPA framework.

54753719.title: Common @Transactional Pitfalls You Must Avoid in Production
54753719.section: Mastering Transactions in Spring Data JPA – From Basics to Real Pro
54753719.transcript: While using @Transactional annotation, there are some common pitfalls that you need to be aware as a backend Java developer. The first pitfall is, if you are calling a @Transactional annotated method from another method inside the same class, the proxy is going to be bypassed and the transaction won't start. For example, here there is a service class with the name UserService. Inside this class, I have this method which is annotated with the @Transactional. In the similar lines, I also have another method with the name setupProfile(). On top of this method also, I am using @Transactional annotation. With this scenario, from the createUser method(), I am trying to invoke this setupProfile() method by using this operator because this method belongs to the same class. In this kind of scenarios, whatever @Transactional annotation that you have mentioned on top of this method, it is not going to have any effect. The reason is, behind the scenes, whenever we are calling a transactional method from within the same class, it is going to skip the proxy entirely no transaction is going to start. Why this behavior? To implement all this transactional related logic behind the scenes, Spring wraps our bean in a proxy object so that it can intercept each called and manage or implement transaction related logic. With the internal calls, you are going to invoke the methods directly, which will bypass the proxy object that the Spring has been created. That's why never ever try to call the methods inside the same service class from another method. Normal methods which does not have any database interaction related logic, such methods you can call them because they don't require any transaction. But if there is a method which is having some data operation related logic, then that is not recommended . instead you need to move that into some other bean and invoke that method. Otherwise, you can club both the methods logic into the same method in the service layer. The next pitfall is around the Non-Public methods. If you are trying to use these @Transactional annotation on top of the private, protect, final or static methods, then this annotation is going to be completely ignored by the framework. Why? because, as I said before Spring framework, it is going to create some proxy objects where it will overwrite the logic that we have provided by appending some transactional related logic. But with the private, protected or final static methods, the proxy cannot overwrite them properly Because in order to overwrite them, the JDK proxies, they need interfaces or public methods. Behind the scenes, JDK is going to use a open-source Java library with the name CGLIB. CGLIB means Code Generated Library. So using this library, behind the scenes, the JDK is going to generate some bytecode or manipulate the existing bytecode as well. So since this CGLIB has some restrictions around these private, protected, final, static methods, using this @Transactional annotation on top of this method is not recommended. It is not going to work. So always keep transactional methods public. Though from Spring 5th version, there are some improvements introduced around the protected methods still, I would recommend you to stick to the public methods. The next pitfall is around the Rollback Rules. We discussed clearly about these rollback in the previous lectures. Let me reiterate one more time, by default, rollback is going to happen only for unchecked exceptions. For the checked exceptions, like IOException or SQLException, the rollback is not going to happen instead, the committing of the data will happen. But you can overwrite this behavior by providing this configuration on top of your method. So this is going to rollback for every exception. In case if you want to ignore the rollback, you need to use the noRollback for property. The last pitfall is around the catching exceptions inside the method. Let's imagine inside your service layer method, you are catching the method and swallowing that method as well. That means inside your catch block, you log the error details and you handle some exception logic, but you never throw that error outside your catch block. And with that, what is going to happen? The framework will never know that there is an exception occurred inside your service method. So that's why the rollback will never come into picture. So here I have given an example inside the catch block, the developer simply logging the exception details and not throwing the exception. So that's why there won't be any rollback in this scenario. For a proper rollback inside your catch block, you can log the details, you can handle the exception, but eventually you need to throw some kind of exception. It can be the same exception or it can be a different exception as well. So please make sure you are aware about all these common pitfalls while you are using @Transactional annotation inside your Spring Boot backend application.

54753729.title: Building Employer Management Features for Admin Users
