54753729.transcript: The next enhancement that we have under the admin login is, around the Employer Management. The purpose of this feature is, whenever a employer want to onboard into the job portal application, first what they're going to do is, they will try to register as a normal user. We know by default, if someone is registering inside this application, we're going to give them job seeker role. But to post the jobs against to a given company, they need an elevated role, which is employer. That's why what an employer will do is, he or she will try to register as a normal user followed by they're going to work with the admin to elevate their role from the normal job seeker to employer. From this employer management screen, the admin can search an end user email address. And based upon the results, the admin is going to elevate the user from normal job seeker to employer, followed by the admin also can assign that employer to a given company that is registered inside the job portal application, so that when the employer logged in again into the job portal application, they should be able to post the job only against to the company which they are tagged. To support all these admin related features around the employer management, we need to build few REST APIs on the backend side. This time also, I'm going to explain you the changes that I have done behind the scenes by taking those code changes as a reference, please try to build these REST APIs on your own. This is the next assignment that you have as part of the section. This time you need to go to the a class with the name UserController. This is a new controller that I have created behind the scenes. On top of these controller, I have mentioned these @RestController annotation, @RequestMapping annotation. . The prefix that I'm using is users. So first, we have a REST API that supports @GetMapping with the path search admin. By invoking this REST API, an admin user can search an user by providing the email as an input. With the input that is being accepted here as a @RequestParam, I'm trying to invoke the searchUserByEmail() method available in the UserServiceImpl class. So this class implements IUserService interface. These are all the new classes and interfaces that I have created for the employer management related functionality. Inside the UserServiceImpl method, I'm trying to search the users table by invoking a derived query method with the name findJobPortalUserByEmail. If I open this, this is a normal derived query method. Since we are trying to fetch the data from a given single table, the derived query method should be fine. Behind the scenes, when we are invoking this method, the framework, it is going to query the users table based upon the email that we are providing. And if there is an user identified, it is going to return as in the form of UserDto. So the copying of the data from the entity object to the DTO, we are doing inside these private method with the name mapToUserDto. Once the DTO is ready, we are trying to return the same from the service method. So on top of this method, I'm not mentioning the Transactional annotation again, because whatever we have mentioned on top of the class, that should be enough. Now from the controller method, you're going to simply return this UserDto object as a response. So in case if this optional object is empty, we are going to throw this NOT_FOUND Exception with this message. Otherwise we will return the whatever object that we have under this optional by invoking these get() method. Once the users are displayed on this page, based upon the email address provided by the admin, the admin first, he has to elevate the user from a normal job seeker to the employer. So for that, I have another REST API with the name @PatchMapping. Here I have used  @PatchMapping because we are going to update a single column role ID under the users table. So this is the REST API path. As part of the path, I'm trying to accept a path variable, which is going to provide me what is the user primary key ID value. So based upon this user ID value, I'm going to invoke these elevate to employer method inside the service layer. So first I'm trying to fetch the user ID details by invoking these findById() present in the Crud Repository. . So if there is an user identified, we will continue with the logic, otherwise we're going to throw an exception saying that User not found with a given ID. So for the given user, I'm checking if the user is already having employer role inside the application constants, I have created both these constants with the name ROLE_ADMIN and role jobseeker. If the user is already assigned to the employer role, I'm not going to do any update operation. I'm going to simply return these user details to the client application in the form of DTO object. So under this mapToUserDto, we're going to transfer the data from user to DTO object. In case if the user is not an employer, then the next if condition is going to be executed. Here, we're checking if the user is having a admin role. In such scenario, we are going to throw a runtime exception saying that we cannot elevate admin user to an employer role, because that is against our business requirements. If both these if conditions are not satisfied, then towards the end, we're trying to fetch the role entity record based upon the role name ROLE_EMPLOYER. So here also I can use the application constant. Let me use the same and once the role employer record is fetched from the database, I'm trying to set the same to the user entity object by invoking the setRole() method. This is going to override the previous role ID with the new role ID that we are trying to assign. Once the user object is updated, here in an ideal world, we had to invoke the save method by using the userRepository. And to this save method, we need to provide the user object. And with that, the framework is going to take care of updating the record inside the database. But using these line is completely optional because when we are fetching the user record from the database by using the findById at the starting of this method, whatever user object that we loaded, it is a managed entity by the Hibernate framework. Any modifications that you do inside this transaction method, the Hibernate framework, it is going to keep tracking all those changes before the transaction exit from this method. The Hibernate framework, it will try to flush the changes to that database, because as soon as this method logic is completed, the transaction is going to be closed and that's why Hibernate will try to commit any of the changes that you have done on top of these user entity object that is being managed by the Hibernate inside this transaction. That's why before executing these return statement, the Hibernate will take care of committing the transactions. . Invoking the save method against the userRepository with the updatedUser entity object is completely optional. So from this method, we are trying to return the user DTO object, which we are going to accept inside the UserController. And from this controller method, we are going to return the updated user details to the client application. Once the user is elevated to the employer role, as a next step, the admin has to tag that employer to a given company. To support that operation, I have developed another REST API by using @PatchMapping and this is the path. Using this path, I'm accepting both user ID and the company ID values as the path variables. By taking both this user ID and company ID, I'm trying to invoke the assign company to employer method in the UserServiceImpl. So here first, I'm trying to fetch the user details from the database by using this findById(). In case if the user is not identified, we are going to throw a runtime exception. Next we're trying to execute a if condition. Here we are executing a business validation, which is if the current loaded user role is not role employer, then we're going to throw a runtime exception saying that user must be an employer to be assigned to a company. In case if this if condition is not executed, then based upon this company ID, we're going to load the company record again by using this company findby ID method against to the Company Repository. In case if there is no company identified, we're going to throw this runtime exception Otherwise, we're going to simply return a company entity object, whatever company object that we have loaded, we are going to set into the user object by invoking the setCompany() method. And here also invoking the save method by using the userRepository is completely optional. By the time this return statement is being executed, the Hibernate framework is going to take care of committing the updated entity data into the database table. So whatever DTO object that we are returning from this method will be returned to these controller method. From this method, we are returning to the client application. Please take some time and try to understand all these test APIs followed by you can try to develop all this code by yourself. Once you feel you are done with your code changes, please come to the next lecture where we are going to do the testing of this test APIs by using both the UI application and the Postman. I forgot to highlight one more change that we have to do. The changes inside the PathsConfig for all the new REST APIs that we have developed so far, for all of them, you need to mention under these admin paths method, because these REST APIs are supposed to be invoked only for the users who has admin role. Please make sure you are also doing these Spring Security code changes.

54764731.title: Section Overview - Authorization & Data Handling
54764731.section: Mastering Authorization, Sorting & Pagination in Spring Boot
54764731.transcript: As of now, inside our job portal application, we only implemented authentication. Any logged in user should be able to access any of the secured APIs that we have on the backend side. But as part of this new section, we are going to focus on authorization as well. In simple words, authorization is nothing but role-based access mechanism. For example, if I try to log in as an Admin, under the admin login, we should be able to see all this menu. As part of this section, we are going to focus on only on these contact messages functionality. On the backend side, we are going to build a REST API that supports contact messages page. Only Admin users should be able to invoke this REST API. If any other user is trying to invoke this API, they should get an error. Let me show you what we are going to build as part of this contact messages. As soon as Admin click on this link, this page is going to appear. Inside this page, we are going to display all the contact messages received to the Admin so that the Admin can review all the queries received and solve them. Once admin talk to the customer or the end user, and after solving the problem, the admin can update the status by clicking on this close button. The admin also has an option to see more details by clicking on the message. So these are the other details where the admin can see what is the subject and message submitted by an end user. Apart from displaying and updating the contact message records, this page is also going to support sorting of the records based upon a given field and direction. So here the admin has an option to sort all these records based upon any of these values. For example, if I select a name, the record sorting should happen by name and direction also the admin has an option which controls on how the records are being displayed on the UI. So whatever records are fetched from the backend, so all of them, they are going to be displayed by using the pagination concept. As of now, inside my backend database table, I have around 16 records. That's why two pages are being displayed here for each page by default, we're going to display 10, but the admin has an option to change this value. For example, if the admin select 25, all the records will be displayed as part of a single page. So this part is called as pagination. So where the admin is going to have to navigate to the next page, previous pages based upon his requirement. So the sorting logic and the pagination logic, we are going to build on the backend side by using the Spring Data JPA framework. Because in real enterprise applications, sometimes we may have thousands of records that we are supposed to be displayed on the UI, but sending all those thousands of records or millions of records at a time is not a recommended approach because it is going to bring a lot of performance issues, both on the UI side and on the backend side as well. Because you have to transmit a lot of data over the network and the UI also has to process all that record to display to the end user. That's why we're going to follow a standard where we are going to implement the sorting and the pagination logic on the backend side. So based upon what my front end application is asking, accordingly, we're going to send only those set of records. For example, from this UI application, if the sorting requirement is based upon name and the direction is descending and per page is 25, then based upon the sorting and pagination requirements, we're going to send only 25 records that are going to be displayed as part of the page one. If there are more records, then the end user is going to see page numbers like two and three. So based upon which page the end user is navigating, a backend request will go and the corresponding page records will be sent back by the backend API. So this is all the functionality that we are going to discuss throughout this section. Primarily, we're going to talk about three important topics. The first one is around the authorization. By using this authorization, we're going to enforce role-based access mechanism for our backend REST APIs. We are going to build a mechanism in such a way that only an admin login user should be able to access the backend REST API that is required by these contact messages page. Apart from authorization, we're also going to focus on second topic, which is sorting followed by the third topic, which is pagination. So these are the primary topics. I hope you are clear about the agenda of this course. If you're not using this UI application, don't worry I'm also going to show you how to test all these features by using the Postman as well.

54764737.title: Authentication(Who Are You) vs Authorization(What Can You Do)
54764737.section: Mastering Authorization, Sorting & Pagination in Spring Boot
54764737.transcript: Before we try to implement Authorization inside our application, first let's try to understand what is the difference between Authentication and Authorization. By using authentication, we are going to identify who is the user that is trying to access and secured system. Whereas by using authorization, we are going to check whether the given authenticated user has enough privileges or authorities or roles to access a secure resource. Always, authentication is going to happen before the authorization. Authentication is also going to be represented in a short form by using AuthN. After the authentication, authorization will come into picture. To perform the authentication, we only require end-user login details like email and password. But to enforce authorization, we require end-user privileges or roles. Usually during the login operation, once the given username and password are correct, we are going to load the corresponding privileges or roles of the user from the database or from any storage system. Once the roles or privileges of the user are loaded, we are going to perform the authorization. If the user has enough privileges or roles, then the authorization is going to pass and the user will be provided an access. In a typical backend application, it is completely possible to build REST APIs that can be invoked by any logged-in user, or we can enforce some extra restrictions as well so that only a logged-in user with so-and-so privileges should be able to access this secure API. We are going to discuss about that in the coming lectures. There are separate status codes for these authentication and authorization. Whenever the authentication itself is failing, as a standard, we need to return 401 error. 401 means the end-user credentials are invalid. Whereas, if the authorization fails, we should return 403 status code. 403 means forbidden error. By using this 403, we are going to tell to the end-user, I accept that you are a valid authenticated user, but you don't have enough privileges to access so-and-so resource. For example, if you take an application where there are two types of users exist, the first one is customer, the other one is employee, both of them, they can perform various kinds of actions inside the application. But to perform those actions, first they need to prove their identity. Once the authentication is completed and logged in into the application based upon their roles and authorities, they can perform various kinds of actions. Let me give a real-life analogy as well to remember these authentication and authorization. Let's imagine you are traveling from New York to Dubai. So to travel from one city to other city, you usually go to an airport. So at the starting of the airport, you need to prove your identity. So the security guy, he's going to ask you to show your passport and ticket details. As long as your passport and ticket details are correct, the security guy, he's never going to worry about which terminal or gate you are going once you enter into the airport. Because the role of the security guy is only to perform the authentication. So how he's performing the authentication by making sure that you have a ticket on the same day and you are the same person who booked the ticket by looking at your passport. After the authentication, you're going to enter into the airport and once you complete all your security checks and baggage check-ins, then you will go to the mentioned gate where your boarding is going to start. When the boarding is happening, the airlines company, they're going to perform one more check. They'll make sure that you are getting onto the right flight, which is traveling to Dubai. For some reason, if you go to some other gate and try to onboard to a different flight, they're going to stop you by saying that you don't have enough privilege to onboard onto this flight because it is traveling to some other city, whereas you booked a ticket from New York to Dubai. So that airline check is a kind of authorization. Only authorized users are allowed to onboard that flight, which is traveling to Dubai from New York. Very similar style of authentication and authorization we should do inside our backend application as well. Because by using only authentication, we can't build enterprise applications, bigger enterprise applications that are going to have complex security requirements. And those security requirements we can only achieve when we implement both authentication and authorization. . I hope you are clear with the differences between Authentication and Authorization. From the next lecture, let's try to understand how to implement Authorization by using spring security framework.

54764741.title: How Spring Security Stores Authorities Internally using GrantedAuthority
54764741.section: Mastering Authorization, Sorting & Pagination in Spring Boot
54764741.transcript: Inside spring security framework, there is an interface with the name GrantedAuthority. So using these interface only, we are going to represent the authority details of an logged in user. This interface has a single abstract method with the name getAuthority(), which is going to return the authority details or authority name in the form of String data type. For this interface, there are implementation classes. Most of the times developers, they will end up using SimpleGrantedAuthority. The same we're going to use inside these codes as well. This class has a constructor, which is going to accept authority details as an input. So whenever someone is trying to create an object of this class, they need to provide the authority detail. The same will be stored inside these role variable. And whenever someone wants to read the role detail or authority detail from this class, they need to simply invoke this getAuthority() method, which is going to return the roles information that we have stored while we are creating the object. Now with this information, let's try to understand what is happening during the login operation. We know the login logic we have implemented as part of the JobPortalUsernamePasswordAuthenticationProvider. Here inside this authenticate method as a very first step, we're trying to load the user details from the database by passing the username as an input. Whatever details that we stored inside the database, they are going to be represented by these object jobPortalUser. If you open these entity class inside this class, we have all the user details along with that we also have role information that is associated for a given user. That means whenever we are loading the user details from the database, the roles information will also get loaded into the jobPortalUser object. Now using this object, we are trying to invoke the getRole() method, which will give us the object of role. And by using the Role object, we are again invoking the name of the role. So whatever name that we stored inside that database for a given user, the same we are trying to convert that as an object of SimpleGrantedAuthority because Spring Security can understand the roles and authorities information only if you are passing that information by using these SimpleGrantedAuthority to the constructor of this class, we are passing the role information and with that, whatever we have provided, the same will be stored. And at any point of time, if the Spring Security framework want to know what is the authority or role information for a given user, it is going to simply invoke this getAuthority() method. Once we created the object of this class, I'm trying to convert that as a list, because inside the authentication object, we have to store the authority details or roles details by using a collection object. Inside our job portal application, we have a business requirement to have a single role for a given user, but in some real enterprise applications, they may have a requirement to have multiple roles or authorities for a given user. In that scenario, what you have to do? You just have to make sure you are defining the One-To-Many mapping inside the jobPortalUser against to the role mapping and whatever database setup that we have right now, it won't work because with the setup right now, we have all the roles, static information we are maintaining inside these roles table. And whenever a new user is getting created, we're simply mentioning these roles table ID value as a value under the role_id column. If I scroll right side there is a role_id, and this will work if the user has only a single role. But in case, if you want to maintain multiple roles, as I said, you need to make sure you are defining One-To-Many relationship mapping inside the entity class. On top of that, you should have one more table as well, which is going to store all the roles information for a given user. You can create a table like user_roles, where you're going to have the user_id along with the role_id. That way you can maintain multiple roles record for a single user. For our scenario, this database setup and whatever configurations that we have done should be good enough. So here, since we have a single role, I am invoking the getRole() method, which is going to give me a single role object. Whereas if you are defining One-To-Many relationship, then you're going to get a collection object, that collection object you need to iterate and from each element, you need to read the name and create the SimpleGrantedAuthority object. And all those SimpleGrantedAuthority objects, you can try to store them inside a list. Once you have the list is ready, when you're trying to create the object of UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken or Authentication object, you have to pass authorities information as a third input parameter. 54764747.title: AUTHORITY vs ROLE -  Which One Should You Use
54764747.section: Mastering Authorization, Sorting & Pagination in Spring Boot
54764747.transcript: Inside spring security framework, you will hear two different jargons with the name Authority and Role. By using any of them, we should be able to implement authorization, but there is a minor difference between these authority and role. Let's try to understand what is the difference. Authority is like an individual privilege or an action that an end user can perform. By using this authority, we're going to restrict the access in a fine grained manner. For example, we can define authorities like VIEWJOB,ADDJOB,ADDCOMPANY for each and every possible action inside our backend application, we have to define an authority, whereas role is like a group of privileges or actions. By using roles, we can restrict access in a coarse grained manner. Few developers, they are not comfortable defining all the actions that are possible inside their backend applications. Sometimes the number of actions that an end user can perform inside an application can be even in thousands as well, maintaining all those thousand action details and along with their authority details, it is going to be a very cumbersome process. Whereas with the role, the developers and the business analysts, they're going to maintain some data in some documentation like in Excel, where a user with a ROLE_ADMIN can perform so-and-so actions like ADDJOB, DeleteJob View job, whereas for a normal user, they will maintain inside the documentation that the normal user can only view the existing job. So this is how they're going to maintain inside the documentation, but coming to the spring security authorization implementation, they're going to enforce authorization by using simple role like ROLE_ADMIN,ROLE-EMPLOYER  and so on. . By using these roles concept, we can group a list of privileges or actions. When we are using these roles, we don't have to communicate to the spring security framework What are those list of privileges or actions? As long as the user has a given role, the spring security framework will allow invoking a REST API. Regardless of which approach you are using, the name of the authorities or roles, they are arbitrary in nature. That means you can have any name as an authority and any name as a role as well. So here I'm using these ADMIN, EMPLOYER, but these names can be any value and can be customized based upon your business requirements, both roles and authorities they're going to be represented by using the same contract to GrantedAuthority that we discussed in the previous lecture, because at the end of the day, what is an authority and the role? It is a normal String value. That's why spring security team, they used the same GrantedAuthority contract to represent both of them, but there'll be a confusion, right for the developers, how to identify whether someone is using authority style of authorization or role style of authorization to show some difference to the developers, what spring security team they recommend is, whenever a developer is following a role based authorization, the developer has to make sure that the roles names, they are starting with a prefix called role underscore. So this prefix specifies the difference between a role and an authority. So this is that default prefix, which I'm also following. If you check our roles database table, as of now, we stored all our roles information by using the same prefix. In case, if you don't want to use this default prefix, and if you want to use some custom prefix, which is specific to your project, you should be able to do that, you just have to configure a bean of type of GrantedAuthorityDefault. You have to create a method inside your project, which is going to return this bean as an output. So while you're creating the object of these GrantedAuthorityDefaults to this constructor, you can provide what is the prefix that you want to follow. So here I have mentioned the prefix as MYPREFIX_  if needed, we can mention company name as well. Like I can mention EAZYBYTES as a prefix as well. I recommend always going with the default role prefix, but if you have a unique requirement to have your own prefix, then this is the configuration that you need to do. But whatever method that you are defining, please make sure it is of type static. Because this will ensure that Spring publishes these configurations before initializing any other methods that has Spring security configurations. Now let's try to understand how to convey our requirements to the Spring security framework. In case, if a developer is using authorities approach to enforce the authorization, the developer can use methods like hasAuthority(), hasAnyAuthority(), hasAllAuthorities() and access() method So this hasAuthority9) method should be used whenever the developer want to enforce a authorization check that a user, which is having exactly same authority should be able to invoke a given API and a search scenarios. This method has to be used. You may have a question? how to invoke this method? So here I have a code snippet. So just like how we are invoking the permitAll() and authenticated() method while we are defining our Spring security configurations, very similarly after the requestMatchers(), you can invoke hasAuthority() method hasAnyAuthority() method hasAllAuthorities() method, but then you can pass your authority details. . So here I have mentioned my authority name as ROLE_ADMIN, but please make sure to use this authority style of authorization only if you want to enforce authorization based upon an individual action here, I have used ROLE_ADMIN, which is not a great example, but as I said, you can use actions like VIEW job, delete job, update job to this hasAuthority() method we need to always pass a single authority details. Coming to the hasAnyAuthority() to this method, you can pass multiple authority details and with that, the endpoint will be configured in such a way that if the end user has any of the authorities that you have provided, then the access will be provided. Whereas with the hasAllAuthorities(), the end user should have all the authorities that you have provided to this method. This method is going to be useful in the scenarios. If an end user can have multiple authorities for our application, this is not going to be useful because we know inside our application, we are maintaining a single authority or a single role for a given user, just like how we can invoke any of these three methods, we can also invoke a special method called access(). This method, it is going to accept spring expression language as an input, which is going to provide you unlimited possibilities to configure authorities, which are typically not possible with the above methods. When you're using this access method, you can use logical operators like and,or, and so on, let me show you a few examples around this access() method. So here I have a requirement to extract the path value from the request and based upon the path value, I want to perform the authorization. First, when I'm invoking the requestMatchers() method, I have mentioned my path. The path is profiles and there is a dynamic placeholder value name. After this request to matches, I'm invoking the access() method to this access() method, I'm trying to pass an object of WebExpressionAuthorizationManager. To this constructor I'm passing an expression saying that whatever dynamic name value that I receive as part of the path, that has to be equal to the name that I stored inside the authentication object. We know that whenever login is completed, that the spring security framework, it is going to represent the end user details by using these authentication object. Basically with this check, we're making sure that an end user should be able to access his own details, but not others details in the next example, I'm trying to check if the user has multiple roles. So here first I'm providing to the requestMatchers() that if the path is using a given rejects expression, then enforce the security requirements, by using this access() method I'm invoking the allOf() static method available inside the spring security framework. To this allOf() static method I'm passing the output that I'm going to get when I'm invoking another static method, like hasAuthority() to this all of we can pass any of the conditions. With these configurations, we are basically checking if the end user has all these authorities that we are providing. So this hasAuthority() method is going to check if the given user has a given authority. Similarly, this is also going to check for the manager authority. If both these methods return true to this all of method, then it is going to provide the access, otherwise that user is going to get 403 error. We can also check if the user has multiple roles by using this format as well. So here, if you see again I'm trying to invoke the constructor of WebExpressionAuthorizationManager, whatever expression that I'm passing inside this expression, I'm using the and operator. So both these hasAuthority() method, unless they return true value, the framework won't allow the request to be processed. These are the few of the common examples on how to use the access() method to enforce authorization, always use this method you have a complex authorization requirements, more and more examples are mentioned inside the official documentation as well, but for simple requirements, we can always use hasAuthority(), hasAnyAuthority() and hasAllAuthorities(). So this is all the story on how to enforce authorization by using authority concept. But what if you want to enforce authorization by using the roles concept? It's very easy. We just have to use the corresponding methods hasRole(), hasAnyRole(), hasAllRoles() method. When you are using roles, you can also use access() method as well, but please make sure that when you are invoking these as a role related methods, you don't have to pass the default prefix value behind the scenes, the Spring security framework is going to add that for you, but the same prefix we need to maintain inside the database or inside the storage system. I'm just to passing ADMIN as the input and behind the scenes, the framework is going to add ROLE_ADMIN, and this should match with exact value that you stored inside your database. So whatever you store inside the database, the same will be loaded as authorities into the authentication object. I hope you're clear on how to configure roles by using any of these methods. So this is all the theory from the next lecture, we can try to implement authorization by building a new REST API.

54764793.title: Understanding Sorting in Spring Data JPA
54764793.section: Mastering Authorization, Sorting & Pagination in Spring Boot
54764793.transcript: With the whatever REST API that we have developed so far inside this section, it has some drawbacks. It is always going to return all the new contact messages that we have inside the database. Think of a scenario where there are thousands of records and sending all those thousands of records in a single shot is going to bring some performance problems both on the backend side and on the client side. And it won't give a good feel as well to the end users if we are trying to display all the data in a single page. That's why as a next step, we're going to focus on on how to sort the data. Once we are clear about the sorting of the data, we are also going to explore about the pagination as well. As of now, when we invoke this API, we are getting the response with the random order. There is no sorting order, but we should give a flexibility to the client applications on how they want to sort the data when they are trying to fetch large amount of data. Sorting of the data by using Spring Data JPA is going to be very easy. Inside the Spring Data JPA framework, we have a sort to class. By creating the object of this class, we should be able to communicate our sorting requirements. Spring Data JPA supports sorting and pagination in the form of PagingAndSortingRepository interface. When we're talking about sorting inside Spring Data JPA, there are two types of sorting. The first one is Static Sorting by using the derived query methods. Static sorting means sort order is going to be fixed at compile time, cannot be changed dynamically by the user. And sorting is defined inside the repository method name or inside the query annotation. Static sorting is best when the sorting requirements are always same. So here I have defined a method by using derived query method concept. The method name is findByStatus and towards the end, I'm using OrderBy. To this OrderBy, we can provide what is a field that we want to consider to perform the sorting. After the OrderBy, since I have mentioned the name behind the scenes, once the framework faced all the records based upon the status, it is going to perform the sorting operation based upon the name value. After the field name, I also provided ASC, ASC means ascending. In case if you are looking to sort the data based upon descending order, you can define a method by similar to this one, here the method name that I provided is findByStatusOrderByCreatedAtDesc, Desc means descending order. In this scenario, what is going to happen is, the Spring Data JPA framework it is going to fetch all the list of customers based upon a given status, followed by it is going to perform the sorting by using the CreatedAt field and descending order. But for the scenarios where you are looking for the dynamic sorting capabilities, you can leverage the sort class that is provided by the Spring Data JPA framework. Using this sort, we can tell which field to sort by either ascending or descending order. And accordingly, behind the scenes, the JPA automatically creates the Order By query for us. In case if you want to provide ascending and descending information as well, after providing the field name details, you just have to invoke the corresponding method like ascending or descending and this object you can pass as an input to the methods that you are trying to invoke. But if you have a scenario where you want to sort based upon multiple fields, here is such example by using sort first, I'm trying to invoke the by() method and to this by() method, I'm providing my various sorting requirements. My first requirement is to sort the records based upon the role field and by using the ascending. So that's why here I can use sort.order.ascending and to this ascending, I can pass the field name. I'm also providing sort.order.descending and to the descending, I'm providing the field name as CreatedAt, with this sort object What is going to happen is, first to sort by role by using ascending order, followed by in case if the role is same sort by CreatedAt and descending order. In case if you are looking to use this sort object along with your derived query methods, then it is very easy. 54764807.title: Understanding Pagination in Spring Data JPA
54764807.section: Mastering Authorization, Sorting & Pagination in Spring Boot
54764807.transcript: Let's imagine an application where it is trying to send 50,000 records as a response to the client application. Sending such humongous amount of data at once will create performance issues and it is going to bring some bad user experience as well. Imagine you opened amazon.com. Inside the homepage, if they try to load all the products that they have, which are in millions, how do you feel? You are going to feel frustrated because it is going to take good amount of time to load all those products and displaying all of them inside one page is not a good user friendly. Instead, if the pagination is implemented, that is going to bring some better user experience. So by using pagination, we can break large data into smaller and manageable pages. We can think of this pagination like a book. While reading a book, we are not going to read all pages at once. Instead, we're going to read one page at a time, followed by we're going to move to the next page when needed. Pagination works the same way with the database records. To implement this kind of pagination functionality on the client side, from the backend, we should support the pagination concept. Luckily, Spring Data JPA also provides pagination support. Since pagination and sorting,they are very common scenarios. The framework team, they thought about it and they provided required interfaces and classes using which we should be able to implement these features very quickly. Around the pagination, inside the Spring Data JPA, we have a pageable interface. This interface is implemented by one of the most commonly used class with the name pageRequest. By using this pageRequest, we can communicate our sorting requirements and which page records we want to see and what is the page size as well. I'm going to show you an example on how to use this pageRequest in few seconds. Coming to the next important component inside the Spring Data JPA pagination, it is page. Whenever you're trying to implement pagination by using Spring Data JPA, the response that you're trying to send to the client application, it needs to be wrapped by using this page. As of now, whenever we want to return list of contact response DTOs, we're sending by using the list of contact response DTO. But with pagination, instead of list, we should use the page. When we're sending our DTOs inside the page, the response, it is going to contain the data along with some pagination related metadata. When we are using pagination, we don't have to write any SQL logic on using limit and offset keywords. Everything will be taken care of by the Spring Data JPA. So this is a basic pagination example, so by using the pageRequest class, I need to invoke the of() method. To this of() method, I'm passing two input parameters. The first input conveys what is the page that we want to access. Always the page index starts from zero, just like inside arrays, the first element start at zero index. In the similar lines, the first page will be represented by zero. So since I'm looking to access the very first page, I'm trying to pass zero here. Coming to the second input parameter, it represents what is a page size , with 10, I'm telling you can send up to 10 records inside a single page. In case if I'm passing 1 ,10, then the next 10 records will be sent inside the response. So once we have defined this pageRequest object, the same we can pass to any of the Spring Data JPA methods. So there is a findAll() method to this, we can pass the pageRequest object. And the return type from these methods will be page of the entity object. So page of entity object means this page will have multiple customer entity objects. This page is more than just a list, it provides methods like getContent(), which is going to provide the actual data that is stored inside these page object. Similarly, we can invoke getTotalElements(), which is going to give us total records that are stored inside this page object, next, we have getTotalPages(), which is going to give what are the total pages that are possible for the number of records that we have inside the database. For example, if I'm going with the page size as 10, and if there are 60 records inside my database, then the total pages will be 6. Even if I have 55, still the number of pages will be 6. It's just that the last page is going to have only five records. Using the page object, we can also invoke has next and hasPrevious() methods to understand if there are any next pages and previous pages. All these methods are going to be super helpful for building UI pagination controls. Now let's see an example where we can apply both pagination and sorting. When we are creating an object of pageRequest by using these of() method, it is also capable of accepting the sort object as the third input parameter. This time, along with the 0 and 10, I'm passing a sort object, So the sort objects can be created by using this by() method to this by() method I'm passing the field name followed by I'm invoking these descending order. So whatever sort object that is going to be created by these code, the same will be provided as an input to these of() method. So this time, this page request is capable of handling both the pagination and sorting. So it is going to fetch 10 records sorted by latest to first, as I said before, pagination and sorting is a best practice to follow. So this is an example on how to use pagination when we are defining our own custom finder methods. So here, this is my derived query method to this I'm passing the where condition followed by pageable object. Since pageRequest is an implementation of pageable, we can pass directly this pageRequest as an input. And whenever we are passing pageable requirements or pagination requirements to the method, the return type of the method has to be page of your entity name. So this is how we can invoke these findByStatus() method. First I'm creating a pageRequest object, the same I'm trying to pass as a second input parameter when I'm invoking these findByStatus() method, let's see that demo of pagination and sorting in the next lecture.

54765209.title: The N+1 Problem - The Silent Performance Killer
54765209.section: Mastering Advanced Queries in Spring Data JPA
54765209.transcript: Inside this section, we're going to explore on how to write custom complex queries by using @Query annotation. @Query is a very beautiful concept inside the Spring DataJPA. As a backend developer, it is mandatory for you to understand on how to use this annotation and under which scenarios it has to be used. Apart from @Query annotation, we're also going to discuss associated concepts as well inside this section. As of now, we are facing a problem while we are implementing a business requirement. The requirement is when we are trying to fetch the company details, we want to fetch only the active associated jobs. So this is the entity relationship that we have inside our application. So we established One-to-Many relationship between Company and Job. As we discussed in the previous lecture, we can't solve this problem by using Derived Query Methods. Like we can't define a method like this because the Derived Query Methods, they have certain problems or limitations. The first one is by using that Derived Query Methods, it is very hard to express complex joins, especially when you're trying to query the data from multiple tables. And these Derived Query Methods, they can't run the queries by using SQL concepts like FETCH JOIN. They also cannot control join type when i say join type, inside SQL, we have various kinds of joins like left join, right join, a simple join. Those kinds of joining of the multiple tables cannot be controlled by using that Derived Queries. And one more major problem with the Derived query Methods is, they cannot award n plus problem. I'll explain to you what is this N+ problem ? in few seconds. Overall, what I want to highlight from this slide is, please use that Derived Queries only for simple queries or for simple requirements. Now let's try to understand what is this N+1 problem. The N+problem occurs when Hibernate executes one query to fetch the parent entity and an additional query to fetch each child collection leading to performance issue. I know this definition is very hard to understand. Let me explain you by taking our company and job scenario. We know as per our database setup, a company can have multiple jobs. So that's why we are using One-To-Many relationship between these two entities. And this is the code that we have written inside the company's entity. Let's imagine we're trying to fetch all the company's data by using findAll() method. With this, what is going to happen? Behind the scenes, the Spring Data JPA will try to fetch all the company's record along with their associated jobs. So first to fetch the company's records data, it is going to first execute the query SElECT star from companies and all the company's data will be assigned to these company entity objects. But inside the company, we also have jobs related field, which is having One-To-Many relationship. By looking at these configurations, what Spring Data JPA will try to do is, it will try to load the jobs data as well for each company. Let's imagine we are using the default fetch type LAZY for this scenario. We know inside Spring Data JPA for the One-To-Many, the default fetch type is LAZY. And with that, when we first execute this method, only company's data will be present, but not the jobs data. Once this company's data is available under this company's list, let's imagine as part of the step two, the developer is trying to iterate all the company's data by using lambda streams or by using a for loop like this. And while iterating these company's data, the developer also trying to access the jobs details associated to each object. So here I'm trying to simply execute the company.getJobs() method followed by size() method to understand what is the size of jobs for a given company. When we are executing this line of code at this point of time, a query will be executed to fetch the jobs associated to this current company. So with that, what is going to happen? If you imagine there are 30 companies that we loaded from our database, this for loop, it is going to be iterated 30 times. And whenever we are executing these line, this kind of SQL query is going to be executed. SELECT star from jobs where company_id is equal to 1. So inside this for loop, this kind of SQL query, it is going to be executed 30 times. And here also, we executed one SQL query and it is a single query. So in total, we are executing 31 queries. Here the company is a parent entity, whereas jobs are the child entities. If you take the data setup of 30 companies, we are going to execute 31 SQL queries. But inside a real enterprise application, we may have thousands of companies or thousands of parent records. For all these thousand parent records, we're going to execute another extra query for each parent object to fetch the child records. If you take a scenario where there are 10,000 parent records available inside a database table, in that scenario we will end up executing 10,001 SQL queries. That's why this name N+1 has been given to this problem. What I'll do is, inside the next lecture, I'll show you a demo of these N+1 problem followed by, I'm also going to explain you what are the various approaches that we have to avoid these N+1 problem. Once we fix this N+1 problem, we can discuss about the @Query annotation as well on how to use them to solve the problem that we are facing with these business requirements.

54765217.title: Fixing the N+1 Problem with Hibernate Batch Fetching
54765217.section: Mastering Advanced Queries in Spring Data JPA
54765217.transcript: We know this API is going to be invoked from the UI application whenever we are trying to access the homepage. We can try to test this API one more time, but this time, we are going to look at the console logs to understand what is really happening. So I'll go to the console logs and I will clean the existing logs, followed by I'll open the application.properties inside the application.properties, please make sure you have these properties with the name spring.jpa.show-sql. So by using these properties, we are trying to log all the SQL queries that are being run by the Spring Data JPA framework. You can trigger the REST API either by using Postman or by using the UI application as well. If you have not set up the UI application, you can use this Postman approach. Since I have my UI application running, what I'll do is, I'll try to refresh this page, which might have invoked the REST API. Let me copy all these logs that I have, and I'm pasting them inside a text editor. As you can see here in total, around 4,000 lines of logs has been printed. So if you look at my logs, there is a SQL query that keep printed onto the console. So where it is trying to fetch all the jobs related data from the jobs table based upon a given company id. If you keep scrolling up, you will see the same query is printed multiple number of times. This is due to the reason of N+1 problem that we have discussed. So this gives a confirmation to us right now, inside our application, we have a N+1 problem. Why this is happening? If you go to the Company Service Impl, we are trying to load all the companies related data by invoking this findAll() method. And with that, all the company's data will be loaded from the database. If you open these companies entity class, as of now, when we defined One To Many configuration details here, we have not configured any fetch strategy we went with the default fetch strategy, which is lazy. And with that, what is going to happen? The child records of jobs, they're not going to be loaded when these line of code is executed. Instead the associated job details will be loaded only when these method is getting executed. So this method is going to be executed for each company object that we have, because here we are using map() method. And inside this method, we are invoking these getJobs() method. And at this point of time, a fresh SQL query will be executed behind the scenes to fetch the associated jobs for a given company. Since we have 30 different companies inside our backend database table, in total 31 SQL queries is going to be executed for this scenario. But if you are invoking the REST API using an UI application, you will see double number of SQL queries, like 31 into 2, you will see 62 queries logged into the console. The reason is whenever using react library inside and development environment, every request it is going to make two times behind the scenes, just to make sure that all the react re-rendering logic is working fine. So that's a behavior of react inside local and development environment. Whereas inside the production, that is not going to happen. But if you are testing the same REST API by using the postman, you will see only the 31 queries. So this gives a confirmation to us that right now there is an N+1 problem inside our application. . You may be thinking to fix this problem by simply using the eager fetch type. So here we can configure the fetch by using this fetch property to this I'm passing FetchType.EAGER. Even after using eager fetch type, still, we are going to see the problem. Let me go to the console and I will clean my entire console. In the UI, I'm going to refresh my homepage and these are the logs. Let me copy this logs and paste it here. This time also I'm seeing the same query is getting printed multiple times. That means our N+1 problem is still there even when we are using the eager fetch type. The reason is due to the hibernate behavior behind the scenes. Let me mention some comments here and inside these comments, I'm going to mention the reason on why it is not working. So whenever we are using Many To One and One To One in this scenario, only one child record will be present for a given parent. This type of associations in other words, we can also call them as single valued associations, with these kind of associations hibernate it is always going to use join in the main query. When I say main query, when it is trying to fetch the parent record at that point of time itself, it is going to use join query and load all the child records as well. But coming to the collection valued associations, like One To Many and Many To Many, where there will be multiple child records for a given parent record under such scenarios, the hibernate default strategy is not to use join by default. Instead, it often uses a secondary select one query per collection unless configured otherwise. Why hibernate following this strategy is, to avoid performance issues. Now, even after configuring these fetch type, our problem is not solved. That's why I'm removing that. But there are some alternatives or solutions to solve the N+1 problem. The first one is inside the application.properties, you just have to define a property here just after these hibernate format SQL property I have pasted a new property with the name spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.default_batch_fetch_size. . To this, I am passing a default value of 50 otherwise I can change the default value by these environment variable as well. Whenever we are using these property inside our project, it is an indication to the hibernate to always try to run the queries as a batch with a given size. Whenever hibernate identifies that the framework is trying to load the child records for a given parent to record lazily under such scenarios, instead of loading the child records for each parent individually, it is going to batch the SQL query and that batch size we can configure as well here as of now, I have mentioned 50. That means up to for 50 parents, the child records are going to be fetched as a batch. As of now, inside our data, we have only 30 parent records, which are company records, but still I'm trying to mention higher value like 50, because this is a reasonable number for production setup as well. So this is a global setting for your entire application. When you are defining this property, this property is going to be leveraged for all entities you have inside your backend application. I'm also going to show you on how to override this global setting for a given specific entity. First, let's try to test our REST API with this new property. I'm doing the build, once a build is completed, I'll go to the console and clear all the logs. This time I'll try to invoke the REST API from my postman and I got my successful response. Let me copy the entire logs. As you can see here, this time, the logs are only of 63 lines. If you closely look at the SQL statement, there are only two. The first one is used to fetch all the company's data from the company's table, followed by we have the second query, which is used to fetch the jobs data from the jobs table by using these where condition. Inside the where condition, the framework this time, it mentioned all the company ID details into these in query. If you try to count all these question marks, there'll be 30 question marks. In case if we have 80 company records, then you will see the second query printed two times, because the batch size that we have set is 50. So there'll be two different SQL queries, one with the 50 and the other one with the 30 company IDs. With this small setting, right now we are saved from N+1 problem, but we didn't solve that problem entirely. In the next lecture, I'll explain you on how to overcome the N+1 problem completely. But whatever solutions right now we are discussing, these are also required under certain scenarios. I'll explain about those scenarios in a few minutes. Now let me go to the company entity here, just above the jobs field. I'm going to mention @BatchSize annotation to these. I'll mention the size as 10 this time, because I want to show you the demo on how this batch size is going to work. So whenever we are mentioning batch size inside an entity, then whatever global batch size that we have configured inside this properties file, it will be overrided by the framework for the specific entity and its child records. So let me save these changes, do the build once the build is completed, I will clean the console followed by, I will invoke the REST API from the postman. These are my logs. Let me copy them and paste it here. So this time we have 130 lines of logs. Coming to the query related to jobs, there'll be three queries. So this is the first one. Next we have the second one followed by third one. And just above these, we are going to have the main query, which is going to fetch the company's data. So this is working based upon our expectation. But if you are testing the same API from the UI application, as I said before, you will see the queries double number of times because the react library, it will send all the requests two times during the local development. We try to solve the N+1 problem by using both these approaches. The first one is by defining this property inside the application.properties file. The next one is by using the @BatchSize annotation on top of the child field inside an entity. You can use both these approaches, but don't be under the assumption this solves the entire N+1 problem. Batch fetching does not remove the N+1 entirely instead, it converts the N+1 queries into one plus N divided by batch size queries. In order to get rid of N+1 problem completely, we need to prefer running the queries by using join fetch. This is what we are going to explore in the next lecture. But I still recommend you maintaining this property inside your backend application always. Because this is a global setting. And even if a junior developer is not doing a good job in querying the multiple tables by using the join fetch, under such scenarios, this property is going to save your application from the performance issues. And this join fetch solution also, it is specific to each use case, and it is not going to solve your problem entirely at the global level. So that's why as a safe net, always define this property inside your application.properties file. With this, I'm assuming you're clear on how to solve the N+1 problem by using the Hibernate batch fetching strategy.

54765223.title: Introducing @Query and JPQL – Writing Custom JPA Queries
54765223.section: Mastering Advanced Queries in Spring Data JPA
54765223.transcript: By using @Query annotation, we can provide our own custom queries to the Spring Data JPA and Hibernate framework. This annotation we need to define on top of an abstract method inside the Repository interface. So here you see, I have a method with the name findAllWithJobsByStatus(). On top of this method, I have mentioned the @Query annotation. To this @Query annotation, we need to provide the query that needs to be executed whenever someone is invoking this abstract method. So whenever a method has a @Query annotation on top of it, then Spring Data JPA is not going to consider the name of the method, which means that derived method name concept is not going to be implemented for this method by the Spring Data JPA. Because the framework identified that there is a @Query annotation on top of this method and it is going to execute this query simply and return the results. To this @Query annotation, we can provide the query in two different formats. The first one is by using the JPQL and the other one is by using the native query. I'm going to explain you the difference between them in a few seconds, but before that, let's try to understand why @Query annotation is the right choice for complex requirements. By using @Query annotation, we can communicate what exactly we want to the framework. There won't be any compromises. We can decide to use which join, like I can decide to use normal JOIN or LEFT JOIN or RIGHT JOIN. We can decide to use fetch strategy or not. We can decide on the filtering conditions as well. These filtering conditions can belong to any table. It can be parent or child table, regardless of where the filtering columns are available we should be able to apply our filtering conditions by using the @Query annotation. And whenever we are using the @query annotation, it is going to solve the N+1 problem completely. So here, if you see inside the query, along with the join, I'm also using the fetch keyword. With this fetch, I'm telling to the Spring Data JPA and the Hibernate framework, please fetch the child records as well while you are trying to fetch the parent records, which means the framework, it is going to fetch the both parent and the child records by using the same query. There won't be any extra SQL calls. Always our intent is going to be very clear to the framework and to other developers as well. When we are defining our requirements by using the @Query annotation, they don't have to do any guessing work from the method names like we have to do when we are using derived query methods. But does that mean derived query methods is bad? Off course not. Derived query methods, they are super helpful for the developers whenever they want to query the data from a single table. Now let's try to understand on what is the JPQL and native query. JPQL means Java Persistence Query Language. Usually whenever we want to execute an SQL query onto the database, we will use SQL syntax. For example, this is an SQL query that I want to run to fetch the company's and its associated jobs based upon the given status. Here I have to use distinct because I want to fetch only the distinct company records. We know for a given company, there can be multiple child job records. And that's why when we run these query, we're going to get thousand records, which are going to have duplicate company's data. But I want to fetch only the distinct company's data that's why we need to use these distinct keyword inside the SQL query. Since I want to fetch all the columns inside the company's table, I need to use C.* here And next after the from, I'm mentioning the company's database table name. So this is the table name that we have inside the database. So for this table, I have given a shortcut synonym C followed by I'm trying to join these company's table with other table that I have inside the database with the name jobs. So here I'm using a simple join in case if you want to use left join, you need to mention left here and to the jobs table also, I'm giving a short synonym j. Whenever we want to run a join query, we need to mention what is the condition that the database has to consider when running that join query. That's why immediately here, we need to mention on and here c.id. So inside the company's table, there is a id column and this has to match with the company_id column inside the jobs table, because this column has a foreign key reference with the primary key column inside the company's table. So this is going to fetch all the companies and its associated jobs. But while fetching the jobs data, we also want to apply an extra filtering condition. That's why we need to mention where condition followed by j.status.  Inside the jobs table we are going to have a status column inside this column I'm going to look for jobs record only having the value active. So this is a normal SQL query. So these type of SQL queries, we call them as Native SQL query. So this is one format of query that we can provide to the @Query annotation. Now let's try to understand what is JPQL. Whenever we want to provide a query by using the JPQL, we need to think in terms of entity objects and properties. We should not write the actual table names and actual column names. Instead, we need to refer to the entity class name and field names that we have defined inside the entities. So here, if you see, most of the native SQL keywords are same inside JPQL as well. That's why here there is a SELECT and DISTINCT. But here instead of C.*, we just have to mention c. This c is a short synonym for the company entity class. Inside JPQL with this syntax, we're telling, I want to read all the fields present inside these entity class. The company's database table inside our backend project, it is represented by using an entity class with the name Company. The same class name I have to mention here. Next I need to mention JOIN and followed by I need to mention fetch as well. Using this fetch, I'm telling to the Spring Data, JPA and Hibernate framework to fetch all the child records as well as part of a single query. So that's the importance of this fetch. Next I need to mention what is another table or the child table that I want to consider while fetching the data, inside the C which means inside the company entity, the jobs relationship is going to be represented by a field with the name jobs. That's why here I need to mention c.jobs. So the join query will be applied between company and c.jobs. And this c.jobs data is going to be represented by using a short notation j. So this time I don't have to mention these on query because based upon the One-To-Many and Many-To-One configurations that we have defined inside the company and the job entity class, the Spring Data JPA is aware about the foreign key relationship. That's why behind the scenes, when it is actually running the SQL query on top of the database server, it is going to add these behind the scenes. Now coming to the where condition I have to mention, because where conditions depends upon my business logic. So under the where condition, we have to use j.status is equal to ACTIVE. So whatever status that you're seeing here, it has to match with the corresponding field name inside the job entity class. In our scenario, both the database column name and the field name are same. That's why here you're able to see exact values. But in case if your column name and field names are different, please make sure to mention the exact column name here and the exact field name here. The primary difference between JPQL and native SQL query is JPQL it is always going to work on entities and its fields, but not on tables and columns. So we can think of JPQL as a SQL that speaks Java. Here you may have a question? Madan, why should we learn JPQL when I'm already familiar with SQL? It's a very fair question. Let me answer the same. When we are using JPQL, we are going to get a lot of advantages. For example, when I'm writing this query, I don't have to provide this on condition. The framework is going to understand automatically. The second advantage is, the developer never have to know what is the table name, what is a column name, because developer can always refer to the entity class so that the developer never have to access the database to understand the table name, column name, and so on. The greatest advantage of JPQL is it is portable across databases. If you are going with the native SQL queries, these native SQL queries, they are going to change from database to database. The way you write for MySQL, Oracle, there'll be some minor differences because few of the keywords supported by Oracle, they are not going to be supported by MySQL. So that kind of precaution you need to take care when you're providing your SQL queries by using the native format. But with JPQL, you don't have to worry about the underlying database. You're always going to define your requirements by using the syntax and behind the scenes, the framework is going to take care of generating the corresponding native SQL query based upon the database that you have connected. So this is the example of JPQL inside the next slide, I also explained about what is a native query as well. The JPQL queries, they works very well with the entity relationships. We're going to see a demo on what are the advantages that we get when we are defining our queries by using JPQL compared to the native SQL queries. I hope you're clear with this discussion. Inside the next lecture, let's see a demo of these JPQL and @Query annotation.

54765233.title: Understanding @NamedQuery and @NamedNativeQuery
54765233.section: Mastering Advanced Queries in Spring Data JPA
54765233.transcript: There is an annotation with the name @NamedQuery, which is closely aligned to the @Query annotation. Let's try to understand the purpose of this annotation. Sometimes we as a developers don't want to clutter our repository interface by defining many @Query annotations. And when we are defining a query directly on top of the repository method, it also brings some drawbacks. We can't reuse that query in some other repository as well. So to overcome these challenges, we can use the @NamedQuery annotation. So this annotation you need to mention on top of your entity class. For example, I have an entity class with the name Company. On top of this class, I have mentioned @NamedQuery annotation. While using this annotation,first we need to define the name attribute. By using this name property, we need to define a name to our query. So usually the standard way of naming the query is first, you need to mention the name of your entity, which is Company dot whatever name that you would like. So this is the name that I'm trying to use, fetchCompaniesWithJobs ByStatus, followed by you need to mention comma, and by using this query property, you can provide your query. Here the query needs to be provided by using the JPQL syntax only. If you want to use native SQL query, there is another annotation, which I'm going to explain to you in few seconds. The beauty of this approach is the same query can be reused in many other repositories. That is one advantage. The other advantage is, your repository interface, it is going to look very clean and simple. If you are maintaining the method name, same as the name that you have defined after the entity name, then the Spring Data JPA framework is going to do the magic. So whenever someone is invoking this method, it is going to look for if there is any query defined with the same method name under the company entity class. Since there is a query defined with this same name, this query is going to be executed. In case if you want to use a different method name inside your repository, that is also perfectly fine but in this scenario, you just need to mention @Query annotation. And by using this @Query annotation, you need to invoke the name property to this name property you need to provide whatever name that you have defined here. So Company.fetchCompaniesWithJobsByStatus. And this is your JPA method name, which is different from what you have defined here. So this is also perfectly possible. But I strongly recommend you going with the same method name as you define here, because you will get saved from not mentioning this @Query annotation. The benefits of named query is all queries that are going to be centralized in one place that is inside the entity class. All these queries, they are going to be validated at startup. Any errors will be caught early. The queries that we have defined here, they can be reusable in multiple repositories and your repository interfaces, they are going to look very clean. Usually developers, they don't open entity classes a lot. They will only open repository interfaces to a great extent because they may want to invoke various kinds of methods based upon their business requirements. So that's the whole reason on why developers, they'll try to keep their repositories clean, even though they're fine making their entity classes looks clutter. And one more advantage is, with the named query, since you are going to mention all your annotations on top of the class, so the content of the class is always going to look clean and simple. When we're using these @NamedQuery annotation, we can define the query only by using the JPQL syntax. But what if you have a requirement to provide the native SQL query? For such scenarios, you need to use another annotation with the name, @NamedNativeQuery. This is also going to work very similarly like nativeQuery. You just have to pass a name, query. This time the query has to be provided by using a native SQL format. But apart from name and query, this time while using the @NamedNativeQuery annotation, you should also invoke the result class property and to this, you need to mention on how the data from the SQL needs to be mapped to a Java class. So here, since we are looking to map the data to a company entity class, I have mentioned company.class. But you may have a question, why this result class is became mandatory for this @NamedNativeQuery, but not for the @NamedQuery. On top of that, you may also argue, why can't the Spring Data JPA framework can guess the returned class name based upon my method signature. So let's try to understand the same. We know JPQL works with entities. So JPA already knows which entity to return from the query itself. So inside the query itself, we are going to mention all the entity class details. Whereas native SQL queries, they are going to work with tables and columns that's why JPA has no idea how to map a row to an entity class. And the framework cannot interpret the return class details from the repository method signatures, because whatever methods that we define inside the repository, they are not going to be parsed while these queries are getting compiled. We know all the queries that we define by using the @NamedNativeQuery and the @NativeQuery, they all are going to be validated during the startup itself. So during the startup, since the method signatures inside the repository is not yet evaluated, the framework cannot interpret the result class details. So that's why we must explicitly specify the result class to tell JPA how to map the result. We also have plural form of this annotation with the @NamedQueries and @NamedNativeQueries. In case, if you are looking to define multiple @NamedQuery and @NamedNativeQuery inside an entity class, you can mention them under these plural form of the annotation. For example, first I have to mention @NamedQueries and inside these, I can mention any number of @NamedQuery, within the parentheses first I need to mention Calibri says, followed by I can mention @NamedQuery and to the @NamedQuery, as usual, we need to provide the name and query in the JPQL format. This way I can provide any number of @NamedQueries, each of them, they should be separated by using comma. The same syntax applies for @NamedNativeQueries as well. Let's see the demo of these @NamedQuery and @NamedNativeQuery in the next lecture.

In all those scenarios, you need to repeat these kind of complex code. This is the very first major problem that you're going to face. All these objects, they are going to work together to execute a flow or a scenario. In this kind of setup, it is the responsibility of the developer to create all those objects manually by using the new operator. On top of that, the developers also, they have to take care of injecting the dependencies. And with this kind of setup, the objects are going to have the tight coupling between them. So here in this scenario, the UserService is tightly coupled with EmailService. Moving on to the next set of problems. The next problem is around the configuration management. In real enterprise applications, we may have to deal with a lot of properties that we have defined inside the files like config.properties. So with the plain code Java, in order to build a REST APIs, the developers have to use the servlets. By using these servlets, the developers, they should be able to build the REST APIs. But it's a very complex process where the developers, they have to configure the servlets inside the web.xml, they have to define the URL mappings, manage the servlet lifecycle. And these servlets, they are going to process the request and response in the form of HttpServletRequest and HttpServletResponse. So inside these objects only, you're going to have the payload data that's sent by the client.
