The developer should also take care of converting the objects to JSON when sending the response to the client applications. When we're using the HTTP protocol for the communication, the Java objects are not going to make any sense. We need to send the data to the client applications, either in the form of XML or JSON. So this way, there are a good amount of problems that developers are going to face when they decide to develop a web application with core Java alone. Once our dependent jars are downloaded, we need to place them under the lib folder and this lib folder we need to manually add to the class path of the web application. Basically class path is a location where your web application is going to look for the dependent classes or dependent libraries. And sometimes one jar may depend on 10 more jars or 10 more libraries. To overcome all these challenges, Maven came into picture Maven is a build tool, which is going to help us as a dependency manager apart from acting as a dependency manager, it is also going to help the developers to compile the application, to deploy the application, to install the application. We're going to discuss a few of the most common Maven commands in a few minutes. But before that, let's try to understand what are the most basic details of Maven. When we are using Maven as a build tool inside a web application, we are going to get a file with the name pom.xml. So what is pom? Project Object Model. Inside these files, we need to define all the dependencies that our project has. For example, if I define these dependencies by mentioning the group ID, artifact ID, and the version number, the Maven is going to take care of downloading these dependencies and setting up them under the classpath. Just like Maven, we have another famous build tool as well with the name Gradle. Gradle is not going to have pom.xml it is going to have another file name since Gradle is out of the scope of this course i'm not discussing about it, but any build tool that you take like Maven or Gradle, they all going to work conceptually in the same style. Maybe the syntax or the file names will keep changing from one build tool to another build tool. As i said, we can define our dependencies inside pom.xml by following the syntax. If you have multiple dependencies, you need to repeat the syntax more number of times. So here we are providing details like group ID, artifact ID, and version. Let me explain the purpose of these properties. Every library or dependency that we want to use, it is going to have an unique address. So just like how our homes has address in the similar line, every dependency or library that we are going to use inside Maven, it is going to have an unique address and at the group ID, we need to mention which company or organization build that dependency or that library or that framework. For example, here I have mentioned the group ID as org.springframework.boot. So this gives a clarity to anyone looking at this group ID. It's a confirmation to them that so-and-so dependency is developed by an organization with the name spring framework under the technology called spring boot or boot. This can be the technology name or framework name or the department name it can be anything. Coming to the artifact ID, it is like the project name. So under the spring boot, we have various dependencies. For example, to develop a web application, we have to mention this artifact ID, which is going to download this dependency from these group ID location. And the last detail that we need to provide is, version. So every framework and dependencies, they are going to evolve from time to time. By mentioning these version number, we can try to use a exact version of a dependency. In simple language, you can assume group ID is like a city name whereas artifact ID is like the street name and the version is like the house number. So if you take the same terminology to the dependency management, so the group ID will be the company name or the organization name and the artifact ID will be the project name and the version will be the specific version that we are trying to use. In case if Google has developed a framework or library, they're going to mention the group ID as com.google. Most of the time, this will be the domain name of their website in the reverse order. Under the project name, they can give any project since Google can develop hundreds of projects so for each project they're going to have a unique name, which can be identified by using this artifact ID. Let's imagine a developer has mentioned that dependency inside the pom.xml. Now let's try to understand what is going to happen behind the scenes. As soon as the dependencies are defined under the pom.xml Maven build tool, it will search for that specific library or dependency. Once the search is completed, it is going to download the dependencies automatically. If the given library is dependent on other libraries, the maven is also going to take care of downloading all the required dependent libraries as well by following the concept called transitive dependencies. Once all the required dependencies are downloaded, maven is going to keep them in the correct class path of your web application. As you can see here, there is no manual effort required. The developer, they just have to define the dependencies that they are planning to use inside their project. Here, you may have a question from where the maven is going to download all these libraries? Does the maven is going to visit all the multiple websites inside the web? Off course not instead Maven, it is going to download the dependencies from a central place on the internet called Maven Central Repository. Any organization or open source community who build a framework or a library, they will make sure to publish their work inside the Central Repository. You can consider this like an Amazon website for Java libraries. So once that downloading of the dependencies is completed, Maven it is going to place all the dependencies inside a Local repository. For example, inside the Mac operating system, the maven it is going to store all the dependent jar files under a path called .m2 / repository. For windows, I'll show you how to check this path in a few minutes. With this, what is going to happen is in future if you end up setting up or developing another project, which requires the same jars then the maven, it is not going to download them again from the Central Repository. Instead, it is going to reuse them, which are present in the Local Repository. With this, what is going to happen? We are going to save a lot of time and network bandwidth. I have given some chat flow on what is going to happen as soon as we define the dependencies under the pom.xml, maven is going to check whether the library is already present inside the Local Repo. If it is not found, it is going to download from the Central Repo, which is from the internet once the download is completed, the same jars will be added to the project class path. So once our dependents are added to the project, we can either build, test, package, or run the application by using various Maven commands. The next command that we can run is mvn validate. When you run this command, it is going to basically check if your project is properly configured. It is going to validate whether your pom.xml is valid XML if you have defined all the required fields, like group ID, artifact ID, version number, is the project structure is correct, are all necessary plugins are available basically the sanity test of your project is going to be performed when you are executing this command. We will see the demo of this as well in a few seconds. After the validate, we have mvn compile, which is going to take care of compiling all your Java source code. When you run this command, it is going to read all your source code from this folder and generate the . class files and place them under the target folder. Next we have mvn test command, which is going to run all your unit testing code. If you write any unit testing code inside the test folder, where do we write the unit testing code? Under the src test java folder. When you trigger this command, it is going to execute all your unit testing code that you might have developed by using JUnit or any other library. Once the execution of the unit testing is completed, this command, it is also going to show you how many unit testing cases are passed, how many of them failed. You're going to have a detailed report. Usually in real applications, while deploying the web application into an higher environment, the deployment tools, they're going to run this command just to make sure the entire application is working properly. After the mvn test, we have mvn package command. When you run this package command, it is going to package the project into a jar file or a War file based upon your configurations inside the pom.xml. So whatever package that got generated, it is going to be placed under that target folder. We can use this jar to deploy our application into a higher environment server like development, uat or production. Next we have mvn verify command. Next we have mvn deploy command. There is a small difference between mvn install and mvn deploy command. When you are running the mvn deploy command, it is going to perform all the previous steps like compiling, triggering unit testing, packaging, verifying and generating the jar once the jar is generated, this time it is going to publish the jar into a remote maven repository so that the same can be used by other developers or other projects or by third party vendors as well. So basically when you are running this command, it is not going to deploy your application into some server. Instead the jar of your application or War of your application will be deployed as an artifact into the remote repository like maven repository or any other repository that you have configured so that the other organizations can reuse your library. Next we have mvn site command. By using this command, we can generate the project documentation website for a maven project automatically. 54765851.title: Spring Core - IoC and DI Explained the Easy Way
54765851.section: Spring Core & Maven — The Fast-Track Foundation
54765851.transcript: So far, we developed a simple Maven project, and inside this project, we also added Spring context-related dependencies so that we can get started with the Spring core concepts. But before we try to see the demo of these core concepts, I want to give a quick introduction about all the Spring core concepts. If you go to the Spring official website, under these projects, you will be able to see there are so many sub-projects like Spring Boot, Spring Framework, Spring Cloud, Spring Data, Spring Batch, Spring Security, Spring AI, and so on. There are a good amount of sub-projects inside the Spring ecosystem. For all these sub-projects, at the center, we have some Spring core concepts. Inside this lecture, let's try to understand what are those concepts. As I said before, at the center of the Spring ecosystem, we have some Spring core concepts and Spring core library. These Spring core concepts, they are going to be leveraged by all other sub-projects in the Spring ecosystem. So what is this Spring core? Spring core is the foundation of the entire Spring ecosystem. It provides the fundamental principles, base classes, and core mechanisms that power all other Spring modules and projects. The entire Spring Framework and its related projects are built on top of the Spring core. You can assume the Spring core as a powerhouse. In real life, we will not see that each of the homes, they are having their own power generated instead, all the homes, they are connected to a power distribution system. From a centralized location, the power will be distributed to all the homes. The same applies here as well. These Spring core concepts will be leveraged by all the projects that we have in the Spring ecosystem. So what are the key components of Spring core? So these are the five important components of Spring core. They are IOC, IOC means Inversion of Control, DI, DI means Dependency Injection next we have Beans, followed by IOC Container, followed by Context. Let's try to understand all of them one by one. The first one that we have is Inversion of Control. So Inversion of Control is a software design principle that defines how objects are created and managed in a program. This principle recommends, don't create the objects by yourself, instead, outline a way for their creation and dependency management. Usually, in traditional core Java programs, developers create objects manually by using new keyword. Whereas with IOC, instead of the programmer managing the flow of the application, a framework or a library or a service takes over this responsibility, ensuring objects and their dependency are handled automatically. Inversion of Control is a software design principle that is not only specific to Spring. If you go to any other languages and their frameworks, you will see the same IOC is being used there. Spring also adopted this design principle to make developer life easy. When we are using Spring framework to develop a web application, by using this IOC, the Spring framework, it is going to manage object creation and their dependencies automatically. So with this, what is the advantage that we are going to get? It reduces the manual coding and make applications more flexible. Inversion of Control is about the objects creation. When an object is getting created, the Spring framework, it is also going to understand what are the dependencies that a given object has. So if a given object has a dependency on another object, the IOC container, it is also going to take care of creating the dependent objects as well. Once all the required objects are created, the wiring between them or the dependencies between them will be established by the Spring itself by using Dependency Injection. So Dependency Injection is a design pattern used to implement Inversion of Control. We can assume Inversion of Control as an interface, whereas Dependency Injection as an implementation class of that interface. So with the Dependency Injection, the responsibility of creating and managing bins is transferred from the application to the Spring IOC container. So inside the Spring framework, there is a software component or there is a library which is responsible to implement these Dependency Injection and IOC. Such a component, we call it as Spring IOC container. I'm going to share more details about Spring IOC container in few seconds. So by adopting IOC and Dependency Injection, we're going to avoid the tight coupling between the objects by allowing the framework to dynamically provide the required dependencies at runtime. Dependency Injection is a way of providing the required dependencies into a class instead of creating them inside the class. Remember, they never have to create the required dependencies inside the class. Instead, the required objects or dependencies, they will be created outside of the class by the Spring IOC container. Once these dependencies are created, they will be injected as dependencies into the target class. So this is going to bring loose coupling between the objects. Based upon the configurations that we provide, Spring automatically injects all the required objects. There are a good amount of advantages when we adopt these IOC and Dependency Injection. The first and major advantage is loose coupling between components. Let's imagine there is a user class and inside this User class, whenever end User done with their registration or whenever the end user placed an order inside an e-commerce application, let's imagine we have to trigger a notification, maybe to the mobile number of the user or to the email of the user based upon our requirements. So the notification can go in multiple ways like email, SMS or WhatsApp. When we are using Spring IOC and Spring Dependency Injection concepts, this user component, it never have to know what is the actual implementation of notification service that is taking care of sending the notifications to the end user. At runtime, based upon our configurations, the Spring framework, it is going to inject one of these three objects as a dependency. For example, if I'm injecting email, then the notification will go through the email. Whereas if I'm injecting SMS object as a dependency into User, then in such scenario, the notification will go through the SMS and the same applies for WhatsApp as well. So if you see here, the User and these components, they are loosely coupled with each other. In future, when I want to switch between these notification providers, or if I want to add a new notification provider, I don't have to make lot many changes and this User logic will never get affected with these notification services. So this is called loose coupling. If you want some real life analogy, I can also give you a good example. What is loose coupling? Loose coupling means that different parts of a system are not tightly dependent on each other. Instead, they are connected in a way that changes in one part have little or no impact on others. You can take a real life example. In all our homes, we have a television and remote control. At any point of time, if remote is not working, what are you going to do? Are you going to buy a new television? Of course not. Instead, you're going to buy a new remote. So this is a classic example of loose coupling. Both this TV and remote, they are connected by a common way of communication like infrared signals. So as long as you get some other remote, which operates by using infrared signals, you should be able to establish the connection between your TV and the new remote. The same applies in real software components as well. When we develop our logic by adopting loose coupling, whenever we want to make modifications, it is going to be easy. We can switch the dependencies or we can try with the new dependencies without breaking other logic. Apart from easier to modification, we are also going to have other advantages like better reusability, improved flexibility. I hope you're clear. Apart from loose coupling, we also have other advantages like minimize the amount of code. Since the Spring framework is going to take the responsibility of objects creation and establishing the dependencies between them, developers, they have to write very less code. These principles, they also make unit testing easy with the different marks. For example, in the QA environment or in the lawyer environment or in my local system, instead of using these actual notification services, I can use some fake notification service, which is going to act like it has sent some notification, but in reality it is not going to send any notification. So that kind of using different marks during unit testing or during the development is going to be easy. The system's maintainability and module reusability is also going to be increased. These principles, they also going to allow concurrent and independent development. Let's consider the same example. Let's imagine there are two developers they are given two different responsibilities. The first developer is given the responsibility of developing user logic, whereas the other developer is given to develop this notification logic. So since these are not tightly coupled with each other, the developer one, he can continue with his logic by using some fake notification service. Once the actual notification services are ready, the integration can be done between them. And coming to the last advantage, replacing of the modules is not going to have any side effect on other modules. For some reason, if the email service is not working properly, then I can replace this email notification service with either SMS service or WhatsApp service. I hope you got some clarity around what is IOC and dependency injection. As part of the next lecture, let's try to understand the remaining three components of the Spring Core.
